On Mondays, the clinic hosted math classes; on Tuesdays, there were courses in reading; Wednesday’s focused on handwriting and composition; and there were lessons in geography and history on Thursdays. On Friday mornings, the children went for walks in the garden, and on Saturdays, they worked on arts and crafts. Afternoons were devoted to rest and play, with plenty of free time built into the schedule so the children could hang out together and pursue their own interests. After church on Sundays, they spent the afternoon organizing group games and putting on plays.

清晨以维克托琳修女带领的一小时体操和运动开始，常常伴随着音乐。随后为孩子们安排学术课程。周一诊所开设数学课，周二有阅读课程，周三专注于书法和写作，周四则教授地理和历史。周五上午孩子们在花园散步，周六进行手工艺术创作。下午是休息和游戏时间，日程中安排了充足的自由活动，让孩子们可以结伴玩耍、发展个人兴趣。周日做完礼拜后，整个下午他们都会组织集体游戏和编排戏剧表演。



Michaels was initially baffled by the apparent lack of systematized methodology for treating the young patients. Upon making inquiries as to the psychoanalytical frameworks that guided the conduct of Asperger and his colleagues, he was told that they had “no such formulations to offer.” In an era when psychology was striving to prove its empirical validity by embracing standardized tests like the Stanford-Binet intelligence scale (commonly known as the IQ test), the ward’s emphasis on “looking with open eyes,” as Asperger put it, seemed like a throwback to the nineteenth century, when clinicians like Jean-Martin Charcot encouraged his patients to make art. Michaels was shocked to see happy children at play, throwing a ball around, instead of sitting “fixed in numbered seats to await their turn, as we in America are accustomed to see them.”

迈克尔斯最初对治疗这些年轻患者时明显缺乏系统化方法感到困惑。当他询问指导阿斯伯格及其同事进行精神分析的理论框架时，得到的答复是他们"没有这样的理论体系可提供"。在那个心理学界正通过采用斯坦福-比奈智力量表（俗称智商测试）等标准化测试来证明其实证有效性的时代，这个病房强调的"睁大眼睛观察"——正如阿斯伯格所言——似乎倒退到了十九世纪，当时像让-马丁·沙尔科这样的临床医生鼓励患者进行艺术创作。迈克尔斯震惊地看到快乐的孩子们在玩耍、互相扔球，而不是像"我们在美国习惯看到的那样，坐在编号座位上等待轮到他们"。



After a few days in Vienna, however, he was won over. In a report published in the American Journal of Orthopsychiatry, Michaels marveled, “In this ‘age of technocracy’ with its overemphasis on technical procedures, it is rather unusual to find a highly personal approach characterized by an appreciable absence of what are ordinarily regarded as rigid methods, apparatus, statistics, formulae and slogans.” Instead, “great value is placed on intuition gained . . . while working, or better, while living with the children.”

然而，在维也纳待了几天后，他被说服了。在一份发表于《美国儿童精神病学杂志》的报告中，迈克尔斯惊叹道：“在这个‘技术统治时代’，人们过度强调技术流程，能发现一种高度个性化的方法实属罕见——这种方法显著缺乏通常被视为刻板的手段、仪器、统计、公式和口号。”相反，“人们高度重视在工作中，更确切地说，在与孩子们共同生活中获得的直觉。”



Even the standards of “normal” conduct on the ward seemed surprisingly open-ended. The criterion for classifying behavior as normal or abnormal was the challenges that it created for the individual child, not whether it strayed from an idealized template of psychological health. “Fundamentally there appears to be no special interest in the differences between normal and abnormal,” Michaels wrote, “as it is felt that theoretically this is unclear, and practically it is of no great importance.”

就连病房里“正常”行为的标准也出人意料地开放。判断行为正常与否的标准，在于它给个别孩子带来的挑战，而非是否偏离了理想化的心理健康模板。“从根本上说，人们对正常与异常之间的差异似乎并无特别兴趣，”迈克尔斯写道，“因为人们认为这在理论上并不明确，在实践中也无足轻重。”



Another valuable window into the mind-set of the staff is provided by Weiss in a paper on conducting “play interviews” with children in nursery school. Play therapy was a fad in the 1930s, promoted by psychoanalysts like Hermine Hug-Hellmuth, Anna Freud, and Melanie Klein, who subjected their young patients’ behavior to heavy-handed interpretations in the Freudian style. (To Klein, for example, one boy’s fascination with doors and door handles was really about “the penetration of the penis into the mother’s body . . . Doors and locks stood for the ways in and out of her body, while the door-handles represented the father’s penis and his own.”)

魏斯在一篇关于在幼儿园对儿童进行“游戏访谈”的论文中，提供了另一个了解员工心态的宝贵窗口。游戏疗法在 20 世纪 30 年代曾风靡一时，由赫敏·胡格-赫尔穆特、安娜·弗洛伊德和梅兰妮·克莱因等精神分析学家推广，她们以弗洛伊德式的方式对年轻患者的行为进行牵强附会的解读。（例如，在克莱因看来，一个男孩对门和门把手的迷恋实际上关乎“阴茎进入母亲身体……门和锁代表进入她身体的方式，而门把手则代表父亲的阴茎和他自己的阴茎。”）



But Weiss took a lighter approach. Instead of setting up a strict schedule of appointments with the children, she simply made it known that she had toys available for kids who wanted to play. How each child responded to that opportunity told her a lot even before the formal interviewing sessions began.

但魏斯采取了更轻松的方式。她没有为孩子们安排严格的时间表，而是简单地告知大家，她准备了玩具，供想玩的孩子使用。每个孩子如何回应这一机会，甚至在正式访谈开始前，就让她了解了很多信息。



Some children frequently volunteered for play turns when it was obvious that they had no chance; others tended to pick the right moment for asking to be admitted. Some came from time to time to see if they could have a turn, and apparently were prepared for either alternative. They would leave again without being upset when they found that another child had come first; or else they decided to wait. Other children, however, came with nothing in mind but a wish to play, and resented finding themselves barred.

有些孩子明明没有机会，却频繁地主动要求参与游戏；另一些孩子则懂得选择恰当的时机请求加入。有些孩子会时不时过来看看能否轮到他们，显然对两种结果都做好了准备——发现别的孩子先轮到时会平静地离开，或者决定等待。然而，另一些孩子来时满心只想玩耍，一旦被拒便愤愤不平。



Weiss’s play sessions were designed to offer the children maximum freedom of expression. An inviting assortment of building blocks, crayons and paper, clay, dolls, cooking utensils, toy cars, trucks, stuffed animals, rags, and scarves was laid out for them to choose from as they pleased. By seeing how each child acted in this situation, she was able to gauge their capacity for social adaptation, imagination, and spontaneous enjoyment. Then she would introduce a rule into each session (such as “Toys must go back in the bag after playing with them”) to observe how they reacted to constraint and authority. No possibility for learning from a child’s behavior was wasted.

韦斯的游戏环节旨在给予孩子们最大限度的表达自由。她准备了琳琅满目的积木、蜡笔、纸张、黏土、玩偶、厨具、玩具车、卡车、毛绒动物、碎布和围巾，让孩子们随心选择。通过观察每个孩子在此情境中的表现，她能够评估他们的社会适应能力、想象力以及自发享受乐趣的能力。随后她会在每次游戏中引入一条规则（例如“玩完玩具后必须放回袋子里”），以此观察他们对约束和权威的反应。她绝不放过任何一个从孩子行为中学习的机会。



Michaels admitted that the clinic’s approach seemed like “more of an art than a science.” But he recognized that the ambitions of this art went beyond the mere formulation of a diagnosis. Instead, he wrote, Asperger and his colleagues aimed at nothing less than “to determine the innate capacities of the child, the alterable components of his personality, the causes of his pathological behavior, what will best assure his personal happiness, security and social welfare, what his right place is in the family, society, what are his personal goals and ambitions, and how these can all be realized.”

迈克尔斯承认，诊所的方法看起来“更像是一门艺术而非科学”。但他认识到，这门艺术的雄心远不止于单纯做出诊断。他写道，阿斯伯格及其同事的目标恰恰是“确定儿童与生俱来的能力、其人格中可改变的部分、病态行为的成因、什么最能保障其个人幸福、安全与社会福祉、其在家庭与社会中的恰当位置、其个人目标与抱负，以及如何实现这一切。”



Even the physical layout of the Heilpädagogik Station—with comfortable chairs and tables and decorative friezes on the walls—made it clear that the ward was not intended to be a custodial institution where demoralized patients would be shut away from the eyes of polite society. It was a place where children and teenagers could rediscover the potential of their humanity.

就连海尔帕达戈基克站（Heilpädagogik Station）的物理布局——舒适的桌椅、墙上装饰性的浮雕——也清楚地表明，这个病房并非旨在成为将沮丧患者与文明社会隔绝的监护机构。它是一个让儿童和青少年能够重新发现人性潜力的地方。



II



On his first day at the hospital, Gottfried did nothing but cry. His tears gradually subsided, but he was still upset that he would have to stay there for a month, trapped in an unfamiliar setting with unfamiliar people. Some kids at the clinic—especially those referred by the juvenile courts—became enraged when they realized that they couldn’t just walk out the door. But Gottfried’s response to his predicament was unusually sober and deliberate.

在医院的第一天，戈特弗里德除了哭泣什么也没做。他的眼泪渐渐止住，但想到要在一个陌生的环境里与陌生的人一起待上整整一个月，他依然感到沮丧。诊所里的一些孩子——尤其是那些由少年法庭送来的——在意识到自己不能随意走出大门时，往往会变得愤怒不已。然而，戈特弗里德对自己处境的反应却异常冷静而审慎。



Instead of throwing a tantrum, he tried to reason with the staff. He patiently explained how unhappy he was, attempting to buttress his case by informing them that his mother was ill, which meant his grandmother was home alone, and was surely at her wits’ end. The upcoming Sunday was a holiday, and he was expected to walk in a procession at his church. Clearly, he should be allowed to go home immediately.

他没有大发脾气，而是试图与工作人员讲道理。他耐心地解释自己有多么不开心，并试图通过告知他们母亲生病、祖母独自在家且肯定已经束手无策来佐证自己的理由。即将到来的周日是一个节日，他本应参加教堂的游行队伍。显然，他应该被允许立即回家。



These attempts at persuasion were unsuccessful, but they cast light on the unusually logical tenor of Gottfried’s mind. For a boy who often came across as immature, he could behave in surprisingly grown-up ways. He often seemed more at ease around adults than his peers, but even his interactions with the staff were strangely impersonal. If a doctor or nurse took a moment to listen to him, his face would brighten and he would become cheerful; but it didn’t seem to matter to Gottfried which adult paid attention to him. Most of the other children, by contrast, quickly developed a passionate attachment to one staff member or another.

这些劝说尝试并未成功，但却揭示了戈特弗里德异乎寻常的逻辑思维。这个常显得稚气未脱的男孩，有时会展现出令人惊讶的成熟举止。比起同龄人，他反而更习惯与成年人相处，但即便与工作人员互动时也透着古怪的疏离感。若有医生或护士驻足倾听，他便会眉开眼笑；可对戈特弗里德而言，究竟是哪位大人在关注他似乎并不重要。相比之下，其他孩子很快就会对某位工作人员产生强烈依恋。



Gradually, Gottfried adapted to his new life on the ward. The reliable rhythms of the daily schedule seemed to comfort him. He studied it diligently, as if he were learning the local customs while exiled in a foreign land. He was at his best in class, where he beamed with pride when a teacher called on him. But given a choice between associating with other children and being alone, he would wander off by himself, unless the children were organizing a game. Then he would lobby a sympathetic adult staff member to be included in the group.

渐渐地，戈特弗里德适应了病房的新生活。规律的日常作息似乎让他感到安心。他像流落异乡的旅人学习当地风俗般，认真研习着作息表。课堂上是他表现最好的时候，被老师点名时总会骄傲地眉飞色舞。但若要在与同伴玩耍和独处之间选择，他宁愿独自游荡——除非孩子们正在组织游戏。这时他就会央求富有同情心的成年工作人员，让自己加入其中。



Sadly, when no adults were around, Gottfried’s peers teased him as mercilessly as they did at home, particularly when they discovered that they could upset him by telling him he’d done something inappropriate. While some kids took pleasure in flouting the clinic’s regulations, Gottfried seemed horrified by the idea—but then he would break the same rules inadvertently, as if they had simply slipped his mind.

遗憾的是，当没有大人在场时，戈特弗里德的同龄人对他进行的嘲弄与他在家中遭受的并无二致——尤其当他们发现只需指责他做了不当之事就能让他心烦意乱时。有些孩子以违反诊所规定为乐，而戈特弗里德似乎对此感到惊恐——但他随后又会无意间触犯同样的规则，仿佛这些规定只是被他抛诸脑后。



—



GOTTFRIED’S COMPLICATED RELATIONSHIP with rules and expectations also came up during a free-association test. Weiss prepared him in the usual manner by presenting the test as a kind of game: Just close your eyes and say whatever comes into your mind. All we’re looking for is words, ordinary nouns; don’t worry about forming complete sentences.

戈特弗里德与规则和期望之间复杂的关系，在一次自由联想测试中也显露无遗。韦斯以惯常方式为他做准备，将测试描述成一种游戏：只需闭上眼睛，说出脑海中浮现的任何内容。我们要的只是词语，普通的名词；不必担心要组成完整的句子。



As the boy tried to do what he thought was expected of him—slowly, haltingly, with a long pause between each answer—it became clear to Weiss that suggesting to the boy that he focus on nouns had been a mistake. He wasn’t freely associating at all; instead, he was scrutinizing each word internally to verify that it was a noun before saying it out loud, and laboring with such intensity that he kept forgetting to close his eyes as she had instructed him to do. Thus his score on the test was merely average, despite the fact that he had worked much harder on it than most kids. But Weiss was too astute an observer to take Gottfried’s middling score at face value. “We cannot be interested so much in the concrete result—average though it may be—as in the particular direction he gave to the test performance,” she noted. “After knowing the child’s way of thinking and acting, it is impossible to believe that he happened to turn in this direction by chance. We know how important laws and rules are to keep him in his psychic balance and so it seems quite natural that he is peculiarly interested in them.”

当男孩试图按照他认为被期望的方式作答时——缓慢、迟疑，每个答案之间停顿良久——韦斯意识到，建议他专注于名词是个错误。他并非在自由联想，而是在内心仔细审视每个词，确认它是名词后才说出口，如此用力专注，以至于总忘记按她的指示闭上眼睛。因此，尽管他比大多数孩子付出更多努力，测试成绩却仅属中等。但韦斯作为敏锐的观察者，不会仅凭戈特弗里德的中等分数就妄下结论。她指出："我们不应过分关注具体结果——即便它可能平庸——而应关注他在测试表现中展现的独特方向。了解这个孩子的思维与行为方式后，绝不可能相信他恰好偶然走向这个方向。我们知道规则与法则对他维持心理平衡有多重要，因此他对它们格外感兴趣，这显得十分自然。"



She then gave the boy a series of pictures depicting the capture and training of a dancing bear, presented out of order. She asked him to think up a story that fit the pictures and put them in the appropriate sequence. Most children had fun with this test, convinced that they were being given a chance to figure out the secret of what really happened to the bear. But instead, Gottfried complained that he couldn’t possibly sequence the pictures correctly without knowing the story first. “G. is not able to escape from his logical attitude,” Weiss observed. “He can recognize the facts, but cannot invent what may lie between them. Many children younger and simpler than he can manage that much better. For them, the picture becomes vivid at once like a fairy tale and they will begin interpreting and not worry about what really is in the pictures or what they have to add to them. But for G. the picture is either real just as it is or unintelligible.”

接着，她给男孩展示了一系列描绘捕捉和训练舞熊的图片，但顺序是打乱的。她让他根据这些图片编一个故事，并按合适的顺序排列。大多数孩子都觉得这个测试很有趣，认为自己有机会弄清楚那只熊到底发生了什么。但戈特弗里德却抱怨说，如果不知道故事内容，他根本不可能正确排列图片的顺序。韦斯观察到：“G.无法摆脱他的逻辑思维。他能识别事实，但无法想象事实之间可能存在的联系。许多比他更小、更简单的孩子都能做得更好。对他们来说，图片立刻变得生动起来，像童话一样，他们会开始解读，而不去担心图片中到底有什么，或者他们需要添加什么。但对 G.来说，图片要么是真实的，要么就是无法理解的。”



The same habit of taking things literally dictated his responses to every test that Weiss gave him. Asked to recall a short story he’d just read, he repeated the text nearly word for word, but he didn’t embellish on the narrative with his own imaginative flourishes. He performed well on a handwriting test—indeed, Weiss described his orthography as “peculiarly regular”—but he became preoccupied with the rules of grammar and the fact that his sheet of paper had a crease in it. Gottfried was acutely aware of details that other children missed, but he was perpetually getting lost in the forest while fretting about individual trees.

同样地，他对韦斯进行的每一项测试都做出了字面理解的反应。当被要求复述刚读过的短篇故事时，他几乎逐字重复了原文，却没有用自己的想象力为叙述增添任何修饰。他在书写测试中表现良好——事实上，韦斯形容他的字迹“异常工整”——但他却过分纠结于语法规则以及纸张上的一道折痕。戈特弗里德对其他孩子忽略的细节异常敏感，却总在担忧个别树木时迷失于整片森林。



Seeing this pattern helped Weiss understand why so many people thought he was slow and stupid at the same time that his grandmother knew he was highly intelligent. Gottfried was highly intelligent—but in ways that didn’t register on the clinic’s standardized tests. As Weiss got to know him better over the course of the month, she came to glimpse the earnest nine-year-old struggling behind the mask of indignation that he adopted to get through social situations that didn’t make sense to him. Asked to compare pairs of words like bush and tree and ladder and staircase, for example, he would preface his replies with the haughty-sounding phrase “Well, good gracious.” This annoyed Weiss at first, but she came to understand that Gottfried didn’t mean to sound rude. Comparing ladders to staircases just seemed like a pointless exercise to him.

看到这种模式后，韦斯明白了为什么那么多人觉得戈特弗里德既迟钝又愚蠢，而他的祖母却知道他非常聪明。戈特弗里德确实智力超群——但他的聪明才智并未在诊所的标准测试中体现出来。随着韦斯在接下来一个月里逐渐了解他，她开始瞥见那个真诚的九岁男孩，正挣扎在他为了应对那些让他困惑的社交场合而戴上的愤怒面具之下。例如，当被要求比较"灌木"和"树"、"梯子"和"楼梯"这类词语时，他总会用一句傲慢的"哎呀，天哪"来开头。这起初让韦斯感到恼火，但她后来明白，戈特弗里德并非有意显得无礼。对他来说，比较梯子和楼梯似乎只是一项毫无意义的练习。



Gottfried was precociously smart, but he was apparently unaware of things that most kids know instinctively. He could see right through the polite façades and social games unfolding all around him, but didn’t know how to play them to his own advantage.

戈特弗里德早慧过人，但他显然对大多数孩子本能知晓的事情浑然不觉。他能看穿周围一切礼貌的伪装和社交游戏，却不知道如何利用这些游戏为自己谋利。



III



Over the course of a decade, Asperger and his staff examined more than two hundred children who displayed a similarly striking cluster of social awkwardness, precocious abilities, and fascination with rules, laws, and schedules. They also saw a number of teenagers and adults who fit the same profile. The most severely disabled children had been branded as feebleminded and warehoused in asylums. Others were prodigies who were failing in school because their teachers interpreted their pedantic mannerisms and failure to obey instructions as willful insurrection. Even the most gifted of these kids found it hard to learn basic life skills like dressing, bathing, and behaving politely at the table. They also tended to be clumsy and inept at sports, which singled them out for mockery in a culture that exalted athletic vigor as a sign of spiritual health.

在十年间，阿斯伯格及其团队检查了超过两百名儿童，这些孩子都表现出一种引人注目的共同特征：社交笨拙、早熟才能，以及对规则、法律和日程的痴迷。他们还接触了许多符合这一特征的青少年和成年人。最严重残疾的儿童曾被贴上智力低下的标签，并被收容在精神病院。另一些则是天才，却因老师将其迂腐的举止和拒绝服从指令解读为蓄意反抗而在学校表现不佳。即便是这些孩子中最有天赋的，也难以掌握穿衣、洗澡和餐桌礼仪等基本生活技能。他们往往动作笨拙、不擅长运动，在一个将体育活力视为精神健康标志的文化中，这使他们成为被嘲弄的对象。



Many of them also struck Asperger as exceptionally beautiful, with finely chiseled, mature-looking features. But they wore grave and serious expressions, as if their constant worrying had aged them prematurely. They seemed particularly disturbed by unanticipated changes in their environment and events turning out in ways that defied their expectations. (“If something was only slightly different from the way that he had imagined it or from what he was used to,” Asperger wrote of one child, “he was upset and confused and would go into long tirades.”) Their behavior tended to become highly regimented—as if by doing things in a routinized manner they could ward off chaos itself.

其中许多人也被阿斯伯格视为异常美丽，拥有精致雕刻、成熟的面容。但他们表情严肃凝重，仿佛持续的忧虑使他们过早衰老。他们对环境中意料之外的变化以及事件超出预期的发展显得尤为不安。（"如果某件事与他想象中或习惯的方式稍有不同，"阿斯伯格曾描述一个孩子，"他就会感到不安和困惑，并开始长篇大论地抱怨。"）他们的行为往往变得高度刻板——仿佛通过按部就班地做事，就能抵御混乱本身。



In its most extreme forms, this rage for order took the form of repetitive, stereotypic movements, such as rocking back and forth, beating on tables and walls, fussing with a shoelace for hours, or repeating the same phrase over and over. The children would line up their toys in rows in accord with laws and patterns that were apparent only to them, and explode in tantrums if these sequences were disturbed by their parents. But they could also turn this need for repetition and symmetry into a source of pleasure. They built up huge collections of treasured objects, which could be as mundane as scraps of cotton thread or as esoteric as a supply of chemicals for a home laboratory. Asperger reported that one boy became determined to collect a thousand matchboxes, “a goal which he pursued with fanatical energy.”

在最极端的形式中，这种对秩序的狂热表现为重复、刻板的动作，例如前后摇晃、敲打桌子和墙壁、花数小时摆弄鞋带，或反复念叨同一句话。这些孩子会按照只有他们自己理解的规律和模式，将玩具整齐排列成行，若父母打乱这些序列，他们便会暴怒发作。但他们也能将这种对重复与对称的需求转化为愉悦的源泉。他们大量收集珍视的物品——可能是棉线头这般寻常之物，也可能是家庭实验室所需的化学试剂这般深奥之物。阿斯伯格曾记录，有个男孩决心收集一千个火柴盒，“他以近乎狂热的精力追逐这个目标。”



Some of the children were astonishingly articulate, even poetic in their speech, and acute observers of their own experience. One boy described to Asperger how he soothed his homesickness at night: “If one lays one’s head on the bolster, then there is such a strange noise in the ear, and one has to lie very quietly for a long time, and that is nice.” But sometimes their florid constructions outstripped the literal meaning of the words, and their delivery took on a stilted, singsong quality, as if they were declaiming in verse. They tended to launch into monologues, with one verbal tributary flooding into the next, whether the person they were talking to was really listening or not. They also had a hard time keeping their pronouns straight. One boy began speaking at a very young age but was never able to learn to use Sie, the polite form of address, employing the more familiar Du instead, which came across as presumptuous.

有些孩子说话惊人地清晰，甚至富有诗意，对自己的经历有着敏锐的观察力。一个男孩向阿斯伯格描述他如何在夜晚缓解思乡之情："如果把头枕在枕头上，耳朵里就会传来一种奇怪的声音，必须静静地躺很长时间，这种感觉很美妙。"但有时他们华丽的措辞超越了词语的字面意义，说话方式变得生硬、带有歌唱般的韵律，仿佛在朗诵诗句。他们倾向于滔滔不绝地独白，一个话题接着另一个话题，无论对方是否真的在听。此外，他们很难正确使用人称代词。一个男孩很小就开始说话，却始终学不会使用敬语"您"，总是用更亲昵的"你"来称呼，显得颇为冒昧。



These children were bundles of paradoxes: precocious and childish, sophisticated and naïve, clumsy but formal, standoffish but lonely, attuned to the music of language but insensitive to the rhythm of reciprocal interaction. They were, as Asperger put it, “a particularly interesting and highly recognizable type of child.” He came to believe that they were representative of a distinct syndrome that was “not at all rare” but had somehow escaped the notice of his predecessors.

这些孩子是矛盾的集合体：早熟又幼稚，世故又天真，笨拙却拘谨，疏离却孤独，对语言的韵律敏感却对互动的节奏迟钝。正如阿斯伯格所言，他们是"一种特别有趣且极易辨识的儿童类型"。他逐渐确信，这些孩子代表了一种独特的综合征，这种综合征"绝非罕见"，却不知为何未被前人所察觉。



—



IN FACT, A YOUNG Russian psychiatrist named Grunia Sukhareva had written about a nearly identical group of young people in Moscow two decades earlier. The focus of her work was an emerging field of psychiatry: adolescent psychosis. Sukhareva made the case that her patients had come into the world with a disorder that resembled schizophrenia but with an essential difference. While adult schizophrenics almost invariably declined, these odd young ducks often made dramatic improvements over time.

事实上，早在二十年前，一位名叫格鲁尼娅·苏哈列娃的年轻俄罗斯精神病学家就曾描述过莫斯科一群几乎完全相同的青少年。她的研究聚焦于精神病学的新兴领域：青少年精神病。苏哈列娃论证指出，她的患者天生患有某种类似精神分裂症的障碍，但存在本质差异。成年精神分裂症患者几乎无一例外地病情恶化，而这些古怪的年轻人却往往能随时间推移取得显著改善。



The parents of a thirteen-year boy Sukhareva called M.Sch. sensed that he was different from his siblings even as a baby. He was excruciatingly sensitive to sound and flinched at every noise in his crib. As he got older, he developed intense phobias—of the dark, of being alone, of locked doors, of monsters that seemed to be hiding everywhere—and, most of all, of other children. He was obsessed with illness, death, and coffins. If M.Sch. heard about someone who died young, instead of expressing sympathy for the deceased and their loved ones, he would sigh, “I shall not live very long.”

一个名叫苏哈列娃的十三岁男孩 M.Sch.的父母察觉到，他自婴儿时期起就与兄弟姐妹不同。他对声音极度敏感，在摇篮里听到任何响动都会畏缩。随着年龄增长，他产生了强烈的恐惧症——害怕黑暗、独处、锁着的门、似乎无处不在的怪物——最严重的是害怕其他孩子。他痴迷于疾病、死亡和棺材。如果 M.Sch.听说有人英年早逝，他不会对逝者及其亲人表示同情，反而会叹息道："我活不了太久。"



It wasn’t hard to figure out why other kids frightened him. M.Sch. walked with a noticeably odd gait, and they bullied him mercilessly for it. But he also had startlingly mature insights into his predicament. He explained to Sukhareva that his classmates “are very good at games and won’t let me play. The character of the children is such that they choose the stronger ones.” He may have felt that he was feeble in body, but he was not feeble in mind. He scored two years ahead of his peers on a scale of intellectual development and showed a natural aptitude for music. Introduced to the violin at age seven, he made such rapid progress that he was admitted to the famed Moscow Conservatory. But he struggled to achieve the self-discipline required to become a successful concert violinist.

不难看出其他孩子为何会让他感到害怕。M.Sch.走路时步态明显异常，他们因此无情地欺负他。但他对自己处境的洞察却惊人地成熟。他向苏哈列娃解释说，他的同学们"很擅长游戏，却不让我参与。孩子们的天性就是会选择更强的人。"他或许觉得自己身体孱弱，但心智绝不软弱。在智力发展量表上，他的得分比同龄人领先两年，并且展现出与生俱来的音乐天赋。七岁开始学习小提琴后，他进步神速，得以进入著名的莫斯科音乐学院。然而，他始终难以培养出成为成功音乐会小提琴家所需的自律能力。



Concerned for his future, his parents checked him into Sukhareva’s inpatient program at the hospital. There he adopted the role of the clown, cracking vulgar jokes and chasing girls around the ward. He knew he was being naughty, but he seemed unable to stop himself. Once M.Sch. started doing something, or even thinking about something, it was almost impossible for him to switch tracks. “It often seems to me that a word is going round and round in my head, and if I do not do something or other, something will happen to me,” he explained. “To start anything, I have to make lengthy preparations, and afterwards it is hard for me to stop.”

父母担忧他的未来，便安排他住进了苏哈列娃医院的住院部。在那里，他扮演起小丑的角色，讲着粗俗的笑话，在病房里追逐女孩。他明知自己调皮捣蛋，却似乎无法自控。一旦 M.Sch.开始做某件事，甚至只是思考某件事，就几乎无法转移注意力。“我常常觉得，有个词在脑海里反复打转，如果我不做点什么，就会发生不好的事情，”他解释道，“要开始任何事，我都得做漫长的准备，而一旦开始，又很难停下来。”



Like Gottfried, he sounded more like a middle-aged fussbudget than a boy barely in his teens. Asked if he had enjoyed a book, he hemmed and hawed and said, “It seems to me that I liked the book, but I am really not sure. The principle of reading is such that one is bound to be taken in.” Yet his art teacher considered him a prodigy. When he was immersed in music, he was “totally transformed,” Sukhareva said, “giving the impression of a confident and sensitive musician.”

和戈特弗里德一样，他说话的语气更像是个爱唠叨的中年人，而非一个刚满十三岁的少年。当被问及是否喜欢某本书时，他支支吾吾地说：“我觉得自己好像喜欢那本书，但真的不确定。阅读的原则就是，人总会被表象所迷惑。”然而，他的美术老师却认为他是个天才。苏哈列娃说，当他沉浸在音乐中时，他“完全变了一个人，给人一种自信而敏感的乐手的印象。”



Another boy, M.R., taught himself all about the War of 1812 by the time he was ten and could expound at length on the events leading up to it. But if these lengthy perorations were interrupted, he would become agitated and start all over again from the beginning. When the nanny of a third boy asked him to sit up straight at the table, he replied, “I have my principles and am pedantic, and therefore I will not do it.”

另一个男孩 M.R.在十岁时就自学了 1812 年战争的全部内容，并能详细阐述其前因后果。但如果他长篇大论的演说被打断，他就会变得焦躁不安，然后从头开始重新讲述。当第三个男孩的保姆要求他在餐桌前坐直时，他回答道：“我有自己的原则，而且很迂腐，所以我不会照做。”



A.D. was fascinated by numbers and counting. As he waited for a play to begin, he would total up the number of spectators in the theater and then dash out to the lobby to add in the latecomers. He took frequent polls of his classmates, firing off questions like “Which party got the most votes in the recent elections in England?” and “What are the best strains of rabbit?” (They cruelly nicknamed him “the talking machine.”) By the time he was thirteen, he had extensive knowledge of politics in the emerging Soviet Union.

A.D.对数字和计数着迷。在等待戏剧开场时，他会先统计剧场内的观众人数，然后冲到大厅把迟到者的人数加上去。他经常在同学中进行民意调查，抛出一连串问题，比如“最近英国大选中哪个政党得票最多？”和“哪种兔子品种最好？”（同学们残忍地给他起了个绰号叫“说话机器”。）到十三岁时，他对新兴的苏联政治已经有了广博的知识。



Two of Sukhareva’s patients started spontaneously rapping in rhyme when they were three years old. All of them seemed to have insatiable appetites for puns, quips, and catchphrases. A boy called K.A. told his nannies that he was planning to deliver a lecture on “the nutritional value of cotton wool” and once slipped a note into his doctor’s bag awarding him a membership in “the Society of Fried Dogs.”

苏哈列娃的两名患者在 3 岁时便开始自发地押韵说唱。所有孩子似乎都对双关语、俏皮话和流行语有着无法满足的渴望。一个名叫 K.A.的男孩告诉保姆，他计划发表一场关于“棉花的营养价值”的讲座，还曾偷偷往医生的包里塞了一张纸条，授予他“炸狗协会”的会员资格。



Despite their shambling exteriors, these children had rich inner lives. They shunned communal games but devoured fairy tales and fantasy books in solitude. When P.P. was three, he astonished his parents by sitting down at a piano and reproducing his favorite melodies note for note. Sukhareva described him as a sensitive child with “deep feelings for the beauty of nature” who would burst into tears at the slightest rebuke. But he had no friends other than his sister. Asked why he avoided his classmates, he replied, “The children are too noisy and disturb my thinking.” At twelve, he moved through the world like a monk in a contemplative order of one.

尽管这些孩子外表邋遢，内心世界却极为丰富。他们回避集体游戏，却独自沉浸于童话和奇幻故事中。P.P.三岁时，他坐在钢琴前，一个音符不差地重现了最爱的旋律，令父母惊叹不已。苏哈列娃形容他是个敏感的孩子，“对自然之美感受深切”，稍受责备便会泪流满面。但他除了妹妹之外没有朋友。当被问及为何避开同学时，他回答：“孩子们太吵了，打扰我思考。”十二岁时，他如同独修僧侣般行走于世，自成一方静思天地。



To describe this curious syndrome, Sukhareva proposed the term schizoid personality disorder. She was uncertain if it had any true relationship to schizophrenia (literally, “splitting of the mind”), named by Swiss psychiatrist Eugen Bleuler fifteen years earlier. These children didn’t seem to be going through any sort of disease process similar to the tragic arc of schizophrenia; they were just deeply, constitutionally different from their peers—more like one of Lazar’s archetypes than patients who could ever be made well. If they found a teacher who protected them from bullies and encouraged them to cultivate their natural talents, they might thrive, though they would always remain eccentric. “All affected patients were under our observation for a number of years and all were seen to make considerable progress,” Sukhareva reported. M.Sch. “had excellent achievements in music and art.” M.R. “did well at school and his personality became significantly better adjusted.” A.D. “made good technical progress in music,” though he remained distinctly reserved.

为了描述这种奇特的综合征，苏哈列娃提出了"分裂样人格障碍"这一术语。她不确定这是否与十五年前瑞士精神病学家尤金·布洛伊勒命名的精神分裂症（字面意为"心灵分裂"）存在真实关联。这些孩子似乎并未经历类似精神分裂症悲剧轨迹的疾病过程；他们只是天生与同龄人存在本质差异——更像是拉扎尔原型理论中的某种类型，而非能被治愈的患者。若能遇到保护他们免受欺凌、鼓励其发展天赋的老师，他们或许能茁壮成长，但始终会保持特立独行。"所有患者均经多年观察，均取得显著进步，"苏哈列娃报告称。M.Sch. "在音乐和艺术方面成就卓越。"M.R. "在校表现优异，性格适应能力显著提升。"A.D. "在音乐技术上取得良好进展"，尽管他仍明显保持疏离。



She cautioned her colleagues that the term schizoid might lead to “conceptual confusion and misinterpretation” if the two conditions turned out to be completely unrelated. Her concerns proved to be well-founded.

她提醒同事们，如果这两种情况最终被证明完全无关，那么“精神分裂样”这一术语可能会导致“概念混淆和误解”。事实证明，她的担忧是有道理的。



—



THOUGH ASPERGER WAS APPARENTLY unaware of Sukhareva’s work, he too saw parallels between his patients’ condition and schizophrenia—particularly a tendency toward what Bleuler called “autistic thinking,” defined as self-centered rumination and retreat into fantasy. These children pursued their own goals tenaciously, like the boy determined to collect a thousand matchboxes, but they seemed immune to the expectations of others. “In everything these children follow their own impulses and interests,” he observed, “regardless of the outside world.”

尽管阿斯伯格显然不了解苏哈列娃的研究，但他也看到了患者状况与精神分裂症之间的相似之处——尤其是布洛伊勒所称的“孤独谱系思维”倾向，即自我中心的沉思和退入幻想。这些孩子执着地追求自己的目标，比如那个决心收集一千个火柴盒的男孩，但他们似乎对外界的期望无动于衷。“这些孩子的一切行为都遵循自己的冲动和兴趣，”他观察到，“完全不顾外部世界。”



Schizophrenic patients typically experience a progressive loss of contact with the social world that begins in adolescence; Asperger’s patients, on the other hand, seemed to come into the world beyond the reach of interpersonal contact—though he astutely noted that this often didn’t become apparent to their parents and medical professionals until they were two years old or older. As they were clearly not psychotic, Asperger coined the term Autistischen Psychopathen (“autistic psychopathy”) to describe their condition, employing a nineteenth-century term for the hazy borderland between mental health and illness. He also employed the simpler term Autismus and referred to it as a “natural entity,” like a field biologist describing a life-form he’d discovered flourishing in plain sight.

精神分裂症患者通常在青春期开始逐渐失去与社会的联系；而阿斯伯格患者则似乎从出生起就处于人际接触无法触及的领域——尽管阿斯伯格敏锐地指出，这种状况往往要到患者两岁或更大时，才会被父母和医疗专业人员察觉。由于这些患者显然并非精神病患者，阿斯伯格创造了"孤独症精神病态"（Autistischen Psychopathen）这一术语来描述他们的状况，借用了 19 世纪用来描述健康与疾病之间模糊边界地带的概念。他还使用了更简洁的术语"孤独症"（Autismus），并将其称为一种"自然实体"，就像野外生物学家描述一种在众目睽睽之下繁衍生息的生命形态一样。



He pointed out that the distinctive characteristics of this natural entity were already familiar in stock characters from pop culture like the “absent-minded professor” and Count Bobby, a fictitious aristocrat who was the butt of many Austrian jokes. Crucially, Asperger also described Autismus as remaining “unmistakable and constant throughout the whole life-span,” and said that it encompassed an astonishingly broad cross section of people, from the most gifted to the most disabled. There seemed to be nearly as many varieties of Autismus as there were autistic people.

他指出，这种自然实体的独特特征早已在流行文化中的典型角色中为人熟知，比如"心不在焉的教授"和鲍比伯爵——一个虚构的贵族，常是奥地利笑话中的笑柄。关键在于，阿斯伯格还将孤独谱系描述为"在整个生命周期中始终清晰可辨且恒定不变"，并称其涵盖了从最具天赋到最严重障碍的惊人广泛人群。孤独谱系的种类似乎几乎与孤独谱系群体的人数一样多。



The range [of this type] encompasses all levels of ability from the highly original genius, through the weird eccentric who lives in a world of his own and achieves very little, down to the most severe, contact-disturbed, automaton-like mentally retarded individual . . . Autistic individuals are distinguished from each other not only by the degree of contact disturbance and the degree of intellectual ability, but also by their personality and their special interests, which are often outstandingly varied and original.

[这类]谱系的范围涵盖了从极具原创性的天才，到活在自己世界里、成就甚微的古怪异类，直至最严重、接触障碍、如自动机般的智力障碍个体……孤独谱系个体之间的区别不仅在于接触障碍的程度和智力水平，还在于他们的个性和特殊兴趣——这些兴趣往往异常多样且富有原创性。



There was no doubt in Asperger’s mind that autism was passed down through the family tree. “We have been able to discern related incipient traits in parents or relatives,” he wrote, “in every single case where it was possible for us to make a closer acquaintance.” But he cautioned his colleagues that it would be folly to search for a single gene responsible for such a complex range of behaviors and traits. “It is a vain hope to think there may be a clear and simple mode of inheritance,” he said. “These states are undoubtedly polygenetic.”

阿斯伯格毫不怀疑孤独症是通过家族遗传的。他写道：“在每一个我们能够深入了解的案例中，我们都从父母或亲属身上发现了相关的早期特征。”但他告诫同事们，试图寻找单一基因来解释如此复杂的行为和特征范围是愚蠢的。他说：“指望存在一种清晰简单的遗传模式，那是徒劳的希望。这些状态无疑是多基因的。”



In a postgraduate thesis submitted to Hamburger in 1943, Asperger described four “prototypical cases” named Fritz V., Harro L., Ernst K., and Hellmuth L.—all boys between the ages of seven and ten. He apologized for not including a young woman in his quartet of prototypes, explaining that he had never seen a full-blown female case in his practice:

在 1943 年提交给汉堡大学的一篇研究生论文中，阿斯伯格描述了四个“原型案例”，分别名为弗里茨·V、哈罗·L、恩斯特·K 和赫尔穆特·L——均为七至十岁的男孩。他为未能在其四个原型案例中纳入一名年轻女性而致歉，并解释称自己在临床实践中从未见过完整的女性病例。



While we have never met a girl with the fully fledged picture of autism, we have, however, seen several mothers of autistic children whose behavior had decidedly autistic features. It is difficult to explain this observation. It may be only chance that there are no autistic girls among our cases, or it could be that autistic traits in the female become evident only after puberty. We just do not know.

虽然我们从未遇到过完全符合典型孤独症特征的女孩，但确实见过几位孤独症儿童的母亲，她们的行为明显带有孤独谱系特征。这一观察结果难以解释。或许只是巧合，我们的病例中没有孤独症女孩；也可能女性身上的孤独谱系特征要到青春期后才显现。我们对此尚不清楚。



He went so far as to characterize Autismus as “an extreme variant of male intelligence,” a view echoed forty years later by British psychologist Simon Baron-Cohen, who linked the development of autism to exposure to high levels of testosterone in the womb. It’s more likely, though, that one of the primary factors contributing to the absence of girls in Asperger’s practice was the fact that teachers and judges of the juvenile court were a major source of referrals for his clinic. The socialization of junge Wienerinnen to be compliant and self-effacing—to fade demurely into the background—undoubtedly led young women to work extra hard to suppress the behaviors that brought their male counterparts to the attention of the authorities. Similar dynamics would contribute to the underestimation of the prevalence of autism in women into the twenty-first century.

他甚至将孤独症描述为“男性智力的极端变体”，这一观点在四十年后得到了英国心理学家西蒙·巴伦-科恩的呼应，后者将孤独症的发展与子宫内接触高水平睾酮联系起来。不过，阿斯伯格诊所中女孩缺失的主要原因之一，很可能是教师和少年法庭法官构成了其诊所的主要转诊来源。维也纳年轻女性被社会化为顺从谦逊——得体地隐没于背景之中——这无疑促使年轻女性格外努力地压抑那些会让同龄男性引起当局注意的行为。类似的机制导致直到二十一世纪，女性孤独症患病率仍被严重低估。



Asperger’s choice of presenting four prototypical cases in his thesis has led many commentators (including the authors of the Asperger FAQ at the National Institutes of Health) to claim that his work in autism was based solely on observations of just four boys. But he was very clear on that point: “We want only to state briefly that over the course of ten years we have observed more than 200 children who all showed autism to a greater or lesser degree.” From these observations (including Weiss’s in-depth case study of Gottfried, which has been overlooked since its publication in 1935) he developed sketches of four characters as memorable as any in popular fiction.

阿斯伯格在他的论文中选择呈现四个典型病例，导致许多评论者（包括美国国立卫生研究院阿斯伯格常见问题解答的作者）声称他对孤独症的研究仅基于对四个男孩的观察。但他在这一点上非常明确："我们只想简要说明，在十年间我们观察了 200 多名儿童，他们都不同程度地表现出孤独症特征。"基于这些观察（包括魏斯对戈特弗里德的深度个案研究——该研究自 1935 年发表后一直被忽视），他勾勒出四个如同流行小说中任何角色般令人难忘的人物形象。



The first boy, Fritz, a lanky scion of poets and recluses, had a precocious vocabulary and a prodigious command of math but was expelled from kindergarten after only a few days for idly strolling around in class and “demolishing the coat-racks.” Harro was a short, muscular boy with a wizened face and resonant voice who tossed off deadpan observations like “I am dreadfully left-handed”—despite the fact that he was only eight years old. “Sometimes he appeared to be in deep thought,” Asperger observed, “then he would draw together his brows and assume a strange, slightly funny dignity.” Ernst was so perpetually overwhelmed that he looked like he had just “fallen from the sky.” He maintained a running commentary on his actions, as if he were the voice-over narrator of his own life: “He had to tell others at once whatever it was that captured his attention. Some of these ‘asides’ were quite remarkable, not only in the sense that they were very adult in diction, but also because they showed good observation.” Finally, there was poor Hellmuth, who was so chubby and ungainly that he was unable to play a game of catch in the schoolyard, standing rigidly among his peers “like a frozen giant.” Yet if Hellmuth was prompted to talk about his favorite subject, poetry, he could be startlingly eloquent and original, “seemingly full of insight and superiority.”

第一个男孩弗里茨，一位瘦高的诗人与隐士后裔，拥有超前的词汇量和惊人的数学天赋，却因在课堂上闲逛并“拆毁衣帽架”而仅在幼儿园待了几天就被开除。哈罗是个矮壮结实的男孩，面容早熟、嗓音洪亮，常面无表情地抛出诸如“我严重左撇子”的评论——尽管他只有八岁。“有时他显得若有所思，”阿斯伯格观察到，“接着他会皱起眉头，摆出一副古怪而略带滑稽的庄重神情。”恩斯特总是显得手足无措，仿佛刚从“天上掉下来”。他不断对自己的行为进行实时解说，就像自己人生的画外音旁白：“他必须立刻把吸引他注意力的任何事告诉别人。其中一些‘插话’相当引人注目，不仅因为措辞极其成人化，更显示出敏锐的观察力。”最后是可怜的赫尔穆特，他过于肥胖笨拙，连操场上的接球游戏都无法参与，僵硬地站在同龄人中“像一尊冻结的巨人”。然而，若赫尔穆特被引导谈论他钟爱的诗歌主题，他会展现出惊人的雄辩与独创性，“仿佛充满洞见与优越感”。”



Like Cavendish standing off to the side as his colleagues conversed at the Royal Society, all four children regarded the world of people slantwise—in fleeting, peripheral glances. But Asperger determined that they took in a lot of information that way: “It is occasionally revealed that they have perceived and processed a surprisingly large amount of the world around them.” This was an extraordinarily prescient insight; later clinicians nearly universally assumed that autistic children were deliberately avoiding looking people in the eye.

如同卡文迪什在皇家学会的同事交谈时悄然立于一旁，这四个孩子都以斜视的方式——用短暂而边缘的瞥视——看待人类世界。但阿斯伯格断定他们通过这种方式获取了大量信息：“偶尔会显露他们已感知并处理了数量惊人、远超预期的周遭世界信息。”这一洞见极具先见之明；后来的临床医生几乎普遍认为孤独谱系儿童是刻意回避与他人目光接触。



Like Sukhareva’s patients, they often had talents that were not apparent to the casual observer. Some were capable of amazing feats of rote memory, such as knowing the names of the saints for every day of the year, or being able to recall the routes of all the streetcar lines in Vienna. Others had developed homegrown methods of rapid calculation, as if they had invented their own kind of math from scratch. Fritz, for example, taught himself about fractions without lessons. He understood the properties of negative numbers and could solve logic problems with ease. Asperger suspected that he could have performed well on intelligence tests, but he refused to cooperate. Instead, he would jump down from his chair and slap the examiner on the hand.

像苏哈列娃的患者一样，他们往往拥有不经意的观察者难以察觉的才能。有些人具备惊人的机械记忆能力，比如能记住全年每日对应的圣徒名字，或能回忆起维也纳所有电车线路的走向。另一些人则自创了快速计算的方法，仿佛从零开始发明了属于自己的数学体系。例如，弗里茨未经教导便自学了分数运算，理解负数的性质，并能轻松解决逻辑问题。阿斯伯格推测他本能在智力测试中表现优异，但他拒绝配合——反而会从椅子上跳下来，拍打测试员的手。



Harro could not only perform complex mathematical operations in his head, he was an avid reader who had a vivid and original way of talking about things. Asked to compare the words fly and butterfly, he launched into an etymological reverie: “The butterfly is colorful and the fly is black. The butterfly has big wings so that two flies could go underneath one wing. But the fly is much more skillful and can walk up the slippery glass and up the wall . . . The microscope explains how the fly can walk up the wall: just yesterday I saw it has teeny weeny claws on the feet and at the ends tiny little hooks.”

哈罗不仅能心算复杂的数学运算，还是一位如饥似渴的读者，谈论事物时生动而独到。当被要求比较“苍蝇”和“蝴蝶”这两个词时，他陷入了一场词源学的遐想：“蝴蝶色彩斑斓，而苍蝇是黑色的。蝴蝶有大翅膀，两只苍蝇可以躲在一只翅膀下。但苍蝇灵巧得多，能在光滑的玻璃和墙壁上爬行……显微镜解释了苍蝇如何能爬上墙壁：就在昨天，我看到它的脚上有极小的爪子，末端还有微小的钩子。”



But Harro was failing in school, because he was very disruptive in class, like Fritz. He would crawl around on all fours and announce that a lesson was “far too stupid” for him. He rarely did his homework, and if a teacher gave him a makeup assignment, he would sneer, “I wouldn’t dream of doing this.” He spent his days immersed in the books he loved, a stranger to the children around him.

但哈罗在学校表现不佳，因为他像弗里茨一样在课堂上非常捣乱。他会四肢着地爬来爬去，并宣称某节课“太愚蠢”了，不适合他。他很少做作业，如果老师给他布置补做任务，他会嗤之以鼻：“我做梦都不会做这个。”他整天沉浸在自己喜爱的书籍中，对周围的孩子们来说像个陌生人。



IV



As Sukhareva had been impressed by her patients’ prodigious abilities in music and art, Asperger was struck by these boys’ natural aptitude for science:

正如苏哈列娃对患者在音乐和艺术方面的惊人天赋印象深刻一样，阿斯伯格也被这些男孩在科学方面的天赋所打动：



We know an autistic child who has a particular interest in the natural sciences. His observations show an unusual eye for the essential. He orders his facts into a system and forms his own theories even if they are occasionally abstruse. Hardly any of this has he heard or read, and he always refers to his own experience. There is also a child who is a “chemist.” He uses all his money for experiments which often horrify his family and even steals to fund them. Some children have even more specialized interests, for instance, only experiments which create noise and smells. Another autistic boy was obsessed with poisons. He had a most unusual knowledge in this area and possessed a large collection of poisons, some quite naively concocted by himself. He came to us because he had stolen a substantial quantity of cyanide from the locked chemistry store at his school.

我们认识一个对自然科学有特殊兴趣的孤独谱系儿童。他的观察展现出对本质异乎寻常的洞察力。他将事实整理成体系，并形成自己的理论——即便这些理论偶尔晦涩难懂。这些知识几乎都不是他听来或读来的，他总是诉诸自身经验。还有一个孩子是"化学家"，他把所有零花钱都用于实验，这些实验常常令家人惊恐，甚至不惜偷窃来筹措经费。有些孩子的兴趣更为专精，比如只对能产生噪音和臭味的实验着迷。另一个孤独谱系男孩痴迷于毒药，他在这方面拥有极不寻常的知识，收藏了大量毒药——有些还是他自己用相当简陋的方法调配的。他之所以来到我们这里，是因为他从学校上锁的化学储藏室偷走了大量氰化物。



One boy sought refuge from neighborhood bullies by engaging in discussions with an old watchmaker who took a liking to him. Another child knew “an incredible amount about complex machinery” and bombarded adults with obscure technical questions that were “nearly impossible to fend off.” This boy also had a fertile imagination and was daydreaming about rocket ships and other “fantastic inventions” long before they became a reality. This inspired Asperger to comment, “Here one observes how remote from reality autistic interests often are.” But the advent of space exploration in the 1950s required him to retract that statement in favor of a suggestion that the designers of spaceships themselves were autistic.

一个男孩为了躲避邻里的欺凌，通过与一位喜欢他的老钟表匠交谈来寻求庇护。另一个孩子“对复杂机械了解得惊人”，并用“几乎无法招架”的晦涩技术问题轰炸成年人。这个男孩还拥有丰富的想象力，早在火箭飞船和其他“奇妙发明”成为现实之前，他就已在白日梦中构想它们。这促使阿斯伯格评论道：“这里可以看到，孤独谱系的兴趣往往与现实多么脱节。”但 20 世纪 50 年代太空探索的到来，迫使他撤回这一说法，转而暗示宇宙飞船的设计者本身就是孤独谱系人士。



Furthermore, Asperger recognized that his patients’ blatant disregard for authority could be developed into the skepticism indispensable to any scientist. When he asked one eleven-year-old boy if he was religious, he replied, “I wouldn’t like to say I’m unreligious, I just don’t have any proof of God.”

此外，阿斯伯格认识到，他的患者对权威的公然蔑视可以发展为科学家不可或缺的怀疑精神。当他问一个 11 岁的男孩是否信教时，男孩回答：“我不想说自己不信教，我只是没有任何关于上帝的证据。”



The pediatrician concluded that the innate gifts of these children were as central to the condition he was describing as their social difficulties. He became convinced that these boys had the potential to become innovators in their fields of interest precisely because they were constitutionally unable to take things on faith.

这位儿科医生得出结论，他所描述的这些儿童先天具备的禀赋与其社交困难一样，都是该状况的核心特征。他确信这些男孩之所以有可能成为各自兴趣领域的创新者，恰恰是因为他们天生无法盲目接受事物。



Autistic children have the ability to see things and events around them from a new point of view, which often shows surprising maturity. This ability, which remains throughout life, can in favorable cases lead to exceptional achievements which others may never attain. Abstraction ability, for instance, is a prerequisite for scientific endeavor. Indeed, we find numerous autistic individuals among distinguished scientists.

孤独谱系儿童能够以全新的视角看待周围的事物和事件，这往往展现出令人惊讶的成熟度。这种伴随终生的能力，在有利条件下可能带来他人永远无法企及的卓越成就。例如，抽象能力是科学探索的先决条件。事实上，我们在杰出的科学家中发现了许多孤独谱系群体。



He christened this distinctive cluster of aptitudes, skills, attitudes, and abilities autistic intelligence, making the bold suggestion that autistic people have played an unappreciated role in the evolution of culture:

他将这种独特的能力、技能、态度与禀赋集群命名为"孤独谱系智能"，并大胆提出：孤独谱系群体在文化进化中扮演着未被充分认识的重要角色。



It seems that for success in science and art, a dash of autism is essential. For success, the necessary ingredient may be an ability to turn away from the everyday world, from the simply practical, an ability to re-think a subject with originality so as to create in new untrodden ways.

看来在科学与艺术领域取得成功，一丝孤独症的特质不可或缺。成功的必要因素，或许正是那种能够远离日常世界、摆脱纯粹实用主义的能力，一种以独创性重新思考课题、在无人涉足的新径上开拓创新的能力。



This was a significant departure from the traditional view of so-called idiot savants in psychology, which was to frame their extraordinary abilities merely as compensation for gross deficits in other areas of development. The nineteenth-century French physician and educator Édouard Séguin, who coined the term idiot savant, described the striking talents of his patients in terms more befitting a malignant tumor. “Among the wealthier classes, idiocy is not only oftener aggravated by accessory diseases, but also complicated with abnormal semi-capacities or disordered instincts, which produce heterogeneous types to an almost unlimited extent,” he said in 1869. “It is from this class, almost exclusively, that we have musical, mathematical, architectural, and other varieties of the idiot savant; the useless protrusion of a single faculty, accompanied by a woeful general impotence.”

这与心理学界对所谓"白痴天才"的传统观点大相径庭——传统观点将其非凡能力仅仅视为对其他发展领域严重缺陷的补偿。19 世纪法国医生兼教育家爱德华·塞甘首创"白痴天才"一词，他用更适用于描述恶性肿瘤的措辞来形容其患者的惊人天赋。"在较富裕阶层中，白痴不仅常因继发性疾病而加重，更伴有异常的半能力或紊乱的本能，几乎无限度地产生异质类型，"他于 1869 年如此论述。"几乎完全来自这一阶层，我们才得以见到音乐、数学、建筑及其他类型的白痴天才；单一才能的无用突显，伴随着可悲的全面无能。"



Asperger was under no illusions that his patients were all budding Beethovens or Newtons. “Unfortunately, in the majority of cases, the positive aspects of autism do not outweigh the negative ones,” he acknowledged. But the mission of the Heilpädagogik Station, in keeping with Lazar’s original vision, was to find individualized approaches to education that would enable these children to make the most of their innate gifts while ensuring that they had the resources to cope with the challenges of their disabilities. As Weiss put it in her case study of Gottfried:

阿斯伯格并未幻想他的患者都是未来的贝多芬或牛顿。“不幸的是，在大多数情况下，孤独症的积极方面并不能抵消其消极方面，”他承认道。但根据拉扎尔最初的愿景，海尔帕达戈基克站的任务是寻找个性化的教育方法，使这些孩子能够充分利用其天赋，同时确保他们拥有应对残疾挑战的资源。正如韦斯在关于戈特弗里德的个案研究中所说：



In the case of learning difficulties, the question is never “How well or how badly does the child learn?” but “Why does the child learn badly?” and “Which is the best teaching method for him?”

在学习困难的情况下，问题从来不是“孩子学得好还是差？”而是“孩子为什么学得差？”以及“对他来说，哪种教学方法最合适？”



The clinic staff did not predicate the eventual success or failure of these children in society on their being cured of their autistic traits. Asperger wrote of a former patient who had done poorly in school, showed little regard for his hygiene and appearance, and seemed so unaware of other people that he failed to recognize acquaintances even when he passed them in the street. But he had been able to make the most of his abilities with the support and encouragement of his mother. Seeing that he was already fascinated by geometry at age three, she drew a triangle (a Dreieck, or three-cornered figure), a square (a four-cornered figure), and a pentangle (a five-cornered figure) for him in the sand. He immediately drew a line and a dot, proclaiming the line a Zwei-eck (a two-cornered figure) and the dot an Ein-eck (a one-cornered figure). Soon he was calculating cubic roots in his head.

诊所工作人员并未将这些孩子在社会中的最终成败，与是否治愈其孤独谱系特质挂钩。阿斯伯格曾记述一位早年患者：该生学业不佳，不修边幅，对他人存在近乎漠视——即便在街头擦肩而过，也认不出熟人。但在母亲的支持与鼓励下，他得以充分发挥自身才能。母亲发现他三岁时便对几何学着迷，便在沙地上为他画出三角形（德语 Dreieck，即三角图形）、正方形（四角图形）和五角形（五角图形）。他当即画出一条线段和一个圆点，宣称线段是"二角形"（Zwei-eck），圆点是"一角形"（Ein-eck）。不久后，他竟能心算立方根。



He had barely been able to get through elementary school because of his uncouth behavior but was spared from expulsion specifically because of his abilities in math. By pleading with his teachers to give him advanced tutoring, he managed to pass the college entrance exam. In his first year at university, he became interested in theoretical astronomy. Taking nothing for granted, he quickly detected an error in one of Newton’s proofs. He wrote his dissertation on the subject and eventually became an assistant professor of astronomy at a prestigious university, though Asperger described his behavior as still “extremely clumsy and gauche.”

他因举止粗鲁几乎无法完成小学学业，但凭借数学天赋才未被开除。通过恳求老师给予超前辅导，他勉强通过了大学入学考试。大学第一年，他对理论天文学产生了兴趣。他从不轻信任何结论，很快便发现牛顿某个证明中的错误。他以该课题撰写论文，最终成为某知名大学的天文学助理教授——尽管阿斯伯格形容他的行为依然"极其笨拙且缺乏教养"。



—

——



THE JOB OF THE staff of the Heilpädagogik Station, as Asperger saw it, was to teach these children how to put their autistic intelligence to work. He began calling them his “little professors.” Instead of treating them merely as patients, he saw them as indispensable allies in developing methods of pedagogy that would be most appropriate and effective for them.

在阿斯伯格看来，特殊教育站工作人员的任务，是教会这些孩子如何运用他们的孤独谱系天赋。他开始称他们为"小教授们"。他不仅将他们视为患者，更视其为发展最适合、最有效的教育方法时不可或缺的盟友。



One of his mentors in this quest was Harro, who behaved so outrageously in school. “Although the boy was aloof from things and people—or perhaps because of this—he had rich experiences and his own independent interests,” Asperger observed. “It was possible to talk to him as to an adult, and one could really learn from him.”

在这场探索中，他的导师之一是哈罗——那个在学校里行为极其出格的孩子。阿斯伯格观察到："尽管这个男孩对事物和人都很疏离——或许正因如此——他拥有丰富的经历和独立的兴趣。可以像与成年人交谈一样与他对话，而且真的能从他身上学到东西。"



Among the things he learned was that trying to leverage peer pressure in the classroom didn’t work with these children, because they were already alienated from their peers. Flattery was equally ineffective, as they were curiously immune to it. What kids like Harro did care passionately about, however, was logic. They had an innate desire—almost a compulsion—to seek out universal laws and objective principles. (Paradoxically, this could result in the appearance of impairment, as when Gottfried got distracted on the free-association test by Weiss’s suggestion that he think of nouns.)

他学到的其中一点是：试图利用课堂上的同伴压力对这些孩子并不奏效，因为他们早已与同龄人疏离。奉承同样无效，他们对此出奇地免疫。然而，像哈罗这样的孩子真正热切关注的是逻辑。他们天生就有一种渴望——近乎强迫症——去寻求普遍规律和客观原则。（矛盾的是，这可能导致出现损伤的表象，就像戈特弗里德在自由联想测试中因韦斯建议他思考名词而分心时那样。）



The primary motivation for learning in typical children was their emotional (“affective”) identification with the teacher. But autistic children sought learning for its own sake in the course of pursuing their passionate interests. They didn’t care how their teachers felt about them; they just wanted to know the facts. The best teachers for these children, Asperger observed, were willing to meet the children halfway, instead of insisting that they act like everybody else.

典型儿童学习的主要动力源于对教师的情感认同。但孤独谱系儿童在追求自己热爱的兴趣过程中，会为了学习本身而学习。他们不在乎老师如何看待自己，只想知道事实真相。阿斯伯格观察到，最适合这些孩子的老师，是那些愿意主动迁就他们，而非坚持要求他们像其他人一样行事的教师。



The teacher must at all costs be calm and collected and must remain in control. He should give his instructions in a cool and objective manner, without being intrusive. A lesson with such a child may look easy and appear to run along in a calm, self-evident manner. It may even seem that the child is simply allowed to get away with everything, any teaching being merely incidental. Nothing could be further from the truth. In reality, the guidance of these children requires a high degree of effort and concentration.

教师必须不惜一切代价保持冷静沉着，并始终掌控局面。他应当以冷静客观的方式下达指令，避免带有侵入性。与这类孩子上课时，课堂看似轻松，教学在平静而理所当然的氛围中进行。甚至可能让人觉得孩子可以为所欲为，教学不过是附带之事。事实远非如此。实际上，引导这些孩子需要付出极大的努力和专注。



He put it even more succinctly in a 1953 textbook that has never been translated into English. “In short,” Asperger wrote, “the teacher has to become somehow ‘autistic.’”

在 1953 年一本从未被翻译成英文的教科书中，他更简洁地写道："简而言之，"阿斯伯格写道，"教师必须变得某种程度上的'孤独谱系'。"



—

——



WHO WAS THIS MAN who identified so strongly with children who no one else wanted to deal with? Not surprisingly, Asperger had been a gifted, eccentric, solitary child himself. He was born in 1906 in the village of Hausbrunn in Lower Austria, the eldest of three boys. But his brothers died young and he became an only child. Asperger’s mother, a pious and affectionate woman, doted on her surviving son. His father, by contrast, was a stern disciplinarian who hated working as an accountant—a tedious job that he felt was beneath him. Asperger attributed his own drive to excel in his studies to his father’s frustration at having been too poor to go to university.

这个与别人不愿打交道的孩子如此强烈共鸣的人是谁？不出所料，阿斯伯格本人也曾是一个天赋异禀、古怪孤僻的孩子。他于 1906 年出生在下奥地利州的豪斯布伦村，是家中三个男孩中的长子。但他的两个弟弟早年夭折，他成了独生子。阿斯伯格的母亲是一位虔诚而慈爱的女性，对幸存的儿子宠爱有加。相比之下，他的父亲则是个严厉的纪律主义者，憎恨自己作为会计的工作——这份乏味的差事让他觉得屈才。阿斯伯格将自己学业上的进取心归因于父亲因家境贫寒无法上大学的遗憾。



Studying the classics in elementary school, young Hans could lose himself in a book for a whole day, only realizing in a panic at night that he still had homework to do. He exasperated his peers by endlessly quoting poetry, particularly the verses of Franz Grillparzer, the wunderkind who wrote the oration read at Beethoven’s funeral before a crowd of twenty thousand weeping mourners.

在小学学习古典文学时，年幼的汉斯能整天沉浸在书中，直到晚上才惊慌地意识到作业还没做。他无休止地引用诗歌，尤其是神童弗朗茨·格里尔帕策的诗句，让同龄人感到恼火。格里尔帕策曾在贝多芬葬礼上为两万名哭泣的哀悼者朗读悼词。



After his five-act satire of the upper classes in Vienna flopped, he penned his own eulogy:

在他那部讽刺维也纳上层社会的五幕剧失败后，他为自己写下了悼词：



As a human being, misunderstood,

作为一个人类，被误解了，



as a civil servant, overlooked,

身为公务员，我被忽视，



as a poet, tolerated at best,

身为诗人，至多被容忍，



I drag my monotonous life away.

我拖着单调的生活前行。



Like a nineteenth-century Goth, Grillparzer the Miserable became a hero to Asperger the Outcast, who attributed his interest in autistic children to his youthful infatuation with the poet. (It’s tempting to speculate that the relative of Fritz he described as “one of Austria’s greatest poets” was Grillparzer himself.) “Reading is bound up with one’s fate and destiny,” Asperger mused in a radio interview in 1974. “A person finds what he needs, or, to put it another way, it finds him.”

如同十九世纪的哥特式人物，可怜的格里尔帕策成了被排斥者阿斯伯格眼中的英雄，后者将自己对孤独谱系儿童的兴趣归因于年轻时对这位诗人的迷恋。（人们不禁猜测，他描述为“奥地利最伟大诗人之一”的弗里茨的亲戚，正是格里尔帕策本人。）“阅读与一个人的命运和定数紧密相连，”阿斯伯格在 1974 年的一次电台采访中沉思道，“一个人会找到他所需要的，或者换句话说，是它找到了他。”



—



YOUNG HANS RESCUED HIMSELF from a life of tedious pedantry by falling in with a group of kindred spirits who called themselves the Wandering Scholars. One of dozens of “Catholic renewal” groups in Austria that aimed to rekindle respect for traditional Teutonic values in the years between the world wars, the Scholars would head off on monthlong treks to the wilderness to hike and read poetry aloud in the bracing Alpine air, which had the added benefit of getting them out from under the prying eyes of their Teutonic parents. Asperger met his wife-to-be, Hanna Kalmon, on one of these trips while taking field notes and honing the powers of observation that would serve him well as Lazar’s successor at the Heilpädagogik Station. Asperger called the Wandering Scholars “one of the noblest flowerings of the German spirit.”

年轻的汉斯通过结识一群志同道合、自称“漫游学者”的人，将自己从枯燥乏味的学究生活中解救出来。这个团体是两次世界大战期间奥地利众多“天主教复兴”组织之一，旨在重振对传统日耳曼价值观的尊重。学者们会踏上为期一个月的荒野徒步之旅，在令人神清气爽的阿尔卑斯山空气中远足并高声朗读诗歌，这还有一个额外的好处——让他们摆脱日耳曼父母窥探的目光。阿斯伯格在一次这样的旅行中遇到了他未来的妻子汉娜·卡尔蒙，当时他正在做田野笔记，并磨练观察能力——这些能力后来在他接替拉扎尔担任海利帕达吉克站负责人时发挥了重要作用。阿斯伯格称漫游学者为“德国精神最高贵的绽放之一”。



The second turning point in his early life was a dissection in biology class, when he noticed an ivory-colored bump on the crimson surface of a mouse’s liver. Slicing into the bump with his scalpel, Asperger was shocked to see a long white worm wriggle out. Fascinated by the uncanny intimacy of the two creatures, he decided to devote his life to medicine. “The way that one life can live within another life,” he said, “shouldn’t one get to the bottom of that?”

他早年人生的第二个转折点，是生物课上的解剖实验。当他注意到老鼠肝脏深红色表面上一个象牙色的凸起时，用手术刀切开那个凸起，阿斯伯格震惊地看到一条长长的白色蠕虫扭动着钻了出来。被这两种生物之间奇异的亲密关系所吸引，他决定将一生奉献给医学。“一种生命如何能在另一种生命体内存活，”他说，“难道不该追根究底吗？”



After enrolling at the University of Vienna, Asperger attracted the attention of his future mentor, Franz Hamburger, a charismatic pediatrician who wrote an exposé of the role of unhygienic living conditions in the prevalence of tuberculosis among Vienna’s poorest families. In this supremely capable physician, Asperger saw an embodiment of the motto of the Wandering Scholars: “To lead and to help.” In 1931, Hamburger assigned his eager young protégé to the Children’s Clinic, where he would work for the next two decades.

进入维也纳大学后，阿斯伯格引起了未来导师弗朗茨·汉布格尔的注意。这位富有魅力的儿科医生曾撰文揭露，不卫生的生活条件是结核病在维也纳最贫困家庭中流行的原因。在这位能力超群的医生身上，阿斯伯格看到了流浪学者格言“引领与帮助”的化身。1931 年，汉布格尔将这位热忱的年轻门生安排到儿童诊所工作，此后二十年他都在此任职。



Near the end of his thesis, which was published as Die “Autistischen Psychopathen” im Kindesalter (“Autistic Psychopathy in Childhood”) in a German journal of neurology in 1944, Asperger struck an oddly strident note:

在其论文接近尾声时，阿斯伯格以异常尖锐的语调写道——这篇题为《儿童期的“孤独谱系精神病态”》的论文于 1944 年发表在德国神经学期刊上：



The example of autism shows particularly well how even abnormal personalities can be capable of development and adjustment. Possibilities of social integration which one would never have dreamt of may arise in the course of development. This knowledge determines our attitude towards complicated individuals of this and other types. It also gives us the right and the duty to speak out for these children with the whole force of our personality.

孤独症的例子充分说明，即便是异常人格也能具备发展与适应的能力。在成长过程中，可能出现我们从未想象过的社会融合可能性。这种认知决定了我们对待此类及其他类型复杂个体的态度，也赋予我们权利与义务，以全部人格力量为这些儿童发声。



It’s easy to misread this passage only as an anodyne testimonial to the value of special education. But the true meaning of these lines becomes clear only when one examines the historical and political context in which they were written. Asperger’s statement was likely a last-ditch plea to his former mentor, who had gone off in a very different direction since his days as a champion of Vienna’s least fortunate families.

人们很容易将这段话仅解读为对特殊教育价值的温和证词。但唯有审视这些文字诞生的历史与政治背景，其真实含义才会显现。阿斯伯格的声明很可能是向昔日导师发出的最后恳求——这位导师自维也纳最不幸家庭的捍卫者时代起，便已走向截然不同的道路。



When Asperger submitted his thesis to Hamburger in 1943, the University of Vienna was a mockery of the prestigious center of learning that it had been five years earlier. Of the nearly two hundred senior members of the medical faculty, fewer than fifty remained, and their replacements were bumbling fanatics. Asperger’s colleagues, Anni Weiss and Georg Frankl, had been forced to flee the country, and many of the other former faculty were in exile, imprisoned in concentration camps, or dead of suicide. The beautiful city of Vienna had become an abattoir of surreal brutality.

1943 年，当阿斯伯格向汉堡提交论文时，维也纳大学已沦为对五年前那所著名学府的讽刺。医学系近两百名资深教授中，仅剩不到五十人，而接替者尽是些笨拙的狂热分子。阿斯伯格的同事安妮·韦斯和格奥尔格·弗兰克尔被迫逃离祖国，其余前教职人员大多流亡异乡、身陷集中营，或自杀身亡。这座美丽的维也纳城，已化作上演超现实暴行的屠宰场。



Asperger was speaking out with the “force of his whole personality” for the sake of children all over Europe who had not yet been murdered by a monstrous idea of human perfectibility—an idea that his supervisors, who were fervent Nazis, had imported from America.

阿斯伯格正以"全部人格的力量"为全欧洲的儿童发声——这些孩子尚未被一种关于人类完美性的可怕观念所戕害，而他的上司们（狂热的纳粹分子）正是从美国引进了这种观念。



V



In October 1921, the National Research Council, under the auspices of the National Academy of Sciences, hosted a gala weeklong event at the American Museum of Natural History in midtown Manhattan. The State Department had been mailing out invitations for months, and eager delegates arrived at the grand edifice on Central Park West from every continent on earth.

1921 年 10 月，美国国家研究委员会在美国国家科学院的支持下，在曼哈顿中城的美国自然历史博物馆举办了一场为期一周的盛大活动。国务院数月前便开始寄送邀请函，热切的代表们从地球各大洲汇聚到中央公园西侧这座宏伟的建筑中。



The Second International Congress of Eugenics was intended to be much more than another celebrity-studded science conference. Backed by the moral authority of one of America’s most prestigious museums and promoted in journals like Science and the Scientific Monthly, the congress was designed to be nothing less than the turning point in history when the human species seized control of its own destiny instead of trusting in the gradual process of natural selection. An icon displayed prominently on the brochures depicted a tree with roots in biology, psychiatry, politics, economics, statistics, genealogy, intelligence testing, and other fields, with the progressive-sounding slogan: “Eugenics is the self direction of human evolution.”

第二届国际优生学大会的意图远不止是一场明星云集的科学会议。它得到了美国最负盛名的博物馆之一的道德权威背书，并在《科学》和《科学月刊》等期刊上广泛宣传。这场大会被设计成历史转折点——人类物种从此将掌控自身命运，而非依赖自然选择的渐进过程。宣传册上醒目地展示着一棵象征性的树，其根系扎根于生物学、精神病学、政治学、经济学、统计学、谱系学、智力测试及其他领域，并配以充满进步意味的口号：“优生学是人类进化的自我导向。”



Museum officials devoted two floors of the building to the event, temporarily changing the names of Darwin Hall and Forestry Hall to Eugenics Hall and lining them with exhibits on heredity, psychology, climate change, human migration, “institutional management of the socially inadequate,” and animal husbandry. Two exhibits were devoted to contrasting aristogenic family lines (those that boasted evidence of specific talents) with cacogenic lines (those that carried specific “degenerate qualities”). A cross section of an ancient sequoia presided over the exhibit, situating the proceedings in the natural order of things. Papers were presented on “Racial Differences in Musical Ability,” “Distribution and Increase of Negroes in the United States,” “Inheritance of Mental Diseases,” and “Some Notes on the Jewish Problem.”

博物馆官员将大楼的两层用于此次活动，临时将达尔文厅和林学厅更名为优生学厅，并陈列了关于遗传学、心理学、气候变化、人类迁徙、“社会不健全者的机构管理”以及畜牧业的展品。其中两个展区专门对比了优生家族谱系（那些拥有特定才能证据的家族）与劣生家族谱系（那些携带特定“退化特质”的家族）。一棵古老红杉的横截面俯瞰着整个展览，将这场活动置于自然秩序之中。会上提交了关于“音乐能力的种族差异”、“美国黑人的分布与增长”、“精神疾病的遗传”以及“关于犹太人问题的若干笔记”等论文。



At one end of Eugenics Hall stood a statue of the “average young American male,” with a reminder that a hundred thousand white veterans had perished in the trenches of Europe two years earlier. At the other end of the hall, another statue depicted the Platonic ideal of the athlete, composited from the physiques of the “50 strongest men at Harvard.”

在优生学厅的一端，矗立着一尊“普通美国年轻男性”的雕像，并附有提示：两年前，十万名白人退伍军人已在欧洲战壕中丧生。而在大厅的另一端，另一尊雕像则描绘了柏拉图式的运动员理想形象，其体态综合了“哈佛大学 50 名最强壮男性”的体格。



The recent war in Europe was much on the mind of Henry Fairfield Osborn, the museum’s president, who delivered the welcoming address. The barrel-chested, blue-eyed son of a railroad magnate, he developed the theory of aristogenesis to explain long-term trends in evolution that he believed couldn’t be accounted for by random mutation and the pressures of natural selection. Attempting to reconcile his deep Presbyterian faith and science, Osborn was a proponent of conscious evolution—the notion that God set the universe in motion to engender the birth of geniuses in genetically superior family lines. A former dean of science at Columbia University, he refused to believe that any creature as noble as Homo sapiens could have evolved from one as lowly as a monkey. Instead, he promoted the theory of an aristocratic predecessor called Dawn Man, based on the alleged discovery of Piltdown Man, which was later revealed to be the most successful hoax in the history of British archaeology.

近期欧洲的战争让博物馆馆长亨利·费尔菲尔德·奥斯本忧心忡忡，他在欢迎致辞中提及此事。这位铁路大亨之子胸膛宽阔、蓝眼睛炯炯有神，他提出了"贵族起源论"来解释进化中的长期趋势——他认为这些趋势无法用随机突变和自然选择的压力来解释。为调和其虔诚的长老会信仰与科学之间的矛盾，奥斯本倡导"意识进化论"——即上帝启动宇宙运转，旨在通过基因优越的家族谱系孕育天才。这位哥伦比亚大学前理学院院长拒绝相信像智人这样高贵的生物竟能由猴子这般低等生物进化而来。相反，他基于所谓"皮尔当人"的发现（后证实为英国考古史上最成功的骗局），大力推崇名为"黎明人"的贵族祖先理论。



Osborn began his address on an ominous note. “Europe, in patriotic self-sacrifice on both sides of the World War, has lost much of the heritage of centuries of civilization which can never be regained.” As a result, he said, “in certain parts of Europe the worst elements of society have gained the ascendancy and threaten the very best.” He was vague about the identity of these “worst elements” but confided that his fellow scientists were gaining a new appreciation of the “spiritual, intellectual, moral, and physical value of the Nordic race.”

奥斯本以不祥的语调开始了他的演讲。“欧洲在世界大战中，双方都出于爱国的自我牺牲，失去了数个世纪积累的文明遗产，这些遗产永远无法挽回。”他说，其结果是，“在欧洲某些地区，社会中最恶劣的分子占据了上风，并对最优秀的分子构成了威胁。”他对这些“最恶劣的分子”的身份含糊其辞，但透露他的科学家同行们正对“北欧种族在精神、智力、道德和身体上的价值”有了新的认识。



At the same time, he insisted that, as a man of science, he was not advocating anything as barbaric as race hatred. “In the selection of the best we should know no prejudice. We write nothing down in malice,” Osborn said. It was just that “500,000 years of human evolution . . . had impressed certain distinctive virtues as well as faults on each race.” Striking a pose of magnanimity, he went on, “If the Negro fails in government, he may become a fine agriculturalist or a fine mechanic.” The Chinese and Japanese, meanwhile, clearly showed a cunning facility for poetry and art, particularly ceramics. The American democratic notion that “all men are born with equal rights,” he cautioned, must not be confused with the “political sophistry that all men are born with equal character and the ability to govern themselves and others.”

同时，他坚称，作为一名科学家，他并不主张任何像种族仇恨那样野蛮的行为。奥斯本说：“在选择最优秀者时，我们不应带有偏见。我们不会心怀恶意地写下任何东西。”只是“50 万年的人类进化……在每个种族身上都留下了某些独特的优点和缺点。”他摆出一副宽宏大量的姿态继续说道：“如果黑人在治理方面失败，他们可能会成为优秀的农业家或优秀的机械师。”与此同时，中国人和日本人显然在诗歌和艺术方面，尤其是陶瓷方面，表现出了一种狡猾的才能。他告诫说，美国民主观念中“人人生而平等权利”的说法，绝不能与“人人生而具有平等品格以及管理自己和他人的能力”这种政治诡辩混为一谈。



One of the most insidious forces undermining the viability of the human species, Osborn added, was the failure of governmental and religious institutions to safeguard the “monogamous” family (which he defined as “one husband, one wife”) against the rampant individualism promoted by decadent art forms that exalted selfish impulses over sober self-discipline. He ended his speech by saying that it was the duty of his fellow scientists to “enlighten government in the prevention of the spread and multiplication of worthless members of society, the spread of feeblemindedness, of idiocy, and of all moral and intellectual as well as physical diseases.”

奥斯本补充道，侵蚀人类物种存续能力的最阴险力量之一，是政府与宗教机构未能捍卫"一夫一妻制"家庭（他将其定义为"一个丈夫、一个妻子"），以对抗颓废艺术形式所助长的猖獗个人主义——这些艺术形式推崇自私冲动，贬抑清醒自律。他在演讲结尾表示，科学家同仁有责任"启迪政府，防止社会中无用成员的扩散与繁衍，防止心智薄弱、白痴以及所有道德、智力与身体疾病的蔓延。"



These views of race and disability were not fringe science—the ranting of a deranged extremist at the academic equivalent of a Ku Klux Klan rally. They were the perspective of a broad swath of the scientific mainstream in America after World War I, backed by ongoing research in the United States and Europe funded by major foundations like the Carnegie Institution and the Rockefeller Foundation. Of the fifty-three papers presented at the conference, forty-one were the work of American scientists.

这些关于种族与残障的观点并非边缘科学——并非精神错乱的极端主义者在学术界的"三 K 党集会"上的狂言。它们是第一次世界大战后美国科学界主流中相当一部分人的观点，并得到卡内基研究所和洛克菲勒基金会等主要机构资助的欧美持续研究的支持。在会议上提交的五十三篇论文中，有四十一篇出自美国科学家之手。



The honorary president of the congress was Alexander Graham Bell, inventor of the telephone and telegraph. Bell had his own theories about the threat that people with disabilities represented to the future of the species. His mother and wife had both been born deaf, and in 1883 he warned the National Academy of Sciences that unless the use of sign language was vigorously discouraged in schools for the deaf, society ran the risk of engendering “a race of deaf-mutes.”

本次大会的名誉主席是电话和电报的发明者亚历山大·格雷厄姆·贝尔。贝尔对残障人士给人类未来带来的威胁有着自己的理论。他的母亲和妻子都天生聋人，1883 年，他向美国国家科学院发出警告：除非在聋人学校中大力禁止使用手语，否则社会将面临产生"一个聋哑人种族"的风险。



The word eugenics (which means “the good birth”) was coined in 1887 by the younger half cousin of Charles Darwin, Francis Galton. A former child prodigy with a striking gift for data mining, he popularized the notion of regression toward the mean in statistical research, launched the science of forensics by discovering that each person possesses a unique set of fingerprints, and created the first weather maps. As Edwin Black described Galton in his history of eugenics in America, War Against the Weak:

"优生学"（意为"良好出生"）一词由查尔斯·达尔文的同父异母表弟弗朗西斯·高尔顿于 1887 年创造。这位曾被誉为神童、在数据挖掘方面天赋异禀的学者，在统计研究中推广了"均值回归"概念，通过发现每个人拥有独一无二的指纹而开创了法医学，并绘制了世界上第一张气象图。正如埃德温·布莱克在其关于美国优生学历史的著作《向弱者开战》中所描述的高尔顿：



He joyously applied his arithmetical prowess and razor-like powers of observation to everyday life, seeking correlation. Galton distinguished himself by his ability to recognize patterns, making him an almost unique connoisseur of nature—sampling, tasting, and discerning new character in seemingly random flavors of chaos.

他兴高采烈地将自己精湛的算术能力和敏锐的观察力应用于日常生活，探寻其中的关联。高尔顿以识别模式的能力而著称，这使他成为几乎独一无二的自然鉴赏家——在看似随机的混沌风味中采样、品味并辨别出新的特质。



Darwin’s son, Leonard, was the star speaker at the congress. Echoing Osborn’s fulminations about the imminent decline of the republic, he warned the assembly, “The inborn qualities of civilized communities are deteriorating, and the process will inevitably lead to an all-round downward movement.” To avert this catastrophe, he prescribed a tax increase on single people and childless couples while promoting childbearing as a patriotic duty for “naturally well-endowed” families. As for the hundreds of thousands of “idiots” confined to institutions, he hailed the American Stock Breeders’ Association’s experiments with sterilization by X-ray as a promising development, particularly since compulsory surgery tended to increase popular “prejudice” against the practice.

达尔文的儿子伦纳德是大会上的明星演讲者。他呼应奥斯本关于共和国即将衰落的激烈言论，警告与会者：“文明社会的先天素质正在退化，这一过程将不可避免地导致全面的下滑趋势。”为避免这场灾难，他提议对单身人士和无子女夫妇增税，同时将生育宣传为“天生禀赋优越”家庭的爱国义务。至于收容在机构中的数十万“白痴”，他称赞美国牲畜育种协会用 X 射线进行绝育的实验是前景可期的发展，特别是因为强制手术往往会增加公众对此做法的“偏见”。



—

——



ON THE LAST DAY of the congress, the delegates filed onto buses for a field trip to the Eugenics Record Office in Cold Spring Harbor on Long Island. Launched in 1910, the ERO was a massively influential organization funded by the widow of Union Pacific railroad baron E. H. Harriman, the Rockefeller family, and the Carnegie Institution. Until its demise in 1939, the organization churned out hundreds of papers on such topics as “fit and unfit matings” and the care and training of the “mentally and physically defective classes.” Teams of ERO investigators compiled voluminous “trait files” to tease out the role of heredity in such characteristics as woolly hair, protuberant noses, and “sinisterity” (left-handedness). They also mapped the pedigrees of such notable personages as Thomas Edison, Abraham Lincoln, Theodore Roosevelt, and Johann Sebastian Bach.

大会最后一天，代表们登上巴士前往长岛冷泉港的优生学记录办公室（ERO）进行实地考察。该机构成立于 1910 年，由联合太平洋铁路大亨 E.H.哈里曼的遗孀、洛克菲勒家族及卡内基研究所资助，曾是一个影响力巨大的组织。直至 1939 年关闭前，该机构持续产出数百篇论文，主题涵盖"适婚与不适婚配对"以及"身心缺陷阶层的护理与训练"。ERO 调查团队编纂了海量的"特征档案"，试图厘清卷发、鹰钩鼻、"左撇子"（sinisterity）等特征中的遗传因素。他们还绘制了托马斯·爱迪生、亚伯拉罕·林肯、西奥多·罗斯福及约翰·塞巴斯蒂安·巴赫等名人的家系图谱。



One of the institute’s primary interests was congenital disorders of the psyche. Field investigators fanned out across the Eastern Seaboard, touring prisons and mental institutions and rifling through medical records in a comprehensive effort to take a census of carriers of the genetic roots of insanity, criminality, perversion, dementia, melancholia, alcoholism, stuttering, lisping, vertigo, migraine, bedwetting, sleepwalking, wanderlust, and other alleged forms of degeneracy.

该研究所的主要关注点之一是先天性精神障碍。实地调查人员遍布东海岸各地，走访监狱和精神机构，翻阅医疗记录，全面普查那些携带疯狂、犯罪、变态、痴呆、忧郁、酗酒、口吃、咬舌、眩晕、偏头痛、尿床、梦游、流浪癖及其他所谓堕落形式基因根源的人。



Campaigns to sterilize the residents of mental asylums and prisons received a significant boost from experts who declared that people with developmental disorders were not only cognitively but morally impaired. Martin Barr, the chief physician at the Pennsylvania School for Feeble-Minded Children, insisted that his students suffered from “exaggerated sexual impulses” that they could not control, describing them as “mere creatures of the moment” and slaves to temptation. “Indeed they are so crooked that they are parallel to nothing,” Barr declared, “and one can hardly fathom how protean are the vagaries of mental defect.” Raising the specter of jails and reform schools filled with defectives waiting out their incarceration so they could return to lives of profligacy, he claimed that “idiots” and “imbeciles” were prone to becoming prostitutes, and “reproduce their kind 2 to 6 times more rapidly than do normal people.” He then took aim at laws intended to shield disabled children from exploitation and abuse:

对精神病院和监狱居民实施绝育的运动，得到了专家们的大力推动。这些专家宣称，发育障碍者不仅在认知上存在缺陷，在道德上同样有缺陷。宾夕法尼亚州弱智儿童学校的首席医师马丁·巴尔坚称，他的学生患有无法控制的“过度性冲动”，称他们为“纯粹的即时生物”和诱惑的奴隶。“事实上，他们如此扭曲，以至于与任何事物都无法平行，”巴尔宣称，“人们几乎无法理解智力缺陷的变幻莫测。”他提出，监狱和教养所里挤满了等待刑满释放的缺陷者，以便他们能重返放荡生活，并声称“白痴”和“低能者”容易成为妓女，并且“繁殖后代的速度是正常人的 2 到 6 倍”。随后，他将矛头指向了旨在保护残疾儿童免受剥削和虐待的法律：



While we have some laws for the protection of the feeble-minded we have accomplished but little to stem the tide of degeneracy, and pollution of our normal population . . . We must face the fact that the very life-blood of the nation is being poisoned by the rapid production of mental and moral defectives, and the only thing that will dam the flood of degeneracy and insure the survival of the fittest, is abrogation of all power to procreate . . . Unconsciously innocent poisoners of a normal race, they are nevertheless its worst enemy.

尽管我们制定了一些保护弱智者的法律，但在遏制退化潮流、防止正常人口受到污染方面，我们取得的成效微乎其微……我们必须正视一个事实：心智与道德缺陷者的快速繁衍，正在毒害国家的命脉。唯一能阻挡退化洪流、确保适者生存的办法，就是剥夺所有生育权……他们虽是无意识中污染正常种族的毒源，却正是其最危险的敌人。



Terrifying predictions of “race suicide” by clinicians like Barr exerted a decisive influence on the lay public’s view of disability. By 1937, a Fortune poll indicated that two-thirds of the magazine’s influential business readership was in favor of forcibly sterilizing mental patients.

巴雷尔等临床医生对"种族自杀"的骇人预测，深刻影响了普通公众对残障的看法。到 1937 年，《财富》杂志的一项民调显示，该杂志三分之二具有影响力的商业读者支持对精神病人实施强制绝育。



That initiative was already well under way. In 1909, a statute had been passed in California granting public-health officials the right to forcibly castrate convicts and the residents of the California Home for the Care and Training of Feebleminded Children, located in Sonoma County. Thirty U.S. states eventually passed similar laws, and a wave of sterilizations swept through asylums and prisons coast to coast.

这一举措早已在推进中。1909 年，加利福尼亚州通过了一项法规，赋予公共卫生官员对罪犯及索诺玛县“加州智障儿童护理与训练之家”的居民实施强制阉割的权力。美国最终有三十个州通过了类似法律，一场绝育浪潮席卷了全国各地的精神病院和监狱。



As influential as they were at home, American eugenicists received an even warmer welcome in Germany, where they found enthusiastic support for their ideas in another country that had recently suffered the loss of a generation of bright, physically fit young men in war. Fearing that this decimation would act like natural selection in reverse, the ambitious leaders of this proud and wounded nation undertook a plan to secure the future of their race by wiping “mental defectives” off the face of the earth forever.

美国优生学家在国内影响力巨大，但在德国受到的欢迎更为热烈。在这个同样因战争而失去一代聪慧健壮青年的国度，他们的理念获得了热切支持。这个骄傲而伤痕累累的国家，其雄心勃勃的领导者们担心这种人口锐减会像逆向自然选择一样发生作用，于是制定了一项计划——通过将“智力缺陷者”永远从地球上抹去，来确保本民族的未来。



VI



One day in 1920, Ewald Meltzer, director of the Katharinenhof State Home for Non-Educable Feebleminded Children in Saxony, sent out a survey to the fathers and male guardians of nearly two hundred children in his care. The survey was worded carefully, because Meltzer was unsure how parents would react to a strategy for reducing a burden on society that was swiftly gaining currency among his colleagues:

1920 年的一天，萨克森州卡塔林霍夫国家收容所（专收无法教育的低能儿童）的所长埃瓦尔德·梅尔策，向近两百名由其照管的儿童的父亲或男性监护人发放了一份调查问卷。这份问卷措辞谨慎，因为梅尔策不确定家长们会如何看待一种正在同事间迅速流行的、旨在减轻社会负担的策略：




Would you give your consent in every circumstance to a painless shortening of your child’s life, after an expert had determined him incurably imbecilic?

如果专家判定您的孩子患有无法治愈的智力障碍，您是否在任何情况下都同意以无痛方式缩短其生命？



Would you give your consent only if you could no longer care for your child, for example if you were about to pass away?

您是否仅在无法继续照顾孩子时（例如您即将离世）才同意这样做？



Would you give your consent if your child were suffering serious physical and mental anguish?

如果您的孩子正遭受严重的身心痛苦，您是否同意这样做？



What is your wife’s opinion of questions 1–3?

你的妻子对问题 1-3 有什么看法？




After reassuring his recipients that his questions were purely theoretical, Meltzer was surprised to discover how many of them responded favorably to the idea of “painlessly shortening” their children’s lives. In fact, some respondents told him that the relevant authorities should simply do whatever they thought wise, without distressing them by asking them for consent.

在向收信人保证他的问题纯属理论探讨后，梅尔策惊讶地发现，竟有如此多的人对"无痛缩短"子女寿命的想法表示赞同。事实上，有些受访者告诉他，相关机构只需自行做出他们认为明智的决定即可，不必征求他们的同意而徒增困扰。



“It would have been better if you hadn’t asked me at all, if you had just put the child to sleep,” one mother replied. “I would have preferred not to have been bothered with this question,” another said. “If it had been news of sudden death, we would have accepted it.” Meltzer concluded that these mothers and fathers “would like to free themselves and perhaps the child as well from a burden, but they would like to do it with a clear conscience.”

"你当初根本不该问我，直接让孩子安睡过去就好了，"一位母亲这样回答。"我宁愿没被这个问题打扰过，"另一位母亲说，"如果听到的是猝死的消息，我们反而能接受。"梅尔策由此得出结论：这些父母"既想让自己——或许也包括孩子——从重负中解脱，又希望问心无愧地做到这一点。"



The results of Meltzer’s survey bolstered popular support for the controversial theories of psychiatrist Alfred Hoche and penal law expert Karl Binding, who co-authored a book in 1920 called The Liberation and Destruction of Life Unworthy of Life. They argued that food and medical care are not everyone’s birthright but are properly earned by doing productive labor. They described disabled people as Lebensunwertes Leben (“life unworthy of life”), calling them “useless eaters” and “human ballast” who consume precious resources without repaying their debt to society. Ending the lives of these “empty human husks”—who were not even aware of the misery that they inflicted on others—was not only a socially beneficial act, Hoche and Binder claimed, it was the most compassionate thing that could be done under the circumstances.

梅尔策的调查结果进一步推动了公众对精神病学家阿尔弗雷德·霍赫和刑法专家卡尔·宾丁所提出的争议理论的支持。两人于 1920 年合著了《不值得生存的生命之解放与毁灭》一书，主张食物和医疗并非与生俱来的权利，而应通过从事生产性劳动来正当获取。他们将残障人士描述为“不值得生存的生命”，称其为“无用的食客”和“人类压舱物”，认为这些人消耗宝贵资源却未偿还社会债务。霍赫与宾丁声称，终结这些“空洞的人形躯壳”——他们甚至意识不到自己给他人带来的痛苦——不仅是一种有益社会的行为，更是当时情境下最富同情心的选择。



Their life is absolutely pointless, but they do not regard it as being unbearable. They are a terrible, heavy burden upon their relatives and society as a whole. Their death would not create even the smallest gap—except perhaps in the feelings of their mothers or loyal nurses.

他们的生命毫无意义，但他们并不认为这种生活难以忍受。他们是亲属和整个社会的沉重负担。他们的死亡甚至不会留下丝毫空缺——或许除了他们母亲或忠诚护士的情感之外。



One of the organizations likely to object was the Catholic Church. But in 1927, the Roman Catholic theologian Josef Mayer provided clerical cover for Hoche and Binding by publishing a book that argued that forced sterilization of “mentally handicapped” people was entirely in accord with Catholic ethics and tradition. Inconveniently, Pope Pius XI issued an encyclical three years later condemning the practice of sterilization for other than “therapeutic uses.” But the concept of “life unworthy of life” left a lasting mark on a culture struggling through a postwar depression, and the encyclical likely played a role in later persecution of Catholic clergy.

一个可能反对的组织是天主教会。但在 1927 年，罗马天主教神学家约瑟夫·迈耶出版了一本书，为霍赫和宾丁提供了教职掩护，声称对“智力障碍者”实施强制绝育完全符合天主教伦理与传统。然而三年后，教皇庇护十一世发布通谕，谴责除“治疗用途”外的绝育行为。但“不值得活的生命”这一概念，在战后萧条中挣扎的文化中留下了深刻烙印，而这份通谕可能间接导致了后来对天主教神职人员的迫害。



Hoche and Binder’s rhetoric resonated deeply with an aspiring politician who had been convicted of high treason for launching an unsuccessful coup against the leaders of the Weimar Republic inspired by Benito Mussolini’s seizure of power in Rome.
