Stewing in the Landsberg Fortress in Bavaria, this young man—whose name was Adolf Hitler—dreamed of leading his people to glory against the corrosive forces of liberal democracy.

霍赫与宾德的言论深深触动了一位野心勃勃的政治家——此人曾因效仿贝尼托·墨索里尼在罗马夺权，试图推翻魏玛共和国领导人未遂而被判叛国罪。在巴伐利亚兰茨贝格要塞监狱中，这个名叫阿道夫·希特勒的年轻人，梦想着带领他的人民对抗自由民主的腐蚀力量，走向荣耀。



Hitler referred to Landsberg as the “university” where he gave himself a crash course in eugenics. (Later, he allowed his name to be used in advertisements for Hoche and Binding’s book.) His bible on the subject was The Passing of the Great Race, a hodgepodge of racist pseudoscience, anti-immigration rants, and archaeological poppycock by a dapper, mustachioed Yale graduate named Madison Grant. Throughout the book, Grant refers to the descendants of the Mayflower families as the real “native Americans.” The thrust of his argument was that the Nordic “race” (a fictitious amalgam of Swedes, Danes, and other Northern Europeans) was rapidly becoming an endangered species, elbowed aside by “swarms” of ignorant Negroes, “servile” Orientals, and Polish Jews that had already “literally driven” whites “of the old stock . . . off the streets of New York City.”

希特勒将兰茨贝格称为“大学”，在那里他给自己上了一堂优生学的速成课。（后来，他允许自己的名字被用于霍赫和宾丁著作的广告中。）他在这方面的圣经是《伟大种族的消逝》，这是一本由一位衣着考究、留着胡子的耶鲁毕业生麦迪逊·格兰特所著的杂烩之作，充斥着种族主义伪科学、反移民的咆哮以及考古学上的胡言乱语。在整本书中，格兰特将五月花号家族的后裔称为真正的“美洲原住民”。他的论点核心在于，北欧“种族”（一个虚构的瑞典人、丹麦人和其他北欧人的混合体）正迅速成为濒危物种，被“成群结队”的无知黑人、“卑躬屈膝”的东方人和波兰犹太人排挤到一边，这些人群已经“实实在在地将‘老派’白人……从纽约市的街道上赶走了”。



Grant concluded that Galton’s strategies for encouraging men and women of the “genius-producing classes” to be fruitful and multiply would be insufficient to stem the rising tide of idiocracy. Instead, he directed his fellow eugenicists to develop more expeditious means of shoving alien usurpers and other forms of human ballast overboard:

格兰特得出结论，高尔顿鼓励“天才生产阶层”的男女多生育的策略，不足以遏制蠢材泛滥的浪潮。相反，他引导他的优生学同僚们开发更迅速的手段，将外来的篡位者和其他形式的人类累赘抛下船去：



A rigid system of selection through the elimination of those who are weak or unfit—in other words, social failures—would solve the whole question in a century, as well as enable us to get rid of the undesirables who crowd our jails, hospitals and insane asylums . . . This is a practical, merciful and inevitable solution of the whole problem and can be applied to an ever widening circle of social discards.

通过淘汰弱者或不适应者——即社会失败者——的严格筛选体系，将在一个世纪内解决整个问题，同时使我们能够摆脱那些挤满监狱、医院和精神病院的不良分子……这是对整个问题的一个实际、仁慈且不可避免的解决方案，并且可以应用于越来越广泛的社会弃儿群体。



This was martial music to the ears of young Hitler, who shared Grant’s visceral revulsion for social failures, defectives, and weaklings, despite the fact that with his dark hair and eyes, he hardly resembled the Nordic types exalted in the book. In Mein Kampf, the manifesto that Hitler dictated to his deputy Rudolf Hess while incarcerated in Landsberg, the future Führer put forced sterilization at the core of his vision of a new society while framing it as a compassionate defense of the lives of children yet unborn. The state, he wrote, “must declare unfit for propagation all who are in any way visibly sick or who have inherited a disease and can therefore pass it on, and put this into actual practice . . . Those who are physically and mentally unhealthy and unworthy must not perpetuate their suffering in the body of their children.”

这对年轻的希特勒而言如同战歌般振奋人心。尽管他本人深色头发与眼睛的相貌特征，与书中推崇的北欧人种标准相去甚远，但他与格兰特一样，对社会失败者、缺陷者和弱者怀有本能的厌恶。在兰茨贝格监狱服刑期间，希特勒向副手鲁道夫·赫斯口述了《我的奋斗》这部宣言。这位未来的元首将强制绝育置于其新社会愿景的核心，同时将其包装成对未出生儿童生命的慈悲捍卫。他写道，国家"必须宣布所有明显患病、或患有遗传性疾病可能传播者均不适宜繁衍，并付诸实践……身心不健康、无价值者不得将痛苦延续至子女身上。"



A 1913 textbook by Géza Hoffman called Die Rassenhygiene in den Vereinigten Staaten von Nordamerika (Racial Hygiene in the United States) became the seminal guide to applied eugenics for German biology students. As the National Socialist party rose to power in the 1930s, the body of American eugenic law became the blueprint for Nazi policies to defend Nordic—rechristened “Aryan”—Blut und Rasse (“blood and race”) from dysgenic influences.

1913 年，盖萨·霍夫曼（Géza Hoffman）所著的教科书《北美洲的种族卫生》（Die Rassenhygiene in den Vereinigten Staaten von Nordamerika）成为德国生物学学生应用优生学的奠基指南。随着 20 世纪 30 年代国家社会主义党崛起，美国优生法律体系成为纳粹政策保护北欧——后被重新命名为“雅利安”——血统与种族（Blut und Rasse）免受劣生影响的蓝图。



“We will not allow ourselves to be turned into niggers,” Hitler bragged to the editor of a conservative German daily in 1931. “The Nordic blood available in England, northern France and North America will eventually go with us to reorganize the world.”

“我们绝不允许自己沦为黑鬼，”希特勒在 1931 年向一家保守派德国日报编辑吹嘘道，“英格兰、法国北部和北美洲现有的北欧血统，终将与我们一同重组世界。”



Unlike their American counterparts, German eugenicists did not plan to limit their efforts to asylums, prisons, and schools for the feebleminded. Instead, they aimed to carry out the implications of eugenic theory to their fullest extent in the population at large.

与美国的同行不同，德国优生学家并不打算将他们的努力局限于收容所、监狱和弱智学校。相反，他们旨在将优生学理论的推论在全体人口中最大程度地付诸实践。



In July 1933, Reich Minister of the Interior Wilhelm Frick put the Law for the Prevention of Genetically Diseased Offspring into effect. Any German citizen who showed signs of schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, epilepsy, inherited blindness or deafness, Huntington’s disease, or alcoholism could be forcibly sterilized. The law also mandated the creation of Genetic Health Courts that decided the outcome of individual cases and heard appeals (which were rarely granted). In 1934 alone, 84,600 cases were brought to the court, resulting in 62,400 forced sterilizations. Eventually, more than four hundred thousand men, women, and children were sterilized against their will by the Nazi regime.

1933 年 7 月，帝国内政部长威廉·弗里克颁布了《预防遗传病后代法》。任何患有精神分裂症、双相情感障碍、癫痫、遗传性失明或聋人、亨廷顿舞蹈症或酒精中毒的德国公民，都可能被强制绝育。该法还规定设立遗传健康法庭，负责裁决个案并受理上诉（但上诉极少获准）。仅 1934 年一年，就有 84,600 起案件提交法庭，导致 62,400 例强制绝育。最终，超过四十万男女老少被纳粹政权违背意愿实施了绝育。



—



AMONG THE AUSTRIAN MEDICAL professionals who viewed the rise of Nazism with alarm was Hans Asperger himself. From April to the end of May in 1934, he took part in a practicum in Leipzig and Potsdam with child psychiatrists Hans Heinze and Paul Schröder, two of the leading eugenicists in Germany. On April 10, he wrote in his travel diary, “An entire people goes in a single direction, fanatically, with narrowed vision, certainly, but also with enthusiasm and dedication, with enormous discipline and control, with a terrible persuasiveness. Now only soldiers, soldierly thinking—ethos—Germanic paganism.”

在奥地利医学界中，汉斯·阿斯伯格本人也对纳粹主义的兴起感到警惕。1934 年 4 月至 5 月底，他在莱比锡和波茨坦与德国优生学领军人物汉斯·海因策及保罗·施罗德两位儿童精神病学家共同参与实习。4 月 10 日，他在旅行日记中写道："整个民族正狂热地朝着同一个方向前进，视野固然狭隘，却带着热情与奉献、巨大的纪律与克制，以及可怕的煽动力。如今只剩下士兵、军人思维——军人精神——日耳曼异教。"



As unsettled as Asperger was by the rapid militarization of German culture, he was willing to look the other way, dispassionately appraising the clinical work of Heinze and Schröder as he would the efforts of any fellow practitioners in the field. He wrote of his experiences during the practicum, “I find the teaching not too bad. To all appearances, the overall structure fits well with our perspectives, certainly in many details . . . well-grounded structure with clear, diagnostically useful concepts. One can learn a great deal there and work well. But I also think about the efforts that Dr. Frankl puts into his diagnostics for special-needs education.”

尽管阿斯伯格对德国文化的迅速军事化感到不安，但他仍选择视而不见，冷静地评估海因策和施罗德在临床方面的工作，就像评估该领域任何其他同行的努力一样。他在实习期间写道：“我觉得教学还不算太差。从各方面来看，整体架构与我们的观点相当契合，当然在许多细节上也是如此……结构扎实，概念清晰且具有诊断价值。在那里可以学到很多东西，工作也能顺利进行。但我也想到了弗兰克尔博士为特殊教育诊断所付出的努力。”



He also made an observation that was likely the first lighthearted comment about autism in history. “We have very good concepts for our own work, but we tend to express them in jargon that is understood very differently by outsiders—talk about autistic!—which makes it hard for us to pass them on to others.” Soon, however, Asperger would no longer have the luxury of being able to look the other way.

他还提出了一个观察，这很可能是历史上第一个关于孤独症的轻松评论。“我们对自己的工作有很好的概念，但往往用行话来表达，而外人对此的理解却大相径庭——这真是孤独谱系！——这使得我们很难将这些概念传递给他人。”然而不久之后，阿斯伯格就不再拥有能够视而不见的奢侈了。



—



TWO MONTHS AFTER ASPERGER returned from his practicum, agents of Hitler’s Schutzstaffel burst into the Chancellery building in Vienna disguised as police officers. Panicked members of the cabinet hid themselves behind thin doors that proved no match for the SS men, who smashed through them with rifle butts as they advanced on the apartment where Chancellor Engelbert Dolfuss had taken refuge. Simultaneously, eight Nazis took over the main radio station in the city. They shot the station manager, killed a radio actor with a hand grenade, and forced a newscaster to go on the air and declare that Dolfuss was resigning.

阿斯伯格结束实习两个月后，希特勒党卫军成员伪装成警察闯入维也纳总理府。惊慌失措的内阁成员躲在薄薄的木门后，却挡不住党卫军士兵——他们用枪托砸碎门板，直逼总理恩格尔伯特·陶尔斐斯藏身的公寓。与此同时，八名纳粹分子占领了市内主要广播电台。他们枪杀了电台经理，用手榴弹炸死一名广播演员，并强迫播音员在直播中宣布陶尔斐斯即将辞职。



Meanwhile, back at the Chancellery, Hitler’s men cornered Dolfuss, shot him in the head, and dumped him bleeding on a sofa, where he begged for a doctor, then for a glass of water, and finally for a priest. Dolfuss was hardly a liberal; rather, he was a proud Fascist who styled himself after Mussolini and had launched his own right-wing party called the Fatherland Front, which took as its symbol the swastika-like Krückenkreuz. The Italian dictator received word of Dolfuss’s assassination while signing off on blueprints for a new mental hospital and delivered the bad news himself to the chancellor’s pregnant widow, who was staying at his villa in Riccione with her two children.

与此同时，总理府内，希特勒的手下将陶尔斐斯逼入绝境，朝他头部开枪，任其倒在沙发上流血不止。他先是哀求找医生，接着要杯水，最后恳求见神父。陶尔斐斯绝非自由派人士——他实为效仿墨索里尼的狂热法西斯分子，曾创立名为"祖国阵线"的右翼政党，其标志是形似纳粹十字的拐杖十字徽章。意大利独裁者收到陶尔斐斯遇刺消息时，正签署新建精神病院的蓝图，随后亲自将噩耗告知总理身怀六甲的遗孀——她正带着两个孩子下榻于墨索里尼在里乔内的别墅。



This brazen attempt at a coup ultimately failed, but the fact that Nazis were assassinating Fascists for being insufficiently loyal to the Führer is an indication of the state of Austrian politics at the time. Dolfuss’s successor, Kurt von Schuschnigg, presided over a right-wing government that became more pro-German and anti-Semitic by the day. The Nazis were also escalating their propaganda war against the residents of Austria’s mental institutions. The official newspaper of the NSDAP, the Nazi party, featured full-page spreads of grinning “idiots,” zombielike “lunatics,” and dysmorphic babies under headlines about the “cruelty of compassion” and the blessings of forced sterilization. Posters appeared at “racial exhibits” showing strapping Aryan workers straining under the burden of scowling mental patients riding seesaws perched across their shoulders, with captions claiming that the cost of housing such patients into old age was fifty thousand reichsmarks.

这场厚颜无耻的政变最终以失败告终，但纳粹分子因法西斯分子对元首不够忠诚而刺杀他们的行径，足以反映当时奥地利政治的混乱状态。多尔夫斯的继任者库尔特·冯·舒施尼格领导的右翼政府日益亲德反犹。纳粹对奥地利精神病院居民的宣传战也在升级。纳粹党机关报《民族观察家报》以整版篇幅刊登咧嘴傻笑的"白痴"、僵尸般的"疯子"和畸形婴儿照片，配以"同情之残酷"和强制绝育之福祉等标题。在"种族展览"上出现的海报中，健壮的雅利安工人肩扛跷跷板，板上坐着愁眉苦脸的精神病人，配文声称将这类病人供养至老需耗费五万帝国马克。



At a time when belonging to NSDAP was practically obligatory at the university, Asperger did not join, according to his daughter. He may have been particularly averse to doing so because of his loyalty to the Wandering Scholars. The network of Catholic youth organizations known as the Neuland-Bund was originally inclined to support the party but turned against them once Nazis began openly persecuting members of the Church.

据阿斯伯格女儿所述，在大学里加入纳粹党几乎成为义务的时代，他本人并未加入。这可能源于他对"流浪学者"组织的忠诚。名为"新土地联盟"的天主教青年组织网络最初倾向于支持纳粹党，但当纳粹开始公开迫害教会成员时，他们转而反对该党。



Progressive youth groups like the Neuland-Bund were eventually banned in Austria, while their right-wing equivalents were assimilated into the Hitlerjugend (Hitler Youth movement), furnishing the Reich with a supply of indoctrinated soldiers. Meanwhile, both Asperger’s former mentor, Franz Hamburger, and his trusted colleague at the Heilpädagogik Station, Erwin Jekelius, became fervent party members.

像"新土地联盟"这样的进步青年团体最终在奥地利被取缔，而与之对立的右翼团体则被并入希特勒青年团，为帝国输送了大量被洗脑的士兵。与此同时，阿斯伯格的前导师弗朗茨·汉布格尔及其在特殊教育站的信任同事埃尔温·耶克利乌斯都成为了狂热的纳粹党员。



—

——



BY 1935, WHEN WEISS published her paper on Gottfried in the American Journal of Orthopsychiatry, a massive exodus from Austria was under way, prompted by new laws stripping Jews of property, jobs, and basic rights of citizenship. Jewish-owned businesses, homes, and tourist attractions all over the city were in the process of being “Aryanized” and turned over to non-Jewish owners. Signs appeared on park benches throughout the city reading NUR FÜR ARIER (“FOR ARYANS ONLY”), while children sang taunting songs about how the only green space left for Jews could be found in the cemetery.

到 1935 年，当韦斯在《美国精神病学杂志》上发表关于戈特弗里德的论文时，奥地利正掀起大规模外逃潮，起因是新法律剥夺了犹太人的财产、工作和基本公民权。全城犹太人的商铺、住宅和旅游景点正被"雅利安化"，转交给非犹太裔所有者。公园长椅上纷纷出现"仅供雅利安人使用"的告示牌，孩子们则唱着嘲讽歌曲，说犹太人仅剩的绿地只能在墓园里找到。



Hundreds of families mobbed the Jewish emigration agency every day, desperate to get out of the country, often leaving nearly everything they owned behind. Many Jews took flight to Palestine, where their parents and grandparents had sought refuge from successive waves of pogroms. Others headed to the United States, where the liberal immigration policies that Osborn had condemned at the Second Eugenics Congress offered them a safe harbor, but only if they could provide proof of employment. The same community of pediatricians, surgeons, psychoanalysts, and specialists in other fields that had turned “Red Vienna” into a global beacon of medical expertise was under siege. Of the nearly 5,000 physicians practicing in the city, 3,200 were Jews—a legacy of the Middle Ages, when medicine was one of the few occupations that Jews were allowed to enter, because doctoring in the era of the Great Plagues was an unenviable high-risk profession.

每天都有数百个家庭蜂拥至犹太移民机构，绝望地想要逃离这个国家，往往几乎抛下所有家当。许多犹太人逃往巴勒斯坦，那里曾是他们的父母和祖父母为躲避一波波迫害浪潮寻求庇护的地方。另一些人则前往美国，奥斯本在第二届优生学大会上谴责的自由移民政策为他们提供了安全港湾，但前提是必须提供就业证明。那个曾将"红色维也纳"打造成全球医学专业灯塔的儿科医生、外科医生、精神分析师及其他领域专家群体正遭受围困。在该市执业的近 5000 名医生中，有 3200 人是犹太人——这源于中世纪的遗留，当时医学是少数允许犹太人从事的职业之一，因为在大瘟疫时代行医是个令人避之不及的高风险行业。



Austria’s loss was the world’s gain. Anni Weiss was the first of Asperger’s team to leave, arriving in America in 1934. The clinic’s gifted diagnostician, Georg Frankl, took flight in 1937, emigrating to Maryland with the aid of a Jewish doctor who had left Austria years earlier. But as the NSDAP’s power and influence grew, the careers of the true believers thrived. Erwin Jekelius—whose sole lasting contribution to the pediatric literature in English was coining the term paradoxal obstipation to describe anal leakage and intestinal inflammation caused by a plug of fecal matter stuck in the rectum—became the city’s chief public-health officer at the urging of the president of the Evangelical Church Council, who was stacking the local government with officials loyal to the Reich. The venerable Society of Physicians on Frankgasse, where Freud had debuted his insights into the psyche, was purged of Jews and renamed the Viennese Medical Society.

奥地利之失，乃世界之得。安妮·魏斯是阿斯伯格团队中首位离开者，于 1934 年抵达美国。诊所里那位天赋异禀的诊断专家格奥尔格·弗兰克尔于 1937 年出逃，在一位多年前离开奥地利的犹太医生帮助下移居马里兰州。但随着纳粹党权势日增，真正信徒们的事业蒸蒸日上。埃尔温·耶克利乌斯——他对英文儿科文献的唯一持久贡献是创造了"矛盾性便秘"一词，用以描述因直肠内粪块堵塞导致的肛门渗漏和肠道炎症——在福音派教会理事会主席的敦促下成为该市首席公共卫生官员，而这位主席正用效忠帝国的官员充实地方政府。位于弗兰克巷的古老医师协会——弗洛伊德曾在此首次阐述其精神洞见——被清除了犹太人，更名为维也纳医学会。



In 1938, Asperger’s mentor Hamburger gave a lecture to the society titled “National Socialism and Medicine” that left no doubts about his loyalties. It was an odd speech for a physician of his stature, more of a rant on the power of faith healing (which he called “nature healing”) than to the work of “so-called scientists,” as he put it. He began by telling the roomful of eminent physicians that sports and tourism did “more for health than all the doctors put together.” Then he extolled the virtues of the “practical country doctor” (a ruddy Aryan, no doubt) who spread “courage and confidence” among the sick, inspiring blind, unquestioning faith among his patients. “This faith, in all cases,” he said, “leads to the improvement—often even to the elimination—of the symptoms of the disease.” Hitler was playing a similar role for the whole Reich, he explained. “Now we must face the fact that a single man, a non-medical man, through his superior qualities, has opened up new avenues of health for the eighty million folk of Germany.”

1938 年，阿斯伯格导师汉伯格在学会发表题为《国家社会主义与医学》的演讲，其政治立场昭然若揭。对于他这样地位的医生而言，这场演讲颇为怪异——与其说是阐述所谓"科学家"的工作，不如说是对信仰疗法（他称之为"自然疗法"）力量的狂热鼓吹。他开场便对满堂名医宣称，体育和旅游"对健康的贡献超过所有医生之和"。接着他盛赞"务实的乡村医生"（无疑是位面色红润的雅利安人）在病患中传播"勇气与信心"，激发患者盲目而绝对的信任。"这种信任在任何情况下，"他说，"都能改善——甚至消除——疾病症状。"他解释道，希特勒对整个德意志帝国正扮演着类似角色。"如今我们必须正视一个事实：一个非医学出身的普通人，凭借其卓越品质，为八千万德国人民开辟了健康的新道路。"



One of these avenues was the Führer’s refusal to coddle the mentally ill in “luxurious” institutions. Only an “over-intellectualized Jewish patient” would question the wisdom of his doctor’s diagnosis, Hamburger pointedly added—implying that only Jews and other undesirables would challenge Hitler’s diagnosis of society’s ills. It was a vision of the Reichsführer as the Grand Placebo, healing the Volk of all manner of maladies and malaises by overwhelming force of his “superior qualities.” Hamburger then affirmed his support for the Law for the Prevention of Genetically Diseased Offspring, adding a word of praise for Aryan women who turned away from the “manifold amenities of life” to do their patriotic duty of bearing an annual child for the Reich. “National Socialism is the true instrument for the achievement of the health of our people,” he thundered. “Under National Socialism, doctors are quite officially the führer of the people, called to the leadership of its health.”

其中一条途径是元首拒绝将精神病患者安置在“奢华”机构中娇生惯养。汉堡格尖锐地补充道，只有“过度理智化的犹太病人”才会质疑医生诊断的智慧——暗示只有犹太人和其他不受欢迎的人才会挑战希特勒对社会弊病的诊断。这是一种将帝国元首视为“大安慰剂”的愿景，通过其“卓越品质”的压倒性力量，治愈民族的各种疾病与不适。随后，汉堡格表达了对《预防遗传病后代法》的支持，并赞扬那些放弃“生活诸多便利”、履行每年为帝国生育一个孩子的爱国义务的雅利安女性。“国家社会主义是实现我们人民健康的真正工具，”他慷慨激昂地宣称，“在国家社会主义下，医生正式成为人民的元首，被召唤来领导人民的健康。”



Inexorably, the shadow of death was falling over Asperger’s little professors.

无情的死亡阴影正笼罩着阿斯伯格的小教授们。



VII



On March 11, 1938, as thousands of Austrians huddled by their radios, von Schuschnigg finally resigned, declaring that his army had been instructed not to oppose the Wehrmacht troops marching across the border. The final strains of the national anthem barely had faded from the airwaves before they were replaced by another sound rising from the streets: thousands of Austrians singing the “Horst Wessel Song,” the bombastic Nazi anthem that von Schuschnigg’s government had explicitly banned.

1938 年 3 月 11 日，当数千名奥地利人围坐在收音机旁时，许士尼格最终辞职，宣称他的军队已接到指令不得抵抗正在越过边境的国防军部队。国歌的余音尚未在电波中消散，便被街头升起的另一种声音取代：数千名奥地利人高唱着《霍斯特·韦塞尔之歌》——这首许士尼格政府曾明令禁止的纳粹党歌，气势汹汹地回荡在街道上。



Clear the streets for the brown battalions,

为褐色纵队清空街道，



Clear the streets for the stormtrooper!

为冲锋队员清空街道！



Millions are looking upon the swastika full of hope,

数百万人满怀希望地仰望纳粹万字旗，



The day of freedom and of bread dawns!

自由与面包之日破晓！



Millions are looking upon the swastika full of hope,

数百万人满怀希望地注视着纳粹标志，



The day of freedom and of bread dawns!

自由与面包之日破晓！



The long-awaited day of Anschluss—the “joining” of Austria and Germany—was at hand. Soon the former First Republic of Austria would be rechristened Ostmark, which meant “Eastern March.” To celebrate the homecoming of the Austrian-born Führer and his troops, swastika flags and banners fluttered from balconies and windows throughout the city. Cheering Austrians lined the streets as women and children showered the incoming convoys with cigarettes.

期盼已久的德奥合并日——奥地利与德国“联合”之日——即将到来。很快，前奥地利第一共和国将被重新命名为“东疆”（Ostmark），意为“东部边疆”。为庆祝这位奥地利出生的元首及其军队的回归，整座城市的阳台和窗户上飘扬着纳粹旗帜与横幅。欢呼的奥地利人夹道而立，妇女和儿童向驶入的车队抛洒香烟。



The official organ of the Wehrmacht described the day’s events like an ecstatic tent revival, tapping into the vein of Germanic paganism that Asperger had mentioned in his diary:

德国国防军的官方喉舌将当天的盛况描述得如同狂热的帐篷复兴会，深入挖掘了阿斯伯格在日记中提及的日耳曼异教脉络：



Everywhere and without exception, there was invisible, spontaneous contact from heart to heart, that mysterious flow of natural connectedness: in the mountains of Tyrolia, in the Salzburg hills, in Upper Austria, on the Danube and the Inn, and then into the farthest corners of Steiermark, Kärnten, the Vienna Woods and the Burgenland. It was more than mere liking—it was love at first sight. Who among our soldiers in gray or blue will ever forget the joy looking his way from the eyes of all the Austrians who lined the streets of the cities and villages! Who will not still hear the enthusiastic shouts that everywhere received him to the end of his days?

无论何处，无一例外，都存在着无形而自发的心灵相通，那种自然联结的神秘流动：在蒂罗尔的山间，在萨尔茨堡的山丘，在上奥地利，在多瑙河与因河之畔，直至施泰尔马克、克恩滕、维也纳森林和布尔根兰的最偏远角落。这已不仅仅是好感——而是一见钟情。我们身着灰蓝军装的士兵中，有谁会忘记那些夹道相迎的奥地利人眼中流露的喜悦？又有谁不会终生铭记那无处不在的热烈欢呼？



But the seasoned British war correspondent G. E. R. Gedye saw it differently:

然而，经验丰富的英国战地记者 G. E. R. 格迪却有着不同的看法：



As I crossed the Graben to my office, the Brown flood was sweeping through the streets. It was an indescribable witches’ Sabbath—storm-troopers, lots of them barely out of the schoolroom, with cartridge-belts and carbines, the only other evidence of authority being Swastika brassards, were marching side by side with police turncoats, men and women shrieking or crying hysterically the name of their leader, embracing the police and dragging them along in the swirling stream of humanity, motor-lorries filled with storm-troopers clutching their long-concealed weapons, hooting furiously, trying to make themselves heard above the din, men and women leaping, shouting and dancing in the light of smoking torches which soon began to make an appearance, the air filled with a pandemonium of sound in which intermingled screams of “Down with the Jews! Heil Hitler! Heil Hitler! Sieg Heil!”

当我穿过格拉本街走向办公室时，褐衫军的洪流正席卷着街道。 那简直是一场难以形容的“女巫安息日”——冲锋队队员，其中许多人刚从教室里出来没多久，腰间挂着弹药带，手持卡宾枪，除了臂上的卐字臂章外别无其他权威标志，正与叛变的警察并肩行进；男女老少歇斯底里地尖叫或哭喊着他们的领袖的名字，拥抱警察并将他们拖入汹涌的人流之中， 满载冲锋队的卡车里，士兵们紧握着长期藏匿的武器，喇叭声震天响，试图盖过喧嚣；男女老少在冒烟的火把光下跳跃、呐喊、起舞，火把很快便开始出现；空气中充斥着一片喧嚣，其中夹杂着“打倒犹太人！希特勒万岁！希特勒万岁！胜利万岁！”的呐喊。



Gangs of civilians calling themselves Rollkommandos roared up to department stores and shops in the Jewish quarter, smashed doors and windows, and dumped the inventory into waiting trucks, often assisted by the police. Mobs roamed the streets, stealing whatever they saw and dragging terrified families out of their homes in their nightclothes. One particularly brutal form of humiliation dished out to the Jews was the organization of Reibpartien (“scrub parties”), where men and women were forced to crawl on their hands and knees with toothbrushes and buckets of corrosive acid to erase anti-Anschluss slogans from the pavement. Gedye saw an elderly man and woman driven down the street by a phalanx of storm troopers to scrub an offending stencil from the base of a statue. As a crowd chanted, “We have found work for the Jews at last, work for the Jews at last!” the old man patted the hand of his wife, who was silently weeping.

自称为“滚轮突击队”的平民团伙呼啸着冲向犹太区的百货商店和商铺，砸碎门窗，将库存货物倾倒进等候的卡车中，而警察常常对此袖手旁观甚至协助。暴徒们在街头游荡，见物即抢，将惊恐的家人从家中拖出，许多人只穿着睡衣。一种尤为残酷的羞辱犹太人的方式是组织“擦洗队”，男女被迫手脚并用，手持牙刷和装有腐蚀性酸液的桶，爬行着擦除人行道上反对德奥合并的标语。盖迪目睹一对老年夫妇被一队冲锋队员押着穿过街道，去擦洗雕像底座上冒犯性的涂鸦。当人群高喊“我们终于给犹太人找到活儿干了，终于给犹太人找到活儿干了！”时，老人轻轻拍了拍默默流泪的妻子的手。



The Nazi medical establishment was preparing the ultimate punishment for those who threatened the Reich from within by passing their inferior qualities on to future generations. At an evening seminar for doctors hosted by the SS, Vienna neurologist Walter Birkmeyer told his colleagues, “Only the purity of our race and the health of our genes can save our people from decadence. It is our duty as fanatical followers to exterminate everything that is morbid, impure, and corruptive.”

纳粹医疗体系正准备对那些通过将低劣品质传给后代而威胁帝国内部的人施以终极惩罚。在一次由党卫军为医生举办的晚间研讨会上，维也纳神经学家瓦尔特·比尔克迈尔告诉他的同行们："唯有我们种族的纯洁与基因的健康，才能拯救我们的人民免于堕落。作为狂热的追随者，我们有责任消灭一切病态、不洁与腐败之物。"



The University of Vienna was transformed into the intellectual center of an academic movement to put Aufartung (racial improvement) and Rassenforschung (racial research) at the top of the medical agenda. When the campus reopened three weeks after the Anschluss, the newly installed dean of medicine, anatomist Eduard Pernkopf, delivered a rousing speech to the faculty before a somber portrait of the Führer, dressed in his storm trooper’s uniform and flanked by a cordon of SS men. He championed Nazism as an all-encompassing worldview that transcended medicine and science, advocated the use of “negative selection” by extinction (Ausmerzung), and praised Hitler as “the greatest son of our home country,” concluding his lecture with a triumphant triple “Sieg Heil!”

维也纳大学被改造为一场学术运动的知识中心，旨在将种族改良（Aufartung）和种族研究（Rassenforschung）置于医学议程的首要位置。在德奥合并三周后校园重新开放时，新任命的医学院院长、解剖学家爱德华·佩恩科普夫，身着冲锋队制服，在两侧党卫军士兵的护卫下，于一幅阴沉的元首肖像前向教职员工发表了激昂的演讲。他将纳粹主义推崇为超越医学与科学的包罗万象的世界观，倡导通过灭绝（Ausmerzung）实施"负向选择"，并称赞希特勒是"我们祖国最伟大的儿子"，最后以三声胜利的"胜利万岁！"结束了他的演讲。



The new dean wasted no time Aryanizing the most prestigious medical school in Europe. He ordered all faculty members to produce the birth certificates of their parents, grandparents, and spouses to “clarify” that they were of Aryan descent. They were also required to sign loyalty oaths to Hitler, and those who refused were forced to retire immediately. Within weeks, 80 percent of the medical faculty had been dismissed. (Among those fired from other departments were Erwin Schrödinger, who shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in physics with Paul Dirac, and the brilliant mathematician Kurt Gödel.) Swastika flags flew from the university’s main building, and the few Jewish students allowed to remain were required to produce “entry permits” to walk onto campus.

新院长上任后迅速着手对欧洲最负盛名的医学院进行雅利安化改造。他要求全体教职员工提交父母、祖父母及配偶的出生证明，以"澄清"其雅利安血统。所有人员还需签署效忠希特勒的誓词，拒绝者立即被强制退休。短短数周内，医学院 80%的教职员工遭到解聘。（其他院系被解聘的学者包括与保罗·狄拉克共同获得 1933 年诺贝尔物理学奖的埃尔温·薛定谔，以及天才数学家库尔特·哥德尔。）纳粹万字旗飘扬在大学主楼上，少数获准留校的犹太学生必须出示"准入证"才能进入校园。



Before the Anschluss, more than 5,000 physicians were practicing in Vienna. By that fall, less than 750 would remain. Many former professors at the university—the brightest minds of their generation—died in concentration camps. Others took their own lives. The zealots who took their places dismissed their former mentors and colleagues as “charlatans.”

在德奥合并前，维也纳有超过 5000 名执业医师。到那年秋天，仅剩不到 750 人。许多曾在该校任教的教授——他们这一代最杰出的头脑——死于集中营。另一些人选择了自杀。取代他们位置的狂热分子将昔日的导师与同事斥为"江湖骗子"。



The Reich rewarded its loyal servants handsomely. Pernkopf was appointed president (Rektor Magnificus) of the university and given special dispensation to work on his magnum opus, an anatomical atlas called Topographische Anatomie des Menschen. This epic multivolume work featured lavish watercolor plates of each organ, bone, and blood vessel in the human body, accurate in every hue and detail. Praised by the Journal of the American Medical Association as “a work of art,” Pernkopf’s Anatomy became the go-to guide for surgeons all over the world who needed to brush up on their knowledge of internal organs before attempting a tricky procedure. Only in 1996, when a Jewish surgeon working with a Holocaust scholar demanded an investigation in the letters column of JAMA, did the medical profession admit that it had been teaching students how to become surgeons for nearly sixty years with paintings of the flayed bodies of disabled children and political prisoners.

帝国对其忠诚的仆从给予了丰厚的回报。佩恩科普夫被任命为大学校长（Rektor Magnificus），并获得特殊许可，得以完成他的巨著——一部名为《人体局部解剖学》的解剖图谱。这部多卷本的史诗级著作以华丽的水彩画呈现了人体每个器官、骨骼和血管，色彩与细节均精准无误。被《美国医学会杂志》誉为“艺术杰作”的《佩恩科普夫解剖学》，成为全球外科医生在进行复杂手术前复习内脏知识的首选指南。直到 1996 年，一位与大屠杀学者合作的犹太外科医生在《美国医学会杂志》的读者来信专栏中要求调查此事，医学界才承认，近六十年来，他们一直用残疾儿童和政治犯被剥去皮肤的尸体画作来教导学生如何成为外科医生。



A decree by the German minister of education ensured that Pernkopf had an adequate supply of source material for his atlas, which proved so effective that some executions had to be delayed as fresh cadavers piled up outside his dissection rooms. The sympathies of the painters who contributed to this massive effort were never in question: their signatures were festooned with swastikas and SS lightning bolts, which were discreetly airbrushed out of later editions of the atlas by the publisher.

德国教育部长的法令确保了佩恩科普夫为其解剖图谱获得充足的素材来源，其效果之显著，以至于有些处决不得不推迟进行，因为新鲜尸体在他解剖室外堆积如山。参与这项浩大工程的画家们的立场从未受到质疑：他们的签名上装饰着纳粹标志和党卫军闪电符号，这些符号在后来的图谱版本中被出版商谨慎地用喷枪修掉了。



—



IN THIS CLIMATE OF rabid fanaticism, on October 3, 1938, Asperger gave the first public talk on autism in history, in a lecture hall at the University Hospital. It’s likely that Hamburger was in attendance, an imposing face in a sea of swastikas, and the children at the Kinderklinik were surely on his radar; a year earlier, the Vienna Psychiatric and Neurological Association issued a decree that “psychopaths” who could not be legally declared insane should be placed under continuous supervision as a permanent menace to society.

在这股狂热的偏执氛围中，1938 年 10 月 3 日，阿斯伯格在大学医院的演讲厅发表了历史上首次关于孤独症的公开演讲。汉堡很可能在场，在纳粹标志的海洋中，他那威严的面孔格外醒目，而儿童诊所的孩子们无疑也在他的关注范围内；一年前，维也纳精神病学与神经学协会发布了一项法令，规定那些无法被合法判定为精神错乱的“精神病患者”应被置于持续监管之下，因为他们是对社会的永久威胁。



Asperger began by stating the obvious: “We are standing in the midst of an enormous change of our intellectual life, which has taken over all aspects of this life, not least, the area of medicine . . . Here, we are dealing with the most precious good of the nation—its health.” He acknowledged that the “thorough change in our whole attitude” demanded by the Führer required medical workers to value the health of the Volk over the needs—and implicitly, the lives—of individual patients. “Many of the cases we deal with here are genetic disorders,” he admitted. Then he paid lip service to the obligation to report such cases to the appropriate committees: “As doctors, we must take the tasks emerging in this area with our full responsibility.” (After a similar statement in 1940, Asperger’s trusted colleague at the clinic, Josef Feldner, advised him that such rhetoric was “a bit too Nazistic for your reputation. I would omit the thanks to the Führer.”)

阿斯伯格以一句显而易见的话开场：“我们正身处一场知识生活的巨大变革之中，这场变革已渗透到生活的方方面面，医学领域也不例外……在这里，我们面对的是国家最宝贵的财富——健康。”他承认，元首所要求的“我们整个态度的彻底转变”，需要医务工作者将民族健康置于个体患者的需求之上——这实际上也意味着置于个体患者的生命之上。“我们这里处理的许多病例都是遗传性疾病，”他坦言。随后，他敷衍地表示有义务向相关委员会报告此类病例：“作为医生，我们必须以完全的责任感来承担这一领域出现的新任务。”（在 1940 年发表类似言论后，阿斯伯格在诊所的信任同事约瑟夫·费尔德纳建议他，这种措辞“对你的声誉来说有点过于纳粹化了。我会省略对元首的感谢。”）



But then Asperger pivoted in an unexpected direction: “Today, let me not discuss the problem from the point of view of the people’s health, for then we would have to discuss the laws for the prevention of diseased genetic material; instead we will address it from the point of view of the abnormal children. How much can we do for these people? That shall be our question.” He then made a radical statement that might have caused his mentor to raise a disapproving eyebrow in the gallery: “Not everything that steps out of the line, and is thus ‘abnormal,’ must necessarily be ‘inferior.’”

但随后阿斯伯格转向了一个出人意料的论述方向：“今天，请允许我不从民众健康的角度来讨论这个问题——因为那样我们就不得不探讨预防遗传病基因的法律——而是从异常儿童的角度来审视。我们能为这些人做些什么？这才是我们的问题。”接着，他发表了一个可能让导师在旁听席上不悦地皱眉的激进观点：“并非所有偏离常规、因而被视为‘异常’的事物，都必然意味着‘低劣’。”



Asperger admitted that this assertion might “initially provoke protest.” But then he did something sly: he launched into the case histories of his patients, putting his audience on reassuringly familiar turf. First he described a boy brought to the clinic by his father with a baffling set of symptoms. He had frequent and explosive tantrums and described himself as anxious and “quite melancholic.” The boy’s hearing was unusually acute, and he could be kept awake by the slightest sound in his room. He was also obsessed with eating sour things, and his diet was extremely limited as a result.

阿斯伯格承认，这一论断“最初可能会引发抗议”。但他随后巧妙地转向了患者的病历介绍，将听众带回到令人安心的熟悉领域。他首先描述了一个被父亲带到诊所的男孩，其症状令人费解：这孩子频繁爆发剧烈脾气，自称焦虑且“相当忧郁”。他的听觉异常敏锐，房间里最细微的声响都能让他保持清醒。他还痴迷于食用酸味食物，因此饮食范围极为有限。



But there was another side to this boy, the pediatrician explained. His vocabulary and syntax were mature beyond his years, and he loved exploring philosophical questions in depth. He also had a keen eye for detecting faults in other people and himself, which indicated that he had sharp powers of observation. Asperger posed a provocative question: Were this boy’s precocious abilities merely the product of “hyper-compensation,” like Demosthenes, driven to become the greatest orator in Greece by his stuttering, which he conquered by learning to enunciate with a mouthful of pebbles? No, said Asperger. “We do not believe this. We claim—not on the basis of theory, but on the basis of our experiences with many children like this—that this boy’s positive and negative qualities are two natural, necessary, interconnected aspects of one well-knit, harmonious personality. We could express it this way: this boy’s difficulties—which particularly affect his relationships with himself and other people—are the price that he has to pay for his special gifts.”

但这位儿科医生解释说，这个男孩还有另一面。他的词汇量和句法结构远超同龄人，并且热衷于深入探讨哲学问题。他还善于发现他人及自身的缺陷，这表明他具有敏锐的观察力。阿斯伯格提出了一个引人深思的问题：这个男孩的超常能力是否仅仅是“过度补偿”的产物，就像德摩斯梯尼一样——他因口吃而被迫含着小石子练习发音，最终成为希腊最伟大的演说家？不，阿斯伯格说。“我们并不这么认为。我们主张——不是基于理论，而是基于我们对许多类似孩子的经验——这个男孩的积极与消极特质，是一个完整、和谐人格中两个自然且必然相互关联的方面。我们可以这样表述：这个男孩的困难——尤其影响他与自己及他人关系的那部分——正是他为了特殊天赋所必须付出的代价。”



Then Asperger proposed a radical way of thinking about cognitive disabilities that was in direct opposition to the dogma of racial hygiene. “The good and bad in a person, their potential for success or failure, their aptitudes and deficits—they are mutually conditional, arising from the same source,” he said. “Our therapeutic goal must be to teach the person how to bear their difficulties. Not to eliminate them for him, but to train the person to cope with special challenges with special strategies; to make the person aware not that they are ill, but that they are responsible for their lives.”

随后，阿斯伯格提出了一种与种族卫生教条直接对立的、关于认知障碍的激进思考方式。“一个人的优点与缺点、成功或失败的潜力、天赋与缺陷——它们互为条件，源于同一根源，”他说道，“我们的治疗目标必须是教会人们如何承受自身的困难。不是替他们消除困难，而是训练他们用特殊策略应对特殊挑战；让他们意识到自己并非患病，而是对自己的人生负有责任。”



Then he described a young patient who may have been the basis for his description of Harro. This boy was disruptive in class and “like a red rag to bulls” in the schoolyard. He was unable to dress himself without his mother’s help and seemed so unaware of other people that Asperger’s staff thought he was hard of hearing at first. But they found that even this “coarse, rough boy” had clever insights into his own behavior and expressed himself in highly creative ways when he was given the chance. All of these qualities together formed the clinical picture of autistic psychopathy, he explained. The special gifts of these children were inextricable from their impairments.

接着，他描述了一位年轻患者，这位患者可能正是他描绘哈罗的原型。这个男孩在课堂上捣乱，在操场上“像公牛眼中的红布”一样惹人注目。没有母亲的帮助，他无法自己穿衣，而且似乎对他人毫无察觉，以至于阿斯伯格的工作人员起初以为他听力有问题。但他们发现，即便是这个“粗鲁、莽撞的男孩”，对自己的行为也有着敏锐的洞察力，一旦给予机会，便会以极具创造力的方式表达自己。他解释道，所有这些特质共同构成了孤独谱系精神病的临床图景。这些孩子的特殊天赋与他们的损伤密不可分。



“Who among us does not recognize the autistic scientist,” Asperger said, “whose clumsiness and lack of instincts have made him a familiar caricature, but who is capable of extraordinary accomplishments in a highly specialized field?” He made a plea to his colleagues to “never give up” on these children, because “strengths and capacities” might appear in them as they came of age that were not immediately apparent. By helping them live up to their full potential, his colleagues would be benefiting society as a whole—a goal even a fervent eugenicist could support.

“我们中谁不认识那位孤独谱系的科学家呢，”阿斯伯格说道，“他的笨拙和缺乏直觉使他成为人们熟悉的讽刺形象，但他却能在高度专业化的领域取得非凡成就。”他恳请同事们“永远不要放弃”这些孩子，因为随着他们长大成人，那些最初并不明显的“优势和能力”可能会显现出来。通过帮助他们充分发挥潜能，他的同事们将造福整个社会——这是一个连狂热的优生学家都会支持的目标。



Then Asperger made a remark that provides crucial insight into why he based his prototypical cases on his chatty little professors rather than on the more profoundly impaired children he saw in institutions. “I thought it more rewarding to choose two not too severe and thus more promising cases,” he said, “and to explain, in reference to them, the path of our therapeutic approach.” Unfortunately, his strategy of accentuating the positive to his Nazi superiors—shaped by the knowledge that the lives of his young patients were at stake—would contribute to widespread confusion in the coming decades.

随后，阿斯伯格发表了一番评论，这为我们理解他为何选择那些健谈的小教授而非他在机构中见到的更严重受损的儿童作为典型案例提供了关键线索。“我认为选择两个不太严重、因而更有希望的案例更有价值，”他说，“并以此为例，解释我们治疗方法的路径。”不幸的是，他为了向纳粹上级强调积极面而采取的策略——这源于他深知年轻患者的生命危在旦夕——将在未来几十年里导致广泛的困惑。



On the basis of the four prototypical boys in Asperger’s thesis, many clinicians and historians have assumed that he saw only “high-functioning” children in his practice, which ended up obscuring his most important discovery. The autism that he and his colleagues learned to recognize in prewar Vienna was “not at all rare,” was found in all age groups, and had a broad range of manifestations, from the inability to speak to an enhanced capacity for focusing on a single subject of interest for an extended period of time without distractions.

基于阿斯伯格论文中四个典型男孩案例，许多临床医生和历史学家曾认为他在诊疗中只接触过"高功能"儿童，这一认知最终掩盖了他最重要的发现。他和同事们在战前维也纳学会识别的孤独症"绝非罕见"，存在于各年龄段，且表现范围广泛——从完全无法说话，到能够长时间专注单一兴趣领域而不受干扰的增强能力。



In other words, it was a spectrum. Once you knew what to look for, you saw it everywhere.

换言之，这是一种谱系。一旦你知道该观察什么，就会发现它无处不在。



—

——



THAT NIGHT, the sun sank over the deserted sidewalk cafés of Vienna, marking the beginning of Yom Kippur, the most sacred day in the Jewish calendar. For the next twenty-four hours, storm troopers and Rollerkommandos made brutal raids in once-thriving neighborhoods, stealing, burning, plundering, and killing.

那夜，夕阳沉入维也纳空寂的露天咖啡馆，标志着犹太历中最神圣的日子——赎罪日的开始。接下来的二十四小时里，冲锋队和滚轮突击队在曾经繁华的街区展开残暴突袭，烧杀抢掠，无恶不作。



This paroxysm of street violence turned out to be only a preview of the horror unleashed a month later on Kristallnacht, the Night of Broken Glass, when ninety-five synagogues in Vienna went up in flames, and Jewish homes, hospitals, schools, and shops were demolished with sledgehammers. In Berlin, more than thirty thousand Jews were dragged off to Dachau, Buchenwald, and other camps, most never to be seen again.

这场街头暴力的爆发，只是一个月后“水晶之夜”（Kristallnacht）恐怖景象的预演。在那场“碎玻璃之夜”中，维也纳的九十五座犹太会堂被火焰吞噬，犹太人的住宅、医院、学校和商店被大锤摧毁。在柏林，超过三万名犹太人被拖往达豪、布痕瓦尔德等集中营，其中大多数人从此杳无音讯。



Meanwhile, Asperger’s old colleague, Erwin Jekelius, was rising swiftly through the party ranks. At the end of the year, he became the director of a former rehabilitation facility for alcoholics called Am Spiegelgrund (formerly known as Am Steinhof). There, he helped Nazi officials draft a secret plan to rid the world of children like Gottfried, Fritz, and Harro that would become Hitler’s blueprint for the Final Solution against the Jews. This monstrous scheme, which Jekelius and his cohorts carried out with brutal efficiency, began with the murder of a single child who had been declared an idiot by his doctors.

与此同时，阿斯伯格的老同事埃尔温·耶克利乌斯在纳粹党内的地位迅速攀升。年底，他成为一家名为“施皮格尔格伦德”（Am Spiegelgrund，原称“施泰因霍夫”）的前酗酒康复机构的负责人。在那里，他协助纳粹官员起草了一项秘密计划，旨在清除像戈特弗里德、弗里茨和哈罗这样的儿童，这项计划后来成为希特勒针对犹太人的“最终解决方案”的蓝图。这一骇人听闻的计划——由耶克利乌斯及其同伙以残酷的效率执行——始于一名被医生诊断为“白痴”的儿童的谋杀。



VIII



On February 20, 1939, a boy named Gerhard Kretschmar was born in the village of Pomssen, southeast of Leipzig, where Bach once played the organ at a funeral. The birth of a boy in rural Saxony would normally be cause for celebration, but Gerhard was born blind and intellectually disabled, with one arm and only a partial leg, and he was prone to seizures. His parents, Richard and Lena, were ardent Nazis. Richard brought his son to the University of Leipzig, where he begged the chief neurologist, Werner Catel, to “put him to sleep.”

1939 年 2 月 20 日，一个名叫格哈德·克雷奇马尔（Gerhard Kretschmar）的男孩出生在莱比锡东南部的波姆森村——巴赫曾在那里为一场葬礼演奏管风琴。在萨克森乡村诞下一个男婴本应是值得庆祝的事，但格哈德天生失明且智力残疾，只有一条手臂和部分残缺的腿，还容易癫痫发作。他的父母理查德（Richard）和莱娜（Lena）是狂热的纳粹分子。理查德带着儿子来到莱比锡大学，恳求首席神经学家维尔纳·卡特尔（Werner Catel）"让他安睡"。



The eminent neurologist was likely to be sympathetic to the request. In his own book Grenzsituation des Lebens (Extreme Situations of Life), Catel called children like Gerhard “such monsters . . . that they are nothing but a massa carnis”—theologian Martin Luther’s term for a heap of insensible flesh lacking a soul. But he informed the Kretschmars that he was powerless to help them, because the mercy killing of children was illegal. He hinted, however, that if Richard sent a letter directly to the Führer, he might be granted a special exemption.

这位著名的神经学家很可能对这项请求抱有同情。在其著作《生命的极限处境》（Grenzsituation des Lebens）中，卡特尔将格哈德这样的儿童称为"如此怪物……不过是堆 massa carnis"——这是神学家马丁·路德（Martin Luther）用来形容缺乏灵魂、毫无知觉的血肉之躯的术语。但他告知克雷奇马尔夫妇，自己无力相助，因为对儿童实施安乐死是违法的。不过他暗示，如果理查德直接向元首写信，或许能获得特别豁免。



The birth of Gerhard Kretschmar provided an opportunity that Hitler had been waiting for since his days in Landsberg prison. He dispatched Karl Brandt, one of his personal physicians, to Leipzig to examine the child. “If the facts given by the father were correct, I was to inform the physicians that [in Hitler’s name], they could carry out euthanasia,” Brandt later testified. “The important thing was that the parents should not have the impression that they were responsible for the death of the child.” He also told Catel and his colleagues that if any charges were brought against them in court, the Führer would personally intervene in their favor.

格哈德·克雷奇马尔的出生为希特勒提供了一个他自兰茨贝格监狱时期以来一直等待的机会。他派遣自己的私人医生之一卡尔·布兰特前往莱比锡检查这个孩子。"如果孩子父亲提供的情况属实，我就要告知医生们，[以希特勒的名义]他们可以实施安乐死，"布兰特后来作证说。"重要的是，不能让父母觉得他们对孩子的死亡负有责任。"他还告诉卡特尔及其同事，如果有人因此事在法庭上对他们提起诉讼，元首将亲自出面为他们辩护。



The doctors in Leipzig replied that euthanasia was already standard practice on the maternity wards—it just wasn’t talked about openly. While Catel conveniently went on vacation, one of his subordinates murdered the baby with an injection as the nurses took their coffee break.

莱比锡的医生们回应说，安乐死在产科病房已是常规做法——只是没有公开谈论而已。当卡特尔恰巧去度假时，他的一名下属趁着护士们喝咖啡休息的时间，用注射方式杀害了这个婴儿。



That summer, another of Hitler’s doctors, Theo Morel, prepared a memo detailing the financial burden on the Reich of long-term care for people with disabilities: “5,000 idiots costing 2,000 RMs [reichsmarks] each per annum = 100 million a year.” (Even his math was wrong, as 5,000 × 2,000 = 10 million, not 100 million.) He stressed that this bottom line was only a fraction of the true cost to the Reich, because these “creatures” aroused horror in normal people, sapping their strength at a time when they needed to prepare for war.

那年夏天，希特勒的另一位医生特奥·莫雷尔撰写了一份备忘录，详细说明了帝国为长期护理残障人士所承担的财政负担：“5000 名白痴，每人每年花费 2000 帝国马克，合计每年 1 亿帝国马克。”（连他的计算都是错的，因为 5000×2000=1000 万，而非 1 亿。）他强调，这一底线数字仅是帝国真实成本的一小部分，因为这些“生物”在正常人心中激起恐惧，在人们需要备战之际削弱了他们的力量。



In August, the Committee for the Registration of Severe Hereditary Ailments issued a decree calling for the registration of all children born with congenital abnormalities of any kind. Doctors and midwives were required to report all cases of deafness, blindness, Down syndrome, hydrocephaly, tic disorders, and other conditions to the committee. In return, they would receive a small sum for each report.

八月，严重遗传性疾病登记委员会颁布了一项法令，要求登记所有出生时患有任何先天性异常的儿童。医生和助产士必须向委员会报告所有聋人、盲人、唐氏综合征、脑积水、抽动障碍及其他病症的病例。作为回报，每报告一例，他们将获得一小笔报酬。



Under the pretext that Polish soldiers had made incursions into German territory, the Wehrmacht invaded Poland on September 1—the official start of World War II. A month later, Hitler signed a secret order authorizing the creation of a program called Aktion T-4, short for Tiergartenstrasse 4, the address of the Charitable Foundation for Curative and Institutional Care in Berlin. The aim of the program was to convert hospitals, clinics, and long-term-care institutions into factories of death for the carriers of heritable diseases and chronic disabilities. To provide legal cover for doctors and nurses who had already started murdering their patients, Hitler backdated the order by a month.

以波兰士兵侵入德国领土为借口，德军于 9 月 1 日入侵波兰——这标志着第二次世界大战正式爆发。一个月后，希特勒签署了一项秘密指令，授权启动名为"T-4 行动"的计划，该名称源自柏林慈善治疗与机构护理基金会所在地蒂尔加滕大街 4 号。该计划旨在将医院、诊所及长期护理机构改造成遗传性疾病与慢性残疾患者的死亡工厂。为给已开始杀害患者的医护人员提供法律掩护，希特勒将指令生效日期倒签了一个月。



Closed-door meetings were held throughout Germany and Austria to educate medical students about child euthanasia and T-4, which primarily targeted disabled adults. Smooth adoption of these programs was facilitated by the development of a sanitized clinical lexicon to discuss formerly unspeakable acts. People with disabilities were to be referred to as refractory therapy cases. Laws promoting euthanasia were dubbed negative population policies. The act of killing was called delivering final medical assistance. Clinics for disabled children were christened Kinderfachabteilungen—“specialist children’s wards.”

全德及奥地利境内举行闭门会议，向医学生传授儿童安乐死及 T-4 计划的相关知识，该计划主要针对残疾成年人。通过构建一套经过净化的临床术语体系来讨论原本不可言说的行为，这些计划得以顺利推行。残疾人被称为"顽固性治疗病例"，倡导安乐死的法律被冠以"负面人口政策"之名，杀戮行为被称作"提供最终医疗援助"，残疾儿童诊所则被命名为"儿童专科病房"。



A blizzard of official paperwork from Berlin enhanced the aura of respectability around these programs. The Reich Committee formulated questionnaires to determine which patients were candidates for final medical assistance, distributing copies by the thousands. Physicians were required to fill out a form on each patient in triplicate. These reports were reviewed by a panel of three medical experts in Berlin, who ticked a box on each form: a plus sign if the child was to die, a minus sign if the child was to be allowed to live, and a question mark for the handful of cases that required further consideration.

来自柏林的官方文件如雪片般纷至沓来，为这些项目增添了体面的光环。帝国委员会制定了调查问卷，用以确定哪些患者有资格接受最终医疗救助，并分发了数千份副本。医生们需为每位患者填写一式三份的表格。这些报告由柏林的三位医学专家组成的评审团审阅，他们在每份表格上勾选一个选项：若孩子将被处死，则标上加号；若允许孩子存活，则标上减号；对于少数需要进一步审议的案例，则标上问号。



Based solely on these forms—and without ever seeing the children—the committee made arrangements with local health officials for young patients marked with plus signs to be transferred to Kinderfachabteilungen. Often what made the difference between a plus sign and a minus sign was nothing more than the score on an IQ test. Now the floodgates were open.

仅凭这些表格——且从未见过这些孩子——委员会便与当地卫生官员安排，将标有加号的年轻患者转送至儿童专科部门。而决定加号与减号之差的，往往不过是一份智商测试的分数。至此，闸门大开。



—



UNDER JEKELIUS’S GUIDANCE, Am Spiegelgrund became the primary children’s killing ward for all of Austria. The institution had 640 beds when he arrived, and he added 240 more in a section of the hospital that he referred to as the Heilpädagogik Clinic, though therapeutic education was not on the agenda.

在耶克利乌斯的指导下，阿姆施皮格格伦德成为全奥地利主要的儿童杀戮病房。他抵达时该机构有 640 张床位，随后在被他称为"治疗教育诊所"的院区增设了 240 张床位——尽管治疗教育并非其真正议程。



Over the next five years, Jekelius and his successors, Ernst Illing and Heinrich Gross, murdered 789 children at the facility, including 336 from the infants’ ward. Most of these children had been diagnosed with feeblemindedness, epilepsy, or schizophrenia—the three diagnoses that autistic children were most likely to receive in the days before autism was an accepted diagnostic category. Nonverbal patients were favored for extermination because they created extra work for the nurses; eventually children who were “simply annoying” were added to the list.

接下来的五年间，耶克利乌斯及其继任者恩斯特·伊林与海因里希·格罗斯在该机构杀害了 789 名儿童，其中包括 336 名来自婴儿病房的受害者。这些儿童大多被诊断为智力低下、癫痫或精神分裂症——在孤独症被确认为诊断类别之前，孤独谱系儿童最常被贴上这三种标签。不会说话的患儿因增加护士工作量而优先被处决，最终连"单纯令人厌烦"的孩子也被列入死亡名单。



A variety of killing methods, all equally barbaric, were employed by Jekelius’s team and by medical staff in other institutions. Some children were killed with an injection of carbolic acid, and some with an excess of barbiturates; others were simply left outside, exposed to the harsh Austrian winter, until they contracted pneumonia. Parents would typically receive a note in the mail informing them that their son or daughter had died of natural causes. (The lesson of Meltzer’s 1920 survey of parents had not been lost on Hitler.) Often these notes also included a bill for cremation or burial expenses.

耶克利乌斯团队及其他机构的医务人员使用了多种同样残忍的杀戮手段。有些孩子被注射石炭酸致死，有些因过量巴比妥类药物丧命，还有些被直接遗弃在奥地利严冬的户外，直至患上肺炎。父母们通常会收到一封邮件通知，告知他们的孩子死于自然原因。（梅尔策 1920 年对父母的调查结果并未被希特勒遗忘。）这些通知中往往还附有火化或安葬费用的账单。



T-4 and the child euthanasia program became fertile ground for medical research that could not have been conducted in contexts where the patient was expected to live. One doctor at the Maria Gugging Psychiatric Clinic in Vienna specialized in killing the children in his care with massive doses of electroconvulsive therapy, which had recently been introduced to psychiatry by the Italian neurologist Ugo Cerletti, who was inspired by seeing a butcher immobilize pigs with shock before slitting their throats. Often children were subjected to elaborate procedures like spinal taps or an excruciating process called pneumoencephalography, which entailed the replacement of their cranial fluids with air or helium before their brains were X-rayed and they were finally allowed to die. After the war, Gross based his career as a prominent psychiatrist and neurologist on his research on hundreds of brains harvested during the program, which were stored in jars in the cellar of Am Spiegelgrund for decades.

T-4 计划与儿童安乐死项目为医学研究提供了沃土，这些研究在患者预期存活的情况下根本无法开展。维也纳玛丽亚·古金精神病诊所的一位医生专门用大剂量电休克疗法杀害他照管的儿童——这种疗法由意大利神经学家乌戈·切莱蒂引入精神病学领域，而切莱蒂的灵感来源于目睹屠夫在割喉前用电击使猪失去知觉。儿童们常常要接受复杂的程序，如脊椎穿刺，或一种名为气脑造影术的酷刑：先将其颅腔内的液体替换为空气或氦气，再进行 X 光拍摄，最后才被允许死亡。战后，格罗斯凭借该项目中收集的数百个大脑的研究成果，成为著名的精神病学家和神经学家，这些大脑标本在阿姆施皮格尔格伦德地下室罐中保存了数十年。



Another enthusiastic participant in the child euthanasia program was a psychiatrist named Hermann Pfannmüller, director of the Eglfing-Haar clinic in Munich, who led tours of his wards to educate psychiatry students about the pressing need to rid the world of these “empty human husks.” He claimed to have received dozens of letters from parents who were grateful to him for putting their sons and daughters out of their misery.

儿童安乐死计划的另一位热心参与者是慕尼黑埃格尔芬-哈尔诊所主任、精神病学家赫尔曼·普法恩穆勒。他带领学生参观病房，向他们灌输清除这些"空壳人类"的紧迫性，并声称收到数十封家长来信，感谢他让他们的子女脱离苦海。



His preferred method of delivering final medical assistance was to put children on his “special diet.” Pfannmüller once explained the rationale behind his diet to a medical student named Ludwig Lehner, who never forgot it. “For me as a National Socialist, these creatures obviously represent only a burden for our healthy national body,” the psychiatrist told Lehner. “We do not kill with poison, injections, and so forth, because that would only provide propaganda material for the foreign press . . . No, our method is, as you can see, much simpler and far more natural.” He explained that his diet consisted of giving the children ever-diminishing portions of food—strictly fat-free—until they were receiving no sustenance at all. As the obese psychiatrist uttered these words, a nurse lifted a skeletal infant from its crib. “This one,” Pfannmüller purred, “has two or three more days left.”

他实施最终医疗援助的首选方式，是让孩子们接受他的“特殊饮食”。潘穆勒曾向一位名叫路德维希·莱纳的医学生解释过这种饮食背后的逻辑，而莱纳对此终生难忘。“作为一名国家社会主义者，这些生物对我们健康的民族体而言显然只是负担，”这位精神病学家对莱纳说，“我们不用毒药、注射之类的手段，因为那只会给外国媒体提供宣传素材……不，你看，我们的方法要简单得多，也更自然。”他解释道，他的饮食方案是给孩子们提供不断减少的食物——严格无脂——直到他们完全得不到任何营养。当这位肥胖的精神病学家说出这些话时，一名护士从婴儿床里抱起一个骨瘦如柴的婴儿。“这个，”潘穆勒轻声说道，“还能活两三天。”



More than two hundred thousand disabled children and adults were murdered during the official phases of the child euthanasia and T-4 programs, and thousands more were killed in acts of “wild euthanasia” by doctors and nurses on their own initiative. Obviously, the notion of transporting hundreds of corpses on roads that had to be kept clear for military convoys was impractical. As clinics, hospitals, and schools throughout the Reich dedicated their resources to the programs, crematoria were built next to these institutions, with conveyor belts to transport the bodies from the Kinderfachabteilungen to the ovens. In some institutions, improvised furnaces on wheels were employed to dispose of the corpses.

在儿童安乐死和 T-4 计划的官方阶段，超过二十万名残疾儿童和成人被杀害，还有数千人死于医生和护士自行发起的"野生安乐死"行动。显然，在必须为军事车队保持畅通的道路上运送数百具尸体的想法并不现实。随着整个德意志帝国的诊所、医院和学校将资源投入这些计划，这些机构旁边建起了火葬场，并配有传送带将尸体从儿童专科部门运往焚化炉。在某些机构中，还使用了临时搭建的轮式焚化炉来处理尸体。



What began in secrecy inevitably became the subject of widespread rumors and gossip. Elderly people told their relatives with a knowing wink that once the feebleminded were gone, they would surely be the next “useless eaters” to go. Children became terrified of going to the doctor for any reason. When a bus pulled up to a clinic, they would say, “Here comes the murder-box again!” As mothers got wind of the fact that their children were not actually dying of natural causes, some made frantic attempts to intervene with the authorities. A nurse named Anny Wödl became frightened about the fate of her son, Alfred, who she had placed in Gugging after he failed to develop speech. (Like Gottfried’s grandmother, Wödl intuited that her son was highly intelligent and “understood everything,” despite the fact that he was nonverbal.) First she took a train to Berlin to make her case to the Reich Committee, but they told her that they were in favor of euthanasia and pointed her to the door.

始于秘密的行动，终究沦为流言蜚语的中心。老人们挤眉弄眼地告诉亲戚：等那些智障者消失后，下一个被清除的"无用饭桶"就该轮到他们了。孩子们从此对就医充满恐惧，每当公交车停在诊所门口，他们就会说："杀人车又来了！"当母亲们逐渐察觉孩子并非自然死亡，有人开始疯狂地向当局申诉。护士安妮·沃德尔担心起儿子阿尔弗雷德的命运——这个因语言发育迟缓被送进古金医院的孩子（就像戈特弗里德的祖母一样，沃德尔直觉认为儿子虽然不会说话，但极其聪慧，"什么都懂"）。她先乘火车前往柏林向帝国委员会申诉，却被告知他们支持安乐死政策，随后便被请出了门。



Finally Wödl made an appointment with Jekelius to plead her son’s case with him directly. “Dr. Jekelius was fully aware of what was happening,” she testified at the Nuremburg Trials. “It was unambiguously clear from his remarks that he totally endorsed the entire operation against ‘life unworthy of life’ and that he was prepared to act as the Nazi state demanded.” She begged Jekelius to at least grant her son a quick and painless death, and he promised to do that. On February 22, 1941, Alfred, six years old, perished of “pneumonia” at Am Spiegelgrund. When Wödl viewed her son’s corpse, it was obvious that he had died in agony.

最终，沃德尔与耶克利乌斯约好，亲自为儿子求情。“耶克利乌斯医生完全清楚正在发生什么，”她在纽伦堡审判中作证说，“从他的言论中可以明确看出，他完全支持针对‘不值得活的生命’的整个行动，并准备按纳粹国家的要求行事。”她恳求耶克利乌斯至少让儿子死得迅速而无痛苦，他答应了。1941 年 2 月 22 日，六岁的阿尔弗雷德在施皮格尔格伦德因“肺炎”去世。当沃德尔看到儿子的尸体时，很明显他是在痛苦中死去的。



The most fateful of these attempted interventions concerned an older female patient with schizophrenia named Aloisia Veit. She had spent most of her life chained to an iron bed, haunted by visions of a grinning skull. One day at his office, Jekelius was told that a distinguished guest had arrived to meet him: the Führer’s sister, Paula Hitler. She argued that Aloisia—who was her second cousin—should be allowed to live. It’s hard to imagine Jekelius turning her down without approval from Berlin, but Paula’s efforts were unsuccessful. (Undoubtedly her brother Adolf was not eager to have it known that he, too, had “cacogenic” influences in his family line.) At age forty-nine, Aloisia died in a room full of carbon monoxide at the Hartheim killing center.

这些干预尝试中最具决定性的一次涉及一位名叫阿洛伊西亚·法伊特的老年女性精神分裂症患者。她一生大部分时间都被铁链锁在铁床上，眼前时常浮现一个咧嘴骷髅的幻象。一天，耶克利乌斯在办公室被告知有位贵客到访：元首的妹妹保拉·希特勒。她为身为远房表亲的阿洛伊西亚求情，希望她能活下来。很难想象耶克利乌斯未经柏林批准就拒绝她的请求，但保拉的努力终究徒劳。（毫无疑问，她的哥哥阿道夫并不愿让人知道自己的家族血脉中也存在"劣生"因素。）四十九岁那年，阿洛伊西亚在哈特海姆杀人中心的毒气室里窒息而亡。



Paula’s passionate entreaty did, however, profoundly affect Jekelius in another way: he fell in love with her. The feeling was mutual, and Paula asked her brother for permission to marry him. But things did not go well. Shortly after she made her request, Heinrich Himmler, Reichsführer of the SS, had a telephone conversation with Reinhardt Heydrich, another high-ranking Nazi official praised by Hitler as “the man with an iron heart.” Among the notes that Himmler scribbled on a notepad during the call were two words: “Arrest Jekelius.”

然而，保拉的恳切请求却以另一种方式深深影响了耶克利乌斯：他爱上了她。这份感情是双向的，保拉向哥哥请求允许她嫁给耶克利乌斯。但事情进展并不顺利。在她提出请求后不久，党卫队全国领袖海因里希·希姆莱与另一位被希特勒赞誉为"铁石心肠之人"的纳粹高官莱因哈德·海德里希通了一次电话。希姆莱在通话时随手记下的笔记中，赫然写着两个词："逮捕耶克利乌斯。"



After a brief stint in jail, Jekelius was drafted into the Wehrmacht and sent to the Russian front, where he was swiftly captured by Red Army soldiers and shipped off to the Lubianka prison camp in Moscow. There, he earned his final footnote in history by befriending a fellow POW who later became a patient of Viktor Frankl, the psychiatrist-author of Man’s Search for Meaning, a memoir of surviving three years in Auschwitz, Theresienstadt, and Dachau. In a section of the book about redemption, Frankl wrote:

在短暂入狱后，耶克利乌斯被征召入国防军，派往东线战场，很快就被红军战士俘虏，押送至莫斯科的卢比扬卡战俘营。在那里，他因与一位后来成为维克多·弗兰克尔病人的战俘结为挚友，在历史上留下了最后注脚。弗兰克尔是《活出生命的意义》一书的作者兼精神科医生，这部回忆录记录了他如何在奥斯维辛、特莱西恩施塔特和达豪集中营幸存三年的经历。在书中关于救赎的章节里，弗兰克尔写道：



Let me cite the case of Dr. J. He was the only man I ever encountered in my whole life whom I would dare call a Mephistophelean being, a satanic figure. At that time he was generally called “the mass murderer of Steinhof” (the large mental hospital in Vienna). When the Nazis started their euthanasia program, he held all the strings in his hands and was so fanatic in the job assigned to him that he tried not to let one single psychotic individual escape the gas chamber . . .

让我引用 J 博士的案例。他是我一生中唯一遇到的一个我敢称之为梅菲斯特式的人物，一个撒旦般的形象。当时，人们普遍称他为“施泰因霍夫（维也纳的大型精神病院）的集体谋杀者”。当纳粹开始实施他们的安乐死计划时，他掌握着所有大权，并且对自己被指派的任务如此狂热，以至于他试图不让任何一个精神病患者逃脱毒气室……



Recently, however, I was consulted by a former Austrian diplomat who had been imprisoned behind the Iron Curtain for many years, first in Siberia and then in the famous Lubianka prison in Moscow. While I was examining him neurologically, he suddenly asked me whether I happened to know Dr. J. After my affirmative reply he continued: “I made his acquaintance in Lubianka. There he died, at about the age of forty, from cancer of the urinary bladder. Before he died, however, he showed himself to be the best comrade you can imagine! He gave consolation to everybody . . . He was the best friend I met during my long years in prison!”

然而，最近有一位前奥地利外交官前来向我咨询。他曾被囚禁在铁幕之后多年，先是在西伯利亚，后来在莫斯科著名的卢比扬卡监狱。在我为他进行神经学检查时，他突然问我是否认识 J 博士。在我给出肯定的回答后，他继续说道：“我是在卢比扬卡认识他的。他在那里去世，大约四十岁，死于膀胱癌。然而，在他去世之前，他展现出了你能想象到的最好的同志形象！他安慰每一个人……他是我在漫长的监狱岁月中遇到的最好的朋友！”



This is the story of Dr. J, the “mass murderer of Steinhof.” How can we dare predict the behavior of man?

这是“施泰因霍夫大屠杀者”J 博士的故事。我们怎敢妄自预测人类的行为？



IX



Though Maria Asperger-Felder’s claims that her father never joined the Nazi party are credible, owing to his loyalty to the Wandering Scholars, it’s unlikely that he would have been allowed to retain his position at the university without signing a loyalty oath to Hitler, given Pernkopf’s 1938 decree.

尽管玛丽亚·阿斯伯格-费尔德声称其父亲从未加入纳粹党的说法可信，这源于他对流浪学者的忠诚，但鉴于佩恩科普夫 1938 年的法令，若未签署对希特勒的效忠宣誓，他不太可能被允许保留大学职位。



Still, Asperger apparently refused to report his young patients to the Reich Committee, which created what he described in a 1974 interview as “a truly dangerous situation” for him. Twice, the Gestapo showed up at his clinic to arrest him. Both times, however, Franz Hamburger used his power as a prominent NSDAP member to intervene in his favor. How can we dare predict the behavior of man?

然而，阿斯伯格显然拒绝将他的年轻患者上报给帝国委员会，这在他 1974 年的一次采访中被描述为“真正危险的处境”。盖世太保曾两次到他的诊所逮捕他。但两次都是弗朗茨·汉布格尔利用其作为纳粹党重要成员的影响力出面干预。我们怎敢妄自预测人类的行为？



At one point, Asperger suggested to his superiors that his little professors would make superior code breakers for the Reich. By the time he filed his thesis to Hamburger in October 1943, however, he must have known that children like them all over Austria and Germany had already been sent to their deaths. Perhaps his concluding statement about “the duty to speak out for these children with the whole force of our personality” was written for the benefit of future generations. His thesis was published the following June in a journal called Archiv für Psychiatrie und Nervenkrankheiten.

曾有一度，阿斯伯格向上级建议，他那些"小教授们"可以为帝国培养成顶尖的密码破译专家。然而，到 1943 年 10 月他向汉伯格提交论文时，他必定已经知晓，在奥地利和德国各地，像这样的孩子们早已被送上了死亡之路。或许他论文结尾那句"以我们全部人格的力量为这些孩子发声是义不容辞的责任"，正是写给后世看的。次年六月，这篇论文发表在《精神病学与神经疾病档案》期刊上。



By then, the Reich needed doctors on the front lines more than it needed them in psychiatric clinics, and Asperger was drafted into the Wehrmacht. He first became an ambulance driver and then worked as a surgeon in a field hospital in Croatia. He maintained his correspondence with Sister Viktorine and the remaining members of his staff at the Heilpädagogik Station, continuing to participate remotely in roundtable discussions about his patients. He also jotted down his observations of Croatian culture in his ever-present pocket notebook. When his unit got lost in the mountains, he employed the orienteering skills he’d learned in the Wandering Scholars to guide them to safety by using his compass and the stars. “The fact that I was never called upon to kill anyone,” he wrote in his diary, “is a great gift of fate.”

那时，帝国在前线更需要医生，而非精神病诊所的医生，阿斯伯格因此被征召入国防军。他先是担任救护车司机，随后在克罗地亚的战地医院做外科医生。他与维克托琳修女及治疗教育站的其他工作人员保持通信，继续远程参与关于他病人的圆桌讨论。他还随身携带的笔记本上记下了对克罗地亚文化的观察。当他的部队在山中迷路时，他运用在流浪学者社团学到的定向技能，借助指南针和星辰引导队伍安全脱险。“我从未被要求杀人，”他在日记中写道，“这是命运赐予的厚礼。”



—

——



THE SUMMER OF 1944 was punishing for the British and American troops trying to fight their way to Vienna. The Austrian capital was nicknamed “the Reich’s air-raid shelter” because it was out of range of long-range bombers from England, and concrete Flaktürme formed a protective ring around the city like Sauron’s towers rising from the valley of Mordor. Between the ground artillery and the crack pilots of the Luftwaffe, one in ten Allied planes was blasted out of the sky.

1944 年的夏天，对于试图向维也纳推进的英美军队而言，是场残酷的考验。这座奥地利首都被称为"帝国的防空避难所"，因为它超出了英国远程轰炸机的航程范围，而混凝土防空塔像索伦之塔从魔多山谷升起般，在城周形成了一道防护环。在地面炮火与德国空军精锐飞行员的夹击下，每十架盟军飞机中就有一架被击落。



But the momentum of the war was at last turning decisively against Hitler. The successful invasion of Italy finally put the Reich’s air-raid shelter within range of the American flotilla stationed off the coast of Foggia, and mining the Danube critically disrupted Nazi fuel lines. That fall, the Allied forces were finally able to punch through the city’s defenses, though many lives were lost in the attempt.

但战争的势头终于决定性地转向不利于希特勒。意大利的成功登陆使驻扎在福贾海岸的美国舰队终于能触及"帝国的防空避难所"，而多瑙河的水雷封锁则严重破坏了纳粹的燃料补给线。那年秋天，盟军最终突破了维也纳的防御体系，尽管付出了惨重的生命代价。



Screaming toward the city at an altitude of twenty-five thousand feet, with hellstorms of small-caliber shells chipping away at their fuselages from below, young pilots would stack flak jackets on the floor, say a prayer, and head in to drop their lethal payloads. One day in September, while Asperger was still serving in Croatia, the University of Vienna became a target for the first time. Allied bombs rained down through the roof of the Children’s Clinic, reducing the Heilpädagogik Station to rubble.

在 25000 英尺的高空向城市俯冲，下方密集的小口径炮弹如地狱风暴般撕咬着机身，年轻的飞行员们将防弹衣堆在机舱地板上，做完祈祷便冲入战场投下致命载荷。九月的一天，当阿斯伯格仍在克罗地亚服役时，维也纳大学首次成为轰炸目标。盟军的炸弹穿透儿童诊所的屋顶倾泻而下，将治疗教育站炸成废墟。



As the ceiling gave way, Sister Viktorine threw her arms around one of her boys to protect him. They were buried together.

天花板坍塌之际，维克托里娜修女张开双臂护住她的一名男孩。两人一同被掩埋。







---


NeuroTribes








Four

四



FASCINATING PECULIARITIES

迷人的奇特之处



Dinosaurs don’t cry.

恐龙不会哭泣。



—ELAINE C.

——伊莱恩·C



Asperger survived the war, but his concept of autism as a broad and inclusive spectrum (a “continuum,” his diagnostician Georg Frankl called it) that was “not at all rare” was buried with the ashes of his clinic and the unspeakable memories of that dark time, along with his case records. A very different conception of autism took its place.

阿斯伯格幸存于战争，但他关于孤独症是一个广泛且包容的谱系（他的诊断师格奥尔格·弗兰克尔称之为“连续谱”）且“绝非罕见”的理念，却随着他诊所的灰烬、那段黑暗岁月中难以言说的记忆以及他的病历记录一同被埋葬。另一种截然不同的孤独症概念取而代之。



By the time Leo Rosa was diagnosed, that model of autism—invented by Leo Kanner—had prevailed for half a century, virtually unquestioned by clinicians who considered the Baltimore child psychiatrist the lone pioneer of the field of autism research. Asperger’s thesis, published in German a year after Kanner’s “Autistic Disturbances of Affective Contact,” became a mere footnote to his landmark accomplishment. All over the world, autism was referred to simply as “Kanner’s syndrome.” The fact that two clinicians, working independently on both sides of the Atlantic, discovered it nearly simultaneously is still considered one of the great coincidences of twentieth-century medicine.

到利奥·罗莎被确诊时，由利奥·坎纳创立的孤独症模型已主导了半个世纪，临床医生几乎从未质疑过这位巴尔的摩儿童精神病学家是孤独症研究领域的唯一先驱。阿斯伯格在坎纳发表《情感接触的孤独性障碍》一年后以德语出版的论文，仅成为其里程碑式成就的一个脚注。全世界都将孤独症简称为“坎纳综合征”。两位临床医生在大西洋两岸独立工作却几乎同时发现这一病症的事实，至今仍被视为二十世纪医学史上最伟大的巧合之一。



The annals of science are replete with episodes of multiple discovery, when a long-hidden pattern in nature suddenly reveals itself to independent investigators at the same time. Isaac Newton and Gottfried Leibniz developed calculus in parallel in the last years of the seventeenth century and then fought a bitter war of words for priority that lasted until Leibniz’s death. If it weren’t for astronomer August Ferdinand Möbius, those ingeniously twisted paper loops would be known as “Listing strips,” after Johann Benedict Listing, who published a paper about them first. “When the time is ripe,” mused mathematician Farkas Bolyai, “these things appear in different places in the manner of violets coming to light in early spring.”

科学史上充满了多重发现的案例，当自然界长期隐藏的规律突然同时向独立研究者显现时尤为常见。十七世纪末，艾萨克·牛顿与戈特弗里德·莱布尼茨各自独立发展出微积分，随后为争夺优先权展开了一场持续至莱布尼茨去世的激烈论战。若非天文学家奥古斯特·费迪南德·莫比乌斯，那些巧妙扭转的纸环本应被称为"李斯廷带"——约翰·本尼迪克特·李斯廷才是最早发表相关论文的人。数学家法卡斯·波尔约曾感慨道："当时机成熟时，这些发现会像早春绽放的紫罗兰般，在不同地方同时显现。"



Kanner himself encouraged the view that Asperger’s work was unworthy of serious consideration by maintaining a Sphinxlike silence about his Viennese counterpart, broken only once in his entire career. The fact that Asperger’s account of autism languished in obscurity, never cited by the world’s leading authority on the subject, is usually explained away by saying that in the aftermath of the atrocities committed by the Reich, clinicians in America and Europe were not eager to read papers translated from the German. Yet Kanner, as a native speaker, would not have required the services of a translator, and his other citations show him to have been intimately familiar with nearly every other paper written in the emerging field of child psychiatry during that era—in German, English, Russian, and any of the other dozen languages he spoke fluently.

坎纳本人通过对其维也纳同行保持斯芬克斯般的沉默，助长了阿斯伯格的研究不值得认真对待的观点——在他整个职业生涯中仅打破过一次沉默。阿斯伯格关于孤独症的论述之所以湮没无闻，从未被世界该领域权威引用，通常的解释是：在纳粹帝国犯下暴行之后，美英临床医生不愿阅读从德语翻译过来的论文。然而，作为德语母语者的坎纳根本不需要翻译服务，而他引用的其他文献表明，他对那个时代儿童精神病学新兴领域几乎每一篇论文都了如指掌——无论是德语、英语、俄语，还是他能流利使用的其他十几种语言。



In recounting the tale of his famous breakthrough to his colleagues, Kanner compared himself to the legendary Persian prince Serendip, who “went out for a stroll one day, with no particular quest in mind, and unexpectedly came upon a hoard of treasures,” as he put it.

在向同事们讲述自己那次著名突破的故事时，坎纳将自己比作传说中的波斯王子塞伦迪普，正如他所说："有一天他出门散步，心中并无特定目标，却意外发现了一堆宝藏。"



It was a good yarn, in keeping with his carefully cultivated image as a man destined from a young age to make a lasting contribution to society. But it wasn’t the whole truth. Kanner’s sin of omission had grave consequences for autistic people and their families, which are still playing out today. And the one clinician in America who knew the real story wasn’t apt to say anything about it in public, because he owed Kanner the ultimate debt: his life.

那是个精心编织的故事，与他自幼便立志为社会做出持久贡献的精心塑造形象相得益彰。但这并非全部真相。坎纳的隐瞒之过，给孤独谱系群体及其家庭带来了深重影响，其后果至今仍在显现。而美国唯一知晓真相的临床医生，却不愿在公开场合提及此事，因为他欠坎纳一个终极恩情：他的生命。



II



The life of Leo Kanner began in a culturally and spiritually rich, erudite, and humane world that was about to vanish. He was born Chaskel Lieb Kanner in 1896, in Klekotów, a tiny Ukrainian village near the Russian border. The mellifluous sounds of Yiddish—the beloved mame-loshn (“mother tongue”) of the shtetls of Eastern Europe—woke him each morning, encouraged him when he acted like a mensch (an honorable person), rebuked him when he committed mischief, and sent him to bed at night.

利奥·坎纳的生命始于一个文化底蕴深厚、精神富足、博学且充满人文关怀的世界，而这个世界即将消失。1896 年，他出生于乌克兰靠近俄罗斯边境的小村庄克莱科图夫，原名查斯凯尔·利布·坎纳。每天清晨，意第绪语那悦耳动听的音调——东欧犹太小镇备受珍视的“母语”——将他唤醒；当他行为端正、像个正派人时，它给予鼓励；当他调皮捣蛋时，它予以斥责；夜晚，它伴他入眠。



When he was five, his father, Abraham, taught him Hebrew by enlisting his help in translating the Torah. As they pondered the meaning of the sacred syllables, Kanner would hear Meir, his grandfather, making tea in a giant samovar in the next room. Abraham was a shy and unworldly man who wrote meticulously cross-referenced books on Jewish law without ever intending to publish them, which Kanner affectionately referred to as his “way of playing solitaire.”

五岁时，父亲亚伯拉罕教他希伯来语，让他帮忙翻译《摩西五经》。当他们思索神圣音节的含义时，坎纳能听到祖父梅尔在隔壁房间用巨大的俄式茶炊煮茶的声音。亚伯拉罕是个腼腆而不谙世事的男人，他精心撰写着相互引证的犹太律法书籍，却从未打算出版——坎纳亲切地称这为他的“单人纸牌游戏”。



Historian Adam Feinstein speculates that Abraham—who was celebrated in Klekotów for his astonishingly prodigious memory—had more than a touch of the syndrome that his son would become famous for discovering. By contrast, Kanner’s mother, Klara, was a brash extrovert who openly mocked her husband’s pious orthodoxy. (Her brother and sisters nicknamed her “Klara the Cossack.”) Kanner claimed that she regarded his father as “a sort of mechanical toy which she thought she could wind up to go in any direction, a walking encyclopedia of knowledge which she regarded as useless but which made her bask in the glory of reflected prestige.”

历史学家亚当·范斯坦推测，亚伯拉罕——在克莱科图夫以惊人的记忆力闻名——多少也带有他儿子后来因发现而闻名的综合征特征。相比之下，坎纳的母亲克拉拉是个粗鲁外向的人，公开嘲笑丈夫虔诚的正统信仰。（她的兄弟姐妹给她起了个绰号叫“哥萨克克拉拉”。）坎纳声称，她将父亲视为“某种机械玩具，以为可以随意操控其方向；一部行走的知识百科全书，虽认为其知识无用，却让她沐浴在反射荣耀的光辉中。”



Despite his mixed feelings for his mother’s worldly ways, Kanner was also drawn to a secular life. As a teenager, he discovered that Lieb is Yiddish for the Hebrew name Aryeh, which means “lion.” So he began calling himself Leo, which sounded more modern, instead of Chaskel Lieb. At the same time, his traditional Jewish upbringing nurtured a keen sense of social justice in him. He was haunted for years by the story of an elderly deaf man with a disabled son who was shot by a sentry when they inadvertently crossed one of the ever-changing local borders without heeding the cry of “Halt!” He waged heroic battles in his imagination against Tsar Nikolai II and other corrupt Russian officials for launching vicious pogroms against the Jews.

尽管坎纳对母亲世俗的生活方式感情复杂，但他同样向往世俗生活。少年时期，他发现"利布"在意第绪语中对应希伯来语名字"阿里耶"，意为"狮子"。于是他开始自称"利奥"——这个听起来更现代的名字，取代了原本的"查斯凯尔·利布"。与此同时，传统的犹太教育在他心中培育出强烈的社会正义感。一位聋哑老人带着残疾儿子，因无意间跨越当地不断变化的边界、未听见"站住！"的喝令而被哨兵射杀的故事，多年来始终萦绕在他心头。他在想象中与沙皇尼古拉二世及其他腐败的俄国官员展开英勇斗争，谴责他们对犹太人发起的残暴大屠杀。



After a business transaction went bad, the Kanners were compelled to move to Brody, the largest town in the region. There, Leo was personally exposed to anti-Semitism for the first time. One of the Polish teachers at his new school would throw open the classroom window saying, “It stinks! The Jews must be behind this.” He began playing hooky to attend meetings of agnostics and other freethinkers, eager to hear their ideas for building a more humane society free of strife between competing religious sects.

在一次商业交易失败后，坎纳一家被迫搬到了该地区最大的城镇布罗迪。在那里，利奥第一次亲身经历了反犹太主义。他新学校的一位波兰老师会推开教室的窗户说："真臭！肯定是犹太人搞的鬼。"他开始逃课去参加不可知论者和其他自由思想者的集会，渴望听到他们关于建立一个没有宗教派系纷争的更人道社会的想法。



After familiarizing himself with the sacred texts of Buddhism, Islam, and Protestantism, he plunged wholeheartedly into secular literature. He began reading Sherlock Holmes stories in pulp magazines and joined a neighborhood theater company that performed Shakespeare’s plays in German. Developing a passion for the poetry of Goethe, he started writing poems of his own and submitting them to literary magazines. He also employed his innate talent for complex forms of wordplay, inherited from his father, to burnish his social standing by writing verses and acrostics that his friends used to woo girls. Later in his life, Kanner would say that if he had been a more successful poet, he would have died in a concentration camp.

在熟悉了佛教、伊斯兰教和新教的圣典之后，他全身心投入世俗文学。他开始阅读廉价杂志上的福尔摩斯探案故事，并加入了一家用德语演出莎士比亚戏剧的社区剧团。对歌德的诗歌产生了热情后，他开始自己写诗并投稿给文学杂志。他还运用从父亲那里继承的复杂文字游戏天赋，通过创作朋友用来追求女孩的诗歌和藏头诗来提升自己的社会地位。卡纳晚年曾说，如果他当初是个更成功的诗人，恐怕早已死在集中营里了。



Based on what he heard in freethinkers’ meetings and his discussions with a bright and rebellious older boy, Kanner developed a personal philosophy that he considered his fundamental approach to life from then on. Most men and women, he believed, were stuck at an intermediate stage of evolution, still enslaved to the crude symbols and primitive superstitions that lurked behind the tenets of every major religion. A handful of bold visionaries, however, had managed to free themselves from the shackles of the old beliefs and were living in the liberated way that everyone would live in the future.

根据他在自由思想者集会上的所见所闻，以及他与一位聪慧叛逆的年长男孩的讨论，坎纳形成了一套个人哲学，并视之为此后人生的根本准则。他认为，大多数男女都困于进化的中间阶段，仍被潜藏在各大宗教教义背后的粗陋符号与原始迷信所奴役。然而，少数勇敢的远见者已挣脱旧信仰的枷锁，以未来人人皆可享有的解放方式生活着。



Kanner felt confident that he was a member of this elite group, destined to play a transformative role in society. Mindful of the poet Horace’s advice that profound truths are often best disguised as jokes (“Ridentem dicere verum quid vetat?” or “What prevents a laughing man from telling the truth?”), he decided to make ironic quips, elaborate double entendres, and droll bon mots his personal trademark—the outward sign of his inner liberation.

坎纳确信自己属于这群精英之列，注定要在社会中扮演变革性的角色。他铭记诗人贺拉斯的箴言——深刻的真理往往最适合以玩笑伪装（"Ridentem dicere verum quid vetat?"即"为何笑者不能道出真相？"），于是决定将讽刺妙语、精妙双关与诙谐警句作为个人标志——这外在表征正是他内在解放的印证。



Kanner also inherited his mother’s adept social skills and craving for public approval. One day during a final exam in his high school—where he was the top student in his class—he got a headache so painful that he was unable to finish his essay. When he got the exam back, however, he was surprised to discover that his teacher had rated it excellent anyway, reassuring him that if he could have finished, he would have surely turned in a masterpiece. He concluded, “Obviously, there is nothing like an established reputation.”

坎纳还继承了母亲娴熟的社交技巧和对公众认可的渴望。高中时，他是班上的尖子生。一次期末考试中，他头痛欲裂，无法完成论文。然而，当他拿回试卷时，却惊讶地发现老师仍给了他优秀评价，并安慰他说，如果他能写完，必定会交出一篇杰作。他由此得出结论："显然，没有什么比既定的声誉更有用了。"



In addition to sharing his father’s love for the intricacies of language, he boasted an equally prodigious memory. By the time Kanner enrolled at the University of Berlin in 1913, he had mastered Old German, Middle High German, modern German, Polish, French, Latin, Greek, Ruthenian, and a dabbling of Sanskrit, though he still spoke no English. Ignoring his grandfather’s advice to become a rabbi, he set out to study medicine, continuing to write poems on the side, which he would do for the rest of his life.

除了继承父亲对语言精妙之处的热爱，他还拥有同样惊人的记忆力。1913 年坎纳进入柏林大学时，已精通古德语、中古高地德语、现代德语、波兰语、法语、拉丁语、希腊语、鲁塞尼亚语，并略通梵语，唯独不会英语。他无视祖父让他成为拉比的建议，转而学医，同时继续写诗——这项爱好伴随了他一生。



Immersed in his studies, he regarded the tumultuous state of German politics from a comfortable distance. But when Austria-Hungary entered World War I in the summer of 1914, he was drafted into the army and ordered to serve in the medical corps. En route to his deployment, he stepped off the train to take a stroll in the woods and came upon the bodies of a dozen of his countrymen beside their dead horses in a clearing, slain in a surprise attack by Russian soldiers. Arriving on the front lines, he was ordered to set up a new field hospital with a jury-rigged operating table. Morphine was perpetually in short supply, and when tetanus swept through the ranks, the mortality rate was 100 percent. It was indeed a medical education, but not the one he’d had in mind. After months of being confronted with death and agony on a mass scale, he felt numb.

他沉浸于学术研究，与德国政局的动荡保持着舒适的距离。但当 1914 年夏奥匈帝国卷入第一次世界大战时，他被征召入伍，奉命在医疗队服役。在前往驻地的途中，他走下火车到林中散步，在林间空地上发现了十几名同胞的尸体，他们与死去的战马倒在一起，是在俄军突袭中遇害的。抵达前线后，他奉命用临时拼凑的手术台搭建新的战地医院。吗啡始终短缺，当破伤风在部队蔓延时，死亡率高达百分之百。这确实是医学教育，却并非他原本设想的那种。在连续数月直面大规模死亡与痛苦后，他感到麻木了。



Then, in a moment of pure grace, everything changed. In a little village in Galicia, Kanner met Dziunia Lewin, a sweet-faced fourteen-year-old girl with long blond braids who was the daughter of his mother’s cousin Chaim. Despite their six-year age difference, he was instantly smitten. He would stand in front of Dziunia’s house for hours, hoping to catch a brief glimpse of her as she ran errands. “All of a sudden, on a sleety winter evening,” Kanner recalled, “the world in which there were unreasonableness and pogroms and wars turned into a delightful paradise illuminated by the existence of one little girl.” When he resumed his studies after the war, he wrote letters to Dziunia every day, sending her more than two thousand pages in total. With her father’s blessing, he married her in 1921.

然后，在一个纯粹恩典的时刻，一切改变了。在加利西亚的一个小村庄里，坎纳遇见了齐乌尼亚·莱温，一个面容甜美、扎着金色长辫的十四岁女孩，她是母亲表亲柴姆的女儿。尽管两人相差六岁，坎纳却一见倾心。他会一连几小时站在齐乌尼亚家门前，只盼在她外出办事时能匆匆瞥见她一眼。“突然，在一个雨雪交加的冬夜，”坎纳回忆道，“那个充满无理、大屠杀和战争的世界，因一个小女孩的存在而变成了一个愉悦的天堂。”战后恢复学业时，他每天给齐乌尼亚写信，总共寄出了超过两千页的信件。1921 年，在齐乌尼亚父亲的祝福下，他娶了她。



By all accounts, Kanner was on the road to a brilliant career—in cardiology. Ironically, the one merely satisfactory grade he received at the university was in a psychology course taught by one of his heroes, Karl Bonhoeffer, a pioneering neurologist. Bonhoeffer parted ways with the father of diagnostic psychiatry, Emil Kraepelin, by pointing out the deceptively seductive power of naming a condition. The problem with labels, he said, is that they seem to correspond to disease entities that live independently of the patient, like types of viruses or bacteria. But in psychiatry, labels describe constellations of behavior that can be related to any number of underlying conditions. Bonhoeffer gave Kanner only a passing grade because he misinterpreted the symptoms of a patient with tabes dorsalis, a form of neural degeneration caused by an untreated syphilis infection. It was not the last time that Kanner would be tripped up by an error of interpretation.

据各方说法，坎纳本将在心脏病学领域拥有辉煌前程。讽刺的是，他在大学唯一获得"及格"成绩的课程，竟是由他崇拜的神经学先驱卡尔·邦赫费尔教授的心理课。邦赫费尔与诊断精神病学之父埃米尔·克雷佩林分道扬镳，指出为病症命名的欺骗性诱惑力。他说，标签的问题在于它们似乎对应着独立于患者存在的疾病实体，就像病毒或细菌的类型。但在精神病学中，标签描述的是可能与多种潜在病症相关的行为群集。邦赫费尔只给了坎纳及格分数，因为他误判了一名脊髓痨患者的症状——这种神经退化性疾病由未经治疗的梅毒感染引发。这并非坎纳最后一次因解读错误而栽跟头。



After earning his degree, he hung out his shingle as a general practitioner in Berlin, opening an office in the tiny apartment that he shared with his wife and their newborn daughter, Anita. He stitched up cuts, lanced boils, soothed queasy stomachs, and performed the other humble duties of a family doctor. In his unpublished memoir, he described an elderly female patient as a tiresome spinster who wasted his time by unburdening herself of her cares and woes during his examinations. “I must confess that, when I gave her a few minutes of my attention,” he wrote, “I felt a bit as John D. Rockefeller may have felt when he stopped to donate a dime to a street urchin.”

获得学位后，他在柏林挂牌成为一名全科医生，在与妻子和刚出生的女儿安妮塔合住的小公寓里开设了诊所。他缝合伤口、挑破疖子、缓解胃部不适，履行着家庭医生的各项平凡职责。在未出版的回忆录中，他将一位老年女病人描述为令人厌烦的老处女，认为她在就诊时倾诉烦恼和痛苦是在浪费他的时间。"我必须承认，"他写道，"当我给予她几分钟关注时，感觉自己有点像约翰·D·洛克菲勒停下来给街头流浪儿施舍一角钱时的感受。"



In a time of runaway inflation, he was clearly dreaming of grander things than consoling aging patients who could only afford to pay for his expertise with health insurance. His capacity for self-reinvention showed itself when the government issued a decree allowing dentists—traditionally low on the totem pole in German medicine—to earn a doctorate by writing a thesis. After a friend mentioned that his proposals to write on cavities and bleeding gums kept getting rejected, Kanner suggested that he collect tooth-related folklore from peasants in rural villages to frame the occupation of dentistry in the broader context of anthropology and psychology. Kanner’s friend’s next proposal was immediately accepted. As word got around, dentists all over the city started commissioning him to work the same magic on their own applications.

在恶性通货膨胀的时代，他显然憧憬着比安抚那些只能靠医保支付诊疗费的老年患者更宏大的事业。当政府颁布法令允许牙医——这个在德国医学界历来地位卑微的群体——通过撰写论文获得博士学位时，他自我重塑的能力便显现出来。有位朋友提到自己关于蛀牙和牙龈出血的论文提案屡屡被拒后，坎纳建议他收集乡村农民与牙齿相关的民间传说，将牙医职业置于人类学与心理学的更广阔背景中。这位朋友的下一份提案立即获得通过。消息传开后，全城的牙医纷纷委托他为自己的申请施展同样的魔法。
