Kanner launched an unlikely business on the side that became a little gold mine for his family: a “Literary Bureau for Dentists.” (Dziunia ended up doing most of the work, composing all the thesis abstracts and doing all the typing, while caring for Anita.) Seeking to raise his public profile even further, he organized public events for prominent Zionists visiting Berlin, including Albert Einstein and Sholem Aleichem. Kanner had a knack for cultivating friendships that he could turn to his social and professional advantage. He once diagnosed himself as a “collector of people.”

坎纳在业余时间创办了一项看似不太可能的副业，却成了他家的“小金矿”：一家“牙医文学局”。（实际上，大部分工作都由朱妮娅完成——她一边照顾安妮塔，一边撰写所有论文摘要并负责全部打字工作。）为了进一步提升公众知名度，他还为访问柏林的著名犹太复国主义者组织公开活动，其中包括阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦和肖洛姆·阿莱赫姆。坎纳擅长培养友谊，并善于将这些关系转化为社会与职业上的优势。他曾自诩为“人际收藏家”。



—



ONE OF THE FRIENDSHIPS he cultivated opened a door to an entirely new life. While substitute-teaching a course in electrocardiography in 1923, Kanner met a visiting American doctor named Louis Holtz who became a frequent dinner companion. Holtz regaled Leo and Dziunia with tales of his life in the United States but also confessed that he felt lonely there after the sudden death of his wife. Kanner had no burning desire to leave Germany, but the economy was in shambles, and his opportunities for advancement were limited. Even before the Nazis came to power, Jewish doctors had to work much harder than their colleagues to earn faculty positions and were only rarely allowed to become department chairs.

他所培养的一段友谊为他打开了一扇通往全新生活的大门。1923 年，坎纳在心电图学课程中担任代课教师时，结识了一位来访的美国医生路易斯·霍尔茨，两人后来经常共进晚餐。霍尔茨向利奥和朱尼娅讲述了自己在美国的生活趣事，但也坦言妻子突然去世后，他在那里感到孤独。坎纳并无离开德国的强烈愿望，但当时经济一片混乱，他的晋升机会也十分有限。甚至在纳粹上台之前，犹太医生就必须比同行付出更多努力才能获得教职，而且极少有机会成为科室主任。



A month after the Beer Hall Putsch that landed Hitler in Landsberg prison, Holtz told Kanner that if he ever wanted to come to America, he would provide the guarantee of employment required by immigration officials to apply for a visa. Two weeks later, Holtz found him a position as a psychiatric assistant at the Yankton State Hospital in South Dakota, with room and board for the whole family. If he wanted to take the job, the family would be required to relocate immediately.

在希特勒因啤酒馆政变被关进兰茨贝格监狱一个月后，霍尔茨告诉坎纳，如果他愿意来美国，自己会提供移民官员要求的就业担保，以便申请签证。两周后，霍尔茨为他在南达科他州的扬克顿州立医院找到了一份精神科助理的工作，并为全家提供食宿。如果他接受这份工作，全家必须立即搬迁。



After consulting an encyclopedia, Kanner’s cousin warned him that Yankton was an infamous “Indian trading post.” This argument proved insufficiently dissuasive. A crowd of relatives and friends came down to the train station to see the Kanners off, and in Cuxhaven, they boarded the SS Albert Ballin, a luxury liner named after the Jewish shipping magnate who invented pleasure cruises. The ship ran into rough seas during the crossing, but Kanner felt completely at peace venturing into the unknown with his beloved wife and daughter beside him.

查阅百科全书后，坎纳的表兄警告他，扬克顿是个臭名昭著的"印第安贸易站"。但这个理由显然不足以劝阻他。一大群亲友来到火车站为坎纳一家送行，他们在库克斯港登上了 SS 阿尔伯特·巴林号——这艘豪华邮轮以发明休闲航行的犹太航运大亨命名。航行途中遭遇了汹涌的海浪，但坎纳与挚爱的妻女相伴，在探索未知的旅程中感到无比安宁。



“The past was behind us and every knot removed us farther from it,” he wrote. “Everything was drenched in beauty.”

"过去已被抛在身后，每前进一海里，我们就离它更远一分，"他写道，"一切都浸润在美好之中。"



III



Kanner’s serene mood was shaken upon arriving in New York City, where a son of a German colleague accompanied him for his first ride on the subway. Seeing the other passengers clenching their teeth and swiveling their jaws in a rotary motion, Kanner ventured that the poor devils had been afflicted by a tic disorder in the wake of the global epidemic of encephalitis lethargica that began in 1918. His young host gently informed him that their fellow straphangers were in the grips of another plague entirely: the craze for chewing Wrigley’s gum, which had not yet caught on in Berlin. Kanner was mortified by his greenhorn error. “For many years to come,” he confessed, “I was embarrassed at the thought of my diagnostic blunder.”

坎纳抵达纽约时，原本平静的心情被打破了。一位德国同事的儿子陪他第一次乘坐地铁。看到其他乘客紧咬牙关、下颌不停旋转，坎纳猜测这些可怜的家伙可能患上了抽动障碍，源于 1918 年开始的全球性脑炎流行。年轻的向导轻声告诉他，这些乘客其实正被另一种"瘟疫"所困扰：嚼箭牌口香糖的热潮，这在柏林尚未流行开来。坎纳为自己初来乍到的错误感到羞愧。"此后多年，"他坦言，"每当想起这个诊断失误，我都感到难为情。"



After crossing the Great Plains by rail, Kanner discovered that the Yankton State Hospital—a sprawling institution that featured a reproduction of the Sistine Madonna in its marble lobby—was surrounded by more than fifteen hundred acres of farmland, which was used to raise pigs, corn, and dairy cattle to feed the patients. He wrote to a friend in Berlin that it was like working in a park. In the weeks to come, though, he was dismayed to find out that only one of his new colleagues—his supervisor, hospital superintendent George Adams—had any formal training in psychiatry. The nursing staff consisted of retirees seeking a productive way of passing the time, and the ward attendants were farmers’ sons and daughters looking to make a little money.

乘火车穿越广袤平原后，坎纳发现扬克顿州立医院——这座大理石大厅里陈列着西斯廷圣母像复制品的庞大机构——被超过一千五百英亩的农田环绕，这些土地用于养猪、种植玉米和饲养奶牛，以供给患者饮食。他在给柏林友人的信中写道，这就像在公园里工作。然而在接下来的几周里，他沮丧地发现，新同事中只有一人——他的上司、医院院长乔治·亚当斯——接受过正规精神病学培训。护理人员由寻求充实退休生活的长者组成，病房护工则是想赚点外快的农家子女。



The style of psychiatry practiced at the hospital seemed astonishingly primitive. Patients were diagnosed by popular vote after performing trivial tasks like counting backward from one hundred by seven and repeating tongue twisters like “truly rural” and “Methodist Episcopal.” Kanner was appalled by the spectacle of his senior colleagues trying “to look erudite when they cast their vote on whether a patient ‘had’ dementia praecox, manic-depressive psychosis, paranoia, general paresis, senile, alcoholic, epileptic, or ‘undiagnosed’ psychosis.” He concluded that the only virtue of this process was to bolster the insecure egos of the staff: “You were clever if you could distinguish unerringly between dementia praecox and manic-depressive psychosis, almost in the same manner that an experienced drinker can distinguish between Old Forester and Old Grand Dad.”

该医院所采用的精神病学诊疗方式原始得令人震惊。患者需完成诸如从 100 开始每隔 7 倒数、重复"真正乡村"和"卫理公会主教"等绕口令的琐碎任务后，由全体投票决定诊断结果。坎纳目睹资深同事们"在投票判定患者是否患有早发性痴呆、躁郁症、偏执狂、全身麻痹症、老年痴呆、酒精中毒性精神病、癫痫性精神病或'未确诊'精神病时，还要故作高深"的荒诞场景深感震惊。他总结道，这套流程的唯一价值就是安抚员工们不安的自尊心："若能像资深酒客区分老福斯特与老爷爷威士忌那样，准确无误地辨别早发性痴呆与躁郁症，便显得格外高明。"



While Kanner was making his rounds of the Stone Room—the staff’s nickname for Ward M, reserved for the most intractable cases of psychosis—a farmer named Charlie Miller who had been mute, catatonic, and bedridden for years sat up and said, “Dr. Kanner, I wish to have an interview with Dr. Adams.” The next morning, Miller got up out of bed and dressed himself with the help of an attendant. He spoke with Adams at length about making arrangements to ensure the financial security of his wife and children. For the next two weeks, he got up every morning, had breakfast in the dining room, and assisted the staff in caring for other patients who had been written off as hopeless. After that, just as abruptly, he refused to get out of bed again and never spoke another word until he died.

当坎纳在石头病房（工作人员对 M 病房的昵称，专门收治最棘手的精神病患者）查房时，一位名叫查理·米勒的农民——多年来一直沉默不语、处于紧张症状态且卧床不起——突然坐起来说道："坎纳医生，我想和亚当斯医生谈谈。"第二天早上，米勒在护理人员的帮助下起床穿衣，与亚当斯进行了长时间的交谈，商讨如何安排以确保妻儿的经济保障。在接下来的两周里，他每天早晨起床，在餐厅用早餐，并协助工作人员照料其他已被判定无望的患者。此后，他又突然拒绝下床，直至去世再未说过一句话。



One day Kanner told a schizophrenic farmer what he assumed would be distressing news: his son had also been diagnosed with schizophrenia and would soon be joining him on the ward. But the farmer, who managed the hospital print shop and spent an hour each day in quiet meditation, was unruffled. When his son arrived at the hospital, he patiently taught him to set type. From then on, they worked side by side, seemingly content in their shared silence.

一天，坎纳告诉一位患有精神分裂症的农民一个他以为会令人痛苦的消息：他的儿子也被诊断出精神分裂症，很快就要和他一起住进病房了。但这位管理着医院印刷厂、每天静默冥想一小时的农民却泰然自若。当儿子来到医院时，他耐心地教他排版。从那时起，他们并肩工作，在共同的沉默中似乎心满意足。



Kanner came to believe that the most astute clinical observer on staff was a disabled volunteer in the Stone Room who treated the patients respectfully as individuals. This man would spend hours just listening as they related stories about growing up and their hopes and aspirations before they were declared insane. Though he was not one of the resident “experts,” he had a decisive effect on Kanner’s approach to psychiatry. Instead of grilling the residents of Yankton with inane questionnaires, he began probing into his patients’ family backgrounds to seek out the deep roots of their illnesses.

坎纳逐渐相信，医院里最敏锐的临床观察者是一位在石室工作的残疾志愿者，他尊重地对待每一位病人，视他们为独立的个体。他会花上数小时倾听病人讲述成长经历，以及他们在被宣告精神失常前的希望与抱负。尽管这位志愿者并非院内的“专家”，却对坎纳的精神病学方法产生了决定性影响。坎纳不再用无聊的问卷盘问扬克顿的病人，而是开始探究患者的家庭背景，寻找他们疾病的深层根源。



—



ON HIS FIRST Christmas Eve at the hospital, Kanner proposed that patients who were not violent should be liberated from their straitjackets and other forms of restraint. When a supervisor objected, he offered to oversee the wards himself on Christmas Day. This humane experiment was a success, and the patients were allowed to move about more freely from then on.

在医院度过的第一个圣诞夜，坎纳提议让非暴力患者摆脱束缚衣和其他约束措施。当主管提出反对时，他主动提出在圣诞节当天亲自负责病房管理。这次人性化实验取得了成功，此后患者们获得了更多自由活动的空间。



After reading a paper about the therapeutic value of art, he distributed paints, crayons, pencils, and paper throughout the hospital and set up a gallery in the administration building that featured rotating exhibits of patients’ work. (In his own way, Kanner was bringing a touch of Heilpädagogik to the Great Plains.) He also invited cooks, gardeners, and ward attendants over to the house to play pinochle. This scandalized his colleagues by violating an unspoken caste system among the staff but made him new friends all over the hospital. A Czech cook began furnishing Kanner and his wife with old-world pastries and green-tomato pies, while a Polish gardener supplied them with home-brewed cherry and rhubarb wine.

在阅读了一篇关于艺术治疗价值的论文后，他在医院各处分发颜料、蜡笔、铅笔和纸张，并在行政楼设立画廊，定期轮换展出患者的作品。（坎纳以自己独特的方式，将教育治疗理念带到了大平原地区。）他还邀请厨师、园丁和病房护理员到家中玩皮诺奇牌。这种打破员工间隐形等级制度的做法令同事们震惊，却让他在医院各处结交了新朋友。一位捷克厨师开始为坎纳夫妇供应欧式糕点和青番茄派，而一位波兰园丁则为他们提供自酿的樱桃酒和大黄酒。



Grateful that he could speak their native language, a group of Mennonite schizophrenics christened Kanner “the doctor from Germany.” But he yearned to be accepted by his colleagues as just a “regular fellow,” as he put it. He joined the Freemasons and took up golf, while Dziunia started calling herself June. Kanner also diligently worked on his English by poring through Book-of-the-Month Club titles, solving New York Times crossword puzzles, and memorizing entries in the dictionary. Though he never shed his mitteleuropäische accent, he acquired a formidable vocabulary, aided by his keen ear for regional dialects and idioms.

一位门诺派精神分裂症患者群体感激坎纳能说他们的母语，称他为“来自德国的医生”。但他渴望被同事们接纳为“普通人”——正如他所说。他加入共济会，开始打高尔夫球，而德祖尼亚则改称自己为琼。坎纳还通过研读每月一书俱乐部的书目、破解《纽约时报》填字游戏、背诵词典词条来刻苦练习英语。尽管他始终未能摆脱中欧口音，但凭借对地区方言和习语的敏锐听觉，他积累了惊人的词汇量。



—



IN 1925, KANNER MADE his professional debut in the Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology, publishing a “psychiatric study” of Henrik Ibsen’s Peer Gynt. Immediately after his paper appeared, Kanner regretted writing it and vowed never again to venture into the dubious genre of psycholiterary criticism. But appearing in a major journal whetted his appetite to make more meaningful contributions to his field.

1925 年，坎纳在《异常与社会心理学杂志》上首次发表专业论文，对亨利克·易卜生的《培尔·金特》进行了“精神病学研究”。论文一经发表，他便后悔撰写此文，并发誓再也不涉足心理文学批评这一可疑领域。但能在重要期刊亮相，反而激发了他为领域做出更有意义贡献的渴望。



He got the opportunity to do so later that year when he saw a notice in a newspaper announcing the imminent arrival of Emil Kraepelin, who was touring North America, Cuba, and Mexico with a serologist named Felix Plaut to investigate the incidence of paresis—a form of dementia caused by untreated syphilis infection—among blacks and Native Americans in mental institutions. Discovering that Kraepelin and Plaut would be spending four days at the Asylum for Insane Indians in nearby Canton, Kanner asked Adams to wangle an invitation for them and their wives from asylum superintendent Harry Hummer.

那年晚些时候，他得到了这样一个机会——他在报纸上看到一则通知，宣布埃米尔·克雷佩林即将到访。克雷佩林正与血清学家费利克斯·普劳特一同巡游北美、古巴和墨西哥，旨在调查麻痹性痴呆（一种由未经治疗的梅毒感染引起的痴呆症）在黑人及美洲原住民精神疾病患者中的发病率。得知克雷佩林和普劳特将在附近坎顿的印第安人精神病院停留四天后，坎纳请求亚当斯设法让精神病院院长哈里·哈默邀请他们及其妻子同行。



Kraepelin and Plaut were convinced that paresis was extremely rare among blacks and Native Americans, despite the fact that rates of syphilis infection in these groups were high. (Meanwhile, syphilitic dementia was so common among institutionalized whites that it was known as “general paresis of the insane.”) When Kanner informed Kraepelin that one of his patients at Yankton was an “almost full-blooded Indian” with paresis, he was fascinated and recommended that he undertake a full-scale investigation of the case.

克雷佩林和普劳特坚信，尽管黑人和美洲原住民的梅毒感染率很高，但麻痹性痴呆在这些群体中极为罕见。（与此同时，梅毒性痴呆在住院的白人患者中如此常见，以至于被称为“精神病人全身麻痹症”。）当坎纳告诉克雷佩林，他在扬克顿的一名患者是“近乎纯血统的印第安人”且患有麻痹性痴呆时，克雷佩林对此很感兴趣，并建议他对该病例进行全面调查。



The following year, Kanner and Adams published a paper in the American Journal of Psychiatry based on their study of this allegedly unusual patient. The authorial voice is unmistakably Kanner’s. He reports that the incidence of paresis is so low among Native Americans that not a single case has “been heretofore reported in literature,” a phrase he would echo nearly verbatim in his first paper on autism. In fact, he declares, “such a case is so rare, that it is really regarded as a curiosity, a fact that very decidedly calls for explanation.”

次年，坎纳与亚当斯在《美国精神病学杂志》上发表了一篇论文，内容基于他们对这位据称罕见的患者的研究。文中笔触无疑是坎纳的风格。他报告称，在美洲原住民中，麻痹症的发病率极低，以至于“此前文献中从未有过一例报道”——这句话几乎原封不动地出现在他关于孤独症的首篇论文中。事实上，他宣称，“此类病例如此罕见，以至于被视为一种奇闻，这一事实显然亟需解释。”



Showing a flair for dramatic narrative, Kanner relates that the patient, a Sioux elder named Thomas Robertson, was once a proud leader of his tribe with a wife, six children, and a harem of pretty “young squaws.” Now tremulous and staggering, Robertson has become a full-time floor polisher at the asylum.

坎纳展现出对戏剧性叙事的偏好，他讲述道，这位患者是苏族长老托马斯·罗伯逊，曾是他部落中一位骄傲的领袖，拥有妻子、六个孩子以及一群年轻貌美的“小妾”。如今，罗伯逊颤抖蹒跚，已成为精神病院里一名全职地板清洁工。



By probing into Robertson’s family background, Kanner discovers that he is not full-blooded Sioux; in fact, his father was a “large and powerful” Scotsman of “good breeding.” Kanner boldly proposes that the reason his patient is suffering from the ravages of paresis is that syphilis was unknown in the Old World in ancient times but was already well established in the Americas, which enabled the indigenous inhabitants of the New World to evolve immunity to the most debilitating aspects of the disease. In other words, Robertson had inherited his unusual susceptibility to paresis from his father, while his full-blooded brothers and sisters were left unscathed.

通过对罗伯逊家庭背景的深入探究，坎纳发现他并非纯血统的苏族人——事实上，他的父亲是一位"体格魁梧、充满力量"且"教养良好"的苏格兰人。坎纳大胆提出，这位患者之所以饱受麻痹性痴呆的摧残，是因为梅毒在古代旧大陆并不为人所知，却早已在美洲扎根，这使得新大陆的原住民逐渐进化出对疾病最具破坏性症状的免疫力。换言之，罗伯逊从父亲那里继承了这种对麻痹性痴呆的异常易感性，而他那些纯血统的兄弟姐妹则安然无恙。



Kanner’s bold notion that syphilis is of New World origin has gained support in recent years from phylogenetic analyses of a family of diseases called treponematoses, which includes syphilis and a skin disease of children called yaws. Epidemiologists now theorize that yaws mutated into the venereal form of syphilis in the Americas and sailed back to Naples with Columbus’s crew in the fifteenth century. From there, the mutated spirochetes spread across the globe.

坎纳关于梅毒起源于新大陆的大胆观点，近年来得到了对密螺旋体病家族（包括梅毒和儿童皮肤病雅司病）进行系统发育分析的支持。流行病学家如今提出理论：雅司病在美洲变异为性传播形式的梅毒，并在十五世纪随哥伦布船队传播至那不勒斯。从那里，这种变异的螺旋体蔓延至全球。



Kanner and Adams should have stopped there, but they went on to claim that Robertson’s status as a “dominant figure among the Indians” was likely a result of his infusion of Anglo-Saxon blood—a speculation uncomfortably close to the racialist theories gaining a deep foothold among their peers at the time.

坎纳和亚当斯本应就此打住，但他们却进一步声称，罗伯逊作为“印第安人中的主导人物”的地位，很可能是他注入盎格鲁-撒克逊血统的结果——这一推测令人不安地接近当时在他们同僚中根深蒂固的种族主义理论。



There was also a tragedy unfolding at the Asylum for Insane Indians that Kanner managed to overlook. Subsequent inspections by the Institute for Government Research and a psychiatrist named Samuel Silk revealed that Hummer had quietly turned the institution into a prison for native men and women on reservations deemed troublesome by federal agents. Diagnosed as insane by Hummer without a shred of medical evidence, they were confined in shackles, chains, and straitjackets, with no possibility of parole to visit their families, often for the rest of their lives. Patients routinely ate on the floor, were locked up each night with no access to toilets, and were denied basic medical care. Hummer, the only doctor at the facility for twenty-three years, barely kept any patient records; even serious accidents and suicides were not noted. Silk described conditions at the asylum as “very much below the standard of a modern prison.” Lacking any legal means to contest their confinement, most of the patients admitted to the asylum also died there, as Hummer placed ads in newspapers inviting the public to a cleaned-up area of the hospital to “come see the crazy Indians.” The secretary of the interior finally shut down the institution in 1934 under a cloud of scandal.

在卡纳所忽视的印第安精神病院里，另一场悲剧正在上演。政府研究所和一位名叫塞缪尔·西尔克的精神病学家随后进行的检查显示，哈默已悄然将这家机构变成了关押保留地内被联邦特工视为麻烦的原住民男女的监狱。这些人被哈默在毫无医学证据的情况下诊断为精神失常，戴着镣铐、锁链和约束衣被囚禁，没有假释探亲的可能，往往终身如此。患者通常在地上吃饭，每晚被锁起来无法使用厕所，并被剥夺基本医疗护理。哈默是这家机构二十三年来唯一的医生，几乎不保留任何病历记录；即便是严重事故和自杀事件也未被记载。西尔克将这家精神病院的条件描述为"远低于现代监狱的标准"。由于缺乏任何法律手段来质疑自己的监禁，大多数被送入这家精神病院的患者也死在了那里，因为哈默会在报纸上刊登广告，邀请公众到医院清理过的区域"来看看这些疯狂的印第安人"。内政部长最终于 1934 年在丑闻阴影下关闭了该机构。



Was the case of Thomas Robertson truly as singular as Kanner claimed? Historical sources suggest that he was stretching the truth. At a symposium on syphilis in 1902, the superintendent of the Binghamton State Hospital noted “a remarkable preponderance” of paresis in his native patients. A comprehensive assessment of the state of Native American health care by physician Anne Perkins in 1927 uncovered numerous problems that interfered with accurate data gathering in this neglected and impoverished segment of the population. Few doctors in the employ of the Bureau of Indian Affairs had any training in psychiatry, and many tribes opposed both the Wasserman blood test for syphilis and autopsy for religious or social reasons. Perkins specifically called out Hummer for his “unsatisfactory” record keeping at the Asylum for Insane Indians.

托马斯·罗伯逊的案件是否真的如坎纳所言那般独特？历史资料表明，他可能夸大了事实。在 1902 年一次关于梅毒的研讨会上，宾厄姆顿州立医院的院长指出，其原住民患者中麻痹性痴呆症的比例"异常偏高"。1927 年，医生安妮·珀金斯对美国原住民医疗状况进行全面评估时，发现诸多问题干扰了对这一被忽视且贫困群体数据的准确收集。印第安事务局雇佣的医生中鲜有接受过精神病学培训者，许多部落因宗教或社会原因拒绝接受瓦瑟曼梅毒血液检测和尸检。珀金斯特别指出哈默在印第安精神病院的记录保存工作"不尽如人意"。



Yet Kanner’s paper succeeded in putting him on the map of American psychiatry. In the case of Thomas Robertson, he found a winning formula for riveting the attention of his colleagues by writing a vivid and engaging account of a case so allegedly rare that it “demanded explanation.”

然而，坎纳的论文成功让他跻身美国精神病学界的版图。在托马斯·罗伯逊的案例中，他找到了一个吸引同行注意力的制胜法宝——通过撰写一篇生动而引人入胜的病例报告，描述了一个据称极为罕见、以至于“需要解释”的案例。



IV



NOW THAT KANNER HAD made his mark in a prominent journal, it embarrassed him that he had been able to obtain his medical license merely by filling out a questionnaire from the state. “I was bothered by the realization that I had come to psychiatry through a back door,” he wrote. “I felt that, under the circumstances, my efforts lacked consistency and direction.”

既然坎纳已在知名期刊上崭露头角，他对自己仅凭填写州政府的一份问卷就获得行医执照一事感到难堪。“我意识到自己是通过后门进入精神病学领域的，这让我困扰不已，”他写道，“我觉得在这种情况下，我的努力缺乏连贯性和方向性。”



He enjoyed his life in Yankton with its quaint main street, five-and-dime, and movie house. He spent weekends playing all-night poker with his wife and the usual crew from the hospital, and during the week he would invite his daughter, now four, to visit his office. Already an observant little girl, Anita noticed that you could tell the doctors and patients apart because doctors always carried keys. When she visited her father at work, Anita would say to anyone who approached her, “Let me see your keys.”

他享受在扬克顿的生活，那里有古色古香的主街、廉价杂货店和电影院。周末他会和妻子以及医院的老牌牌友们通宵打扑克，工作日则邀请当时四岁的女儿来办公室玩。早慧的安妮塔注意到，医生和病人很容易区分——因为医生总是带着钥匙。当安妮塔去父亲工作的地方时，她会向每个走近的人说：“让我看看你的钥匙。”



But Kanner’s young wife was miserable. June was a bright, culturally savvy woman who had left her childhood friends behind in Berlin with barely any time to think about it. In her new life, she wasn’t even allowed to clean her own house, because the ubiquitous ward attendants did all the washing and dusting. The family ate three meals a day at a long communal table in the doctors’ dining room under the watchful eye of the director’s wife, a domineering busybody who banished Anita’s high chair to the Siberian end of the table. After four years, June was ready to move on. She told her husband that unless he found a new job soon, somewhere very far from South Dakota, she would move to Chicago and take Anita with her.

但坎纳年轻的妻子却感到痛苦。琼是个聪明、有文化素养的女性，她几乎来不及思考就离开了柏林，与童年好友们分别。在新生活中，她甚至不被允许打扫自己的房子，因为无处不在的病房护理员包揽了所有洗涤和除尘工作。全家每天三顿饭都在医生餐厅的长桌上吃，在院长夫人——一个专横好事、把安妮塔的高脚椅发配到餐桌西伯利亚般角落的女人——的监视下进行。四年后，琼已准备好离开。她告诉丈夫，除非他尽快在远离南达科他州的地方找到新工作，否则她就带着安妮塔搬到芝加哥去。



Then fate intervened to save the day. Kanner spotted an ad in the American Journal of Psychiatry for a fellowship at Johns Hopkins under the directorship of the Swiss neurologist Adolf Meyer, who was president of the American Psychiatric Association (APA). “If possible,” the ad stipulated, applicants should “have a working knowledge of German and French” and “spontaneity and energy in work and capacity for independent investigation.” Kanner felt he was reading an ad for himself. He requested an audience with Meyer at an upcoming APA convention in Minneapolis. At the registration desk, Kanner saw every head in the room turn as a short, nimble man with a superbly groomed goatee walked in. He picked up traces of a mitteleuropäische accent in the man’s speech and asked a colleague if he happened to know who he was. “Why, that’s Adolf Meyer!” the man replied.

命运适时介入，扭转了局面。坎纳在《美国精神病学杂志》上看到一则广告，招聘约翰·霍普金斯大学的研究员，由瑞士神经学家阿道夫·迈耶领导，他当时担任美国精神病学协会主席。广告规定，申请人应“具备德语和法语的工作能力”，并“在工作中展现自发性、精力以及独立研究的能力”。坎纳觉得这则广告仿佛是为自己量身定做。他在即将于明尼阿波利斯举行的美国精神病学协会年会上请求与迈耶会面。在登记处，坎纳看到一位身材矮小、动作敏捷、留着精心修剪的山羊胡的男子走进来，房间里所有人的目光都转向了他。他听出对方说话中带着中欧口音，便问一位同事是否碰巧知道他是谁。“哎呀，那就是阿道夫·迈耶！”同事回答道。



The neurologist interviewed Kanner for the fellowship at length the following day and also talked to George Adams about his performance at Yankton. But no word came back from Baltimore for three months, and Kanner berated himself for not being up to snuff. He finally got a cryptic note from Meyer saying that his hesitation was that Kanner seemed “more inclined toward literary work than toward concrete occupation with specific facts.” Sensing his destiny near at hand, he fired off a telegram asking Meyer if he could arrive at Johns Hopkins a month later to start the fellowship immediately.

第二天，神经科医生与坎纳进行了长时间的深入面谈，并询问了乔治·亚当斯他在扬克顿的表现。但三个月过去了，巴尔的摩那边仍无音讯，坎纳自责自己不够出色。最终他收到迈耶一封简短难懂的信，信中表示犹豫的原因是坎纳似乎“更倾向于文学工作，而非专注于具体事实的具体工作”。预感命运近在咫尺，他立即发电报询问迈耶，自己能否在一个月后抵达约翰·霍普金斯大学，立即开始研究职位。



Meyer’s reply was swift and oracular: “We’ve been expecting you.”

迈耶的回复迅速而神秘：“我们一直在等你。”



—



ARRIVING IN BALTIMORE WITH his family, Kanner checked into a hotel, pulled out a phone book, and chose a real estate agent at random to inquire about houses for rent. “What is your persuasion?” the agent asked him. At first, Kanner didn’t even understand the question. The agent casually informed him that Hebrews were not welcome in certain neighborhoods, and that some landlords outside these restricted areas preferred not to rent to them.

带着家人抵达巴尔的摩后，坎纳入住酒店，翻出电话簿，随机选了一位房地产经纪人询问租房事宜。“您的信仰是什么？”经纪人问道。起初，坎纳甚至没理解这个问题。经纪人随口告诉他，某些社区不欢迎希伯来人，而一些限制区域外的房东也不愿租房给他们。



Kanner was stupefied. Yankton had been relatively free of anti-Semitism, probably because there were hardly any other Jews there. (A reverend had assured him that if he enrolled Anita in Sunday school, he would not try to convert her.) But in Baltimore, the same discrimination he’d fled in Germany was right out in the open.

坎纳目瞪口呆。扬克顿相对没有反犹主义，可能是因为那里几乎没有其他犹太人。（一位牧师向他保证，如果让安妮塔上主日学校，他不会试图让她改宗。）但在巴尔的摩，他在德国逃离的那种歧视公然存在。



He discovered that the city he thought of as a lighthouse of democracy—the home of freethinkers like H. L. Mencken—was also segregated by race. Black children were excluded from many public schools, and their families were barred from theaters, department stores, restaurants, hotels, swimming pools, and churches. Yet the locals simply took this state of things for granted—“as if it were as natural a phenomenon as the rising and setting of the sun,” as Kanner put it. Even his new employers were not immune to this insidious disease. The faculty of the Johns Hopkins department of psychiatry was strictly white for years, though many of the patients treated there were black.

他发现，这座他视为民主灯塔的城市——自由思想家如 H.L.门肯的故乡——也实行种族隔离。黑人儿童被许多公立学校拒之门外，他们的家庭被禁止进入剧院、百货商店、餐馆、酒店、游泳池和教堂。然而，当地人却对这种状况习以为常——“仿佛这是像日出日落一样自然的现象，”坎纳如是说。就连他的新雇主也未能幸免于这种阴险的弊病。约翰·霍普金斯大学精神病学系的教职员工多年来一直是清一色的白人，尽管那里治疗的许多病人是黑人。



But Kanner thrived under Meyer’s firm guiding hand, in part because he had much in common with his new mentor, who had also paid his dues in America by working in an asylum in corn country. In the late 1890s, little or no psychiatric training was offered in U.S. medical schools, and most of the jobs available were strictly custodial: overseeing human warehouses filled with patients in restraints who “have lost even the memory of hope, sit in rows, too dull to know despair, watched by attendants: silent, grewsome [sic] machines which eat and sleep, sleep and eat,” as neurologist Silas Weir Mitchell put it in a scathing indictment of his colleagues in 1894. In one such institution, Mitchell was unable to find even a stethoscope to examine his patients.

但坎纳在迈耶严格的指导下茁壮成长，部分原因是他与这位新导师有许多共同之处——迈耶也曾在美国玉米地的一家精神病院工作，以此偿还债务。19 世纪 90 年代末，美国医学院几乎不提供精神病学培训，大多数可用的工作纯粹是看护性质的：监管那些关押着被束缚患者的“人类仓库”，这些患者“甚至失去了对希望的记忆，成排坐着，麻木到不知绝望，由看护者监视：沉默、可怕（原文如此）的机器，吃睡、睡吃”，正如神经学家西拉斯·威尔·米切尔在 1894 年对同行们的严厉批评中所描述的那样。在这样一家机构里，米切尔甚至找不到听诊器来检查病人。



The Illinois Eastern Hospital for the Insane in Kankakee, where Meyer began working after immigrating from Zürich, was no exception. To establish a basic knowledge of the relationship between brain function and mental illness, he undertook a series of patient autopsies but gave up when he realized that it was a futile exercise in the absence of comprehensive medical records. He offered neurology classes to the staff but quit when he discovered that his students hadn’t even been schooled in the elemental techniques of clinical observation. Even under these conditions, he pioneered the modern form of psychiatric history taking by having a stenographer accompany him as he made the rounds of the wards.

位于坎卡基的伊利诺伊东部精神病院也不例外，迈耶从苏黎世移民后便在此工作。为了建立大脑功能与精神疾病之间关系的基础知识，他进行了一系列患者尸检，但在意识到缺乏全面的医疗记录使这项工作徒劳无功后放弃了。他为员工开设神经学课程，但发现学生们甚至没有接受过临床观察基本技术的训练后便停止了。即便在这样的条件下，他仍开创了现代精神病学病史采集方式——让一名速记员陪同他巡视病房。



Meyer advanced swiftly through the ranks of his profession, taking a post as the head of the Pathology Institute of the New York State Hospitals, the biggest network of mental institutions in the country. He argued that no aspect of human behavior could be understood in isolation: neurology, genetics, family background, and social dynamics all had to be considered to properly evaluate a patient’s mental state. In 1908, he was invited to oversee the newly endowed Henry Phipps Psychiatric Clinic at Johns Hopkins, which was expressly built on the Viennese model of combining teaching and practice in a single institution. As a walking embodiment of the European tradition, he became enormously influential. At one point, one in ten of the academic psychiatrists in the United States trained directly under him, launching a school of psychiatry that was dubbed Meyerian. He also introduced the field to its standard experimental subject, the albino rat. Coining the word psychobiology, he constantly exhorted his students to set theories aside and seek the facts.

迈耶在职业生涯中晋升迅速，担任了纽约州立医院病理研究所主任一职，这是全美最大的精神病院网络。他认为，人类行为的任何方面都不能孤立地理解：神经学、遗传学、家庭背景和社会动态都必须综合考虑，才能正确评估患者的精神状态。1908 年，他受邀管理约翰·霍普金斯大学新成立的亨利·菲普斯精神病诊所，该诊所明确按照维也纳模式建造，将教学与实践结合在同一机构中。作为欧洲传统的活化身，他变得极具影响力。曾几何时，美国每十位学术精神病学家中就有一位直接师从于他，开创了被称为"迈耶学派"的精神病学流派。他还为该领域引入了标准实验对象——白化大鼠。他创造了"精神生物学"一词，不断告诫学生要搁置理论，寻求事实。



Visiting Meyer’s office for the first time on an October afternoon in 1928, Kanner was awestruck. An impeccably polite secretary invited him to wait in an adjacent library that seemed to extend for miles, with a convenient array of stepladders for retrieving volumes on the upper shelves. After twenty minutes, the secretary bowed deferentially and invited him into Meyer’s inner sanctum, which was sparsely but elegantly furnished, its very air charged by the presence of the man behind the desk.

1928 年 10 月的一个下午，坎纳首次造访迈耶的办公室时，不禁心生敬畏。一位彬彬有礼的秘书请他到隔壁的图书馆稍候，那里书架绵延如无尽长廊，还贴心地备有梯子方便取阅上层书籍。二十分钟后，秘书恭敬地欠身引他进入迈耶的内室，室内陈设简约而雅致，空气中弥漫着办公桌后那位人物带来的威严气场。



Kanner felt that he had finally arrived in his element. His new boss was a landsman who shared his fascination for linguistics, semantics, and philology, as well as his skepticism for psychoanalysis. Meyer was also a dashing and charismatic figure (described by a former student as possessing a “quiet, epic grandeur”), with eyes that could lift you up or annihilate you with a glance. Kanner was admittedly less prepossessing, with floppy ears, puffy eyes, bad teeth, and the woebegone countenance of a sad beagle, but Meyer did his best to make the young man feel at home, suggesting that he seek a rental in a neighborhood where other Johns Hopkins doctors were living. (Upon receiving his application for a house on Lake Avenue, the landlord told Kanner, “I knew you folks were Jewish right away, but I like you, and no one will raise any objection.”)

坎纳感到自己终于如鱼得水。他的新上司是位同乡，两人都对语言学、语义学和语文学着迷，也都对精神分析学持怀疑态度。迈耶还是一位风度翩翩、魅力十足的人物（曾被学生形容为拥有"静默的史诗气度"），他的眼神既能让人振奋，也能瞬间将人击溃。坎纳显然没那么出众——招风耳、肿眼泡、牙齿不齐，活像一只愁眉苦脸的小猎犬——但迈耶尽力让这位年轻人感到自在，建议他去约翰·霍普金斯大学其他医生聚居的街区租房。（当坎纳申请湖畔大道一处房屋时，房东告诉他："我一眼就看出你们是犹太人，但我喜欢你，没人会反对的。"）



The daily schedule at Phipps began with a conference in Meyer’s library that unfolded with the solemnity of a holy ritual. As a stenographer readied her pads and pencils, the fellows sat down, leaving three chairs open. The eminent neurologist walked in with a resident and a resident’s assistant, and the fellows rose until Meyer sat down. The reverent silence would be broken when he turned to the resident and said with his usual air of unassailable calm, “And what do we have this morning?”

菲普斯诊所的日常始于迈耶书房里的一场会议，其庄重程度堪比神圣仪式。当速记员备好记录本和铅笔时，研究员们落座，留下三把空椅。这位杰出的神经学家带着一名住院医师和一名住院医师助理走进来，研究员们起身站立，直到迈耶坐下。当他转向住院医师，以惯常的不可动摇的平静语气问道：“今天早上我们有什么病例？”时，那肃穆的沉默才被打破。



Kanner learned the price of violating the sanctity of this ritual when a distinguished guest from Vienna, an associate of Freud’s named Paul Schilder, joined the morning meetings for a semester. Kanner was taken aback when his hero Meyer showed deference to a man who didn’t seem to know his basic neurology. After Schilder mentioned that he had treated a schizophrenic teenager with psychoanalysis because the “sex center” and “fear center” of the brain are adjacent, he could no longer contain himself. Kanner pointedly asked if people call their spouses “honey” because the sex center and the sugar center of the brain are also close together. A pained silence fell over the room, and Meyer quietly instructed the stenographer to strike Kanner’s remark from the record.

坎纳得知了违反这一仪式神圣性的代价。当时，一位来自维也纳的尊贵客人——弗洛伊德的同事保罗·席尔德——加入了一个学期的晨会。当坎纳发现自己的偶像迈耶竟对一个连基础神经学都不懂的人表示敬重时，他大为震惊。席尔德提到，他曾用精神分析法治疗一名患有精神分裂症的青少年，因为大脑的“性中枢”和“恐惧中枢”相邻。坎纳再也按捺不住，尖锐地问道：人们叫配偶“亲爱的”，难道是因为大脑的“性中枢”和“糖中枢”也挨得很近吗？房间里陷入一阵痛苦的沉默，迈耶轻声指示速记员将坎纳的发言从记录中删除。



On another occasion, Meyer chastised his young disciple for speaking his mind in a way that “antagonized a sector of the profession.” Kanner was learning a heavy lesson: the way to get ahead in psychiatry was to hold your tongue, even when your esteemed colleagues were speaking nonsense.

另一次，迈耶训斥这位年轻的门徒，说他直言不讳的方式“激怒了业内一部分人”。坎纳学到了沉重的一课：在精神病学领域想要出人头地，就得管住自己的嘴，即便尊敬的同事在胡说八道。



—



WHEN THE TERM of Kanner’s fellowship expired, Meyer worked behind the scenes to secure additional funding so his eager protégé could be retained on staff. He had a far-reaching mission in mind for him: setting up a new child-behavior clinic that would act as a bridge between the domains of pediatrics and psychiatry at Johns Hopkins. The two departments were located in adjacent buildings, and the door between them was kept locked. By the end of Kanner’s first year, no one ever thought about locking that door again.

坎纳的奖学金期满后，迈耶在幕后运作争取额外资金，以便这位充满热情的弟子能继续留任。他心中为坎纳规划了一项影响深远的使命：建立一所新的儿童行为诊所，作为约翰·霍普金斯大学儿科与精神病学领域之间的桥梁。这两个科室位于相邻的建筑中，之间的门一直锁着。到坎纳任职第一年结束时，再也没有人想过要锁上那扇门了。



The Behavior Clinic was located in the Harriet Lane Dispensary, a once-impressive edifice that had fallen into disrepair since 1911 when it was built as a home for invalid children. Kanner’s new office was located in a former pantry in the old infectious-diseases annex, complete with a sink, a leaky ceiling, and rats that ventured up from the cellar to nibble away at his lunch. Despite these shabby quarters, he was ecstatic about his new mission. “I was free to proceed according to my own convictions and at my own pace,” he wrote. “We were the shapers of our plans, methods, and practices . . . We were grateful for the one magnificent gift which outweighed everything else—the opportunity to work unhampered, to develop and pursue our curiosities, to test our theories, and at all times to be true to ourselves.”

行为诊所位于哈丽特·莱恩药房内，这座曾气派非凡的建筑自 1911 年作为残疾儿童之家建成后便日渐破败。坎纳的新办公室设在旧传染病附楼的一间储藏室里，配有水槽、漏水的天花板，还有从地窖窜上来啃食他午餐的老鼠。尽管环境简陋，他对新使命却欣喜若狂。"我可以自由地按照自己的信念和节奏开展工作，"他写道，"我们是自己计划、方法和实践的塑造者……我们感激这份无与伦比的馈赠——得以不受束缚地工作，自由探索好奇心，检验理论，始终忠于自我。"



In an uncanny coincidence, the dispensary had been built to the specifications of Clemens von Pirquet, the pioneering immunologist who also designed Asperger’s clinic in Vienna. Recruited from the Children’s Clinic in 1908 to take the first chair of pediatrics at Johns Hopkins, he expanded the fledgling department from three beds to the formidable facility that Kanner inherited twenty years later. But a year and a half in Baltimore was enough for von Pirquet, and when he was offered another prestigious position in Vienna, he took it. In 1929, after a lengthy period of depression, he committed suicide with his wife, leaving behind a curious legacy—two buildings, on two continents, where two clinicians would claim to have discovered autism independently.

巧合的是，这家诊疗所是按照克莱门斯·冯·皮尔奎特的规格建造的，这位开创性的免疫学家也设计了维也纳的阿斯伯格诊所。1908 年，他从儿童诊所被招募到约翰·霍普金斯大学担任首任儿科学教授，将初具雏形的科室从三张病床扩展为二十年后坎纳接手时的庞大机构。但巴尔的摩的一年半时间已让冯·皮尔奎特感到满足，当他在维也纳获得另一个 prestigious 职位时，便接受了。1929 年，在经历长期抑郁后，他与妻子一同自杀，留下了一份奇特的遗产——两座位于两大洲的建筑，两位临床医生在此声称独立发现了孤独症。



—



WITH MEYER’S ENCOURAGEMENT, Kanner embarked on his most ambitious project to date: writing the first textbook of child psychiatry in English. In practical terms, he was not just writing a book, he was creating a new field of medicine by drawing on elements of other disciplines, including psychiatry, pediatrics, and even a dash of Heilpädagogik.

在迈耶的鼓励下，坎纳开始了当时最雄心勃勃的项目：撰写第一本英文儿童精神病学教科书。实际上，他不仅是在写一本书，更是在通过汲取精神病学、儿科学甚至一点治疗教育学的元素，开创一个全新的医学领域。



The first edition of Child Psychiatry, published in 1935, was cast in a distinctly Meyerian mold. The essence of his mentor’s approach, as Kanner framed it, was to regard the child as a whole, rather than as a jumble of symptoms and dysfunctions. His goal for the book was to set forth practical and teachable ways to help children without being constrained by the dogma of any particular school of psychiatry.

1935 年出版的《儿童精神病学》第一版，明显带有迈耶学派的烙印。坎纳将其导师方法的精髓概括为：将儿童视为一个整体，而非症状与功能障碍的杂乱集合。他撰写此书的目标，是提出实用且可传授的方法来帮助儿童，而不受任何特定精神病学流派教条的束缚。



There was a poignant aspect to Kanner’s act of channeling his mentor that was invisible to all but Meyer’s students. Though the Swiss neurologist was a brilliant thinker, he has been virtually forgotten because he was an abstruse writer and lecturer who produced no books of his own. (Kanner graciously observed that Meyer did his best teaching with his eyes.) The young psychiatrist’s gift to his mentor was to distill his tedious expostulations into clear guidelines that even lay readers could follow: “Work with the child. Work with the family. Work with the community.”

坎纳传承导师思想之举，蕴含着一种只有迈耶的学生才能体会的辛酸。这位瑞士神经学家虽为杰出的思想家，却因文风晦涩、著述稀少而几乎被世人遗忘。（坎纳曾优雅地指出，迈耶最出色的教学是用眼神完成的。）这位年轻精神病学家献给导师的礼物，是将他冗长繁琐的论述提炼成连外行读者都能遵循的清晰准则：“与儿童合作。与家庭合作。与社区合作。”



Child Psychiatry was immediately hailed as “a remarkable achievement” and became a runaway best seller. The first edition alone went into five printings, followed by three revised editions translated into multiple languages. With every iteration, the text became less Meyerian and more Kannerian. Ponderous formulations like “Personality Differences Expressing Themselves in the Form of Involuntary Part-Dysfunctions” (which sounds like a Google translation from the Swiss) yielded to straightforward headings like “Intelligence,” “Emotion,” and “Problems of Speech and Language.” It remained the last word on the subject through the 1960s and stayed in print for an astonishing sixty-seven years.

《儿童精神病学》一经出版便被誉为"非凡成就"，迅速成为畅销书。仅第一版就重印五次，后续三个修订版被翻译成多种语言。随着每次修订，文本逐渐褪去迈耶风格，转向坎纳风格。诸如"以非自主性部分功能障碍形式表现的人格差异"（听起来像是从瑞士语谷歌翻译过来的）这类冗长表述，被"智力"、"情感"、"言语与语言问题"等直白标题取代。直至 20 世纪 60 年代，该书始终是该领域的权威著作，持续印刷长达六十七年之久，令人惊叹。



Kanner leveraged his new visibility to air opinions on hot-button issues like sex education, thumb sucking, and phobias. “How can we blame the children for being afraid of thunderstorms if mother shrieks and shivers every time she sees a flash of lightning or hears the sound of thunder? How can we blame a child for restlessness and impatience if father shows the same traits?” he declared in the Washington Post. He was superbly positioned to proffer advice to a generation of parents convinced by psychologists that their role was to be “virtual middle managers in what was imagined as a national child-rearing project . . . [using] tools provided by professionals,” as culture critic Nicholas Sammond put it.

坎纳利用自己新获得的名气，就性教育、吮拇指和恐惧症等热点问题发表看法。“如果母亲每次看到闪电或听到雷声就尖叫发抖，我们怎么能责怪孩子害怕雷雨？如果父亲表现出焦躁不安和缺乏耐心，我们又怎能责怪孩子同样如此？”他在《华盛顿邮报》上这样写道。他处于绝佳的位置，可以向一代父母提供建议——正如文化评论家尼古拉斯·萨蒙德所言，这些父母被心理学家说服，相信自己的角色是“在一个被视为全国性育儿项目的工程中充当虚拟中层管理者……使用专业人士提供的工具”。



When behaviorist John B. Watson panicked the readers of his popular guide to childrearing by insisting, “The oldest profession in the race today is facing failure. This profession is parenthood,” Kanner responded by writing a soothing, chatty book called In Defense of Mothers. He coyly mocked his Freudian colleagues for worshipping “the Great God of the Unconscious” while advising parents to set aside all the “mythical spooks and bugaboos which theorizing busybodies have thrown around them.”

当行为学家约翰·B·华生在其广受欢迎的育儿指南中惊恐地告诫读者："当今人类最古老的职业正面临失败，这个职业就是为人父母"时，坎纳以一本名为《为母亲辩护》的温馨随笔集作为回应。他狡黠地嘲讽弗洛伊德学派同仁对"潜意识大神"的崇拜，同时建议父母们抛开"那些理论家们强加给他们的神话鬼怪与无稽之谈"。



Preaching from the bully pulpit of the leading medical school in the country, he no longer had to worry about having slipped into psychiatry through a back door. Now he was on center stage.

凭借全美顶尖医学院的权威讲坛，他再不必担心自己是通过后门溜进精神病学领域的。此刻他正站在舞台中央。



—



KANNER MADE HEADLINES coast-to-coast in 1937 by exposing a major scandal in Baltimore. Acting on a tip from the superintendent of a vocational center for the disabled called the Rosewood State Training School, he discovered that a local lawyer had been making a fortune by offering up the school’s “feebleminded” female residents as cheap domestic help to wealthy families after obtaining writs of habeas corpus from an obliging judge.

1937 年，坎纳通过揭露巴尔的摩一桩重大丑闻登上全国头条。根据残障人士职业培训中心"罗斯伍德州立培训学校"主管提供的线索，他发现当地一名律师通过贿赂法官获取人身保护令，将该校"智力低下"的女性学员作为廉价家政工输送给富裕家庭，从中牟取暴利。



The city’s society matrons had come to regard the girls as disposable commodities, paying them little or nothing for their labor and simply dumping them on the street before taking an extended holiday. One client had fired thirteen young women in succession with no questions asked by the judge. The toll on the victims was enormous: eleven girls died, six were serving lengthy prison sentences, and twenty-nine were prostitutes, while others married alcoholic husbands who deserted them shortly thereafter. One client complained after physically abusing a girl and throwing her out of her house, “Instead of being a member of the animal kingdom, she was a vegetable.”

城里的贵妇们将这些女孩视为可随意处置的商品，对她们的劳动几乎不付或完全不付报酬，只是在长期休假前简单地将她们赶到大街上。一位雇主连续解雇了十三名年轻女性，而法官对此未加过问。受害者付出的代价极其惨重：十一名女孩死亡，六人正在服长期徒刑，二十九人沦为妓女，其余人则嫁给了酗酒的丈夫，不久后便被抛弃。一名雇主在虐待一名女孩并将其赶出家门后抱怨道：“她不是动物王国的一员，而是一株植物。”



After conducting an investigation, Kanner presented his report to the APA annual convention in May, which unleashed a frenzy of headlines. The New York Times hailed the psychiatrist for revealing “The ‘Slavery’ of 168 Imbeciles.” “Scheme to Set Morons Free to Work in Homes Charged,” blared the Baltimore Evening Sun. “Record of Misery Traced in Freeing of Moronic Girls,” echoed the Washington Post. The scandal gave Kanner a rare opportunity to focus the national spotlight on the vulnerability of disabled people in institutions and the pervasive lack of oversight in the mental health care system.

经过调查，坎纳在五月向美国精神病学协会年会提交了报告，随即引发媒体争相报道。《纽约时报》盛赞这位精神病学家揭露了"168 名智障者的'奴役'状况"。《巴尔的摩晚报》以"释放低能者到家庭中工作的计划遭指控"为标题。《华盛顿邮报》则呼应道："追踪释放低能女性背后的悲惨记录"。这桩丑闻让坎纳获得罕见机会，将全国目光聚焦于机构中残障人士的脆弱处境，以及精神卫生保健系统普遍缺乏监管的问题。



But that isn’t what he did. Instead, he portrayed the innocent victims of this ghastly scheme as a menace to their community. Telling a New York Times reporter that more than a hundred children born to these girls were “obviously and uncontestably feeble-minded,” he lent his moral authority to the classic narrative used to justify forced sterilization laws. “Time alone will tell,” he said, “how many more feeble-minded, illegitimate, neglected children this group of released patients will in the future bestow on a Commonwealth that can do nothing but look on and pay the penalty for the indiscriminate habeas corpus release by its courts of justice.”

但他并没有这样做。相反，他将这一骇人计划中的无辜受害者描绘成社区的威胁。他向《纽约时报》记者表示，这些女孩所生的上百名儿童"明显且无可争议地存在智力缺陷"，并以此赋予其道德权威，为强制绝育法的经典叙事提供支持。"唯有时间能证明，"他说，"这些被释放的患者群体未来还会给这个联邦带来多少智力低下、非婚生、被忽视的儿童——而联邦除了袖手旁观，为法院随意签发的人身保护令释放付出代价外，别无他法。"



Then Kanner ensured that the Commonwealth could do nothing to punish the offenders by refusing to release their names to the American Bar Association. He claimed that doing so would be redundant, because the judge had retired and the lawyer had been disbarred for “behavior even more unethical than that described by me” (whatever that might have been). He expressed hope that “the publicity . . . will contribute much toward precluding similar incidents in the future.”

随后，坎纳拒绝向美国律师协会公布涉案者姓名，确保联邦无法惩罚违法者。他声称此举毫无必要，因为法官已退休，而那名律师因"比我描述的更不道德的行为"（无论那可能是什么）被吊销了律师资格。他表示希望"此次曝光……将极大有助于防止未来发生类似事件。"



The Rosewood affair established Kanner in the public mind as a voice for the voiceless and a defender of the defenseless. But his failure to name those responsible, and his statements to the press, rendered unclear whom exactly he was defending. He maintained support for sterilization of “those intellectually or emotionally unfit to raise children” for years, though he opposed euthanasia in a public debate with prominent neurologist Foster Kennedy, who advocated killing “those hopeless ones—Nature’s mistakes” that “so largely fill our mental hospitals” in an editorial in the American Journal of Psychiatry.

罗森伍德事件使坎纳在公众心目中树立了为无声者发声、为无助者辩护的形象。但他未能指明责任方，加之对媒体发表的言论，令其究竟在捍卫谁变得模糊不清。多年来，他始终支持对"智力或情感上不适合养育子女者"实施绝育，尽管他在与著名神经学家福斯特·肯尼迪的公开辩论中反对安乐死——后者曾在《美国精神病学杂志》的社论中主张消灭"那些无望之人——大自然的错误"，称他们"大量充斥我们的精神病院"。



Still, Kanner’s view of the lives that “mentally deficient” people were fit to lead was relentlessly grim. His main argument with Kennedy was that such people are capable of fulfilling useful roles in society:

然而，坎纳对"智力缺陷者"适合过何种生活的看法始终冷酷无情。他与肯尼迪的主要分歧在于：他认为这类人能够承担有益于社会的角色——



Sewage disposal, ditch digging, potato peeling, scrubbing of floors and other such occupations are as indispensable and essential to our way of living as science, literature and art. Cotton picking is an integral part of our textile industries. Oyster shucking is an important part of our seafood supply. Garbage collection is an essential part of our public hygiene measures.

污水处理、挖沟、削土豆皮、擦地板等职业，与科学、文学和艺术一样，对我们的生活方式不可或缺且至关重要。摘棉花是纺织工业的组成部分。剥牡蛎是海产品供应的重要环节。垃圾收集是公共卫生措施的关键部分。



“Do we really wish to deprive ourselves,” he concluded, “of people whom we desperately need for a variety of essential occupations?”

“我们真的愿意剥夺自己，”他总结道，“那些我们迫切需要的、从事各种重要职业的人吗？”



—



BY THE FALL OF 1937, the Reich’s eugenics machine was accelerating into high gear and the forced exodus of Jews was under way. As Hitler’s henchmen rampaged through Kanner’s homeland, the plight of his family members and colleagues became a matter of grave concern. With some of the best medical minds in Europe clamoring to flee the oncoming storm—including Freud himself—the modest quota of German immigrants allowed to enter the United States annually (less than twenty-six thousand) wasn’t even being met, in part because the State Department instructed consular officials to deny visas to applicants who might require public assistance. Jews could obtain visas only by presenting affidavits from American citizens providing proof of future employment, as Holtz had done for Kanner.

到 1937 年秋天，帝国的优生学机器已加速运转至全速，犹太人的强制驱逐正在进行。当希特勒的爪牙在坎纳的祖国肆虐时，他的家人和同事的处境成了令人忧心的问题。随着欧洲最顶尖的医学人才——包括弗洛伊德本人——争相逃离即将来临的风暴，美国每年允许入境的德国移民配额（不到两万六千人）甚至未能满额，部分原因是国务院指示领事官员拒绝可能需公共援助的申请人签证。犹太人只有像霍尔茨为坎纳所做的那样，提交美国公民提供的未来就业证明宣誓书，才能获得签证。



Medical workers with non-Aryan spouses or relatives, and those judged insufficiently obedient by the Führer’s regime, were also subject to expulsion. Kanner felt embittered by the willingness of U.S. officials to look the other way as an unprecedented human catastrophe took shape.

拥有非雅利安配偶或亲属的医务人员，以及被元首政权判定为不够顺从者，同样遭到驱逐。当一场前所未有的人道灾难逐渐成形时，美国官员却选择视而不见，这让坎纳深感愤懑。



Emma Lazarus’ heartwarming words of invitation, written in 1886 and inscribed on the Statue of Liberty, were no longer applied fully to the tired, the poor, the homeless, tempest-tossed. Sanctuary was apportioned sparingly through a narrow slit in the golden door to those fortunate enough to have found affiants in time to save their lives.

埃玛·拉撒路于 1886 年写下并镌刻在自由女神像上的温暖邀约之词，已不再完全适用于那些疲惫、贫困、无家可归、颠沛流离的人们。庇护权通过金色大门上狭窄的缝隙被吝啬地分配，只有那些及时找到担保人得以保命的人才有幸获得。



—



GROUPS FORMED SPECIFICALLY TO assist doctors seeking refuge in the United States, such as the National Committee for the Resettlement of Foreign Physicians, faced numerous obstacles beyond the quotas and visa requirements. Fearing competition from refugees, state medical boards erected a maze of resolutions requiring applicants for licenses to be U.S. citizens, to have degrees from American schools, or to produce extensive records of their education in Europe. Nazi-run universities simply ignored these requests. Even doctors who successfully relocated and tried to go into practice faced rumors that they were spies, sent overseas to poison their patients.

专门为帮助寻求在美国避难的医生而成立的团体，例如外国医生重新安置全国委员会，除了配额和签证要求外，还面临诸多障碍。由于担心难民带来的竞争，各州医疗委员会设置了一系列复杂的决议，要求执照申请人必须是美国公民、拥有美国学校的学位，或提供其在欧洲接受教育的详细记录。而纳粹控制的大学则直接无视这些要求。即使成功搬迁并试图行医的医生，也面临谣言称他们是间谍，被派往海外毒害病人。



The Kanners rose to this historic challenge by doing something heroic. Starting that fall, they acted as an unofficial immigration agency for Jewish doctors, nurses, and researchers, providing them with the documentation they needed to obtain visas while helping them to find jobs. As Kanner buttonholed hospital superintendents at conferences to inquire about vacancies on staff for physicians, June networked with a local cardiologist to find in-home placements for nurses. Furthermore, Kanner convinced the Maryland medical board to liberalize its licensing requirements. In total, Leo and June rescued nearly two hundred colleagues from the Nazis while providing clinics, hospitals, and research labs across the country with an influx of superbly trained talent. Furthermore, they graciously opened their home in Baltimore to assist émigrés adapting to life in a new culture.

坎纳夫妇以英勇之举迎接了这一历史性挑战。从那年秋天开始，他们充当起犹太医生、护士和研究人员的非官方移民机构，为他们提供获取签证所需的文件，同时帮助他们寻找工作。坎纳在会议上拉住医院主管询问医生职位空缺时，琼恩则与当地一位心脏病专家合作，为护士们安排居家护理岗位。此外，坎纳还说服马里兰州医学委员会放宽执业许可要求。里奥和琼恩总共从纳粹手中救出了近两百名同事，同时为全国各地的诊所、医院和研究实验室输送了大量训练有素的人才。他们还慷慨地开放巴尔的摩的家，帮助移民适应新文化的生活。



They also saved the lives of Kanner’s brother Max, who married an Iowa girl and became a furrier, and his brother Josef, who migrated to Palestine. Tragically, his seventy-year-old mother, Klara, was dragged from her home and gassed. His brother Willy was shot to death in Poland, and his aunt and uncle were murdered in Holland. Thankfully, his sister Jenny and her family made it to safety by hiding under a truckload of coal bound for Switzerland. His old hometown of Brody had once been home to a vibrant community of ten thousand Jews and was celebrated throughout Europe as a center of learning, philosophy, art, music, and culture. After the war, only eighty-eight Jews were left alive.

他们还拯救了坎纳的兄弟马克斯（Max）的生命，马克斯娶了一位爱荷华州的姑娘，成为了一名皮货商；以及他的兄弟约瑟夫（Josef），后者移居到了巴勒斯坦。不幸的是，他七十岁的母亲克拉拉（Klara）被从家中拖出并遭毒气杀害。他的兄弟威利（Willy）在波兰被枪杀，他的姑妈和姑父在荷兰遇害。值得庆幸的是，他的姐姐珍妮（Jenny）和她的家人藏在一辆运往瑞士的煤车下，得以安全脱险。他的故乡布罗迪（Brody）曾是一个拥有一万犹太人的繁荣社区，在整个欧洲被誉为学习、哲学、艺术、音乐和文化的中心。战后，只有八十八名犹太人幸存下来。



Kanner was rewarded for these selfless efforts when one of the colleagues he rescued from the Nazis provided him with crucial assistance at the moment he made the breakthrough that assured him of the destiny he had always yearned for—a lasting place in the history of medicine.

坎纳的这些无私努力得到了回报：当他取得突破性进展，确保了自己一直渴望的命运——在医学史上留下永恒地位时，他从纳粹手中救出的一位同事为他提供了关键帮助。



V



On a September day in 1938, a lawyer named Oliver Triplett Jr. sat down in his office in Forest, Mississippi, to dictate a letter to his secretary. The letter, which ran on for thirty-three single-spaced pages, concerned his eldest child, a five-year-old boy named Donald. It was addressed to the one man in America who he hoped might be able to help him: Leo Kanner.

1938 年 9 月的一天，一位名叫小奥利弗·特里普利特的律师坐在密西西比州福里斯特的办公室里，向秘书口述了一封信。这封信长达三十三页，采用单倍行距，内容关乎他的长子——一个名叫唐纳德的五岁男孩。收信人是全美唯一一位他希望能提供帮助的人：利奥·坎纳。



Oliver and his wife, Mary, were an exceptionally bright and successful couple, and their families had played prominent roles in Forest for three generations. The economy of the region was suffering through its own sequel to the Great Depression as the pine groves on which mill owners had staked their fortunes were exhausted, but the Tripletts remained financially secure. Mary’s father was the chairman of the board of the Bank of Forest, and she was an impressive woman in her own right. In an era when few women had degrees, she was former president of her class at Belhaven University and taught English in a local high school. Oliver—known in Forest by his middle name, Beaman—graduated from Yale Law School with honors, was admitted to the bar of the U.S. Supreme Court, and became the town attorney. The couple lived in a cozy house on a seven-acre lot on the outskirts of downtown, with a screened-in porch and big windows overlooking a broad lawn.

奥利弗和他的妻子玛丽是一对极为聪慧且成功的夫妇，他们的家族三代以来在福里斯特镇都扮演着重要角色。随着工厂主们押注财富的松树林逐渐枯竭，该地区经济正经历着大萧条后的又一次衰退，但特里普利特家族依然财务稳固。玛丽的父亲是福里斯特银行董事会主席，而她本人也是一位令人钦佩的女性。在那个女性鲜少拥有学位的年代，她曾是贝尔黑文大学班级的前任主席，并在当地一所高中教授英语。奥利弗——在福里斯特镇以其中间名比曼为人所知——以优异成绩毕业于耶鲁法学院，获准进入美国最高法院律师协会，并成为镇上的律师。这对夫妇住在市中心边缘一栋占地七英亩的舒适房子里，配有纱窗门廊和大窗户，俯瞰着宽阔的草坪。



Known as an intense and meticulous man, Beaman was perhaps a bit too hardworking. By the time his son was born, he had suffered two nervous breakdowns. He was prone to taking long walks in a kind of fugue state; when he came home, he would remember nothing and no one that he’d seen along the way. But these things were mere eccentricities compared to his son’s behavior.

比曼以严谨细致著称，或许还有些过于勤勉。儿子出生时，他已两次精神崩溃。他常陷入恍惚状态漫无目的地长途跋涉，回家后对沿途所见所闻毫无记忆。但这些与其子的行为相比，不过是些怪癖罢了。



Donald had been a decidedly solitary and remote child from the moment he was born. The faces of his parents gazing down at him in his crib never inspired the usual cascades of happy wriggling and gurgling. Mary breast-fed him for seven months, supplementing his diet with formula, but nothing seemed to sit right in his stomach. He was clearly happiest when he was left alone and barely seemed to notice if another person entered the room. When his grandparents came over to the house, he ignored them. Even the sight of a man dressed as Santa Claus failed to impress him.

唐纳德自出生起就是个孤僻疏离的孩子。父母俯视婴儿床时的面容，从未引发他惯常的欢快扭动与咯咯笑声。玛丽用母乳喂养了他七个月，辅以配方奶粉，但任何食物似乎都让他肠胃不适。独处时他显然最快乐，旁人进屋也几乎引不起他的注意。祖父母来访时，他视若无睹。就连圣诞老人装扮的人也无法令他动容。



But he was also a bright boy, a prodigy in some ways. Blessed with the rare gift of absolute pitch, he could hum and sing many of his favorite tunes accurately by his first birthday. He also had an unusually retentive memory. At age two, he could count to one hundred, repeat the alphabet backward and forward, say the Lord’s Prayer, recite the twenty-five questions and answers of the Presbyterian catechism, and name every U.S. president and vice president. He would amuse himself for hours with Compton’s Encyclopedia, flipping instantly to the pictures that he liked best. He also memorized the locations of many houses in Forest, as if he were plotting a map in his head. In his letter to Kanner, Beaman observed that he “appears to be always thinking and thinking, and to get his attention almost requires one to break down a mental barrier between his inner consciousness and the outside world.”

但他也是个聪明的孩子，在某些方面堪称神童。他天生拥有绝对音准这一罕见天赋，一岁时就能准确哼唱许多喜爱的曲调。他的记忆力也异常惊人。两岁时，他能数到一百，正背倒背字母表，背诵主祷文，复述长老会教义问答的二十五道题目及其答案，还能说出历任美国总统和副总统的名字。他会独自翻阅《康普顿百科全书》消磨数小时，瞬间翻到自己最爱的图片。他还记住了福里斯特镇许多房屋的位置，仿佛在脑海中绘制了一幅地图。比曼在给坎纳的信中写道，他"似乎总在不停地思考，要引起他的注意，几乎需要打破他内心世界与外部世界之间的那道精神屏障。"



To coax him out of his shell, the Tripletts went to the trouble of finding a handsome boy at an orphanage and inviting him to stay at their house for a summer. But Donald simply ignored this unexpected addition to the family, never asking the boy a single question. His parents tried to teach him to ride a bicycle, but he became panic-stricken; they bought him a fancy Taylor Tot walker and he refused to have anything to do with it. Finally, they installed a slide in their backyard and invited the neighborhood kids to play, thinking Donald might learn by their example. But when they sat him at the top of the slide and pushed him down, he was terrified. (The next morning he quietly slipped out of the house and slid down himself.)

为了让他走出自己的小世界，特里普利特夫妇费尽心思，从孤儿院找来一个英俊的男孩，邀请他在家里住了一个夏天。但唐纳德完全无视这个突然加入的家庭成员，从未问过那个男孩一个问题。他的父母试图教他骑自行车，他却惊恐万分；他们给他买了一辆精致的泰勒托特学步车，他却拒绝使用。最后，他们在后院安装了一个滑梯，邀请邻居的孩子们来玩，希望唐纳德能通过观察学会。但当他们把他放在滑梯顶端推下去时，他吓坏了。（第二天早上，他悄悄溜出房子，自己滑了下来。）



Donald’s sticky memory also had a downside. He tended to repeat phrases precisely as he heard them without modifying the pronouns appropriately. If he wanted a glass of milk, he would say, “Donnie, do you want your milk?” At dinner, he would tell his mother, “Say ‘If you drink to there, I’ll laugh and I’ll smile,’” reproducing Mary’s original intonations faithfully—as if he were sampling his mother’s voice instead of truly understanding the meaning of her words.

唐纳德那过目不忘的记忆力也有其弊端。他总是一字不差地重复听到的句子，却不会恰当转换人称代词。想喝牛奶时，他会说："唐尼，你要喝牛奶吗？"吃饭时，他会对母亲说："说'如果你喝到那里，我会笑，我会微笑'"，完美复刻玛丽最初的语调——仿佛在采样母亲的声音，而非真正理解话语的含义。



Like Gottfried, Donald also had a curious attraction to rules and order. Once he learned to read, it never seemed right to him that bite wasn’t spelled bight, like light. He would line up his toys in strict sequences and throw tantrums if anyone disrupted them. But his favorite thing to do was to set his toys spinning on the floor like tops. The sight of almost any spinning thing—even a pot lid from the kitchen—made him jump up and down in ecstasy.

和戈特弗里德一样，唐纳德对规则与秩序也有着奇特的执念。学会阅读后，他始终觉得"bite"不该拼写成"bight"（像"light"那样）是件怪事。他会把玩具严格按顺序排列，若有人打乱顺序便大发脾气。但他最爱做的事，是让玩具像陀螺般在地板上旋转。几乎任何旋转的东西——哪怕是厨房的锅盖——都能让他兴奋得又蹦又跳。



The Tripletts’ family doctor suggested that the cause of Donald’s odd behavior was that his parents had “overstimulated” him, and he prescribed a radical change of environment. Willing to try anything that might help their son, Mary and Beaman committed him to the Mississippi Tuberculosis Sanatorium, forty miles away, for a stay of indefinite length. He was three years old.

特里普利特家的家庭医生认为，唐纳德异常行为的根源在于父母对他"过度刺激"，并建议彻底改变生活环境。为了帮助儿子，玛丽和比曼愿意尝试任何方法，于是将年仅三岁的唐纳德送进了四十英里外的密西西比结核病疗养院，接受无限期住院治疗。



Built by a lake, the enormous Victorian facility—a relic of the time when tuberculosis was blamed on poverty, immigrants, and overcrowded cities—must have seemed like a promising refuge for a child overwhelmed by everyday life. It was renowned for the compassionate ministrations of its nursing staff. Donald was admitted to a special ward called the preventorium, usually reserved for TB-infected patients who did not yet have the active form of the disease. In keeping with their doctor’s advice, his parents visited him only twice a month. But, deprived of his familiar surroundings and routines, Donald did not fare well. He developed a new habit of nodding his head from side to side and withdrew to the point where he barely ate and sat in a fixed position for hours, “paying no attention to anything.”

这座建于湖畔的维多利亚式庞大建筑——曾是结核病被归咎于贫困、移民和城市拥挤时代的遗存——对那个被日常生活压垮的孩子而言，本该是充满希望的避风港。它以护理人员慈悲的照料而闻名。唐纳德被安排进名为"预防病房"的特殊病区，这里通常收治尚未发展为活动性结核病的感染者。遵照医嘱，父母每月只探望他两次。然而，失去熟悉的环境与生活节奏后，唐纳德的情况并未好转。他养成了不停摇头的新习惯，变得极度孤僻，几乎不吃东西，能纹丝不动地坐上几个小时，"对周遭一切视若无睹"。



Gradually, he learned to adapt to this strange new environment. He started eating again and allowed himself to sit near other children. After nearly a year had passed, however, Mary and Beaman decided to take Donald home over the vigorous protests of the sanatorium director. He told them to leave him alone, saying, “It looks that now he is going to be perfectly all right.”

渐渐地，他学会了适应这个陌生的新环境。他开始重新进食，并允许自己坐在其他孩子附近。然而，将近一年过去后，玛丽和比曼不顾疗养院院长的强烈反对，决定带唐纳德回家。院长告诉他们不要打扰他，并说：“现在看来，他完全没问题了。”



When the Tripletts asked him to provide a detailed account of Donald’s condition, he dashed off a note concluding that their son had “some glandular disease.” Beside herself with grief and frustration, Mary referred to Donald as her “hopelessly insane child.” At this point, the family pediatrician referred the Tripletts to Kanner, by then the most prominent child psychiatrist in the country.

当特里普利特夫妇要求他详细说明唐纳德的状况时，他匆匆写下一张便条，结论是他们的儿子患有“某种腺体疾病”。玛丽在悲痛和绝望中几乎崩溃，称唐纳德为“无可救药的精神病孩子”。此时，家庭儿科医生将特里普利特夫妇介绍给了卡纳——当时国内最杰出的儿童精神病学家。



He was immediately intrigued by Donald’s case and fascinated by the sheer volume of information in Beaman’s letter. Kanner invited the Tripletts to bring Donald to Johns Hopkins for a thorough clinical evaluation. In October, they boarded a train to see the great man in Baltimore.

卡纳立即对唐纳德的病例产生了兴趣，并被比曼信中详尽的信息量所吸引。他邀请特里普利特夫妇带唐纳德到约翰·霍普金斯医院进行全面临床评估。十月，他们登上了前往巴尔的摩的火车，去拜见这位伟人。



—



AT FIRST, Kanner didn’t know what to make of Donald’s behavior. After giving him a preliminary exam at the Harriet Lane, he dispatched the Tripletts to the Child Study Home of Maryland, a Johns Hopkins affiliate launched that year under his supervision. Only a handful of clinicians could have made sense of Donald’s condition at this point, and most of them were working in Vienna at the Heilpädagogik Station. One of them, however, had just been brought over from Austria by Kanner to become the full-time psychiatrist-pediatrician at the Child Study Home: Asperger’s former diagnostician, Georg Frankl.

起初，坎纳并不清楚该如何解读唐纳德的行为。在哈丽特·莱恩医院对他进行初步检查后，他将特里普利特一家转介至马里兰州儿童研究之家——这是约翰·霍普金斯大学附属机构，当年刚在他的督导下成立。当时能理解唐纳德病情的临床医生屈指可数，且多数都在维也纳的 Heilpädagogik 诊疗站工作。不过，其中一位刚从奥地利被坎纳聘请而来，担任儿童研究之家的全职精神科儿科医生：正是阿斯伯格的前诊断医师格奥尔格·弗兰克尔。



This crucial link between the two pioneers of autism has escaped the attention of historians until now, mostly because Kanner studiously avoided mentioning it. He never acknowledged Asperger’s contributions to the field—a fact that has puzzled autism scholars for decades. His unpublished memoir, written in the 1950s, names Frankl as one of many clinicians whom he helped immigrate to America in the years leading up to the war but comes to a mysteriously abrupt end just before the breakthrough that made him famous. Kanner’s colleagues maintained that he was simply unfamiliar with the parallel work unfolding in Vienna at the time, and he never corrected them.

这两位孤独症先驱之间的关键联系至今未被历史学家注意，主要是因为坎纳刻意避而不谈。他从未承认阿斯伯格在该领域的贡献——这一事实数十年来一直困扰着孤独症研究者。坎纳在 1950 年代撰写的未出版回忆录中，提到弗兰克尔是他帮助在战前移民美国的众多临床医生之一，但这份回忆录在他取得使其成名的突破性发现前，却神秘地戛然而止。坎纳的同事坚称他当时根本不了解维也纳正在进行的平行研究，而他从未纠正过这种说法。



In fact, Frankl was not the only member of Asperger’s core team in Baltimore when Kanner made his momentous discovery. Upon arriving in New York City in November 1937, the former chief diagnostician of the Children’s Clinic was reunited with his colleague Anni Weiss, the young psychologist who wrote the case history of Gottfried. In a poignant affirmation of life by two survivors, the couple got married a couple of weeks later. The following April, they joined Kanner’s inner circle at Johns Hopkins, moving into a quaint shingled house a couple of blocks from the Child Study Home.

事实上，当坎纳做出这一重大发现时，弗兰克尔并非他在巴尔的摩核心团队中唯一的阿斯伯格成员。1937 年 11 月抵达纽约后，这位前儿童诊所首席诊断师与他的同事安妮·韦斯重逢——正是这位年轻的心理学家撰写了戈特弗里德的病历。两位幸存者以动人的生命礼赞结为连理，几周后便举行了婚礼。次年四月，他们加入坎纳在约翰·霍普金斯大学的内部圈子，搬进了距儿童研究之家仅几个街区的一栋古朴木瓦房。



For two years, Kanner and Frankl hosted “mental clinics” together in nearby towns, where groups like the Children’s Aid Society presented children for their evaluation before audiences of parents drawn by articles in local newspapers. Meanwhile, Weiss (now Weiss-Frankl) became an enthusiastic participant in Meyer’s seminars at the Phipps and told him that she found his seminars more enlightening than anything else she’d done since leaving Austria.

两年间，坎纳与弗兰克尔在邻近城镇共同主持"心理诊所"，儿童援助协会等组织会将儿童带来接受评估，而当地报纸的文章则吸引着家长群体前来旁听。与此同时，韦斯（婚后改姓韦斯-弗兰克尔）热忱参与迈耶在菲普斯诊所举办的研讨会，并向他坦言，自离开奥地利后，这些研讨会使她获得的启迪远超其他任何经历。



Kanner may never have heard Asperger’s name before hiring Frankl, but he was certainly familiar with the work of Erwin Lazar, the founder of the Children’s Clinic. In a letter to Meyer in 1939, Kanner touted Frankl’s “good background in pediatrics and close connection for eleven years with the Lazar Clinic in Vienna.” With a staff of teachers, an occupational therapy department, and living quarters for nearly fifty infants and children, the Child Study Home was as close to the Heilpädagogik Station as America had to offer. There, Frankl employed the style of intimate observation that he had developed with his colleagues in Vienna to make autism visible to medicine for the second time.

坎纳在雇佣弗兰克尔之前可能从未听说过阿斯伯格的名字，但他对儿童诊所创始人埃尔温·拉扎尔的工作肯定非常熟悉。在 1939 年写给迈耶的一封信中，坎纳称赞弗兰克尔"拥有扎实的儿科背景，并在维也纳拉扎尔诊所工作了十一年"。这所儿童研究之家配备了教师团队、职业治疗部门以及可容纳近五十名婴幼儿的居住空间，堪称美国最接近"教育治疗站"的机构。在那里，弗兰克尔运用了他在维也纳与同事们共同发展的细致观察方法，使孤独症第二次进入医学界的视野。



—



OVER THE COURSE OF two weeks in October 1938, Frankl and a psychiatrist named Eugenia Cameron worked up a detailed portrait of Donald’s behavior that proved indispensable to Kanner as he struggled to understand the boy’s “fascinating peculiarities.”

1938 年 10 月的两周时间里，弗兰克尔与精神病学家尤金妮娅·卡梅伦共同为唐纳德的行为绘制了详尽画像。当坎纳试图理解这个男孩"令人着迷的奇特之处"时，这份记录成为不可或缺的关键资料。



[Donald] wandered about smiling, making stereotyped movements with his fingers, crossing them about in the air. He shook his head from side, whispering or humming the same three-note tune. He spun with great pleasure anything he could seize upon to spin. He kept throwing things on the floor, seeming to delight in the sounds they made . . .

[唐纳德]面带微笑地四处走动，手指做着刻板动作，在空中交叉比划。他左右摇晃着脑袋，低声哼着或轻唱着同一段三个音符的旋律。他极其愉悦地旋转任何能抓到的东西，不断把东西扔到地上，似乎很享受它们发出的声响……



Most of his actions were repetitions carried out in exactly the same way in which they had been performed originally. If he spun a block, he must always start with the same face uppermost. When he threaded buttons, he arranged them in a certain sequence that had no pattern to it but happened to be the order used by the father when he first had shown them to Donald.

他的大部分动作都是重复性的，且每次重复时都严格遵循最初完成时的相同方式。如果他旋转积木，必定要让同一面朝上才开始。当他串纽扣时，会按照某种特定顺序排列——这种顺序毫无规律可言，却恰好是父亲第一次教他串纽扣时所用的顺序。



As the boy did these things, he would repeat cryptic phrases to himself like “The right one is on, the left one is off,” “Through the dark clouds shining,” and “Dahlia, dahlia, dahlia.” At first, Kanner characterized these statements as “irrelevant utterances,” but they often turned out to be more relevant than they first appeared. While drawing with crayons, Donald kept saying over and over, “Annette and Cécile make purple.” Only later did Kanner figure out that he had named each of his five watercolor bottles after one of the Dionne quintuplets; the red bottle was called “Annette” and the blue bottle was “Cécile.” Blended together, they made purple.

男孩在做这些事情时，会反复对自己念叨一些晦涩的短语，比如“右边亮着，左边灭了”、“穿过乌云闪耀”和“大丽花，大丽花，大丽花”。起初，坎纳将这些表述定性为“无关话语”，但它们往往比表面看起来更有深意。当唐纳德用蜡笔画画时，他不停重复着“安妮特和塞西尔调出了紫色”。直到后来坎纳才明白，他给五瓶水彩颜料分别取了迪翁五胞胎的名字：红色瓶子叫“安妮特”，蓝色瓶子叫“塞西尔”——混合在一起就变成了紫色。



In addition to having an extraordinarily precise memory for numbers, dates, addresses, and encyclopedia entries, Donald performed better on a test of visual matching and dexterity called the Séguin form board than his typical peers. Asperger and his colleagues would have viewed the boy’s superior visual skills, extraordinary memory, and precocious attempts to put the world in order as aspects of his autistic intelligence. They appreciated the fact that a boy fascinated by triangles drawn in the sand might someday grow up to become a professor of astronomy. But Kanner wasn’t running a school for children with special needs. He was launching a new field of psychiatry.

除了对数字、日期、地址和百科全书条目拥有异常精准的记忆力外，唐纳德在一项名为塞甘形板测验的视觉匹配与灵巧度测试中，表现甚至优于同龄的典型儿童。阿斯伯格及其同事会将这个男孩卓越的视觉能力、非凡的记忆力以及试图将世界秩序化的早熟尝试，视为其孤独谱系智力的体现。他们欣赏这样一个事实：一个痴迷于沙地上画三角形的男孩，有朝一日或许能成长为天文学教授。但坎纳并非在经营一所特殊需求儿童学校，他正在开创一个精神病学的新领域。



As a clinician who specialized in the emotional disturbances of children, he was particularly interested in the fact that Donald seemed more engaged by inanimate objects than by his own mother, which seemed to flout the most basic instincts of the human species.

作为一名专攻儿童情绪障碍的临床医生，他尤其关注这样一个事实：唐纳德似乎对无生命物体的兴趣远胜于对自己的母亲——这似乎公然违背了人类物种最基本的本能。



He paid no attention to persons around him. When taken into a room, he completely disregarded the people and instantly went for objects, preferably those that could be spun. Commands or actions that could not possibly be disregarded were resented as unwelcome intrusions. But he was never angry at the interfering person. He angrily shoved away the hand that was in his way or the foot that stepped on one of his blocks, at one time referring to the foot on the block as “umbrella.” Once the obstacle was removed, he forgot the whole affair. He gave no heed to the presence of other children but went about his favorite pastimes, walking off from the children if they were so bold as to join him.

他对周围的人毫不在意。被带进房间时，他完全无视他人，径直走向物品，尤其是那些可以旋转的东西。那些无法忽视的指令或行为，会被他视为不受欢迎的干扰而心生抵触。但他从未对干扰者发怒——他只是愤怒地推开挡路的手，或踩到他积木的脚，有一次还把踩到积木的脚称为“雨伞”。一旦障碍被移除，他便将整件事抛诸脑后。他对其他孩子的存在视若无睹，只顾沉浸在自己喜爱的游戏中，若孩子们胆敢加入，他便径自走开。



Kanner was particularly struck by Mary and Beaman’s recollections that their son had never responded to people in the usual ways, even as an infant. This suggested that Donald’s condition was innate and inborn rather than a response to some kind of psychological trauma inflicted by his environment. In the case of “Donald T.,” he recognized the outline of a major breakthrough for his field: the discovery of the first form of major psychosis that was endemic to infancy.

坎纳尤其被玛丽和比曼的回忆所触动：他们的儿子从婴儿时期起就从未以正常方式回应他人。这表明唐纳德的情况是与生俱来的，而非环境造成的某种心理创伤所致。在“唐纳德·T”的案例中，他意识到自己的领域即将迎来重大突破：发现了第一种婴幼儿特有的严重精神障碍。



VI



If Frankl ever proposed the term Autistischen Psychopathen as a name for the boy’s condition, Kanner would have likely rejected it out of hand for two reasons. The term autism, in the way that Eugen Bleuler originally used it, implied a gradual withdrawal into a private life of fantasy. But Donald showed no signs of having an overactive imagination, and he had not withdrawn from the social world; he had been born outside it. And Kanner loathed the term psychopathy as much as he loathed the terms introvert, extrovert, and neurotic, all cocktail-party buzzwords at a time when psychoanalysis was on the rise. In his book on mothers, he mockingly defined psychopath as “a fellow the expert does not want to be bothered with.” Thus his records of the Tripletts’ first visit concluded on a note of clinical uncertainty: “?schizophrenia.”

如果弗兰克尔曾提议用“孤独症精神病态者”一词来描述这个男孩的状况，坎纳很可能会出于两个原因断然拒绝。孤独症一词，在尤金·布洛伊勒最初使用的意义上，暗示着逐渐退缩到私人幻想生活中。但唐纳德并未表现出过度活跃的想象力，也并非从社交世界退缩——他生来就处于社交世界之外。而坎纳对“精神病态”一词的厌恶，不亚于他对“内向者”“外向者”和“神经症”这些术语的憎恶——在精神分析学兴起之时，这些都是鸡尾酒会上的流行词汇。在他关于母亲的著作中，他讽刺地将“精神病态者”定义为“专家不愿费心应付的家伙”。因此，他对特里普利特一家首次就诊的记录以临床不确定性的结论收尾：“？精神分裂症。”



While this preliminary diagnosis may now seem like a shot in the dark, Kanner had many good reasons to suspect that Donald’s behavior was related to schizophrenia—specifically, the early-onset form of the condition initially proposed by Sukhareva in reference to her young patients, a concept that was rapidly gaining acceptance among Kanner’s colleagues.

尽管这一初步诊断如今看来可能像是盲目猜测，但坎纳有充分理由怀疑唐纳德的行为与精神分裂症有关——具体而言，是指苏哈列娃最初针对其年轻患者提出的早发型精神分裂症概念，这一概念当时正迅速获得坎纳同僚们的认可。



The first account of “childhood schizophrenia” in America, published in 1933 by Howard Potter of the New York Psychiatric Institute, outlined a set of behaviors that overlapped closely with later descriptions of autism, including a “defect in emotional rapport,” disturbances of language development, “diminution of affect,” and “bizarre behavior with a tendency toward perseveration and stereotypy.”

美国关于“儿童精神分裂症”的首篇报告发表于 1933 年，由纽约精神病学研究所的霍华德·波特撰写。该报告描述的一系列行为与后来对孤独症的描述高度重合，包括“情感共鸣缺陷”、语言发展障碍、“情感淡漠”，以及“伴有持续重复和刻板行为的怪异举动”。



One boy that Potter described could have been Donald’s brother. As an infant, he refused to come when his mother called, and he fretted and cried frequently, which she blamed on an ongoing ear infection. In kindergarten, he would wander around the classroom, giggling and talking to himself while ignoring the other children. He played incessantly with light switches, collected pieces of paper that he lined up in rows, and made “stereotyped motions with his hand in the air as though writing,” blinking while repeating in a monotone, “Coo-koo, Coo-koo.” About the only activities that engaged the boy’s “indifferent attention,” according to Potter, were “singing and dancing games in the gymnasium.”

波特描述的一个男孩，简直像是唐纳德的兄弟。婴儿时期，母亲呼唤时他拒绝回应，且频繁烦躁哭闹，母亲将其归咎于持续的耳部感染。上幼儿园后，他会在教室里游荡，咯咯傻笑并自言自语，完全无视其他孩子。他不停地摆弄电灯开关，收集纸片并排成一行，还会“像写字一样在空中做刻板的手部动作”，同时眨着眼睛用单调的声音重复：“咕咕，咕咕”。据波特所言，唯一能吸引这个男孩“漠然注意力”的活动，是“体育馆里的唱歌和跳舞游戏”。



It soon became obvious that the patients in Potter’s practice represented just the tip of an iceberg. A year before the Tripletts made their pilgrimage to Baltimore, Louise Despert of the Payne Whitney Psychiatric Clinic told the first international congress of child psychiatry in Paris about kids in her own practice whose “affective rapport with the environment” was either attenuated or “completely severed.” One boy in this group, S.K., could recite more than a hundred nursery rhymes by his second birthday, though his expressive vocabulary was limited. His nursemaid would take him on daily outings to the park, where he happily amused himself, until his father was laid off and the family had to move into a cramped apartment with S.K.’s grandparents. The abrupt loss of his familiar surroundings and his outings with his beloved nursemaid triggered a dramatic regression. Unless his parents took him back to the specific place in the park where he had played every day, he would wave his fingers in the air while repeating over and over like a soothing mantra, “The boy played—the boy in the park.” S.K.’s parents brought him to a neurological institute for evaluation, but his condition declined precipitously there, so they committed him to the New York Psychiatric Institute, where he developed “severe compulsions” and his estimated IQ plummeted by seventy points. At age eleven, he was already in custodial care.

很快便显而易见，波特医生诊所里的这些患者只是冰山一角。在特里普利特一家前往巴尔的摩求医的前一年，佩恩·惠特尼精神病诊所的路易丝·德佩特在巴黎举行的首届国际儿童精神病学大会上，讲述了她自己诊所里那些"与环境的感情联系"要么减弱、要么"完全断裂"的孩子们。其中有个叫 S.K.的男孩，到两岁时就能背诵一百多首童谣，尽管他的表达词汇量很有限。他的保姆每天带他去公园玩，他在那里自得其乐，直到父亲失业，全家不得不搬去与祖父母同住一间狭小的公寓。熟悉环境的突然失去，以及与他心爱保姆的出游中断，引发了他急剧的退化。除非父母带他回到公园里他过去每天玩耍的那个特定地点，否则他就会在空中摆动手指，像念咒语般一遍又一遍地重复："男孩玩过——公园里的男孩。"S.K.他的父母带他去了一家神经学研究所进行评估，但他的病情在那里急剧恶化，于是他们将他送进了纽约精神病学研究所。在那里，他出现了“严重的强迫行为”，智商估计值骤降了 70 分。11 岁时，他已经需要接受监护照料。



By the end of the 1930s, Despert had emerged as the leading figure in the field of childhood “affect disorders” by identifying a cluster of behaviors and traits that could have been lifted directly from Asperger’s files. She described children (both boys and girls) who seemed more interested in the form of words than in their communicative function; showed little regard for their parents and had no regular playmates; rifled through dictionaries and encyclopedias while they were still in diapers; were precociously fascinated by “abstract” pursuits like mathematics, archaeology, and astronomy; became “excessively preoccupied” with calendars, license plates, and telephone numbers; and exhibited “bizarre” repetitive movements and bouts of “intense, purposeless behavior.” She concluded, “Schizophrenia in children is probably not so rare as it has long been thought.”

到 20 世纪 30 年代末，德佩特通过识别出一系列行为和特征——这些内容几乎可以直接从阿斯伯格的研究档案中提取——成为儿童“情感障碍”领域的领军人物。她描述的儿童（包括男孩和女孩）表现出：对词语的形式比其交流功能更感兴趣；对父母漠不关心，没有固定的玩伴；还在穿尿布时就翻阅词典和百科全书；过早地对数学、考古学、天文学等“抽象”领域着迷；“过度沉迷”于日历、车牌号和电话号码；并表现出“怪异”的重复动作和“强烈而无目的的行为”发作。她得出结论：“儿童精神分裂症可能并不像人们长期以来认为的那样罕见。”



Kanner was certainly aware of Despert’s work and its deeper context in the history of psychiatry. The last chapter of his textbook was devoted to “pre-psychotic” children who were allegedly destined to become schizophrenic as adults. He cited Kraepelin’s accounts of “quiet, shy, retiring children” who ended up living solitary lives. He described children who endlessly repeated phrases that became “detached from their original meaning” while they displayed “abnormal motions of a rhythmical character.” He quoted Meyer on “unusually precocious” children with a “specially great tendency to shyness” that tended to develop “complex fixations” and “one-sided preoccupations.” (Meyer believed that these children were worth the strongest therapeutic efforts that his colleagues could muster.)

坎纳无疑了解德斯珀特的研究及其在精神病学史上的深层背景。他教科书的最后一章专门探讨了那些据称注定会在成年后发展为精神分裂症的“前精神病态”儿童。他引用了克雷佩林对“安静、害羞、孤僻的儿童”的描述，这些儿童最终过着孤独的生活。他描述了那些无休止重复短语的儿童，这些短语“脱离了原本的含义”，同时表现出“有节奏的异常动作”。他还引用了迈耶关于“异常早熟”儿童的观点，这些儿童“特别容易害羞”，往往形成“复杂的固着”和“片面的执念”。（迈耶认为，这些儿童值得同事们付出最强烈的治疗努力。）



From Kanner’s perspective, however, the model of childhood schizophrenia that was rapidly taking hold in his field had several problems. The most obvious one was that the theory that this condition was a prodromal phase of adult psychosis was still untested. In most cases of schizophrenia, the first signs become apparent only after puberty. The notion of nursery-school-age psychotics not only challenged the time-tested arc of the natural course of the disorder, it subverted the psychodynamic theories in vogue at the time to explain its causation, which leaned heavily on the alleged role of “schizophrenogenic” mothers. Despert’s case study of S.K., for example, began with an ominous reference to the boy’s mother, described as an “aggressive, oversolicitous, American-born Jewish woman who dominates her husband”—a classic description of the type.

然而，在坎纳看来，当时其领域内迅速流行的儿童精神分裂症模型存在若干问题。最明显的一点是，关于这种状况是成人精神病前驱期的理论尚未得到验证。在大多数精神分裂症病例中，最初症状仅在青春期后才显现。学龄前儿童出现精神病的概念不仅挑战了经时间检验的疾病自然病程轨迹，更颠覆了当时盛行的用以解释病因的心理动力学理论——这些理论严重依赖所谓"致精神分裂症母亲"的假定作用。例如，德斯佩特对 S.K.的案例研究开篇便以不祥的笔触提及男孩的母亲，将其描述为"一位具有攻击性、过度溺爱、在美国出生的犹太女性，她支配着丈夫"——这正是该类型的经典写照。



While the invention of this devouring Medusa is often ascribed to Freud, it actually marked a departure by American psychiatrists from Freud’s belief that the etiology of schizophrenia was rooted in biology rather than psychology. In truth, the concept of the schizophrenogenic mother bloomed in a hothouse of cultural anxieties in the post–World War I era, when women who had been previously subservient and self-effacing began cutting their hair short, smoking cigarettes, demanding the right to vote, and taking jobs in fields like education that had been formerly reserved for men, replacing them as primary breadwinners in many families. One of the psychoanalysts who laid the groundwork of the schizophrenogenic mother concept was Harry Stack Sullivan—another prominent Meyerian at Johns Hopkins.

尽管这种吞噬一切的“美杜莎”式母亲的发明常被归功于弗洛伊德，但实际上它标志着美国精神病学家与弗洛伊德信念的分道扬镳——弗洛伊德认为精神分裂症的病因根植于生物学而非心理学。事实上，“致精神分裂症母亲”这一概念在第一次世界大战后的文化焦虑温室中蓬勃生长。彼时，那些曾经顺从谦卑的女性开始剪短头发、吸烟、争取选举权，并进入教育等原属男性的领域工作，在许多家庭中取代男性成为主要经济支柱。为“致精神分裂症母亲”概念奠定基础的精神分析学家之一，是约翰·霍普金斯大学另一位杰出的迈耶学派学者——哈里·斯塔克·沙利文。



If Donald’s condition was present at birth, however, arguing that Mary Triplett’s personality had somehow played a role in it seemed doomed from the start. Kanner was also likely put off by Despert’s style of clinical interpretation, which seemed designed to fit her patients into prefabricated pigeonholes. For example, she sorted her patients into categories of “acute onset,” “insidious onset,” and “insidious onset with an acute episode.” She classified S.K. as a case of acute onset because of his dramatic regression, but it’s clear that the development of his speech was atypical before that. Despert also often attributed her patients’ “bizarre” movements to hallucinations but admitted that this was speculation on her part for the children under six, since they never spoke of seeing or hearing things that weren’t there.

然而，若唐纳德的情况是先天存在的，那么认为玛丽·特里普利特的性格在某种程度上对其产生影响的观点从一开始就注定站不住脚。坎纳很可能也被德斯珀特的临床解读风格所困扰，这种风格似乎是为了将患者套入预设的分类框架。例如，她将患者分为"急性发作"、"隐匿性发作"和"隐匿性发作伴急性发作"等类别。她将 S.K.归类为急性发作病例，原因是其出现了戏剧性的退化，但在此之前他的语言发展显然已存在异常。德斯珀特还常将患者的"怪异"动作归因于幻觉，但承认对于六岁以下儿童而言这只是她的推测，因为这些孩子从未提及看到或听到不存在的事物。



Furthermore, Despert’s concept of childhood schizophrenia seemed to grow broader every year, sucking in an increasingly heterogeneous mass of patients, which was a problem with the whole field of research into allegedly schizoid children. In his textbook, Kanner quoted one clinician as saying, “If we wished, we could forms as many groups as there were individuals.” That wasn’t very helpful for a man striving to establish child psychiatry as a rigorously empirical field of medicine.

此外，德斯珀特关于儿童精神分裂症的概念似乎逐年扩大，吸纳了越来越庞杂的患者群体——这恰恰是整个所谓"类精神分裂儿童"研究领域普遍存在的问题。坎纳在教科书中引用一位临床医生的话说："如果我们愿意，完全可以根据个体差异划分出同样多的亚型。"对于一位致力于将儿童精神病学建设为严格实证医学领域的人来说，这显然毫无助益。



—



IN APRIL 1939, Kanner sent another child to Frankl and Cameron for evaluation: a seven-year-old girl named Elaine C. who had been diagnosed as feebleminded and possibly deaf. She was surely neither. Elaine would run out to the garage with her hands clapped over her ears when her mother cleaned house, terrified by the roar of the vacuum cleaner. She could say a handful of words by her first birthday but learned no new words after that for four years. Doctors reassured her parents that she would grow out of her eccentricities, but she did not. During a lesson in flower arranging in nursery school, she ate the leaves and drank the water.

1939 年 4 月，坎纳将另一名儿童转诊给弗兰克尔和卡梅伦进行评估：七岁女孩伊莱恩·C，曾被诊断为智力低下且可能患有听力障碍。但她显然两者都不是。每当母亲打扫房间时，伊莱恩会双手捂住耳朵冲进车库，被吸尘器的轰鸣声吓得魂不附体。她一岁生日前能说几个单词，但此后四年间再未学会任何新词。医生们宽慰其父母说，这些怪癖会随着成长自然消失，但事实并非如此。在幼儿园插花课上，她竟吃掉了叶片并喝光了花瓶里的水。



Elaine adored animals and would sometimes get down on all fours to imitate their cries. Her mother filled her room with toy dogs and rabbits, which she treated like friends.
