He would view the behavior of autistic children as essentially the same phenomenon on an individual scale. But where Kanner saw a refrigerator, Bettelheim saw a concentration camp, with the mother as Kommandant.

他会将孤独谱系儿童的行为视为本质上相同的现象，只是发生在个体层面。但坎纳看到的是"冰箱"，而贝特尔海姆看到的却是"集中营"，母亲则是"指挥官"。



—



WITH THE POPULAR FASCINATION for psychoanalysis at its peak, Bettelheim’s work at the Orthogenic School, his books like Love Is Not Enough and Truants from Life, his echt Viennese accent, and his paternalistic manner made him an enormously charismatic figure. In a series of articles for mainstream publications like Parents and Popular Science, he commented on a wide range of social issues, from the impact of anti-Semitism on children to schizophrenic art.

随着精神分析风靡一时，贝特尔海姆在矫正学校的工作、他的著作如《爱还不够》和《生活的逃兵》、他那地道的维也纳口音以及家长式的作风，使他成为一个极具魅力的公众人物。在《父母》和《大众科学》等主流刊物的一系列文章中，他评论了广泛的社会议题，从反犹太主义对儿童的影响到精神分裂症患者的艺术创作。



By 1956, when the Ford Foundation awarded the Orthogenic School a $342,500 grant for a five-year study of autism, he was on his way to becoming the first celebrity “shrink” in America—the psychoanalytic equivalent of Dr. Oz. Bettelheim cited Kanner’s paper in his grant application, and the model of autism employed at the school was based on his 1943 case descriptions and commentary about the role of “refrigerator” parents. “We believed that autistic children were usually attractive, probably above normal intelligence, and showed not even ‘soft signs’ of organic damage,” recalled Jacqueline Seevak Sanders, who worked as Bettelheim’s assistant for fourteen years and eventually took over as director of the Orthogenic School. While many staff members quietly considered the possibility that the children did have some kind of inborn neurological difference that made them unusually vulnerable to the influences of their psychological environment, their assumption in practice was that the primary cause of autism was bad parenting, and that years of milieu therapy could produce a complete cure.

到 1956 年，福特基金会向孤儿症学校拨款 34.25 万美元用于为期五年的孤独症研究时，贝特尔海姆正逐步成为美国首位明星级"心理医生"——相当于精神分析界的奥兹医生。他在拨款申请中引用了坎纳的论文，该校采用的孤独症模型正是基于坎纳 1943 年的病例描述及对"冰箱父母"角色的评论。"我们相信孤独症儿童通常相貌端正，智力可能高于常人，甚至没有器质性损伤的'软体征'，"曾在贝特尔海姆手下担任助理十四年、最终接任孤儿症学校校长的杰奎琳·西瓦克·桑德斯回忆道。尽管许多工作人员私下认为这些孩子可能确实存在某种先天神经差异，使他们异常容易受到心理环境影响，但实际操作中他们仍假定孤独症的主要病因是教养不当，并相信多年的环境疗法能够实现完全康复。



This assumption was simply not questioned, at least in public. The leading psychoanalytic theorist in America, David Rapaport, who was on the school’s evaluation team, believed it; the renowned developmental psychologist Erik Erikson, who visited the school, supported it; and, perhaps most importantly, the parents who brought their children to Bettelheim (typically referred by psychoanalysts) were also convinced that “their treatment of the child had caused the problem,” as Sanders put it. They also provided developmental histories that seemed to confirm that belief. It was a closed loop. Research that suggested an organic etiology—like Bender’s papers on childhood schizophrenia—was simply ignored by the psychoanalysts who had eagerly rushed into the field.

这一假设根本无人质疑，至少在公开场合如此。美国顶尖精神分析理论家、曾参与该校评估团队的戴维·拉帕波特对此深信不疑；著名发展心理学家埃里克·埃里克森在访问该校后也表示支持；而最关键的是，那些将孩子送到贝特尔海姆处治疗的父母（通常由精神分析师推荐）也坚信"他们对孩子的抚养方式导致了问题"，正如桑德斯所言。他们提供的成长史资料似乎也印证了这一观点。这形成了一个闭环。那些暗示器质性病因的研究——比如本德关于儿童精神分裂症的论文——则被急于投身该领域的精神分析师们完全忽视了。



For the young psychologists and counselors (many of whom were women) who were the lifeblood of the Orthogenic School and most intimately involved in the day-to-day lives of the children, it was a tremendously inspiring place to embark on a career. As Kanner had done at Yankton, Bettelheim instituted many reforms to humanize the institution he had inherited. He had the locks on the doors changed so that one key opened them all, allowing the counselors to look less like jailhouse guards with keys bristling on their belts. He took down the funereal black curtains covering the windows and replaced them with pretty drapes and swapped out the EEG machine and surgical bed for a Ping-Pong table. Children who wet their beds at night were no longer to be punished or shamed, and he personally ripped down the chart in the bathroom used to track the administration of laxatives. The design of the facility was to serve the psychological needs of the children, not the logistical convenience of the staff. Instead of institutional bunk beds, the children slept in custom-built wooden beds with matching dressers, and their own drawings were exhibited on the walls. Sanders described her time there in glowing terms echoed by many former staff members: “This was characteristic of the atmosphere of the school: brilliant minds at work on a new frontier of the greatest human significance, and with greatest hopefulness.”

对于年轻的心理学家和辅导员（其中许多是女性）而言，作为奥托金学校的核心力量并最密切参与孩子们日常生活的地方，这里是一个开启职业生涯的极具启发性的场所。正如坎纳在扬克顿所做的那样，贝特尔海姆推行了许多改革，使他接手的机构更加人性化。他更换了门锁，使一把钥匙能打开所有门，这样辅导员看起来就不像腰带上挂满钥匙的狱警了。他取下覆盖窗户的阴森黑色窗帘，换上漂亮的窗帘，并用乒乓球桌替换了脑电图仪和手术床。尿床的孩子不再受到惩罚或羞辱，他亲自撕掉了浴室里用于记录泻药使用情况的图表。设施的设计旨在满足孩子们的心理需求，而非工作人员的后勤便利。孩子们不再睡在机构式的双层床上，而是睡在定制的木床上，配有配套的梳妆台，墙上还展示着他们自己的画作。 桑德斯用许多前员工都曾提及的溢美之词描述了她在那里的时光：“这正是这所学校的氛围特点：杰出的头脑在最伟大的人类意义的新前沿上工作，并怀揣着最大的希望。”“



Richard Pollak offered a darker view of life in the school in his biography of Bettelheim, The Creation of Dr. B, depicting him as a despotic tyrant who struck children for minor infractions, whipped them with belts, dragged them out of the shower, and verbally humiliated them. Former student Ronald Angres, diagnosed as autistic by Bettelheim, wrote that in his twelve years at the school, he lived in terror of hearing the squeak of his crepe-soled shoes in the dorms—“in abject, animal terror.”

理查德·波拉克在其为贝特尔海姆所著的传记《B 博士的诞生》中，描绘了这所学校更为阴暗的生活图景：他将贝特尔海姆刻画成一个专制暴君，会因孩子轻微违规而动手打人，用皮带抽打他们，将他们拖出淋浴间，并对其进行言语羞辱。曾被贝特尔海姆诊断为孤独谱系的前学生罗纳德·安格雷斯写道，在这所学校的十二年里，他生活在恐惧中，害怕听到贝特尔海姆那双胶底鞋在宿舍里发出的吱嘎声——“处于一种卑贱的、动物般的恐惧之中。”



Beyond his conduct at the school, the primary damage that Bettelheim did to a generation of autistic children and their families was spreading Kanner’s theories of toxic parenting even further in pop culture than Kanner himself could have. Other than the occasional quote in Time, Kanner’s comments were mostly confined to professional journals. But Bettelheim was everywhere by the 1960s, publishing articles in Harper’s (“Growing Up Female”), Redbook (“Why Working Mothers Feel Guilty”), the New York Times Magazine (“Children Must Learn to Fear”), Life (“Why Does Man Become a Hater?”), and Ladies’ Home Journal, where he had a regular column (“The Danger of Teaching Your Baby to Read,” “Am I Ruining My Child for Life?”). He stated his elaboration of Kanner’s theory in the starkest possible terms in The Empty Fortress, the book he developed from his progress reports to the Ford Foundation, which became a best seller. “The precipitating factor in infantile autism is the parent’s wish that his child did not exist,” he wrote. “Infants, if totally deserted by humans before they have developed enough to shift for themselves, will die. And if their physical care is enough for survival but they are deserted emotionally, or are pushed beyond the capacity to cope, they will become autistic.”

除了他在学校的行为之外，贝特尔海姆对一代孤独谱系儿童及其家庭造成的主要伤害，是将坎纳的“有毒父母”理论在流行文化中传播得比坎纳本人所能做到的更远。除了偶尔在《时代》杂志上被引用外，坎纳的评论大多局限于专业期刊。但到了 20 世纪 60 年代，贝特尔海姆无处不在，他在《哈珀斯》（《女性成长》）、《红皮书》（《职场母亲为何感到内疚》）、《纽约时报杂志》（《孩子必须学会恐惧》）、《生活》（《人为何成为仇恨者？》）以及《妇女家庭杂志》（他定期撰写专栏，如《教婴儿阅读的危险》、《我是否在毁掉孩子的一生？》）上发表文章。他在《空堡垒》一书中以最直白的措辞阐述了他对坎纳理论的延伸，这本书是根据他向福特基金会提交的进展报告整理而成，并成为畅销书。“引发婴儿孤独谱系的诱因是父母希望自己的孩子不存在，”他写道，“婴儿如果在发育到足以自食其力之前完全被人类抛弃，就会死亡。” 如果他们的身体照料足以维持生存，但在情感上被遗弃，或被迫承受超出其应对能力范围的压力，他们就会变得孤独谱系。”



The book was widely and enthusiastically reviewed and was many lay readers’ introduction to the subject. Referring to autism as “an illness, a suicide really, of the soul,” Eliot Fremont-Smith of the New York Times called The Empty Fortress “an extraordinary book” and chose it as one of the top nonfiction titles of the year. It was, in essence, Bettelheim’s notice to the Ford Foundation that he had given them their money’s worth. He claimed “good” or “fair” outcomes for 92 percent of the speaking children in his sample, saying “the seventeen children whose improvement we classified as ‘good’ can for all intents and purposes be considered ‘cured.’”

这本书获得了广泛而热烈的评论，成为许多普通读者了解该主题的入门读物。《纽约时报》的埃利奥特·弗雷蒙特-史密斯将孤独症称为“一种疾病，实际上是灵魂的自杀”，并称《空堡垒》是“一本非凡的书”，将其选为年度最佳非虚构类作品之一。这本质上就是贝特尔海姆向福特基金会发出的通知：他让他们花的钱物有所值。他声称样本中 92%的口语者儿童取得了“良好”或“尚可”的改善效果，并表示“我们归类为‘良好’改善的 17 名儿童，几乎可以认为已经‘治愈’。”



Behind the bright yellow door of the Orthogenic School, however, Bettelheim’s staff knew his claims were hyperbolic at best. Sanders would later admit that Bettelheim had “exaggerated . . . so that success appeared both greater in kind and in quantity than it actually was.” The treatment of their first cohort of eleven autistic children, which ended in 1958, had not even come close to anything resembling a cure. “None of them were ‘successful’ in that we had no hope of any of them being at any time able to live independently,” she reported. “To us, this was failure, since we believed that any child admitted to the Orthogenic School had the potential to lead a full and independent life. I, and probably my coworkers, viewed the failure as mine and the staff’s.” But, she added, “we did not view it as evidence that we might be working with the wrong premises.”

然而，在矫治学校那扇亮黄色大门背后，贝特尔海姆的员工们深知他的说法充其量只是夸大其词。桑德斯后来承认贝特尔海姆"夸大了事实……使得成功的程度和数量都显得比实际情况更可观"。他们于 1958 年结束的首批 11 名孤独谱系儿童的治疗，甚至远未达到任何类似治愈的效果。"他们中没有一个是'成功的'，因为我们根本不指望他们中的任何人能在任何时候独立生活，"她报告说，"对我们而言，这就是失败，因为我们相信任何进入矫治学校的孩子都有潜力过上充实独立的生活。我和我的同事们大概都认为失败在于我和员工们。"但她补充道，"我们并不认为这可能是我们基于错误前提工作的证据。"



Sanders claimed to see significant improvement in some of the autistic children admitted to the school later, which must have been encouraging, considering that the average length of their twenty-four-hour immersion in an environment entirely devoted to their well-being was ten years. But when she took over as director in the 1970s, she became “very reluctant” to admit such children to the school, no longer believing that the staff “could have the same goals for them” as they did for the other students. She was heartbroken to see the children who didn’t appear in Bettelheim’s heroic narratives of redemption return to lockdown wards. Though the approach to treating autism at the Orthogenic School was based on a complex web of misconceptions and fabrications that caused their parents untold grief, the children were generally treated better there—at least by Bettelheim’s staff—than they were in the brutal institutions that the school was intended to replace.

桑德斯声称，后来入学的孤独谱系儿童中，有些孩子出现了显著改善——考虑到他们全天候沉浸在完全以他们福祉为中心的环境中平均长达十年之久，这想必令人鼓舞。但当她于 20 世纪 70 年代接任校长后，却变得"非常不愿"接收这类儿童入学，因为她不再相信教职员工能像对待其他学生那样"为他们设定相同目标"。看到那些未能出现在贝特尔海姆英雄式救赎叙事中的孩子们重返封闭病房时，她心碎不已。尽管奥赛杰尼克学校治疗孤独症的方法建立在由误解与捏造构成的复杂网络之上，给这些孩子的父母带来了难以言说的痛苦，但至少贝特尔海姆团队对待这些孩子的方式，比这所学校本欲取代的那些残酷机构要好得多。



IV



Once a child diagnosed with autism or childhood schizophrenia was placed in a state hospital, he or she was no longer treated as a child. Instead, such children were blasted with the whole armamentarium of powerful drugs, last-ditch methods, and experimental treatments that the psychiatric establishment usually reserves for its most intractable adult psychotics.

一旦被诊断为孤独症或儿童精神分裂症的孩子被送入州立医院，他们便不再被视为儿童。相反，这些孩子会遭受精神病学机构通常为最难治的成年精神病患者保留的全套强效药物、最后手段和实验性治疗的猛烈冲击。



Bender’s preferred method of treatment at Bellevue was electroconvulsive therapy (ECT). Her young patients typically received twenty courses of ECT or more, which she claimed boosted their IQ, “stabilized” their electroencephalograms, improved their body image, made them “more normal” in general, and prompted complete “remission” in some cases. To supplement ECT, Bender also employed subcoma insulin shock and Metrazol, a drug that produces convulsions.

本德在贝尔维尤医院首选的疗法是电休克治疗（ECT）。她治疗的幼小患者通常要接受二十次或更多次电休克治疗，她声称这能提升他们的智商、"稳定"脑电图、改善身体意象、让他们总体上"更正常"，并在某些病例中促使完全"缓解"。为辅助电休克治疗，本德还采用了亚昏迷胰岛素休克疗法和痉挛剂美曲唑。



Her pharmacopeia for treating “autistic thinking” included chlorpromazine and prochlorperazine, first-generation antipsychotics that became infamous for causing an irreversible tic disorder known as the “Thorazine shuffle.” She also employed Benzedrine, the classic pep pill of Beat Generation lore, which she found particularly helpful for autistic teenagers with “sexual preoccupations.” She felt that another antipsychotic called reserpine was “among the best drugs” for treating children, despite a roster of side effects that included nightmares, vomiting, and suicidal ideation.

她治疗“孤独谱系思维”的药物清单包括氯丙嗪和丙氯拉嗪，这些第一代抗精神病药因引发不可逆的抽动障碍（俗称“索拉嗪步态”）而臭名昭著。她还使用了苯丙胺——垮掉一代传说中经典的提神药片，她发现这对伴有“性执念”的孤独谱系青少年尤其有效。她认为另一种抗精神病药利血平是治疗儿童“最佳药物之一”，尽管其副作用清单包括噩梦、呕吐和自杀意念。



Another drug that Bender felt showed great promise was LSD, which she obtained legally from Sandoz Pharmaceuticals under the brand name Delysid. Every day for two months, she administered doses of the potent hallucinogen to fifty-four autistic children, ages six to fifteen. She reported that the drug made her patients more aware, talkative, and “reality-oriented,” though she also noted an increase in their “anxious and depressive attitudes.” By keeping her patients on a daily regimen of Delysid, she claimed, she was able to wean them off their usual diet of tranquilizers.

本德认为另一种前景可观的药物是 LSD，她以 Delysid 的品牌名从山德士制药合法获取。在两个月里，她每天给 54 名 6 至 15 岁的孤独谱系儿童服用这种强效致幻剂。她报告称该药物使患者更清醒、健谈且“更贴近现实”，尽管她也注意到他们的“焦虑和抑郁倾向”有所增加。她声称，通过让患者每日服用 Delysid，能够逐渐减少他们常规使用的镇静剂剂量。



In the age before informed consent, Bender’s use of these drugs and treatments was virtually unmonitored, particularly because she was the chief of psychiatry at the hospital. She wasn’t even required to submit the designs of her uncontrolled trails for review by an ethics board before launching them.

在知情同意尚未普及的年代，本德对这些药物和疗法的使用几乎不受监控，尤其是因为她本人就是医院的精神科主任。她甚至无需在启动那些不受控制的试验前，将设计方案提交伦理委员会审查。



One of the kids unlucky enough to become a subject of her experiments was Guy Susann, the son of the popular novelist Jacqueline Susann, author of Valley of the Dolls, and her husband, Irving Mansfield. For the first three years of his life, Guy was an affectionate and playful baby, but one afternoon his nanny brought him home from the park because he had begun screaming for no apparent reason. His disconsolate wailing lasted for the rest of the night and into the following day. On the advice of a pediatrician, his parents brought him to Bellevue, where Bender subjected Guy to a week of shock treatments that left him “destroyed . . . numb, with no expression, almost lifeless,” Mansfield recalled in his autobiography.

不幸成为她实验对象的孩子之一，是畅销小说《娃娃谷》作者杰奎琳·苏珊与丈夫欧文·曼斯菲尔德之子——盖伊·苏珊。盖伊三岁前是个充满爱意、活泼爱玩的婴儿，但某天下午，保姆从公园将他带回家，因为他开始无缘无故地尖叫。他悲痛欲绝的哭喊持续了一整夜，直到第二天。在儿科医生的建议下，父母将他送往贝尔维尤医院。曼斯菲尔德在自传中回忆，本德对盖伊实施了一周的电击治疗，这让他"彻底崩溃……麻木、毫无表情、近乎失去生命"。



The little boy never spoke another word, with a single harrowing exception. “When are you going to talk?” Guy’s anguished mother asked him one day in the car. “When I’m ready,” he replied.

那个小男孩从此再没说过一句话，唯有一次令人心碎的例外。“你什么时候才肯说话？”盖伊的母亲在车里痛苦地问他。“等我准备好了，”他回答。



Susann and Mansfield placed him in a residential facility, telling their A-list friends that he had been sent to specialists in Arizona for his asthma. Mansfield attributed his wife’s drive to churn out potboilers like The Love Machine and Once Is Not Enough to her anxiety that they would run out of money for Guy’s custodial care.

苏珊和曼斯菲尔德将他安置在一家寄宿机构，并告诉他们的顶级明星朋友，他被送到亚利桑那州的专家那里治疗哮喘。曼斯菲尔德将妻子拼命创作《爱情机器》和《一次不够》这类畅销小说的动力，归因于她担心盖伊的看护费用会耗尽他们的积蓄。



—



MEANWHILE, LEADING neo-Freudian analysts like Rudolf Ekstein were putting their patients on the couch for years at a time. Because the childhood schizophrenia “spectrum” was much more inclusive than Kanner’s conception of his syndrome, children who displayed many traits now considered classic signs of autism but showed no delay in acquiring language often ended up with that diagnosis.

与此同时，鲁道夫·埃克斯坦等领先的新弗洛伊德学派分析师，正让他们的病人接受长达数年的精神分析治疗。由于当时儿童精神分裂症的“谱系”比坎纳对孤独症的定义更为宽泛，那些表现出许多如今被视为孤独症典型特征、但语言发展并未延迟的儿童，往往最终被诊断为精神分裂症。



At a meeting of the American Psychiatric Association in Atlantic City in 1952, Ekstein described such a child: an eleven-year-old boy named Tommy, who told his therapists that he knew more about geology and biology than his teachers and that he had daydreams of becoming a wise and powerful five-star general commanding a fleet of spaceships. Ekstein described his capacity for interpersonal relationships as “almost absent.” Dubbing him “the Space Child,” he turned Tommy into a cottage industry, churning out papers on him for more than a decade.

1952 年，在大西洋城举行的美国精神病学协会会议上，埃克斯坦描述了一个这样的孩子：一个名叫汤米的 11 岁男孩，他告诉治疗师，自己对地质学和生物学的了解比老师还多，并且幻想自己成为一位睿智而强大的五星上将，指挥着一支太空舰队。埃克斯坦形容他的人际交往能力“几乎为零”。他将汤米称为“太空男孩”，并以此为主题，在十多年间不断发表论文，几乎将其变成了一个个人研究产业。



Like Bettelheim, Ekstein was a product of the Viennese school. Growing up in the Austrian capital in the 1930s, he would talk philosophy with a friend who lived directly across from Freud’s office and was thrilled to glimpse the great man’s silhouette occasionally passing in front of the window. Ekstein’s epic psychoanalysis of the Space Child revealed more about the degenerate state of Freud’s legacy in the 1950s than it did about his patient’s psyche.

与贝特尔海姆一样，埃克斯坦也是维也纳学派的产物。20 世纪 30 年代，他在奥地利首都长大，常与一位住在弗洛伊德办公室正对面的朋友讨论哲学，并因偶尔瞥见这位伟人的身影从窗前掠过而激动不已。埃克斯坦对“太空男孩”进行的宏大精神分析，与其说揭示了患者的心理状态，不如说更多地暴露了 20 世纪 50 年代弗洛伊德遗产的衰败状况。



Ekstein theorized that Tommy’s precocious interest in science was likely the result of “early childhood intensive sexual traumata” caused by seduction by his mother or a nursemaid. He pondered the meaning of the boy’s erections during sessions with a female therapist. The overarching theme of his analysis was that Tommy’s fantasies of space travel represented an unconscious effort to put distance between himself and his “compulsive” parents. The inspiration for this “very intense destructive phantasying,” he surmised, was Tommy’s obsession with “science fiction, science fiction movies, and other similar literary productions.”

埃克斯坦推测，汤米对科学早熟的兴趣很可能是由母亲或保姆引诱导致的“童年早期强烈性创伤”所致。他思考了男孩在接受女性治疗师治疗时勃起的含义。其分析的核心主题是，汤米对太空旅行的幻想代表了一种无意识的努力，试图让自己与“强迫性”的父母保持距离。他推测，这种“非常强烈的破坏性幻想”的灵感来源于汤米对“科幻小说、科幻电影及其他类似文学作品的痴迷”。



For the first years of his analysis, Tommy was confined to the Southard School for emotionally disturbed children in Topeka, Kansas. Located in a Prairie-style farmhouse—with a lookout on the roof to discourage unscheduled outings—the school was affiliated with the Menninger Clinic, where Judy Garland and Marilyn Monroe spent stints in rehab. The Southard approach was sold to parents as “a mixture of Freud and friendliness,” backed up with the ever-present threat of being sent to a lockdown ward. (One teenage patient named Dick was put on lockdown for three months for seeking out “the company of lower class colored people exclusively.”) The first months of Tommy’s analysis were devoted to assuaging his anxieties about activities like crossing the street. What this “scared little boy” really needed, Ekstein snidely observed, was a “personal policeman to protect him from the wrath of the world.”

在分析治疗的头几年，汤米被限制在堪萨斯州托皮卡市的南纳德学校，这是一所针对情绪障碍儿童的特殊教育机构。学校坐落在一栋草原风格的农舍里——屋顶设有瞭望台以防止学生擅自外出——隶属于门宁格诊所，朱迪·嘉兰和玛丽莲·梦露曾在此接受康复治疗。南纳德学校的治疗理念被包装成"弗洛伊德理论与友善态度的结合"向家长推销，背后则始终伴随着被送入封闭病房的威胁。（一名叫迪克的青少年患者因"只与下层有色人种交往"被关禁闭三个月。）汤米分析治疗的头几个月，主要致力于缓解他对过马路等活动的焦虑。埃克斯坦不无讥讽地指出，这个"受惊的小男孩"真正需要的，是一个"能保护他免受世界伤害的私人警察"。



Within the walls of the bucolic facility, Tommy’s science fiction fantasies only became more fantastic. At one point, he announced to his doctors that “Tommy” was no longer in the institution, having escaped to Arizona, where he was helping physicists to upgrade the design of the atom bomb. He informed his therapist that he had built a machine that enabled him to travel back in time to the moment that life began, envisioning himself as a fish that might be eaten by bigger fish if he couldn’t swim away. Moving forward through time, he witnessed William the Conqueror’s invasion of England and took a sightseeing tour through medieval Europe. Four centuries later, he barely avoided being tried for witchcraft.

在这座田园般的机构围墙内，汤米的科幻幻想愈发天马行空。他曾向医生宣称"汤米"早已逃离此地，此刻正在亚利桑那州协助物理学家升级原子弹设计。他告诉治疗师自己造出了一台时光机，能回溯到生命诞生的瞬间——他想象自己是一条鱼，若游得不够快就会被大鱼吞噬。顺着时间洪流前行，他目睹了征服者威廉入侵英格兰，又在中世纪的欧洲观光游览。四个世纪后，他险些因巫术罪名受审。



Tommy explained that the goal of his time tripping was to intervene at critical junctures and save himself from the mysterious affliction that had resulted in his disappointing his parents. But Ekstein ventured that these fantasies enabled him “to deny his helplessness, his lonesomeness, his castration fear, his fear of being devoured, [and] his fear of dying or killing someone.” When Tommy employed a phrase like “hundreds and thousands of light years,” he was not really talking about science, Ekstein explained to his colleagues in Atlantic City. He was making “an allusion to psychological problems which he could not present in any other way.” When Tommy shared a new set of fantasies about retiring to a farm in the country to raise dinosaurs, inviting his female therapist to come along, it was judged to be a critical breakthrough in his analysis.

汤米解释说，他时间旅行的目的是在关键时刻介入，拯救自己于那种导致父母失望的神秘痛苦。但埃克斯坦认为，这些幻想让他能够“否认自己的无助、孤独、被阉割的恐惧、被吞噬的恐惧，以及死亡或杀人的恐惧”。当汤米使用“成千上万光年”这样的词语时，他并非真的在谈论科学，埃克斯坦在大西洋城向同事们解释道。他是在“暗示那些他无法以其他方式呈现的心理问题”。当汤米分享了一组新的幻想——退休到乡下的农场饲养恐龙，并邀请他的女治疗师一同前往时，这被认为是他心理分析中的关键突破。



After two years at the Southard School, Tommy was allowed to move to a boardinghouse, where he lived with a foster family. His newfound interest in baseball spawned a fantasy of managing an all-girl team that played “meticulously according to national baseball rules.” After calculating each player’s statistics for the season, he admitted to his therapists that fantasy baseball was not as exciting as waging intergalactic battles in his head. But he had decided that his fantasies must now be “logical” and “scientific” above all. He was growing up, as even Space Children are wont to do.

在南亚德学校待了两年后，汤米获准搬进一家寄宿公寓，与寄养家庭同住。他对棒球新燃起的兴趣催生了一个幻想：管理一支完全由女孩组成的球队，她们会“严格遵循全国棒球规则”进行比赛。在计算完每位球员的赛季数据后，他向治疗师坦言，幻想棒球远不如在脑海中发动星际战争来得刺激。但他已下定决心，自己的幻想如今必须首先做到“合乎逻辑”和“科学”。他正在成长，即便是太空孩子也难免如此。



He enrolled at a local university and started taking courses in science. Ekstein noted that Tommy’s foster family had come to their own understanding and acceptance of his behavior. He said that they were delighted to watch him develop into a mature and independent person, though his manner around them was “brusque and detached” and he “might never directly express his feelings of warmth and gratitude for their efforts.” He added that they found deep gratification in “seeing Tom improve and their realization of the part they played helping him achieve these modifications.”

他进入当地一所大学，开始修读科学课程。埃克斯坦指出，汤米的寄养家庭已经理解并接纳了他的行为。他说，他们欣喜地看着他成长为一个成熟独立的人，尽管汤米在他们面前表现得“粗鲁而疏离”，并且“可能永远不会直接表达对他们付出的温暖与感激之情”。他补充道，他们从“看到汤姆进步，以及意识到自己在帮助他实现这些改变中所扮演的角色”中获得了深深的满足感。



By the time Tommy was twenty-three, he had been through 1,236 hours of psychoanalysis. With the support of his foster family, he had earned a graduate degree in physics and was teaching science courses at a local college. Ekstein described him as a “personable, shy, and somewhat tense” young man who was still obsessed with “space” (in scare quotes) and most comfortable around people who shared his obscure interests.

汤米二十三岁时，已经接受了 1236 小时的精神分析。在寄养家庭的支持下，他获得了物理学研究生学位，并在当地一所大学教授科学课程。埃克斯坦形容他是一个"亲切、害羞、略显紧张"的年轻人，仍然痴迷于"太空"（带引号），只有与那些同样热衷冷门兴趣的人相处时才会感到自在。



“Whether his achievements up to now and in years to come as well as our own advance in work with such youngsters justify the tremendous commitment of treatment and research time, we do not know,” Ekstein mused. But he dismissed the notion that he had been on the wrong track all along in subjecting Tommy to a decade of psychoanalysis. “One cannot successfully treat children such as he,” Ekstein concluded, “if one must constantly answer the question as to the worthwhileness of the treatment.”

"我们不知道他迄今为止以及未来取得的成就，以及我们在这类青少年工作中的进展，是否足以证明投入大量治疗和研究时间是值得的，"埃克斯坦沉思道。但他否定了自己让汤米接受十年精神分析始终是错误方向的观点。"如果必须不断回答治疗是否值得的问题，"埃克斯坦总结道，"就无法成功治疗像他这样的孩子。"



Tommy (who called himself “Tom” by then) told his former psychotherapist that he had a new daydream: joining the research organization that would eventually become NASA. By then, space travel was no longer just a “phantasy.” It was a national obsession.

汤米（那时已自称"汤姆"）告诉他的前心理治疗师，他有了一个新的白日梦：加入那个最终将成为 NASA 的研究机构。到那时，太空旅行已不再只是"幻想"，而是举国痴迷的目标。



—



THE PSYCHIATRIC ESTABLISHMENT would eventually come up with a diagnostic label for kids like Tommy: Asperger’s syndrome. But Asperger’s work, which had never found a wide readership outside of Eastern Europe, had been virtually forgotten. Even the handful of clinicians who read his paper in the original German assumed that Kanner had somehow managed to overlook it.

精神病学界最终为像汤米这样的孩子找到了一个诊断标签：阿斯伯格综合征。但阿斯伯格的研究成果在东欧以外从未获得广泛读者，几乎已被遗忘。就连少数读过他德语原版论文的临床医生也认为，坎纳不知何故竟忽略了这篇论文。



Still, the gifted loners that Asperger wrote about kept popping up, like a lost tribe moving through the underbrush of psychiatry, occasionally glimpsed from the air. In 1953, two psychiatrists from Pennsylvania, J. Franklin Robinson and Louis J. Vitale, described a group of young patients with “circumscribed interest patterns” at a residential facility in Wilkes-Barre called the Children’s Service Center. The fascinations of these children tended to cluster in “rather odd spots,” like astronomy, chemistry, bus schedules, calendars, and maps. They had precocious vocabularies, extraordinary memories, and a passion for science and science fiction. But they had a hard time making friends their own age.

尽管如此，阿斯伯格笔下那些天赋异禀的孤独者仍不断涌现，如同一个在精神病学灌木丛中穿行的失落部落，偶尔从空中才能瞥见其踪迹。1953 年，宾夕法尼亚州的两位精神病学家 J.富兰克林·罗宾逊和路易斯·J·维塔莱，在威尔克斯-巴里一家名为"儿童服务中心"的寄宿机构中，描述了一组具有"局限兴趣模式"的年轻患者。这些孩子的痴迷往往集中在"相当古怪的领域"，比如天文学、化学、公交时刻表、日历和地图。他们拥有早熟的词汇量、非凡的记忆力，以及对科学和科幻小说的热情。但他们很难与同龄人交朋友。



One boy named Tom became interested in chemistry in grade school, which led his father to decide that he was “hiding” in books. To encourage him to be more social, his mother started trailing him to school, where she would shout at him to be more outgoing, which only succeeded in turning him into the school pariah. At the Children’s Service Center, Tom began reading up on corporate finance, nuclear physics, and botany. He took long walks through the woods to learn the names of the local plants and trees. (In Tom’s case, “circumscribed” interests apparently meant being curious about nearly everything around him except for the other children, who nicknamed him “Creepy” and “Brains.”)

一个名叫汤姆的男孩在小学时对化学产生了兴趣，这让他父亲认为他是在“躲”进书堆里。为了鼓励他多社交，母亲开始尾随他上学，在校门口大声催促他要更外向些，结果反而让他成了全校的异类。在儿童服务中心，汤姆开始研读企业金融、核物理和植物学。他常在树林里长时间漫步，学习辨认当地草木的名称。（对汤姆而言，“受限”的兴趣显然意味着对周遭万物充满好奇——唯独对同龄孩子例外，他们给他起了“怪胎”和“书呆子”的绰号。）



In his first interview with one of the center’s psychologists, he briefly perked up when he spotted a Bunsen burner on a shelf in the office. “Do you have a scientist’s laboratory here?” Tom asked, a smile flickering over his face. The psychologist told him he must think about why his parents committed him to a residential facility. “It’s supposed to be a nice school,” Tom replied agreeably. The psychologist reminded him that it was not a nice school but a home for emotionally troubled youths. “That’s it,” the boy acknowledged, in a tone of voice described as “flat.”

在首次与中心心理医生的访谈中，汤姆瞥见办公室架子上的一盏本生灯时，短暂地精神一振。"你们这里有科学家的实验室吗？"他问道，脸上掠过一丝笑意。心理医生告诉他，必须思考父母为何将他送入寄宿机构。"这应该是一所不错的学校，"汤姆顺从地回答。心理医生提醒他，这不是学校，而是收治情绪问题青少年的疗养院。"没错，"男孩用被描述为"平淡"的语调承认道。



A psychiatrist asked another boy at the center, John, what he wanted to be when he grew up. He said he was interested in astronomy and had given a four-hour lecture on the subject in eighth grade. The psychiatrist wanted to know more about the lecture, but John explained that the science behind it was “quite difficult.” John then asked the doctor to name the nine planets in the solar system. The psychiatrist was unable to do so, even after being prompted with a hint that one was named after the Greek god of the sea. John quickly became uncomfortable in the interview and began drawing spaceships. After he had been at the center for several months, a resident coordinator asked John how he was doing. “Lots of children want to play outside while I want to play inside,” he said. “They know pretty much about cowboys. I know about astronomy. We could know a little about each, but that has actually never been solved.” When members of the staff tried to involve John in games, he would slip off to the showers to deliver talks on “the mysteries of the planets” that were eagerly attended by the younger children. The staff considered John a conduct problem.

一位精神科医生询问中心里的另一个男孩约翰长大后想做什么。约翰说自己对天文学很感兴趣，八年级时还就此主题做过一场四小时的讲座。医生想了解更多讲座内容，但约翰解释说背后的科学原理"相当深奥"。随后约翰让医生说出太阳系九大行星的名称。即便医生得到提示"其中一颗以希腊海神命名"，仍未能答出。约翰在访谈中很快变得局促不安，开始画起宇宙飞船。在中心待了几个月后，一位住地协调员问约翰近况如何。"很多孩子想出去玩，而我想待在屋里，"他说，"他们很懂牛仔，我懂天文学。我们本可以互相了解一点，但这问题始终没解决。"当工作人员试图让约翰参与游戏时，他会溜到淋浴间去讲"行星之谜"，那些讲座总吸引着年幼的孩子们。工作人员认为约翰是个行为问题儿童。



Robinson and Vitale noted that children like Tom and John were routinely diagnosed with schizophrenia, but they pointed out that such children “call to mind the syndrome described by Kanner under the designation of ‘Early Infantile Autism,’” with certain differences. Unlike the Tripletts and the Muncies, the parents of these children felt that they were “normal babies” for the first years of their lives; only as they grew older and failed to make friends their own age (preferring to hang around adults) did their eccentricities become clear. They were capable of “good emotional responsiveness” to other people, but tended to be consumed with their special interests to the exclusion of more social activities. Robinson and Vitale made the interesting observation that the pursuit of these interests did not seem to be motivated by a craving for approval and reinforcement from others, but were driven by a feeling of “satisfaction from within the child.” They enjoyed learning for its own sake, as Asperger had observed a decade earlier.

罗宾逊和维塔莱指出，像汤姆和约翰这样的孩子通常被诊断为精神分裂症，但他们强调，这些孩子“让人想起坎纳所描述的‘早期婴儿孤独症’综合征”，只是存在某些差异。与特里普利特家和芒西家的孩子不同，这些孩子的父母认为他们在生命最初几年是“正常婴儿”；直到他们逐渐长大，无法结交同龄朋友（更愿意与成年人相处）时，他们的异常才变得明显。他们能对他人表现出“良好的情感回应”，但往往沉迷于自己的特殊兴趣，排斥更多社交活动。罗宾逊和维塔莱提出了一个有趣的观察：这些孩子追求兴趣似乎并非出于渴望他人的认可或强化，而是源于“来自孩子内心的满足感”。他们为学习本身而享受学习——正如阿斯伯格十年前所观察到的那样。



Furthermore, unlike Kanner’s patients, they had no delays in acquiring language and did not speak in surreal aphorisms, opaque neologisms, or echolalic references to themselves in the third person. In fact, they tended to be precociously articulate—particularly when they were expounding on the subjects that fascinated them. (“One 13-year-old boy, after a brief acquaintance, wanted to talk about mortgages,” they reported.) These children only decisively withdrew from interactions with adults at the center when they figured out that they weren’t really interested in what they were saying.

此外，与坎纳的患者不同，他们在语言习得方面没有延迟，也不会用超现实的格言、晦涩的新词或重复性的第三人称自指方式说话。事实上，他们往往过早地表现出出色的语言能力——尤其是在阐述自己着迷的话题时。（他们报告称：“一名 13 岁男孩在短暂相识后，就想谈论抵押贷款。”）这些孩子只有在意识到中心的大人们并不真正对他们所说的话感兴趣时，才会果断地退出与成年人的互动。



In an afterword, Kanner insisted that the difference between the children Robinson and Vitale described and those with his syndrome was that “in the autistic group, the circumscribed interest has often been foisted on them by their parents.” He cited a paper in a German journal on a large group of children in Tel Aviv who “were addicted to voracious reading to the exclusion of other interests and activities,” and attributed their disinterest in social interaction to “maternal overprotection.” It was another closed loop: if children came up with special interests on their own instead of being “stuffed” by their parents, they couldn’t be truly autistic—QED.

在后记中，坎纳坚持认为，罗宾逊和维塔莱描述的儿童与患有他综合征的儿童之间的区别在于，“在孤独谱系群体中，狭隘的兴趣往往是由父母强加给他们的。”他引用了一篇德国期刊上的论文，该论文研究了特拉维夫的一大群儿童，这些儿童“沉迷于贪婪的阅读，排斥其他兴趣和活动”，并将他们对社交互动的漠不关心归因于“母亲的过度保护”。这又是一个闭环：如果儿童自己产生特殊兴趣，而不是被父母“填鸭”，那么他们就不可能是真正的孤独谱系——证毕。



That same year, after a particularly disheartening roundtable on childhood schizophrenia in Cleveland, Georg Frankl tried to explain to his colleagues what had been forgotten in the endless debates about clinical nomenclature and toxic parenting. In a draft for a paper he never published, “Autism in Childhood: An Attempt of an Analysis,” he described a “brilliant autistic child prodigy,” an adult “schizoid genius,” and a child who abruptly stopped speaking when he was two, saying that “a continuum seems to stretch out” between all three cases. “We know of this continuum, and we can point out a few of its common characteristics,” he said; “however, most of the research in this area is still to be done.”

同年，在克利夫兰一场令人格外沮丧的关于儿童精神分裂症的圆桌会议后，乔治·弗兰克尔试图向同事们解释，在关于临床命名法和有毒养育方式的无休止争论中，人们究竟遗忘了什么。在他从未发表的一篇论文草稿《儿童孤独症：一种分析尝试》中，他描述了一个"才华横溢的孤独谱系神童"、一位成年"分裂型天才"，以及一个在两岁时突然停止说话的孩子，并指出这三个案例之间"似乎存在一个连续谱系"。他说："我们知道这个连续谱系的存在，也能指出它的一些共同特征；然而，这一领域的大部分研究仍有待开展。"



—



ANOTHER TWENTY-FIVE YEARS would pass before it could even get started. During that time, a consensus developed among autism researchers that the reason Kanner never discussed Asperger’s work was that the two men had described two very different groups of children—one “high-functioning” (Asperger) and the other “low-functioning” (Kanner). Though Asperger had made clear that he had seen children (as well as adults) at all levels of ability his paper had not yet been translated into English, and the fact that he had intentionally highlighted his “most promising” cases to deflect the wrath of the Nazis was still unknown.

又过了二十五年，这项研究才得以真正启动。在此期间，孤独症研究者们逐渐形成共识：坎纳之所以从未提及阿斯伯格的研究，是因为两人描述的是两类截然不同的儿童群体——一类是“高功能”（阿斯伯格），另一类是“低功能”（坎纳）。尽管阿斯伯格明确表示自己见过各种能力水平的儿童（包括成人），但他的论文尚未被翻译成英文，而他刻意突出“最有希望”的病例以规避纳粹迫害的事实，当时仍不为人知。



By 1955, however, Kanner was finally beginning to see the extent of variation in the continuum for himself by following up on his original patients. Even “low-functioning” children could grow up to become “high-functioning” adults, but only if they managed to stay out of an institution and were given a chance to develop their innate gifts—just as Asperger had predicted back in 1938.

然而到 1955 年，坎纳通过追踪自己最初的病例，终于开始认识到孤独谱系内部的巨大差异。即便是“低功能”儿童，也可能成长为“高功能”成人——前提是他们能避免被送入收容机构，并获得发展天赋的机会。这恰恰与阿斯伯格早在 1938 年的预测不谋而合。



One of Kanner’s patients, Robert S., had “unquestionably” shown the characteristic signs of early infantile autism at age eight. By twenty-three, however, he had served two years in the Navy as a meteorologist, was studying musical composition, and was happily married with a son. “Some of his works have been performed by chamber orchestras,” Kanner reported, kvelling like a proud father. His description of another boy could have been lifted directly from Asperger’s files:

坎纳的一位患者，罗伯特·S，在八岁时“毫无疑问”地表现出了早期婴儿孤独症的典型特征。然而，到了二十三岁，他已在海军担任气象学家服役两年，正在学习作曲，并且婚姻幸福，育有一子。“他的一些作品已被室内乐团演奏过，”坎纳像一位骄傲的父亲般满怀欣慰地报告。他对另一个男孩的描述几乎可以直接从阿斯伯格的文件中摘录：



Jay S., now almost 15 years old, presented in the lower grades considerable difficulties to his teachers, who were exceptionally understanding and accepting. He wandered about the classroom, masturbated openly, and staged temper tantrums. He learned to conform, did phenomenally well in mathematics, was sent to an accelerated school, and is now finishing the eleventh grade with top marks. He is a peculiar child, rather obese, who spends his spare time collecting maps and postage stamps and has little more to do with people than is absolutely necessary for the maintenance of a superficial relationship. He achieved a Binet IQ of not less than 150.

杰伊·S，如今将近十五岁，在低年级时给老师们带来了相当大的困扰，尽管这些老师异常理解并接纳他。他在教室里游荡，公开自慰，并大发脾气。他学会了遵守规矩，在数学上表现惊人，被送入一所加速学校，现在以顶尖成绩完成十一年级。他是个古怪的孩子，相当肥胖，业余时间收集地图和邮票，与人交往仅限于维持表面关系所必需的最低限度。他的比奈智商测试结果不低于 150。



A third boy had earned a scholarship to Columbia, where he “excelled in mathematical physics.” Tragically, his life was cut short when he was run over by a car while crossing Broadway in New York City.

第三个男孩获得了哥伦比亚大学的奖学金，在那里他“在数学物理方面表现卓越”。不幸的是，他在纽约市穿越百老汇时被一辆汽车撞倒，英年早逝。



Donald T. was also doing very well by that point. In 1942, the Tripletts sent him to live on a farm ten miles from their home, where he thrived under the care of a compassionate couple named Mr. and Mrs. Lewis. Three years later, Kanner took a trip from Maryland to Mississippi to visit the farm.

到那时，唐纳德·T. 也过得很好。1942 年，特里普利特夫妇把他送到离家十英里外的一个农场生活，在那里，他在一对善良的夫妇刘易斯先生和夫人的照顾下茁壮成长。三年后，坎纳从马里兰州前往密西西比州，参观了那个农场。



I was amazed at the wisdom of the couple who took care of him . . . They made him use his preoccupation with measurements by having him dig a well and report on its depth. When he kept collecting dead birds and bugs, they gave him a spot for a “graveyard” and had him put up markers; on each he wrote a first name, a type of animal as a middle name, and the farmer’s last name, e.g., “John Snail Lewis. Born, date unknown. Died (date on which he found the animal).” When he kept counting rows of corn over and over, they had him count the rows while plowing them . . . It was remarkable how well he handled the horse and plow and turned the horse around. It was obvious that Mr. and Mrs. Lewis were very fond of him and just as obvious that they were gently firm. He attended a country school where his peculiarities were accepted and where he made good scholastic progress.

我惊叹于照顾他的那对夫妇的智慧……他们利用他对测量的痴迷，让他挖一口井并报告深度。当他不断收集死鸟和虫子时，他们给他留出一块"墓地"，让他立上标记；每个标记上他都会写上一个名字，中间名是一种动物，姓氏则是农场主的姓，例如"约翰·蜗牛·刘易斯。出生日期不详。死亡日期（他发现这只动物的日期）。"当他一遍又一遍地数玉米行数时，他们让他在犁地时数行数……他驾驭马匹和犁具以及调转马头的技巧令人惊叹。显然刘易斯夫妇非常喜欢他，同样明显的是他们温和而坚定。他上的是乡村学校，那里接纳他的怪癖，他的学业也取得了良好进步。



As Asperger’s team had done for their own patients, the Lewises had found ways for Donald to put his autistic intelligence to work, rather than treating his passions for counting and collecting as pathological obsessions inflicted on him by his parents. “If one factor is significantly useful, it is a sympathetic and tolerant reception by the school,” Kanner concluded. “Those of our children who have improved have been extended extraordinary consideration by their teachers.”

正如阿斯伯格团队为自己的患者所做的那样，刘易斯夫妇找到了让唐纳德发挥其孤独谱系智慧的方法，而不是将他热衷于计数和收集的爱好视为父母强加给他的病态执念。“如果有一个因素显著有效，那就是学校给予的同情和宽容的接纳，”坎纳总结道，“那些有所改善的孩子，都得到了老师们非同寻常的关照。”



By 1958, Donald had earned a bachelor’s degree in French and taken a job as a teller at a local bank, where he “meets the public real well,” his mother reported. He was playing golf four or five days a week at a country club and had earned six trophies in local tournaments. He was active in an investor’s club, the Jaycees, and his Presbyterian church, having served a term as president of the local Kiwanis Club. He owned two cars, enjoyed reading and listening to his record player, and played bridge (though he rarely initiated games). His mother’s main complaint was that she wished she knew what her son’s “inner feelings really are.”

到 1958 年，唐纳德已获得法语学士学位，并在当地一家银行担任出纳员。据他母亲报告，他“与公众打交道非常得体”。他每周有四到五天在乡村俱乐部打高尔夫球，并在当地锦标赛中赢得了六座奖杯。他活跃于投资者俱乐部、青年商会以及长老会教堂，还曾担任过一任基瓦尼斯俱乐部主席。他拥有两辆汽车，喜欢阅读和听唱片机，还会打桥牌（尽管他很少主动发起牌局）。他母亲的主要抱怨是，她希望自己能了解儿子“内心的真实感受”。



But there were also cautionary tales in Kanner’s follow-ups about what could happen to a child who ended up in custodial care. Elaine C. did well for a few years in private school. Her father reported “rather amazing changes,” describing her as a “tall, husky girl with clear eyes” who “speaks well on almost any subject,” drawing from a “range of information” that was “really quite wide” with an “almost infallible” memory. But he was still unnerved by her “rambling” conversations (“frequently with an amusing point”), her “odd intonation,” and her lack of “proper emphases” in speech, so he committed her to the Letchworth Village State School for the Epileptic and Feebleminded outside New York City. There, she rapidly declined, becoming “distractible” and “assaultive,” and speaking in an “irrational manner with a flat affect.” She ran through the wards naked, growling like an animal and banging her head against the walls.

但坎纳的后续研究中也包含了一些警示故事，讲述了被送入监护机构的儿童可能遭遇的境况。伊莱恩·C 在私立学校度过了几年不错的时光。她的父亲报告了"相当惊人的变化"，形容她是一个"高大健壮、眼神清澈的女孩"，能"就几乎任何话题侃侃而谈"，其"信息储备"之"广博"令人惊叹，记忆力"几乎无可挑剔"。但他仍对她"漫无边际"的谈话（"常带着有趣的见解"）、"古怪的语调"以及说话时缺乏"恰当的重音"感到不安，于是将她送进了纽约郊外的莱奇沃思村癫痫与低能者州立学校。在那里，她迅速退化，变得"注意力涣散"且"具有攻击性"，说话"语无伦次，情感平淡"。她赤身裸体地跑过病房，像动物一样咆哮，用头撞墙。



Though Letchworth was promoted to families as a progressive and humane institution, behind its ivy-covered façade it was Bedlam for children. By the 1950s, when Elaine was placed there, four thousand boys and girls were crowded into joyless dormitories built for twelve hundred patients. A photograph of the residents dressed up for a Christmas play looks like a macabre tableau from the art of Edward Gorey. The gruesome conditions there were finally exposed to the public in the same TV broadcast by Geraldo Rivera in 1972 that revealed equally appalling conditions at Willowbrook, a state-run institution on Staten Island. After a public outcry, both facilities were shut down. For Elaine, it was too late. She lasted six months at Letchworth before being transferred to the Hudson Valley State Hospital, where she was fed a stew of tranquilizers, antipsychotics, and other drugs. The staff described her at age thirty-nine as unable to “participate in conversation except for the immediate needs.”

尽管莱奇沃思被宣传为进步和人道的机构，但其常春藤覆盖的外表下，对儿童而言却如同疯人院。到 20 世纪 50 年代伊莱恩被安置在那里时，四千名男女儿童挤在原本为 1200 名患者建造的阴郁宿舍里。一张居民们为圣诞剧盛装打扮的照片，看起来就像爱德华·戈里艺术中令人毛骨悚然的场景。1972 年，杰拉尔多·里维拉在同一电视报道中揭露了那里骇人听闻的状况，同时也曝光了史坦顿岛州立机构威洛布鲁克同样令人震惊的条件。公众抗议后，这两所机构均被关闭。但对伊莱恩来说，为时已晚。她在莱奇沃思待了六个月，随后被转至哈德逊河谷州立医院，在那里她被喂食了镇静剂、抗精神病药和其他药物的混合物。工作人员描述 39 岁的她"除了即时需求外，无法参与对话"。



A similarly tragic fate befell Virginia S., the tidy eleven-year-old in Kanner’s original group. By 1970, she too had been confined to a state hospital—a former home for tuberculosis patients in Maryland—where she was warehoused on a ward for “adult retardates.” The staff there reported that she could tell time and “care for her basic needs, but has to be told to do so.” At least her caretakers no longer assumed that she was deaf: she could clearly understand what was being said to her and used “noises and gestures” to communicate. At forty, she spent her days assembling jigsaw puzzles, as she had done as a little girl. The staff noted that she chose “to keep to herself rather than associate with the other residents.”

同样悲惨的命运降临到了弗吉尼亚·S 身上，她是坎纳最初研究组中那个整洁的十一岁女孩。到 1970 年，她也被送进了一家州立医院——马里兰州一家曾用于收治结核病患者的旧院——被安置在"成年智障者"病房。工作人员报告说，她能认时间，"能照顾自己的基本需求，但需要别人提醒她去做"。至少她的看护者不再认为她是聋人：她能清楚理解别人对她说的话，并用"声音和手势"进行交流。四十岁时，她像小时候一样，整天拼着拼图。工作人员注意到，她选择"独处，而不与其他居民交往"。



“One cannot help but gain the impression that State Hospital admission was tantamount to a life sentence,” Kanner reasonably concluded. Even the precocious skills and abilities of his former patients withered in such settings.

"人们不禁会得出这样的印象：进入州立医院无异于被判了无期徒刑，"坎纳合理地总结道。就连他以前那些患者早熟的技能和能力，在这样的环境中也枯萎了。



Richard M., Barbara K., Virginia S., and Charles N. (Cases 3, 5, 6, and 9), who spent most of their lives in institutional care, have all lost their luster early after their admission. Originally fighting for their aloneness and basking in the contentment that it gave them, originally alert to unwelcome changes and, in their own way, struggling for the status quo, originally astounding the observer with their phenomenal feats of memory, they yielded readily to the uninterrupted self-isolation and soon settled down in a life not too remote from a nirvana-like existence. If at all responsive to psychological testing, their IQ’s dropped down to figures usually referred to as low-grade moron or imbecile.

理查德·M.、芭芭拉·K.、弗吉尼亚·S.和查尔斯·N.（案例 3、5、6 和 9）一生大部分时间都在机构照料中度过，入院后不久便都失去了往日的光彩。他们最初为独处而抗争，并沉浸于独处带来的满足感中；最初对不受欢迎的变化保持警觉，并以自己的方式为维持现状而挣扎；最初以其惊人的记忆力令观察者叹服——然而，他们轻易地屈服于不间断的自我隔离，很快便安顿在一种近乎涅槃般的生活中。即便对心理测试有所反应，他们的智商也降至通常被称为低能或愚鲁的数值。



The dramatic differences in the life courses of his patients finally led Kanner to question his belief that his syndrome was narrowly defined and monolithic. “It is well known in medicine that any illness may appear in different degrees of severity, all the way from the so-called forme fruste to the most fulminant manifestation,” he wrote in 1971. “Does this possibly apply also to early infantile autism?”

患者人生轨迹的戏剧性差异最终使坎纳开始质疑自己原先的信念——即他所定义的综合征是狭隘且单一的。他在 1971 年写道："医学界众所周知，任何疾病都可能以不同的严重程度出现，从所谓的顿挫型到最暴发性的表现。这种情况是否也可能适用于早期婴儿孤独症？"



—



IT WAS A QUESTION Georg Frankl could have answered in the affirmative in 1938. But Kanner seemed resistant to ceding an inch of his authority to his Viennese counterparts, even if it meant consigning his former assistant to historical oblivion. When Kanner became the editor of a new quarterly called the Journal of Autism and Childhood Schizophrenia in 1971, the premiere issue featured an article by Dutch psychiatrist Dirk Arn Van Krevelen that reaffirmed his myth of serendipity:

1938 年，乔治·弗兰克尔本可以对此问题给出肯定回答。但坎纳似乎不愿向维也纳同行让渡丝毫权威，即便这意味着将他的前助手埋没于历史尘埃。1971 年，当坎纳成为新创刊的《孤独症与儿童精神分裂症杂志》主编时，创刊号刊登了荷兰精神病学家迪克·阿恩·范·克雷维伦的文章，该文再次强化了坎纳的机缘巧合神话：



New discoveries are period-bound rather than time-bound; they often emerge at the same time in different geographic sections. The history of autism offers a striking example. Kanner in Baltimore published his paper on inborn disturbances of effective contact in 1943, referring to a group of patients which had come to his attention during the preceding 5 years. One year later, the Viennese pediatrician Asperger reported a number of children as autistic psychopaths. We can take it for granted that neither was then aware of the other’s work.

新发现往往受限于时代而非时间；它们常在不同地理区域同时涌现。孤独症的历史便是一个显著例证。巴尔的摩的坎纳于 1943 年发表关于先天性有效接触障碍的论文，研究对象是此前五年间引起他注意的一组患者。一年后，维也纳儿科医生阿斯伯格将一批儿童报告为孤独谱系精神病态者。我们可以确信，当时两人均不知晓对方的研究成果。



A few months later, Kanner mentioned Asperger’s name in print for the first and last time, in a magisterially disdainful review of a book called The Autistic Child by pediatrician Isaac Newton Kugelmass. For daring to credit Asperger (misspelled “Ansperger”) for independently confirming Kanner’s discovery, Kugelmass reaped a whirlwind from Baltimore. Calling the book a “laborious enterprise,” Kanner dispatched the potential competition in a single withering sentence cast in the third person:

几个月后，坎纳首次也是最后一次在出版物中提及阿斯伯格的名字，那是在一篇对儿科医生艾萨克·牛顿·库格尔马斯所著《孤独症儿童》一书充满权威蔑视的书评中。库格尔马斯因胆敢将阿斯伯格（拼写为"Ansperger"）视为独立证实坎纳发现的贡献者，而招致巴尔的摩的猛烈抨击。坎纳称该书为"费力的工程"，并以第三人称用一句尖刻的句子击溃了这位潜在竞争者：



The name is Asperger, and the man, at that time, could have no knowledge of Kanner’s publication; instead, he independently described what he called “autistic psychopathy,” which, if at all related to infantile autism, is at best a 42nd cousin which merits, and has received, serious attention from investigators.

此人名为阿斯伯格，当时他不可能知晓坎纳的著作；相反，他独立描述了自己所谓的"孤独谱系精神病态"，即便与婴儿孤独症有关，充其量也只是远房表亲，值得且已获得研究者的严肃关注。



In fact, Asperger’s work was still virtually unknown in America, primarily because Kanner never mentioned it in his papers and lectures. Because the two men’s conceptions of autism were so different, there was much more at stake than the usual inside-baseball priority dispute. From the top of his pyramid at the Harriet Lane, Kanner declared in 1957 that he had seen only 150 true cases of autism in his entire career, or eight patients a year, while fielding referrals from as far away as South Africa. He also told researcher Bernard Rimland that he turned away nine out of ten children referred to his office as “autistic” by other clinicians without an autism diagnosis.

事实上，阿斯伯格的研究在美国几乎仍无人知晓，主要是因为坎纳从未在论文或讲座中提及过它。由于两人对孤独症的理解截然不同，这远非寻常的学术优先权之争那么简单。1957 年，坎纳在哈丽特·莱恩医院金字塔顶端宣称，他整个职业生涯仅见过 150 例真正的孤独症病例，相当于每年接诊八名患者，而转诊来源远至南非。他还告诉研究员伯纳德·里姆兰，其他临床医生转诊到他办公室的儿童中，每十人就有九人被他以"非孤独症"为由拒诊。



In real-world terms, being locked out of a diagnosis often meant being denied access to an education, speech and occupational therapy, counseling, medication, and other forms of support. For undiagnosed adults, Kanner’s insistence that autism was a disorder of early infancy meant decades of wandering in the wilderness, with no explanation for constant struggles in employment, dating, friendships, and simply navigating the chaos of daily life.

在现实层面，被排除在诊断之外往往意味着无法获得教育、言语治疗、职业治疗、心理咨询、药物及其他形式的支持。对于未被确诊的成年人而言，坎纳坚持孤独症是婴幼儿期障碍的观点，导致他们数十年如置身荒野——在就业、约会、友谊乃至应对日常生活的混乱中持续挣扎，却始终找不到解释。



While the psychiatric establishment was debating theories of toxic parenting and childhood psychosis, however, Asperger’s lost tribe was putting its autistic intelligence to work by building the foundations of a society better suited to its needs and interests. Like Henry Cavendish, they refused to accept their circumstances as given. By coming up with ways of socializing on their own terms, they sketched out a blueprint for the modern networked world.

然而，当精神病学界的权威们还在争论"有毒教养"与"童年精神病"的理论时，阿斯伯格综合征的失落部落早已将孤独谱系的智慧付诸实践——他们正在构建一个更契合自身需求与兴趣的社会根基。如同亨利·卡文迪许一般，他们拒绝被动接受既定的生存境遇。通过开创符合自身特质的社交方式，他们为现代网络世界绘制了最初的蓝图。







---


NeuroTribes








Six

六



PRINCES OF THE AIR

空中王子



Write me a creature that thinks as well as a man, or better than a man, but not like a man.

请为我创造一个思考能力与人类相当、甚至超越人类，但思维方式却与人类截然不同的生物。



—JOHN W. CAMPBELL

——约翰·W·坎贝尔



The curious fascination that many autistic people have for quantifiable data, highly organized systems, and complex machines runs like a half-hidden thread through the fabric of autism research—from Asperger’s teenage scientist stealing chemicals for his home experiments, to Donald T.’s preoccupation with measurement, to A.D.’s habit of calculating the number of people attending a theatrical performance. Asperger may have been the first clinician to notice that his patients’ imaginations occasionally anticipated developments in science by decades, forcing him to amend his statement that the interests of his little professors were “remote” from real-world concerns. But his joking suggestion that the designers of spaceships themselves must be autistic also turned out to be prescient.

许多孤独谱系群体对可量化数据、高度组织化系统和复杂机器怀有奇特迷恋，这条若隐若现的线索贯穿孤独症研究史——从阿斯伯格笔下偷取化学试剂做家庭实验的少年科学家，到唐纳德·T 对测量的执念，再到 A.D.计算剧场观众人数的习惯。阿斯伯格或许是首位注意到患者想象力偶尔会超前科学数十年的临床医生，这迫使他修正了关于"小教授们"的兴趣"脱离"现实世界的论断。而他戏谑地提出宇宙飞船设计师本身必定属于孤独谱系的观点，后来也被证实具有先见之明。



Tommy the Space Child was not the only member of Asperger’s forgotten tribe to turn his youthful obsession with science fiction into a career in science. For many people on the spectrum in the years when they were still invisible to medicine, science fiction fandom provided a community where they finally felt like savvy natives after years of being bullied and abused by their peers for seeming naïve, awkward, and clueless. Another community that enabled autistic people to make the most of their natural strengths in the early and mid-twentieth century was amateur radio. By routing around the face-to-face interactions they found so daunting, even people who found it nearly impossible to communicate through speech were able to reach out to kindred spirits, find potential mentors, and gain the skills and confidence they needed to become productive members of society.

太空小子汤米并非阿斯伯格遗忘部落中唯一一个将对科幻小说的年少痴迷转化为科学事业的成员。在那些仍被医学界忽视的岁月里，许多谱系人士在科幻迷社群中找到了归属——这个群体让他们在被同龄人因天真、笨拙和懵懂而欺凌多年后，终于感觉自己成了游刃有余的原住民。二十世纪早期至中期，另一个帮助孤独谱系群体充分发挥天赋的社群是业余无线电爱好者。通过绕开令他们望而生畏的面对面交流，即便是那些几乎无法用言语沟通的人，也能与志同道合者建立联系、寻找潜在导师，并掌握成为社会有用之才所需的技能与自信。



Amazingly, both of these communities were launched by the same man who was likely on the autism spectrum himself: a visionary entrepreneur named Hugo Gernsback, who foresaw the decentralized, intimately interconnected nature of twenty-first-century society before nearly anyone else with the help of his equally eccentric friend, the prolific inventor Nikola Tesla. Along the way, Gernsback and Tesla anticipated the development of television, online news, computerized dating services, videophones, and many other conveniences that we take for granted a century later.

令人惊叹的是，这两个社群竟由同一个人创立，而他本人很可能也处于孤独谱系——这位富有远见的企业家名叫雨果·根斯巴克，在同样特立独行的好友、多产发明家尼古拉·特斯拉的协助下，他几乎比任何人都更早预见到 21 世纪社会去中心化、紧密互联的本质。在此过程中，根斯巴克与特斯拉还预见了电视、网络新闻、电脑婚恋服务、可视电话等众多便利技术的发展——这些在一个世纪后已成为我们习以为常的事物。



Born Hugo Gernsbacher in 1884, Gernsback was the son of a Jewish wine merchant in Luxembourg. He became fascinated by electricity on his eighth birthday when the handyman on his father’s estate made him a gift of an electric bell, a wet-cell battery, and a length of wire. When he hooked up the wire to the electrodes of the battery, the bell rang amid a shower of sparks; he was immediately hooked. Young Hugo sent away to Paris for some lightbulbs and battery-powered telephones and wired the family house for electricity. He also started working to improve battery design by developing dry cells with solid electrolyte cores, which had the virtue of being portable, because they didn’t contain corrosive liquid that could slosh and spill. Though he was still in grade school, he had already pinpointed one of the factors holding back the widespread adoption of portable electronic devices.

雨果·根斯巴克出生于 1884 年，是卢森堡一位犹太酒商的儿子。八岁生日那天，父亲庄园的杂役送给他一个电铃、一组湿电池和一段电线，从此他对电产生了痴迷。当他把电线连接到电池电极上时，电铃在四溅的火花中响起，他立刻被迷住了。小雨果从巴黎订购了灯泡和电池驱动的电话，并为整栋房子铺设了电线。他还开始研究改进电池设计，开发出带有固体电解质芯的干电池，这种电池便于携带，因为不含可能晃动泄漏的腐蚀性液体。尽管还在上小学，他已经找到了阻碍便携式电子设备普及的关键因素之一。



Two years later, while attending technical classes at the Ecole Industrielle, he had another life-changing experience: reading a translation of a book by the American astronomer Percival Lowell called Mars as the Abode of Life. A provocative fusion of planetology and evolutionary theory, the book was illustrated with the eminent astronomer’s own sketches. Lowell risked the ridicule of his colleagues by venturing that traces of water would someday be discovered on our rust-colored neighbor in the solar system (a prediction confirmed in 2009 by the Phoenix Mars Lander). He further theorized that intelligent life had arisen there, and that the extreme conditions on the planet—its vast Saharas of dust with seasonal concentrations of ice at the poles—had practically required the natives to develop a sophisticated system of aqueducts controlled by a global data infrastructure to provide them with drinkable water year-round.

两年后，他在工业技术学校参加技术课程时，又经历了一次改变人生的体验：阅读了美国天文学家珀西瓦尔·洛厄尔所著《火星：生命之居所》的译本。这本融合行星学与进化论的挑衅性著作，配有这位著名天文学家的亲笔素描。洛厄尔冒着被同行嘲笑的风险，大胆预言有朝一日人类将在太阳系这颗锈红色的邻居星球上发现水的痕迹（这一预测在 2009 年由凤凰号火星着陆器证实）。他进一步提出理论，认为那里曾存在智慧生命，而该星球的极端环境——广袤的撒哈拉式沙尘地带，两极季节性冰层聚集——几乎迫使原住民开发出由全球数据基础设施控制的精密水渠系统，以全年提供可饮用水。



Lowell speculated that the cross-hatching of lines on the planet’s surface first spotted by Giovanni Schiaparelli in 1877 (which looked “to have been laid down by rule and compass,” as the Italian astronomer put it) was an intricate system of canals connecting a network of artificial oases where the inhabitants of the planet had sought shelter from the desiccating Martian winds. He then declared that these crafty creatures were likely “of an order whose acquaintance was worth the making”—if we could develop the technological know-how to communicate with them. The book had a decisive, even devastating impact on the future entrepreneur. As historian Sam Moskowitz put it:

洛厄尔推测，1877 年乔瓦尼·斯基亚帕雷利首次发现的火星表面交叉线条（正如这位意大利天文学家所言，这些线条"仿佛是用尺规绘制而成"）是一个复杂的运河系统，连接着由人工绿洲构成的网络——火星居民曾在那里躲避干燥的风沙。他继而宣称，这些灵巧的生物很可能"属于值得结交的物种"——前提是我们能掌握与之沟通的技术。这本书对未来企业家产生了决定性甚至毁灭性的影响。正如历史学家萨姆·莫斯科维茨所言：



The concept that intelligent life might exist on other worlds had never occurred to young Hugo . . . he lapsed into delirium, raving about strange creatures, fantastic cities, and masterly engineered canals of Mars for two full days and nights while the doctor remained in almost constant attendance. The direction of Hugo Gernsback’s future thinking was greatly conditioned by that experience. He was never to be content with the accumulated scientific knowledge of his day. Now he was to search the libraries for books that opened up imaginative vistas beyond the scientific knowledge of the period.

年轻时的雨果从未想过其他世界可能存在智慧生命……他陷入谵妄状态，连续两天两夜胡言乱语着奇异生物、奇幻城市和火星上精妙绝伦的运河，医生几乎寸步不离地守候在旁。这次经历极大地影响了雨果·根斯巴克未来的思维方向。他从此不再满足于当时积累的科学知识，转而开始在图书馆中搜寻那些能超越时代科学认知、展现想象图景的书籍。



While refining his battery designs, Gernsbacher immersed himself in gripping adventure tales by Jules Verne and H. G. Wells. At thirteen, the precocious boy installed an intercom system in a local Carmelite convent. Such an amenity was unheard-of in most private homes at the time, much less in nunneries; he was awarded a special dispensation from Pope Leo XIII to visit the sisters once a year so he could keep his system in good working order, along with a certificate from the Mother Superior praising him as a “budding electrician.”

在完善电池设计的同时，根斯巴克沉浸于儒勒·凡尔纳和 H.G.威尔斯扣人心弦的冒险故事中。十三岁时，这个早熟的男孩在当地的加尔默罗修女院安装了一套内部通话系统。这种便利设施在当时大多数私人住宅中都闻所未闻，更遑论修道院了；他获得了教皇利奥十三世的特别许可，每年可探访修女们一次以维护系统正常运行，同时还收到了院长嬷嬷颁发的证书，称赞他为"崭露头角的电工"。



Despite this early recognition from his elders, Gernsbacher felt like an outsider in society. At seventeen, he wrote a sixty-thousand-word novel called Ein Pechvogel (basically, “A Jinxed Person” or “A Schlemiel”) about a hapless, unworldly boy whose obsessive tinkering—which included an attempt to use solar energy to roast coffee beans—constantly got him in trouble.

尽管早年得到了长辈们的认可，根斯巴赫却感觉自己像个社会边缘人。十七岁时，他写了一部六万字的小说《倒霉蛋》（Ein Pechvogel，字面意为"被厄运缠身的人"或"倒霉鬼"），讲述一个笨拙不谙世事的男孩，因痴迷于各种小发明——包括尝试用太阳能烘烤咖啡豆——而不断惹上麻烦的故事。



But he also learned in a very dramatic way that he could employ his specialized knowledge to get out of trouble. One icy-cold winter’s day when his parents were on vacation, he was exploring an empty cellar when a gust of wind blew the door shut behind him. The only window in the cellar was open but barred from the outside, putting him in danger of freezing to death. Fortunately, he had brought along a lantern powered by two dry-cell batteries. He extracted a thin copper wire from the lantern and used it to short-circuit the cells, making the wire white-hot. He then touched it to a piece of paper, which burst into flames. Then he used the smoldering paper to start a fire of scrap wood and burned down the cellar door so he could make his escape. Science!

但他也以一种戏剧性的方式认识到，自己可以利用专业知识摆脱困境。一个天寒地冻的冬日，父母外出度假时，他正在探索一个空置的地窖，一阵风突然将身后的门吹得关上了。地窖里唯一的窗户虽然开着，但外面装有铁栅栏，他面临冻死的危险。幸运的是，他随身带了一盏用两节干电池供电的提灯。他从提灯里抽出一根细铜线，用它短路电池，使铜线变得白炽。接着他将铜线触碰纸张，纸张瞬间燃起火焰。随后他用冒烟的纸引燃废木料堆，烧毁了地窖门，成功脱身。科学的力量！



After Gernsbacher’s father died in 1903, the quaint charms of old Luxembourg couldn’t hold him for very long. He borrowed $100 from the family fortune and boarded a steamship from Hamburg to Hoboken, drawn to the United States by the wit of Mark Twain, the martial music of John Philip Sousa, and the notion that America was a place where an industrious young inventor could reinvent himself. Upon disembarking, he spent $20 of the $100 in his wallet on a silk hat so he would look appropriately distinguished and ordered a stack of business cards billing himself as “Huck” Gernsback after the hero of Twain’s picaresque odyssey down the Mississippi River. To obtain parts for his battery business, he launched a venture called the Electro Importing Company, the first mail-order supplier for home electronics buffs in the country. At age nineteen, he was already managing two startups.

1903 年根斯巴克的父亲去世后，古老卢森堡的质朴魅力已无法留住他太久。他从家族遗产中借了 100 美元，登上从汉堡驶往霍博肯的蒸汽船，被马克·吐温的机智、约翰·菲利普·苏萨的军乐，以及"美国是勤勉年轻发明家重塑自我的地方"这一理念所吸引，踏上了前往美国的旅程。下船后，他用钱包里 100 美元中的 20 美元买了一顶丝绸礼帽，让自己显得体面不凡，又定制了一叠名片，自称"哈克"·根斯巴克——这个绰号取自吐温笔下密西西比河冒险小说中主角的名字。为了给自己的电池生意采购零件，他创办了"电子进口公司"，这是美国首家面向家庭电子爱好者的邮购供应商。十九岁时，他已同时经营着两家初创企业。



In addition to his technical prowess, Gernsback was also a genius at marketing. Instead of dumping the contents of Electro Importing’s vast catalog onto the market as a jumble of geeky gadgets, Gernsback framed them as hip accessories for a twentieth-century lifestyle based on scientific discovery and excitement. “This machine will give you more amusement than anything you have ever had,” promised an ad for an electrostatic generator. “Charges leyden jars, fires powder, works Wireless Stations, raises a person’s hair, etc.” This kind of branding proved to be catnip for nerdy outcasts, who became heroic young “experimenters” in the pages of his catalogs.

除了技术上的卓越才能，根斯巴克还是一位营销天才。他没有将《电子进口公司》庞大目录中的内容作为一堆古怪的小玩意儿推向市场，而是将它们包装成基于科学发现与刺激的 20 世纪生活方式中的时尚配件。“这台机器将带给你前所未有的乐趣，”一则静电发电机的广告承诺道，“它能给莱顿瓶充电、引燃火药、驱动无线电台、让人的头发竖起来等等。”这种品牌策略对书呆子式的边缘人群来说如同猫薄荷，他们在其目录中化身为英勇的年轻“实验者”。



Opening a retail store at the bustling intersection of Wall Street and Broadway, Gernsback displayed a precocious knack for salesmanship by offering ten-cent crystal detectors that could pick up any radio signals in the area. Soon he was selling a thousand a day and could barely keep up with the demand.

在华尔街与百老汇交汇的繁华路口开设零售店后，根斯巴克展现出早熟的销售天赋——他推出售价十美分的晶体探测器，能接收该区域任何无线电信号。很快他每天能售出一千台，几乎供不应求。



These simple semiconductor devices were only an enticement—a “come-along”—for the real product: the first radio transmitter and receiver kit designed for amateurs, the Telimco Wireless Telegraph. (A Telimco now resides at the Henry Ford Museum in Dearborn, Michigan.) The company reps, dressed dapperly in high, stiff collars and derby hats, were so adept at getting young stockbrokers on their lunch breaks excited about wireless—price for the whole outfit, $7.50, instead of the $50,000 cost of a standard commercial rig—that the mayor of New York banned the exhibitions that drew huge crowds to the shop. A policeman burst into Gernsback’s office on Park Place one day to investigate a complaint that his company was flogging a device that couldn’t possibly work at the advertised price. A simple demonstration was enough to avert legal action, but the cop remained skeptical. “I still think you’re fakers,” he snarled, glancing suspiciously around the room. “Your ad says it’s a wireless set. Then what are all these wires for?”

这些简单的半导体器件只是诱饵——一种“引子”——真正的产品是首个为业余爱好者设计的无线电发射与接收套件：Telimco 无线电报机。（如今，一台 Telimco 陈列在密歇根州迪尔伯恩的亨利·福特博物馆。）公司代表们身着高挺硬领和圆顶礼帽，打扮得十分时髦，他们擅长在年轻股票经纪人午休时激发他们对无线技术的兴趣——整套设备售价仅 7.5 美元，而标准商用设备要花费 5 万美元——以至于纽约市长下令禁止这些吸引大批人群涌入商店的展览。一天，一名警察冲进根斯巴克位于公园广场的办公室，调查一项投诉：他的公司正在推销一款不可能以广告价格正常工作的设备。一次简单的演示足以避免法律诉讼，但那名警察仍持怀疑态度。“我还是觉得你们是骗子，”他咆哮道，狐疑地扫视着房间，“你们的广告说这是无线设备。那这些电线是干什么用的？”



The first version of the Telimco was relatively primitive, though it was still the most advanced radio available to amateurs at the time. It enabled an amateur radio operator (commonly known as a “ham”) to transmit and receive snippets of Morse code (no voice signals yet) over a range of a mile. But the notion of communicating at a distance, with no visible connection, was so magical that Gernsback’s kits flew off the shelves, not only at his shop but also at department stores like Macy’s, Gimbels, and Marshall Field’s. “We feel sure,” he exulted in an early catalog, “that every wide-awake American boy and every young man will feel the necessity of procuring one of these outfits, because he fully realizes that wireless telegraphy will play a very important role in the business world in the immediate future.” This future was getting more immediate every day, Gernsback believed, precisely because amateur demand was now helping to drive the development of the technology. Within a year, the Telimco would transmit and receive voice signals as well as dots and dashes.

Telimco 的第一个版本相对原始，但仍是当时业余爱好者能获得的最先进的无线电设备。它能让业余无线电操作员（俗称"火腿族"）在一英里范围内收发摩尔斯电码片段（尚不能传输语音信号）。但无需可见连接即可远距离通信的概念如此神奇，以至于根斯巴克的套件不仅在他的商店里被抢购一空，在梅西百货、金贝尔百货和马歇尔·菲尔德百货等商场也销售一空。"我们确信，"他在早期目录中欣喜地写道，"每个机灵的美国男孩和年轻人都将感到有必要购买一套这样的设备，因为他们完全意识到无线电报在不久的将来将在商业世界中扮演非常重要的角色。"根斯巴克认为，这个未来正日益临近，正是因为业余爱好者的需求正在推动技术的发展。一年之内，Telimco 不仅能收发点和划，还能传输语音信号。



As Gernsback became wealthy, he cultivated the air of a bon vivant, packaging himself as adroitly as he packaged his crystal sets by dressing in bespoke suits and silk ties. But he inevitably struck people as odd, rude, self-centered, and even callous. On train trips to Chicago to pick up parts for his company, he would stop off in Cleveland to visit his seven-year-old cousin, Hildegarde. The entrepreneur would terrify the girl by launching into windy soliloquies about a society in which domed cities in orbit, robot doctors, and retirement colonies on Mars were commonplace. (Meanwhile, horse-drawn carts were still plying the streets outside.) If a ringing telephone interrupted him in midreverie, he would raise an admonishing finger and say to his cousin in his bristling Germanic accent, “Hildegarde, fix your hair. It won’t be long before the caller can see your face over the wires.”

随着根斯巴克变得富有，他培养出一种享乐主义者的气质，像精心包装他的矿石收音机一样，通过定制西装和丝绸领带精心塑造自己的形象。但他不可避免地给人留下古怪、粗鲁、以自我为中心甚至冷漠的印象。在乘火车前往芝加哥为公司采购零件的途中，他会在克利夫兰停留，探望他七岁的表妹希尔德加德。这位企业家会滔滔不绝地独白一个轨道穹顶城市、机器人医生和火星退休殖民地司空见惯的社会，把小女孩吓得够呛。（而此时，窗外的街道上仍有马车穿梭。）如果电话铃声打断了他的遐想，他会竖起一根手指警告，用他那刺耳的德国口音对表妹说：“希尔德加德，整理好你的头发。用不了多久，打电话的人就能通过电线看到你的脸了。”



II



The incident with the skeptical policeman stuck in Gernsback’s craw for a long time. “It rankled me that there could be such ignorance in regard to science,” he told an audience of hams and engineers in Michigan fifty years later. “I vowed to change the situation if I could.” He came up with a plan to educate the next generation of scientists in a way that would also give him a powerful vehicle for promoting his business. He would launch the first magazine for ham radio operators.

那位持怀疑态度的警察事件让根斯巴克耿耿于怀了很长时间。“科学领域竟存在如此无知的现象，这让我深感愤懑，”五十年后，他在密歇根州对一群业余无线电爱好者和工程师说道，“我发誓要尽己所能改变这种状况。”他构思了一个计划，旨在以既能教育下一代科学家，又能为自己提供强大商业推广平台的方式付诸实施——他要创办第一本面向业余无线电操作员的杂志。



To open the bright red and orange cover of Modern Electrics, which appeared on newsstands in 1908, was to enter a world where the marvels of the future could be soldered together in a garage from off-the-shelf parts (available, of course, from the Electro Importing Company). More staid publications like Scientific American targeted scientists and inventors by running news blips from the U.S. Patent Office, but Gernsback pitched his magazine to a much broader readership of aspiring boy geniuses and weekend tinkerers. Its motto—“The Electrical Magazine for Everybody”—anticipated Apple’s populist tagline for the Macintosh, “Computing for the rest of us,” by eighty years. Like Steve Jobs, Gernsback didn’t just dominate markets; he invented them.

翻开 1908 年出现在报摊上的《现代电气》杂志那鲜红与橙黄相间的封面，便踏入了一个未来奇迹可在车库里用现成零件（当然，可从电气进口公司购得）焊接而成的世界。像《科学美国人》这类更为稳重的刊物，通过刊登美国专利局的新闻简讯来瞄准科学家和发明家，但根斯巴克却将他的杂志定位给更广泛的读者群——那些胸怀大志的少年天才和周末动手爱好者。其标语——“人人皆宜的电气杂志”——比苹果公司为麦金塔电脑推出的民粹主义口号“为其余的人而造的计算”早了八十年。如同史蒂夫·乔布斯，根斯巴克不仅主导市场，更创造了市场。



With a curious amalgam of whiz-bang enthusiasm and mitteleuropäische sophistication, Modern Electrics embraced a wide range of innovations beyond amateur radio, featuring articles, editorials, and special issues on airships, electronic photography, radiotelegraphy, model railroading, and a proto-Internet scheme for “typewriting by wire.”

《现代电气》融合了令人惊叹的热情与中欧式的精致，其涉猎范围远超业余无线电领域，涵盖了飞艇、电子摄影、无线电报、模型铁路，以及一种名为“有线打字”的原始互联网方案，并为此刊登文章、社论和专刊。



The December 1909 issue was devoted to a technology then still in its experimental infancy: television. Gernsback’s international network of correspondents also tackled such far-out subjects as the potential for harnessing tides and sunlight as limitless sources of power, and investigating whether radio signals affected the navigational abilities of homing pigeons.

1909 年 12 月刊聚焦于一项当时仍处于实验初期的技术：电视。根斯巴克国际通讯员网络还探讨了诸如利用潮汐和阳光作为无限能源的潜力，以及研究无线电信号是否影响信鸽导航能力等前沿课题。



The magazine also ran a monthly photo contest for the subscriber who built the coolest wireless rig, making membership in a clued-in community part of the “product” that Gernsback was selling. It was the perfect approach for the kind of reader who was likely to be tinkering with electrostatic generators and Leyden jars alone in his basement rather than out carousing with his friends.

该杂志还每月举办摄影比赛，奖励搭建出最酷无线电装置的订阅者，使成为知情社群的一员成为根斯巴克所售"产品"的一部分。这正适合那些宁愿独自在地下室摆弄静电发电机和莱顿瓶，也不愿外出与朋友狂欢的读者。



—



THE FOLLOWING APRIL, Gernsback took the magazine in a bold new direction—from merely speculating about the technology of the future to imagining it out of whole cloth. The cryptic string of characters on the cover—Ralph 124C 41+—marked the editor in chief’s debut as a novelist. Standing on the shoulders of his heroes Wells and Verne, he inaugurated a genre of popular storytelling that blended hard science and speculative fiction, with a strong emphasis on gadgetry. He christened this genre “scientifiction,” even taking out a patent on the awkward term, which was quickly superseded by “science fiction.”

接下来的四月，根斯巴克带领杂志走向了一个大胆的新方向——从单纯推测未来技术，转向完全凭空想象。封面上那串神秘的字符——Ralph 124C 41+——标志着这位主编作为小说家的首次亮相。站在他崇拜的威尔斯和凡尔纳的肩膀上，他开创了一种将硬科学、思辨小说与强烈强调新奇装置相结合的流行叙事体裁。他将这种体裁命名为"科学传奇"，甚至为这个拗口的术语申请了专利，但很快就被"科幻小说"所取代。



Ralph 124C 41+ anticipated a broad swath of technological marvels, including TV, radar, fluorescent lighting, stainless steel, videophones, night baseball games, speech-to-text software, and continuously updated news. (It also predicted the development of aspects of the future that haven’t arrived yet, including wireless power transmission, a “Menograph” to transcribe thoughts, and electronic weather control.) Its wonky title was Gernsback’s orthographic pun on his idea of the author of scientifiction as a cultural prophet: “One to foresee for more than one.”

《Ralph 124C 41+》预见了众多技术奇迹，包括电视、雷达、荧光灯、不锈钢、可视电话、夜间棒球赛、语音转文字软件以及持续更新的新闻。（它还预测了未来尚未实现的某些方面，如无线电力传输、用于转录思想的"思维记录仪"和电子天气控制。）这个古怪的书名是根斯巴克对"科学传奇"作者作为文化先知这一理念的文字游戏："一人预见，惠及众人"。



If his prophecies were unusually accurate, it was because he befriended someone already living in the future: Nikola Tesla, the brilliant Serbian inventor whose wireless experiments preceded those of the “father of radio,” Guglielmo Marconi. A former lab assistant of Thomas Edison’s, Tesla did trailblazing research in an astonishing array of fields, including robotics, home lighting, X-rays, proto-transistors, remote control, and alternating current. Tesla even predicted the chilling face of twenty-first-century warfare—semiautonomous drones, which he called Telautomata.

如果他的预言异常准确，那是因为他与一位来自未来的人结为挚友：尼古拉·特斯拉，这位才华横溢的塞尔维亚发明家，其无线实验早于“无线电之父”古列尔莫·马可尼。作为托马斯·爱迪生前实验室助理的特斯拉，在机器人技术、家庭照明、X 射线、原始晶体管、远程控制及交流电等众多领域进行了开创性研究。他甚至预言了 21 世纪战争令人不寒而栗的面貌——半自主无人机，他称之为“远程自动机”。



“When wireless is perfectly applied, the whole earth will be converted into a huge brain,” Tesla told an interviewer in 1926. “We shall be able to communicate with one another instantly, irrespective of distance. Not only this, but through television and telephony we shall see and hear one another as perfectly as though we were face to face, despite intervening distances of thousands of miles; and the instruments through which we shall be able to do this will be amazingly simple compared with our present telephone. A man will be able to carry one in his vest pocket. We shall be able to witness and hear events—the inauguration of a President, the playing of a World Series game, the havoc of an earthquake or the terror of a battle—just as though we were present.” Gernsback, who was twenty-eight years younger, became Tesla’s most prominent advocate. The first theme issue of Modern Electrics was wholly devoted to his work.

“当无线技术得到完美应用时，整个地球将变成一个巨大的大脑，”特斯拉在 1926 年接受采访时说道。“无论距离多远，我们都能即时相互沟通。不仅如此，通过电视和电话，即便相隔数千英里，我们也能像面对面一样清晰地看到彼此、听到彼此的声音；而实现这一切的仪器，将比我们现在的电话简单得令人惊叹。人们可以把它放在背心口袋里随身携带。我们将能亲眼目睹并亲耳聆听各种事件——总统就职典礼、世界大赛的盛况、地震的浩劫或战场的恐怖——仿佛身临其境。”比特斯拉年轻二十八岁的根斯巴克，成为了他最坚定的支持者。《现代电气》的首期专题号完全聚焦于他的研究成果。



Whatever Tesla was, the word typical didn’t describe him. Eccentric genius ran in his family: his mother was an expert weaver from a long line of inventors who designed her own sewing tools. His older brother was a child prodigy who died when Tesla spooked the horse he was riding, causing lifelong feelings of guilt. The future inventor suffered from a “peculiar affliction” as a boy that would likely now be diagnosed as epilepsy, marked by visions of “strong flashes of light” and elaborate hallucinations. Like Asperger’s little professors, he could be honest to a fault, as when his two elderly aunts asked him to choose which one was prettier and he replied that one was “not as ugly as the other.” He felt compelled to calculate the precise volume of coffee cups, soup bowls, and morsels of food at the table, and counted the exact number of steps he took when he went out for a walk. (Like Cavendish and Dirac, he developed the habit of taking extended perambulations on a rigid timetable, covering eight to ten miles every day in Manhattan.) As a teenager, Tesla developed rigid habits and aversions, along with a fascination for certain shapes. The mere sight of a pearl made him feel ill, but the glittering of objects with flat surfaces mesmerized him.

无论特斯拉是什么样的人，“典型”这个词都无法形容他。他家族中不乏古怪的天才：他的母亲是一位来自发明世家的编织高手，曾设计过自己的缝纫工具。他的哥哥是个神童，却因特斯拉惊了所骑的马而意外身亡，这让他终生背负愧疚。这位未来的发明家童年时患有一种“奇特病症”，如今很可能被诊断为癫痫，症状包括眼前闪过“强烈的光芒”和逼真的幻觉。像阿斯伯格综合征中的“小教授”一样，他有时诚实得过分——比如当两位年迈的姑妈让他评判谁更漂亮时，他回答其中一位“没有另一位那么丑”。他忍不住要精确计算桌上咖啡杯、汤碗和每口食物的体积，出门散步时还会数清自己走了多少步。（和卡文迪许、狄拉克一样，他养成了严格按固定时间长时间散步的习惯，每天在曼哈顿走上八到十英里。）少年时期，特斯拉形成了刻板的习惯和厌恶感，同时对某些形状产生了痴迷。 仅仅看到珍珠就会让他感到恶心。但那些表面平坦的物体所闪烁的光芒令他着迷。



He embarked on his career as an inventor when he discovered that he could visualize theoretical machines in minute detail and even set them running in his mind, tweaking his design as parts wore out. “I needed no models, drawings, or experiments,” Tesla recalled in his memoir, which was published by Gernsback. “I could picture them all as real . . . It is absolutely immaterial to me whether I run my turbine in thought or test it in my shop. I even note if it is out of balance. There is no difference whatever, the results are the same.” (Temple Grandin’s account of her own design process is virtually identical: “Before I attempt any construction, I test-run the equipment in my imagination. I visualize my designs being used in every possible situation, with different sizes and breeds of cattle and in different weather conditions. Doing this enables me to correct mistakes before construction.”) Together, the inventor and editor forged a mutually beneficial alliance.

当他发现自己能在脑海中细致入微地构想理论机器，甚至让它们在思维中运转，并在零件磨损时调整设计时，他便踏上了发明家之路。"我不需要模型、图纸或实验，"特斯拉在由根斯巴克出版的回忆录中写道，"我能将它们全部想象成真实存在……对我来说，在思维中运转涡轮机还是在车间里测试它，完全无关紧要。我甚至能注意到它是否失衡。两者毫无区别，结果完全相同。"（坦普尔·葛兰汀对自己设计过程的描述几乎如出一辙："在尝试任何建造之前，我会在想象中试运行设备。我设想自己的设计在各种可能的情境中被使用，面对不同体型和品种的牛群，以及不同的天气条件。这让我能在建造前纠正错误。"）这位发明家与编辑共同缔造了互利共赢的联盟。



But as prescient as Gernsback was about technology, spinning out a believable love story was beyond his powers. Throughout Ralph 124C 41+, the eponymous hero (not coincidentally, a reclusive “great American inventor”) and his muse, a Swiss ham radio operator named Alice 212B423, address one another as if they’re reading from technical manuals, complete with an abundance of brand names and calculations carried to several decimal places. “Both the Power mast and the Communico mast were blown down the same day, and I was left without any means of communication whatsoever,” Alice informs Ralph when they meet by chance owing to the equivalent of a Skype glitch. Ralph then rescues Alice by remotely directing his microwave beams to melt an onrushing avalanche. Vive l’amour!

尽管根斯巴克对技术的预见性令人惊叹，但他却无力编织出令人信服的爱情故事。在《拉尔夫 124C41+》中，这位同名英雄（并非巧合的是，一位隐居的"伟大美国发明家"）与他的缪斯——一位名叫爱丽丝 212B423 的瑞士业余无线电操作员——交谈时，仿佛在朗读技术手册，充斥着大量品牌名称和精确到多位小数的计算。"动力塔和通讯塔在同一天被吹倒了，我完全失去了任何通讯手段，"爱丽丝在因类似 Skype 故障的意外相遇中告诉拉尔夫。随后，拉尔夫通过远程操控微波束融化奔涌而来的雪崩，拯救了爱丽丝。爱情万岁！



—



THOUGH GERNSBACK’S OWN ATTEMPTS to write fiction were invariably clunky and stiff, he was brilliant at fostering the formation of communities of shared interest. He began publishing his subscribers’ names, radio call numbers, and addresses in a wireless registry that appeared at the back of Modern Electrics, and in three years, his circulation base soared from eight thousand to fifty-two thousand. By creating a decentralized network of radio enthusiasts who could get in touch with one another directly over the airwaves or by mail, he provided his magazines and gadgets with an ever-expanding market. This community would also prove indispensable once federal bureaucrats began making moves to regulate the airwaves in favor of military communications and commercial broadcasters.

尽管根斯巴克自己的小说创作总是笨拙生硬，但他却极擅长培育共同兴趣社群的形成。他开始在《现代电气》杂志末尾的无线登记簿上刊登订阅者的姓名、无线电呼号及地址，短短三年内，其发行量便从八千份飙升至五万二千份。通过构建一个无线电爱好者组成的去中心化网络——他们能通过电波或信件直接联系彼此——他为自己的杂志和装置创造了持续扩张的市场。当联邦官僚开始着手规范电波资源以偏向军事通信和商业广播时，这个社群更显现出不可或缺的价值。



In the mid-1920s, Gernsback turned his full attention to growing the market for science fiction. He started running ads announcing the launch of a new publication devoted to the genre to be called Amazing Stories. He also came up with a reliable formula for making his new publication popular with his target demographic, with lurid cover art depicting avenging aliens, marauding robots, giant insects, and scantily clad women perpetually at their mercy. Amazing Stories represented not just the emergence of a form of popular literature but the dawn of a new sensibility, embodied by coolly rational, sardonic, tech-savvy heroes of the type later to be played with consummate flair by Harrison Ford and Patrick Stewart (and all too rarely by strong women like Sigourney Weaver and Kate Mulgrew). The bold tagline—“Extravagant Fiction Today, Cold Fact Tomorrow”—practically dared its readers to build labs in their garages and help invent the marvelous future.

20 世纪 20 年代中期，根斯巴克将全部精力转向开拓科幻小说市场。他开始刊登广告，宣布将推出一本专注于该类型的新刊物，名为《惊奇故事》。他还摸索出一套可靠模式，使这本新刊物在目标读者群中广受欢迎：封面采用耸人听闻的插画，描绘复仇的外星人、横行的机器人、巨型昆虫，以及永远任其摆布的衣着暴露的女性。《惊奇故事》不仅代表一种通俗文学形式的诞生，更标志一种新感性的觉醒——这种感性体现在冷静理性、玩世不恭、精通科技的主人公身上，后来由哈里森·福特和帕特里克·斯图尔特演绎得淋漓尽致（而像西格妮·韦弗和凯特·穆格鲁这样强悍的女性角色则极为罕见）。其大胆的宣传语——“今日荒诞小说，明日冰冷现实”——几乎是在挑战读者在自家车库建造实验室，共同创造那个奇妙的未来。



Within a decade, bookstore shelves and drugstore racks all over the United States and Europe were bulging with knockoff titles like Air Wonder Stories, Science Wonder Quarterly, and Astounding Stories of Super-Science. Printed on coarse, untrimmed wood-pulp pages, these affordable gateways to awe and mystery (cover price, ten cents) became collectively known as the pulps.

不到十年间，美国和欧洲各地的书店货架和药店陈列架上便塞满了《空中奇谭》《科学奇闻季刊》《超科学惊异故事》等仿冒刊物。这些印在粗糙未裁切木浆纸上的廉价读物（每本售价十美分），成为通往敬畏与神秘世界的平价入口，被统称为"纸浆杂志"。



—



THE CONTEMPORARY CULTURE OF fandom in America—the whole thriving multiverse of Trekkers, Whovians, Twihards, and Potterheads—had its humble beginnings in the letters-to-the-editor column of Amazing Stories. There, Gernsback carried on the tradition of his wireless registry by printing his readers’ names and addresses along with their letters. The exchanges in this column were often more sophisticated than the stories around them. There was more fervent discussion of Einstein’s theory of relativity in the letters column of Amazing Stories than in mainstream science journals.

当代美国粉丝文化——这个由《星际迷航》迷、《神秘博士》迷、《暮光之城》迷和《哈利·波特》迷构成的蓬勃多元宇宙——其卑微起源可追溯至《惊奇故事》杂志的读者来信专栏。赫恩斯巴克延续其无线电台注册的传统，在刊登读者来信时一并印出姓名与地址。这个专栏中的思想交锋往往比同期小说更为深刻——关于爱因斯坦相对论的激烈讨论，在《惊奇故事》的读者来信栏中竟比主流科学期刊还要热烈。



Soon pulp fans everywhere started compiling networks of pen pals, which led to the formation of organizations like the Science Correspondents Club in Chicago and the Scienceers, a group of New York City teens who met in the Harlem apartment of its first president, an African American space buff named Warren Fitzgerald, encouraged by one of Gernsback’s editors. Using early methods of duplication like mimeography and hectography, these groups churned out their own hand-stapled publications with names like The Comet and The Planet—the first “fanzines” in history.

很快，各地的通俗杂志爱好者开始建立笔友网络，进而催生了芝加哥科学通讯员俱乐部等组织，以及"科学迷"团体——一群纽约青少年在首任主席沃伦·菲茨杰拉德位于哈莱姆区的公寓里聚会，这位非裔美国太空爱好者得到了根斯巴克一位编辑的鼓励。这些团体利用油印和胶版复印等早期复制技术，大量制作了手工装订的出版物，如《彗星》和《行星》——历史上最早的"同人杂志"。



Pulp devotees did not invent the word fan (derived from the Latin fanaticus, “possessed by divine madness”), but they established the first fandom in the modern sense, with its own elaborate customs, art forms, specialized jargon, conventions, and absurdly bombastic internecine warfare.
