(Sam Moskowitz’s 1954 chronicle of the early days of fandom, The Immortal Storm, inspired one critic to quip, “If read directly after a history of World War II, it does not seem like an anticlimax.”) This fractious and fertile milieu nurtured the careers of many writers who went on to mainstream fame, including film critic Roger Ebert and screenwriter Leigh Brackett, celebrated for her work on The Big Sleep, The Long Goodbye, and The Empire Strikes Back. Other fans became science fiction immortals themselves, including Ray Bradbury, Isaac Asimov, Frederick Pohl, and Ursula K. LeGuin.

通俗杂志的爱好者们并非“粉丝”（fan）一词的发明者（该词源自拉丁语 fanaticus，意为“被神圣的疯狂所占据”），但他们建立了现代意义上的第一个粉丝圈，拥有其独特的繁复习俗、艺术形式、专业术语、集会活动，以及荒谬夸张的内部争斗。（萨姆·莫斯科维茨 1954 年撰写的粉丝圈早期编年史《不朽风暴》，曾引得一位评论家调侃道：“若在读完二战史后直接翻阅此书，竟不觉得有虎头蛇尾之感。”）这个充满纷争又孕育生机的环境，滋养了许多后来跻身主流文坛的作家，包括影评人罗杰·伊伯特，以及因《夜长梦多》《漫长的告别》和《星球大战：帝国反击战》而闻名的编剧莉·布拉克特。其他粉丝则自身成为了科幻界的永恒传奇，包括雷·布拉德伯里、艾萨克·阿西莫夫、弗雷德里克·波尔和厄休拉·K·勒古恩。



Most importantly, magazines like Amazing Stories and Weird Tales fired up the imaginations of those who turned the extravagant visions of their favorite authors into cold fact. The original members of the British Interplanetary Society, founded in 1933 to promote space exploration, were avid readers of the pulps. Arthur C. Clarke observed in 1948 that many American scientists were also fervent fans, and that “aeronautics would never have reached the stage it has now if it wasn’t for science fiction, which has done much to break down the psychological barriers that retard our progress.” Clarke himself circulated copies of Thrilling Wonder Stories, another title on Gernsback’s ever-expanding roster, at the Cavendish Laboratory in Cambridge.

最重要的是，像《惊奇故事》和《怪谭》这样的杂志点燃了那些将钟爱作家天马行空的想象变为冰冷现实的人们的热情。成立于 1933 年、旨在推动太空探索的英国星际学会的创始成员们，正是这些廉价杂志的忠实读者。阿瑟·C·克拉克在 1948 年指出，许多美国科学家也是狂热的科幻迷，并认为“如果没有科幻小说，航空学绝不可能达到今天的高度，科幻小说在打破阻碍我们进步的心理壁垒方面功不可没。”克拉克本人曾在剑桥大学卡文迪许实验室传阅《惊险奇谭》——这是根斯巴克不断扩张的杂志名单上的另一本刊物。



Darko Suvin, a leading scholar of the genre, described the subversive impulse at the heart of science fiction as an expression of “cognitive estrangement” from the mainstream. Fandom tapped into a deep yearning to rise above the circumstances of humdrum existence and become part of something noble, deeply informed, and not widely understood. The thrill of being part of something that few people could appreciate was particularly keen for those who had spent their lives being ridiculed. No one could make you a fan—or prevent you from being one—but yourself, and no one could judge you but your peers of choice: your fellow “fen.” Early fans indulged these newfound feelings of confidence and superiority to the hilt, referring to the clueless nonfans who ran the world as “mundanes.”

该类型文学的重要学者达科·苏文将科幻小说核心中的颠覆性冲动描述为对主流“认知疏离”的表达。粉丝群体挖掘出深层的渴望——超越平庸生活的桎梏，成为崇高、深邃且鲜为人知的事物的一部分。对于那些一生饱受嘲笑的人来说，能成为少数人才能欣赏的事物的一部分，这种兴奋尤为强烈。没有人能让你成为粉丝——或阻止你成为粉丝——除了你自己，也没有人能评判你，除了你选择的同好：你的“粉丝同胞”。早期的粉丝们尽情沉浸在这种新生的自信与优越感中，将那些掌管世界却一无所知的非粉丝称为“凡人”。



Unlike cult followings based on sports teams or rock stars, science fiction fandom was rooted in an essentially solitary activity: reading. Traits typically viewed as pathological or pathetic in the mainstream (like obsessing over trivia while accumulating vast hoards of treasured ephemera) were rewarded in the community as signs of “trufan” commitment. Fandom offered what every homesick space child yearned for: membership in an elite society of loners united by their belief in the future. For those who had felt like exiles their whole lives, forced to live among strangers, becoming a fan was like finally coming home.

与基于运动队或摇滚明星的狂热追随不同，科幻迷的根基本质上是一种孤独的活动：阅读。那些在主流的眼光中通常被视为病态或可悲的特质（比如痴迷于琐碎细节，同时积累大量珍贵的短暂收藏品），在这个社群中却被视为“真粉丝”忠诚度的标志。科幻迷提供了每个思乡的太空少年所渴望的东西：成为一个由对未来信念凝聚而成的精英孤独者社群的成员。对于那些一生都感觉自己像流亡者、被迫生活在陌生人中间的人来说，成为粉丝就像终于回到了家。



III



As an editor, Gernsback was primarily a hardware man. He favored galactic potboilers crammed with fantastic gizmos, cunning contraptions, and diabolical engines of mayhem (death rays were a perennial favorite). Subtler masters of the art later ridiculed his school of the genre—in which technology took precedence over psychology, and plot and character were secondary to product placement—as “gadget fiction.” Reading it, one learned a lot about the tools of the future but very little about the people who used them. Nuances of interpersonal interaction were irrelevant, women existed as hapless props to be rescued, and heroes were monastically chaste. The real protagonist of scientifiction was science itself, conquering the dark forces of irrationality and ignorance.

作为编辑，根斯巴克本质上是个硬件迷。他偏爱那些塞满神奇装置、精巧机关和毁灭性机械（死亡射线始终是他的心头好）的太空冒险故事。后来更精于此道的文学大师们嘲笑他开创的这种流派——技术凌驾于心理之上，情节与人物沦为产品展示的附庸——称之为"小玩意小说"。阅读这类作品，读者能了解大量未来工具的知识，却对使用工具的人一无所知。人际互动的微妙之处毫无意义，女性只是等待救援的可怜道具，英雄们则保持着僧侣般的禁欲。科学传奇的真正主角是科学本身，它征服着非理性与无知的黑暗力量。



You could thumb through a dozen of Gernsback’s titles at the drugstore and not discover that there was a Great Depression or Dust Bowl going on. When a reader objected to Wonder Stories publishing translations from the German as Hitler rose to power, Gernsback (or one of his editors) sniffed that the magazine remained “perfectly neutral” on the subject: “What the leader of Germany does to or for the Germans is for the Germans to think about.”

你在药店翻阅十几本根斯巴克主编的杂志时，根本不会发现当时正发生大萧条或沙尘暴。当有读者抗议《奇谭》在希特勒掌权时刊登德文译作时，根斯巴克（或他某位编辑）嗤之以鼻地表示，该杂志对此事保持"绝对中立"："德国领袖对德国人做了什么或为德国人做了什么，那是德国人该思考的事。"



—



IN 1940, A CANADIAN defense specialist named A. E. van Vogt published a serial called Slan in Astounding Science Fiction that raised the bar for the whole genre by taking the theme of cognitive estrangement to the next level, helping to inaugurate what historians call the golden age of science fiction.

1940 年，一位名叫 A·E·范·沃格特的加拿大国防专家在《惊奇科幻》上连载了名为《斯兰》的系列小说，通过将认知疏离主题推向新高度，提升了整个科幻类型的标杆，由此开启了史学家所称的科幻黄金时代。



Published in three parts, it was the story of a race of humanoids—the “Slans” of the title—who had been genetically engineered to handle the accelerated pace of mechanized civilization. This race of elegant mutants was the creation of a twenty-first-century biologist named Samuel Lann, who began his project by experimenting on his own children. The conceptual breakthrough of Slan was portraying “normal” human beings not as saviors but as the enemy.

这部作品分三部分出版，讲述了一个人形种族的故事——即标题中的“斯兰人”——他们经过基因改造，以适应机械化文明日益加速的节奏。这个优雅的突变种族是由 21 世纪一位名叫塞缪尔·兰的生物学家创造的，他通过在自己的孩子身上做实验来启动这个项目。《斯兰人》的概念性突破在于，它将“正常”人类描绘成敌人而非救世主。



As the story opens, the book’s genetically modified protagonist, Jommy Cross, and his kind are being hunted to extinction in the decaying streets of a sprawling megacity called Centropolis. Jommy’s mother is forced to sacrifice her own life so that Jommy may live; with the help of a crafty old homeless woman, the boy takes shelter in an underground society surviving in the nooks and crannies of the urban landscape.

故事开篇，书中经过基因改造的主角乔米·克罗斯及其同类，在名为“中心城”的庞大都市的破败街道上遭到猎杀，濒临灭绝。乔米的母亲被迫牺牲自己的生命，以换取乔米的生存；在一个狡猾的老流浪妇人的帮助下，男孩躲进了城市角落中幸存的地下社会。



Reprinted as a stand-alone novel after World War II, Slan caused a sensation. Its tropes echo through later generations of science fiction: the political machinations in Dune, Star Trek’s half-Betazoid counselor Deanna Troi, the hunt for rogue replicants in Blade Runner, the mutant superpowers of the X-Men. For first-generation fans, Slan had special resonance, because they saw a reflection of their own predicament in this tale of superintelligent, supersensitive, and profoundly misunderstood mutants struggling to survive in a world not built for them. No one carried this notion further than one of the most outrageous fans that ever lived, a renegade space child named Claude Degler.

《斯兰》在二战后作为独立小说再版时引起了轰动。其叙事模式在后世科幻作品中不断回响：《沙丘》中的政治权谋、《星际迷航》中半贝塔佐德族顾问迪安娜·特洛伊、《银翼杀手》中对逃亡复制人的追捕、《X 战警》中的变种人超能力。对于第一代粉丝而言，《斯兰》具有特殊共鸣——他们在这群超智慧、超敏感却饱受误解的变种人挣扎求存的故事中，看到了自身处境的倒影。而将这个理念推向极致的，正是史上最狂热的粉丝之一——一位名为克劳德·德格勒的叛逆太空之子。



Degler’s background, like every other aspect of his life, was shrouded in the mists of his own hyperbole. According to a dossier compiled by the first historian of fandom, a lawyer named Jack Speer, Degler was born in Missouri in 1920. Shortly thereafter, his father left the family, and young Claude and his mother moved to Indiana. Like Gernsback, he was obsessed with electricity at a very young age and plunged into science fiction like a fish rediscovering the sea. He immersed himself in the pulps and signed up for the Buck Rogers Club—an early attempt to commercialize fandom that offered “ray repellent” rings in boxes of Cream of Wheat—but the neighborhood kids only taunted him for being such a nerd.

德格勒的背景，如同他生活的其他方面一样，笼罩在他自我夸大的迷雾之中。根据首位粉丝历史学家、律师杰克·斯皮尔编纂的档案，德格勒 1920 年出生于密苏里州。不久后，父亲离家出走，年幼的克劳德随母亲迁居印第安纳州。与根斯巴克相似，他自幼痴迷电力，投身科幻小说如同鱼儿重归大海。他沉迷于廉价杂志，加入巴克·罗杰斯俱乐部——这是早期将粉丝文化商业化的尝试，在麦片盒里附赠"防辐射戒指"——但街坊邻居的孩子只嘲笑他是个书呆子。



Precociously bright, Degler made the honor roll in high school, but when he was fifteen his anxiety, depression, and violent outbursts—exacerbated by constant bullying—resulted in his expulsion. His mother was advised to enroll him in a school for the feebleminded, but she refused. After he set fire to a county prosecutor’s toolshed in 1936, however, she committed him to the Eastern Indiana Hospital for the Insane. The following year, his doctors issued a certificate mandating his forced sterilization, as he was likely to father “mentally incompetent” or “socially inadequate” children. Instead of going under the knife, Degler somehow got himself furloughed against his doctors’ orders.

德格勒早慧，高中时便登上荣誉榜，但十五岁时，他的焦虑、抑郁和暴力倾向——因长期遭受欺凌而加剧——导致他被开除。母亲被建议送他去弱智学校，但她拒绝了。然而，1936 年他纵火烧了县检察官的工具棚后，母亲将他送进了东印第安纳精神病院。次年，他的医生出具证明，强制要求对他进行绝育，因为他很可能生出“心智不健全”或“社会适应不良”的孩子。但德格勒没有接受手术，反而不知何故违背医嘱获得了假释。



Then he read Slan. The book had an electrifying effect on Degler, and suddenly his true destiny became clear. He and his fellow fen were “star-begotten” mutants trapped behind enemy lines. Science fiction fandom was no mere diversion for daydreaming teenagers and egghead professors; it was the first stirrings of a geek uprising against the mundanes who had oppressed them for so long. He came up with a rallying cry that spread through fandom like a viral meme: “Fans are Slans!”

随后他读到了《斯兰》。这本书对德格勒产生了震撼性的影响，他突然看清了自己真正的命运。他和他的同好们是“星辰所生”的变种人，被困在敌后。科幻迷圈不再是白日梦少年和书呆子教授们的消遣；它是极客们对长期压迫他们的庸常之辈发起反抗的初次躁动。他提出了一句口号，像病毒式迷因一样在迷圈中传播：“粉丝就是斯兰人！”



Degler set off on an epic coast-to-coast hitchhiking trip to raise the consciousness of his fellow “Cosmen” and “Coswomen,” mining science club mailing lists for names of potential members of a vast network that he dubbed the Cosmic Circle. In 1941, he showed up in Denver at one of the first science fiction conventions, delivering a speech that he claimed had been written by Martians. He couch-surfed his way from Los Angeles to New York, designating fans to become officers of organizations like the Circle of Aztor, the Valdosta Philosophers, the Cosmic Thinkers, the Rose City Science Circle, the Florida Cosmos Society, the Dixie Fantasy Federation, the Empire State Slans, and the Muncie Mutants.

德格勒开启了一场史诗般的横跨大陆搭便车之旅，旨在唤醒他的“宇宙人”同胞们的意识。他通过挖掘科学俱乐部的邮件列表，为这个被他命名为“宇宙圈”的庞大网络寻找潜在成员。1941 年，他出现在丹佛最早的一届科幻大会上，发表了一场据称由火星人撰写的演讲。他从洛杉矶一路借宿到纽约，指定粉丝们担任各类组织的负责人，如阿兹托尔圈、瓦尔多斯塔哲学家、宇宙思想家、玫瑰城科学圈、佛罗里达宇宙学会、迪克西奇幻联盟、帝国州斯兰人以及曼西突变者。



He also advocated the formation of all-fan households called Slan shacks, where his comrades could pursue their passions with minimal interference from pesky mundanes, and championed the launch of a Cosmic Camp in the Ozarks where virile Cosmen and fecund Coswomen could breed the next generation of genetically superior humanoids, complete with its own “laboratory-library” for housing vast collections of ephemera. “Fight to make the world safe for science fiction!” Degler cried in a fanzine called Voice of the Imagi-Nation, one of a dizzying array of publications that he contributed to in the 1940s.

他还倡导组建名为“斯兰小屋”的全粉丝家庭，让同好们能在不受无聊凡人过多干扰的环境中追求热爱，并力推在欧扎克地区启动“宇宙营地”计划，让充满活力的宇宙男女繁衍下一代基因优越的人形生物，营地内还配备“实验室图书馆”，用于收藏大量短期资料。“为科幻小说争取安全的世界而战！”德格勒在一本名为《想象之声》的粉丝杂志中呐喊，这是他在 20 世纪 40 年代参与撰稿的众多令人眼花缭乱的出版物之一。



His Cosmic Camp never got off the ground, but the first Slan shack—touted as “a fannish island in a sea of mundania”—was founded in Battle Creek, Michigan, in 1943. “Our planning included a fanzine room where all occupants would share access to a mimeo, and apartments with northern light for the artists,” recalled Dalvan Coger, a former resident. Fans arrived from everywhere—by car, train, bus, and thumb—to savor “the feeling of closeness, of being able to be open in our ideas, that we as fans could express most easily in each other’s company.” A sign over the front door read simply, “Civilization.”

他的宇宙营地从未真正实现，但第一间斯兰小屋——被宣传为“平凡世界中的粉丝之岛”——于 1943 年在密歇根州巴特尔克里克建立。“我们的规划包括一间同人志室，所有住户可共享一台油印机，以及为艺术家准备的朝北采光公寓，”前住户达尔万·科格回忆道。粉丝们从四面八方赶来——乘汽车、火车、巴士，甚至搭便车——只为品味“那种亲密感，那种能自由表达想法的感觉，这种感受在同好相伴时最能自然流露”。前门上方挂着一块牌子，上面只写着：“文明”。



Slan shacks with names like Oblique House, the Epicentre, Station X, the Ivory Birdbath, Prime Base, and Tendril Towers popped up all over the United States and the United Kingdom. A scheme was hatched to transform a whole city block of Los Angeles into a full-fledged Slan Center, complete with prefab housing units, hydroponic farms, and communal publishing facilities. Degler claimed that the owner of a large ranch in Arizona had granted the Cosmic Circle permission to initiate rocketry experiments there after the war, so that his star-begotten cohorts could get down to the business of turning extravagant fiction into cold fact. Ambitious projects like this, he promised, were just the beginning of a new intergalactic society: “Our children shall inherit not only this earth—but this universe! Today we carry 22 states, tomorrow, nine planets!”

名为“斜屋”、“震中”、“X 站”、“象牙鸟浴盆”、“主基地”和“卷须塔”的斯兰棚屋在美国和英国各地如雨后春笋般涌现。一项计划正在酝酿，要将洛杉矶整个街区改造成一个功能齐全的斯兰中心，配备预制住宅单元、水培农场和公共出版设施。德格勒声称，亚利桑那州一个大牧场的所有者已允许宇宙圈在战后进行火箭实验，以便他的星裔同伴们能将荒诞的小说变成冰冷的现实。他承诺，像这样雄心勃勃的项目只是星际新社会的开端：“我们的孩子不仅将继承地球——还将继承整个宇宙！今天我们拥有 22 个州，明天将是九颗行星！”



Alas, the Los Angeles Slan Center never came to fruition. Degler himself proved to be fandom’s most ephemeral shooting star, falling out of favor with his fellow fen when it became obvious that many of the organizations in the Cosmic Circle existed only in the universe of his brain. Even the slogan “Fans are Slans!” was eventually ridiculed as a self-parodic joke—a caricature of fandom at its most messianic and overwrought.

唉，洛杉矶斯兰中心终究未能建成。德格勒本人也成了粉丝圈中最短暂的流星——当人们发现宇宙圈中的许多组织只存在于他脑海中的宇宙时，他便失去了同好的青睐。就连"粉丝即斯兰人！"这句口号，最终也沦为自我嘲讽的笑柄——成为粉丝圈最狂热、最夸张时期的讽刺漫画。



But there was more than a grain of truth in Degler’s insistence that science fiction fans were mutants struggling to survive in the margins of a society that did not understand them. A significant minority of his fellow fen—including Gernsback—would likely have been eligible for a diagnosis of Asperger’s syndrome had one been available, says prominent science fiction historian Gary Westfahl. For people on the autism spectrum before it had a name, he explains, the alternate universes of science fiction may have felt less alien than the baffling sea of mundania in which they found themselves marooned.

但德格勒坚持认为科幻迷是挣扎在不解社会边缘的突变体，这一观点并非毫无道理。著名科幻史学家加里·韦斯特法尔指出，包括根斯巴克在内的相当一部分同好，若当时有相关诊断标准，很可能符合阿斯伯格综合征的诊断标准。他解释道，对于那些在孤独谱系尚未被命名时就身处其中的人来说，科幻小说中的平行宇宙或许比他们被困其中的那片令人困惑的凡俗之海更不显陌生。



Looking back at the science fiction of the 1930s pulp magazines, filled with lonely adventurers on solitary quests to distant planets and the far future, one can easily see how these stories would appeal to those young men (and some young women), then regarded only as “reclusive” or “eccentric,” who we would now classify as undiagnosed cases of Asperger’s Syndrome . . . to a teenager in the 1930s with Asperger’s Syndrome, a story about an astronaut encountering aliens on Mars might have had an air of comforting familiarity, in contrast to stories set in the bizarre, inexplicable, and thoroughly socialized worlds of Andy Hardy and the Bobbsey Twins.

回顾 20 世纪 30 年代那些廉价杂志上的科幻小说，满篇都是孤独的探险家独自踏上前往遥远星球和遥远未来的旅程。不难看出，这些故事对那些当时被视为“孤僻”或“古怪”的年轻人（其中也包括一些年轻女性）有着怎样的吸引力——如今我们会将他们归类为未被确诊的阿斯伯格综合征患者。对于 20 世纪 30 年代一位患有阿斯伯格综合征的青少年来说，一个关于宇航员在火星上遭遇外星人的故事，或许会带来一种令人安心的熟悉感，这与那些设定在古怪、费解且完全社会化世界里的安迪·哈迪和博比·双胞胎的故事形成了鲜明对比。



The colorful cast of characters in Harry Warner’s All Our Yesterdays, an eyewitness account of nascent fandom, includes many fans, both male and female, who are described as “hermits,” “extremely introverted,” lacking in social graces, gifted but awkward, and focused on “fanac” (“fan activity”) with a single-minded intensity that could easily be considered obsessive. In the 1940s, Jack Speer speculated that most fans were “handicapped” in some way that made it difficult for them to thrive in the mainstream world.

在哈里·华纳的《我们的昨日》这部关于早期粉丝圈的第一手记录中，那些形形色色的人物包括许多男女粉丝，他们被描述为“隐士”、“极度内向”、缺乏社交礼仪、天赋异禀却笨拙不堪，并且以近乎偏执的专注力投身于“粉丝活动”（fanac）。在 20 世纪 40 年代，杰克·斯皮尔推测，大多数粉丝在某种程度上都“有缺陷”，这使得他们难以在主流世界中茁壮成长。



Many fans were also ham radio operators, and there was significant crossover between the two subcultures. If real Slans had existed in the early twentieth century, you might have expected to find them poring through Gernsback’s catalogs, trying to piece together the technology of a more advanced civilization out of whatever crude equipment was available, like Mr. Spock assembling a communicator out of vacuum tubes in Edith Keeler’s basement in a celebrated episode of the original Star Trek series.

许多粉丝同时也是业余无线电爱好者，这两个亚文化群体之间存在显著的交集。如果 20 世纪初真的存在斯兰人，你可能会发现他们像《星际迷航》经典剧集中斯波克先生在伊迪丝·基勒的地下室里用真空管组装通讯器那样，埋头翻阅根斯巴克的产品目录，试图用当时简陋的设备拼凑出更先进文明的技术。



The future couldn’t arrive fast enough: a significant number of first-generation fans with keen interests in science and engineering ended up working in menial jobs because of their limited social skills. “Fans today can’t imagine the threadbare existence of many fans of the 1930s and early ’40s, riding the boxcars to Worldcons or rummaging through the trash behind hotels to recover copies of Amazing Stories discarded by departing patrons,” wrote historian David B. Williams. “There’s a reason fans were greyhound-thin in those days—food cost money.”

未来总是来得太慢：相当数量的第一代粉丝虽然对科学和工程抱有浓厚兴趣，却因社交能力有限最终只能从事卑微的工作。历史学家大卫·B·威廉姆斯写道："今天的粉丝无法想象上世纪 30 年代至 40 年代初许多粉丝的窘迫生活——他们扒货运火车去参加世界科幻大会，或在旅馆后巷翻捡被离店客人丢弃的《惊奇故事》杂志。那个年代粉丝们瘦得像灰狗是有原因的——食物是要花钱的。"



Both amateur radio and science fiction fandom offered ways of gaining social recognition outside traditional channels. There was even a fannish word for the thrill of being respected by your peers for your contributions to the community: egoboo. For people who found open-ended conversations daunting, the byzantine customs and rituals of fandom furnished reassuring scripts for interaction. The elaborate jargon developed by fans in the early days (which one critic called “an addiction to obscure lingo for its own sake”) was practically its own dialect and acted as a verbal force field that kept clueless mundanes at bay.

业余无线电和科幻迷圈都提供了在传统渠道之外获得社会认可的方式。甚至有一个粉丝圈术语来形容因对社群做出贡献而受到同辈尊重的兴奋感：自我满足（egoboo）。对于那些觉得开放式对话令人望而生畏的人来说，粉丝圈错综复杂的习俗和仪式提供了令人安心的互动脚本。早期粉丝们发展出的复杂行话（有评论家称之为"为晦涩而晦涩的术语癖好"）几乎自成一种方言，如同语言力场般将懵懂的圈外人隔绝在外。



Obviously, fandom was a community that was unusually accepting of individual quirks and differences. The term fanzine was coined in 1940 by a deaf fan named Louis Russell Chauvenet, who was also a tournament-winning chess player and a computer technician for the Defense Department. Another fan who became a star writing for Gernsback, David Keller, was diagnosed as feebleminded as a child and spoke a private language understood only by his sister until he was six years old. One of Degler’s companions on the road was a physically disabled man named Jim Kepner, who became one of the first openly gay journalists, encouraged by “reading and conjecturing about worlds in which customs might differ from ours.” He went from obsessively hoarding issues of Amazing Stories and Galaxy to hunting down newsletters from pioneering gay groups like the Mattachine Society and the Daughters of Bilitis. Kepner’s personal library now forms the core of ONE, the largest archive of gay history in the world, housed at the University of Southern California.

显然，粉丝圈是一个异常包容个人怪癖与差异的社群。"粉丝杂志"一词由聋人粉丝路易斯·拉塞尔·肖夫内于 1940 年创造，他既是锦标赛冠军棋手，也是国防部的计算机技术员。另一位为根斯巴克撰稿而成为明星的粉丝大卫·凯勒，幼时曾被诊断为智力低下，直到六岁前都只说一种只有姐姐能懂的私人语言。德格勒的旅伴中有一位名叫吉姆·凯普纳的残障人士，他受"阅读并构想那些习俗可能与我们不同的世界"的启发，成为最早公开同性恋身份的记者之一。他从痴迷囤积《惊奇故事》和《银河》杂志，转而搜寻马塔辛协会、比利提斯之女等先驱同性恋团体的通讯简报。凯普纳的个人藏书如今构成了南加州大学全球最大同性恋历史档案馆 ONE 的核心馆藏。



Gernsback biographer Gary Westfahl believes that it’s “reasonable to assume” that the influential editor and entrepreneur was an undiagnosed Aspergian. His peers regarded him as an unsociable figure who remained coolly distant from the communities he created. The people he counted as friends tended to be prominent scientists, influential politicians, and other notable figures with whom he corresponded by mail; historian James Gunn observed in Alternate Worlds that he was “a strange mixture of personal reserve and aggressive salesmanship.”

根据《惊奇故事》传记作者加里·韦斯特法尔的观点，“有理由认为”这位颇具影响力的编辑兼企业家是一位未被确诊的阿斯伯格综合征患者。他的同行认为他性格孤僻，对自己创建的社群始终保持着冷淡疏离的态度。被他视为朋友的人往往是著名科学家、有影响力的政要及其他通过书信往来的知名人士；历史学家詹姆斯·冈恩在《异世界》中评价道，他是“个人内敛与激进推销的奇特混合体”。



After Gernsback’s first two marriages ended in divorce, he decided that the whole messy business of matrimony was crying out for a high-tech solution. To launch this massive undertaking, a team of scientists would first need to interview thousands of couples applying for marriage licenses to interrogate them about every aspect of their lives: their health histories, their aptitudes for music and art, the texture of their hair and skin, their favorite smells, the presence of hereditary diseases in their family lines, and a hundred other “vital aspects.” Then his army of researchers would deploy an arsenal of diagnostic instruments (including electrocardiographs and lie detectors) to arrive at each person’s “S.Q.”—their Sexual Quotient. Once the fate of these initial couplings was ascertained, algorithms to maximize the chances of success would be fed into a computer. From that point on, fickle Cupid would yield to the unassailable objectivity of Big Data.

在根斯巴克的前两段婚姻以离婚告终后，他认定婚姻这桩麻烦事亟需高科技解决方案。要启动这项浩大工程，科学家团队首先需要采访数千对申请结婚的伴侣，盘问他们生活的方方面面：健康史、音乐与艺术天赋、发质与肤质、偏好的气味、家族遗传病史，以及上百项其他"关键指标"。随后，他的研究员大军将动用包括心电图仪和测谎仪在内的诊断仪器，计算出每个人的"性商指数"。待这些初始配对的结果尘埃落定后，计算机将输入能最大化成功几率的算法。从那一刻起，善变的丘比特将臣服于大数据不容置疑的客观性。



Gernsback was equally unsentimental about enforcing his editorial directives. “Short lines are easier to read than long ones,” he advised potential contributors to his magazines. “This is due to a well-known optical law.” His rejection slips listed thirty mistakes commonly made by writers, with a tick box next to each one including “plot stale” and “material offensive to moral standards.” He demanded that all scientific theory in stories he published be verifiable—an insistence on literal accuracy dubbed “the Gernsback Delusion” by his stable of authors. He even applied this law to comics in the Sunday paper, fuming if he spotted an astronaut clad in an imperfectly sealed space suit.

根斯巴克在执行编辑指令时同样毫不留情。“短句比长句更容易阅读，”他在向潜在投稿者建议时写道，“这是由一条众所周知的光学定律决定的。”他的退稿单列出了作家常犯的三十种错误，每项错误旁都附有勾选框，包括“情节老套”和“内容有违道德标准”。他要求其出版的所有故事中的科学理论必须可验证——这种对字面准确性的执着被他的作者群称为“根斯巴克妄想”。他甚至将这条定律应用于周日版报纸的漫画中，若发现宇航员穿着密封不完美的太空服便会勃然大怒。



Acutely sensitive to sound, he would withdraw to his sumptuously appointed “think room” in his West End Avenue penthouse to visualize the shape of things to come in uninterrupted silence and solitude. When he ventured out in public, he carried himself with “an air of ducal authority,” holding forth in drafty perorations on his favorite subjects like “Bismarck directing the Congress of Berlin,” noted a journalist who profiled him in Life magazine. He was equally imperious in exercising his dietary preferences. Arriving at one of the restaurants where he ate religiously (Delmonico’s was a favorite), he would pop in a monocle and scrutinize the offerings du jour like a surgeon conducting a biopsy. He never hesitated to send a dish back to the kitchen that had been served on a plate he deemed insufficiently warmed, and he once dismissed three bottles of wine in one sitting. David Keller, who joined him for lunch at the Astor Hotel, recalled that the multimillionaire methodically ticked off the price of every item they ate, including the iced coffee (eighty-five cents).

他对声音极为敏感，常常退回到位于西区大道顶层公寓那间陈设奢华的“思考室”，在绝对的寂静与独处中构想未来的蓝图。一位曾在《生活》杂志上为他撰写专题的记者写道，当他外出公开露面时，总是带着“公爵般的威严气度”，滔滔不绝地谈论他钟爱的话题，比如“俾斯麦主持柏林会议”。在饮食偏好上，他同样专横跋扈。每当他走进一家常去的餐厅（德尔莫尼科餐厅是他的最爱），便会戴上单片眼镜，像外科医生做活检一样仔细审视当日的菜品。如果发现盛菜的盘子温度不够，他会毫不犹豫地让侍者端回厨房重换；有一次，他甚至在一次用餐中接连退掉了三瓶酒。大卫·凯勒曾与他在阿斯特酒店共进午餐，回忆道这位千万富翁精确地计算了他们所吃每样东西的价格，包括那杯冰咖啡（八十五美分）。



Each weekday morning at precisely 8:30, he would arrive at his immaculate offices on West Fourteenth Street, doused in his favorite scent of toilet water and looking as though he were “carrying the world on his shoulders,” a local store owner recalled. His telephone, desk set, thermos bottle, and office walls were all tinted the same shade of green (à la Cavendish’s Bedford Street library), and at periodic intervals throughout the day, the dapper bow-tied editor would blow across his desk to keep it clear of offending soot. After Tesla died in 1943—impoverished and emaciated in his room at the Hotel New Yorker with a “do not disturb” sign permanently affixed to his door—Gernsback mounted his death mask in the corner of his office as a macabre tribute.

每个工作日的早晨，他都会在 8 点 30 分准时抵达位于西十四街一尘不染的办公室，身上洒满了他最爱的花露水，看起来仿佛“肩负着整个世界”，一位当地店主回忆道。他的电话、办公桌、保温瓶和办公室墙壁都染成了同一种绿色（仿效卡文迪什在贝德福德街的图书馆），在一天中的固定时段，这位衣着考究、系着蝴蝶结的编辑会对着办公桌吹气，以清除恼人的灰尘。1943 年特斯拉去世时——他穷困潦倒、骨瘦如柴地躺在纽约客酒店的房间里，门上永久挂着“请勿打扰”的牌子——根斯巴克将他的死亡面具挂在办公室角落，作为阴森的纪念。



Though Gernsback’s career as an inventor was overshadowed by his Serbian mentor’s (whose wasn’t?), he earned more than eighty patents in his lifetime, encompassing a range of innovations including the first walkie-talkie, one of the first bone-conduction hearing aids, a design for TV glasses (complete with a tiny aerial), and a submersible Ferris wheel.

尽管根斯巴克作为发明家的职业生涯被他的塞尔维亚导师所掩盖（谁又不是呢？），但他一生中获得了八十多项专利，涵盖了一系列创新，包括第一台对讲机、最早的骨传导助听器之一、一副电视眼镜设计（配有微型天线），以及一座可潜水的摩天轮。



But his most blatantly autistic creation was a contraption for reducing distracting sensory input in noisy offices called “the Isolator.” The July 1925 issue of Science and Invention featured a surreal illustration of the editor modeling his creation, looking like a deep-sea diver in a particularly cumbersome helmet, complete with a private air supply furnished by a nearby tank. So the wearer could focus on a single line of text at a time, there were two slits drilled in the helmet. With “outside noises being eliminated,” the caption advised, “the worker can concentrate with ease on the subject at hand.”

但他最明显的孤独谱系特质之作，是一台名为"隔离器"的装置，旨在减少嘈杂办公室中的感官干扰。1925 年 7 月的《科学与发明》杂志刊登了一幅超现实插图，描绘了编辑本人穿戴这一发明的模样——他看起来像戴着特别笨重头盔的深海潜水员，旁边还配有气罐提供独立空气供应。头盔上钻有两个狭缝，以便佩戴者每次只能聚焦一行文字。图注写道："消除外界噪音后，工作者可以轻松专注于手头的工作。"



Though his Isolator never caught on, Gernsback’s amateur radio network turned out to be a boon for those most likely to yearn for such a device. One ham alone in a garage with a spark transmitter was a nerd—but a network of hams was a force to be reckoned with. By chaining stations together in relays, a Chicago amateur could “work” his equivalent in Christchurch, passing messages around the globe. Planet Earth suddenly became a very small and convivial place for a ham in a room with a couple of dry cells, a spark transmitter, a “cat’s whisker” receiver, and a headset.

尽管他的隔离器从未流行起来，但根斯巴克的业余无线电网络却成了那些最渴望此类设备之人的福音。一个独自在车库里摆弄火花发射器的无线电爱好者是个书呆子——但一群爱好者组成的网络却是一股不可忽视的力量。通过中继站串联，芝加哥的爱好者可以"联络"远在基督城的同行，将信息传递到全球各地。对于那个在房间里摆弄几节干电池、一台火花发射器、一个"猫须"接收器和一副耳机的无线电爱好者而言，地球突然变成了一个非常小而友好的地方。



Wireless was not for everyone, Gernsback acknowledged—the learning curve was too steep for poor dullards who lacked what he called a “radio mind.” A boy with such a mind (and it was nearly always a boy in his imagination, though not in reality) didn’t have the same compulsion to waste time in foolish pursuits that other boys did; instead, he practically had to be forced to leave the house.

格恩斯巴克承认，无线电并非适合所有人——对于那些缺乏他所谓的“无线电头脑”的愚钝者而言，学习曲线过于陡峭。拥有这种头脑的男孩（在他的想象中几乎总是男孩，尽管现实中并非如此）不会像其他男孩那样，有同样的冲动将时间浪费在愚蠢的追求上；相反，他几乎需要被强迫才能离开屋子。



During World War II, the British spy agency MI8 secretly recruited a crew of teenage wireless operators (prohibited from discussing their activities even with their families) to intercept coded messages from the Nazis. By forwarding these transmissions to the crack team of code breakers at Bletchley Park led by the computer pioneer Alan Turing, these young hams enabled the Allies to accurately predict the movements of the German and Italian forces. Asperger’s prediction that the little professors in his clinic could one day aid in the war effort had been prescient, but it was the Allies who reaped the benefits.

二战期间，英国间谍机构 MI8 秘密招募了一批青少年无线电操作员（甚至禁止他们与家人讨论自己的活动），用于截获纳粹的加密信息。通过将这些传输内容转发给由计算机先驱艾伦·图灵领导的布莱切利园顶尖密码破译团队，这些年轻的业余无线电爱好者使盟军能够准确预测德国和意大利军队的动向。阿斯伯格曾预言，他诊所里的小教授们有朝一日能助力战争，这一预言颇具先见之明，但最终受益的却是盟军。



With the rise of wireless, the scattered members of his tribe finally had a way to become a collective force in the public sphere. Ham radio was an activity that rewarded fascination with apparatus, systems, and complex machines, and amateurs with keen memorization abilities had an advantage, because all hams in the United States were required to learn Morse code to earn their FCC licenses until 1990. With parts available by mail at reasonable prices from Gernsback and his competitors, it was an affordable hobby that could be pursued in solitude. Hams who struggled with spoken language could avoid talking altogether by communicating in code. (A photograph of an early gathering of hams shows two men sitting across a table from one another, communicating by tapping out dots and dashes on milk bottles with spoons.) But those who enjoyed gabbing away could “chew the rag” with other hams for hours, employing a lexicon as witty and ritualized as the jargon of fandom. The culture of wireless was also a strict meritocracy where no one cared about what you looked like or how gracefully you deported yourself in public. If you knew how to set up a rig and keep it running, you were welcome to join the party.

随着无线电的兴起，他部落中分散的成员终于有了在公共领域凝聚成集体力量的方式。业余无线电是一项奖励对设备、系统和复杂机器着迷的活动，而记忆力敏锐的业余爱好者具有优势，因为直到 1990 年，美国所有业余无线电操作员都必须学习摩尔斯电码才能获得联邦通信委员会（FCC）执照。通过邮购方式从根斯巴克及其竞争对手处以合理价格购买零件，这是一项可以独自进行的平价爱好。那些在口语表达上有困难的业余无线电爱好者可以通过代码交流来完全避免交谈。（一张早期业余无线电爱好者聚会的照片显示，两名男子隔着桌子相对而坐，用勺子敲击牛奶瓶发出点划信号进行交流。）但那些喜欢闲聊的人可以与其他爱好者"嚼碎布"（指长时间闲聊）数小时，使用着如同粉丝圈行话般诙谐且仪式化的词汇。无线电文化也是一个严格的精英制度，没有人关心你的外表或你在公共场合的举止是否优雅。 如果你懂得如何搭建并维护一个电台，那么欢迎你加入这场派对。



The bible of hams was a book called Calling CQ by an amateur named Clinton DeSoto. (The title was the phrase hams have used since the days of Marconi to invite any operator within earshot to reply.) His description of the ethos of amateur radio laid out a blueprint for a new kind of community that was ideally suited to mentoring gifted, socially awkward young people.

火腿族的圣经是一本名为《呼叫 CQ》的书，由业余爱好者克林顿·德索托所著。（书名中的“CQ”是自马可尼时代以来，火腿族用来邀请任何能听到信号的电台回复的短语。）他在书中描绘的业余无线电精神，为一种理想化的社群模式勾勒出蓝图——这种社群尤其适合培养那些天赋异禀却社交笨拙的年轻人。



The neophyte does not metamorphose easily into the full-fledged amateur. But when he does leave his chrysalis a new world is opened up to him. First he gets a new name—his radio call letters. Thenceforth he has a new identity—even a new personality and new social status. He is not known by the company he keeps nor by the clothes he wears, but by the signal he emits. He enters a new world whose qualifications for success are within his reach. Without a pedigree, a chauffeur, or an old master decorating his living room, he can become a prince—of the air.

新手要蜕变为真正的业余爱好者并非易事。但当他终于破茧而出时，一个崭新的世界便向他敞开。首先，他获得了一个新名字——他的无线电呼号。从此，他拥有了新的身份——甚至新的性格和新的社会地位。人们不再以他结交的朋友或他穿着的衣服来认识他，而是通过他发出的信号来了解他。他进入了一个新世界，在这里，成功的条件触手可及。无需显赫的家世、无需私人司机、也无需客厅里挂着大师名画，他就能成为一位王子——电波中的王子。



One of the radio-minded boys who answered DeSoto’s call—and would later be diagnosed with Asperger’s syndrome—was Robert Hedin, who received the Rensselaer Medal as the outstanding math and science student in high school but never fit in with his peers. He was drawn to amateur radio, he says, because it offered “an opportunity for people who are less sociable to socialize with others in a nonthreatening way.” It also furnished ways of receiving recognition for skills that came naturally to him, with global competitions for activities like “DXing” (making new contacts in foreign countries) and designing transmission equipment and antennas. The only body language involved was your “fist”—the term of art for how quickly and accurately you could input your keystrokes.

响应德索托号召的无线电爱好者中，有一位名叫罗伯特·赫丁的男孩——他后来被诊断出患有阿斯伯格综合征。赫丁在高中时曾获得伦斯勒奖章，是数学和科学领域的杰出学生，但始终无法融入同龄人群体。他表示，自己之所以被业余无线电吸引，是因为这项活动为"不善社交的人提供了以非对抗方式与他人交往的机会"。这项活动还为他与生俱来的技能提供了获得认可的途径，例如通过"DXing"（与外国建立新联系）等全球性竞赛，以及设计传输设备和天线。其中唯一的肢体语言就是你的"手键"——这个专业术语指的是你敲击按键的速度和精准度。



Wireless also offered ways into the job market for people who couldn’t depend on their ability to charm interviewers or cultivate networks of in-person contacts. Using a transmitter he’d built himself, Hedin had a chance encounter over the airwaves with the chief engineer of a local TV station, who said that he’d be willing to hire him if he obtained his FCC license within six months. (The station’s engineering department had already been thoroughly infiltrated by hams.) Hedin borrowed a guidebook from the public library, holed up in his ham shack, and earned his First Class Radiotelephone License within six months. He worked behind the scenes in broadcast television for the rest of his life.

无线通信也为那些无法依靠魅力打动面试官或建立人际网络的人提供了进入就业市场的途径。赫丁用自己制作的发射器，在电波中偶然遇到当地电视台的总工程师，对方表示如果他能在六个月内获得联邦通信委员会（FCC）执照，就愿意雇用他。（电视台的工程部门早已被业余无线电爱好者全面渗透。）赫丁从公共图书馆借来指南，埋头在他的业余无线电小屋里，在六个月内考取了第一级无线电话执照。此后他一生都在广播电视行业从事幕后工作。



After discovering that he and his sons were on the spectrum, Hedin joined the Global and Regional Asperger Syndrome Partnership (GRASP), one of the largest support groups for people with autism in the United States. Looking back, he feels certain that a number of hams he knew in the course of fifty-five years of surfing the airwaves would have qualified for a diagnosis.

在发现自己和儿子们属于孤独谱系群体后，赫丁加入了全球与地区阿斯伯格综合征伙伴组织（GRASP）——这是美国最大的孤独谱系群体支持团体之一。回顾往昔，他确信在五十五年电波遨游中认识的许多业余无线电爱好者，都符合诊断标准。



The society of hams also enabled shy introverts to study the protocols of personal engagement from a comfortable distance. “Through amateur radio . . . I’ve learned so much about communication between people. I’ve had the opportunity to observe and participate in the giving and getting process, which is what communication is all about,” recalled Lenore Jensen, who co-founded the Young Ladies’ Radio League in 1939 to encourage more women to join the conversation. By interacting with other hams over the airwaves, she learned to conduct herself in social situations gracefully and went on to become an actress celebrated for her performances in The Beverly Hillbillies, General Hospital, and Father Knows Best.

火腿社群也让害羞的内向者得以在舒适的距离外学习人际交往的规则。1939 年共同创立"少女无线电联盟"以鼓励更多女性参与对话的莱诺尔·詹森回忆道："通过业余无线电……我学到了太多关于人际沟通的知识。我有机会观察并参与给予与接收的过程，这正是沟通的本质所在。"通过电波与其他火腿爱好者互动，她学会了在社交场合中得体地表现自己，后来成为因出演《贝弗利山人》《综合医院》和《父亲最懂》而备受赞誉的女演员。



For some autistic people, the attraction of wireless was more strictly technical. They simply wanted to get their hands on the gadgets that fascinated them. The first word that a ham named Mark Goodman ever spoke, at age four, was ra-yo—radio. He found the soothing tones emanating from the console in the living room less intimidating than the voices of grown-ups. “That sonorous hulk of varnished wood,” he recalled, “became my constant companion.” Assembling a crystal radio kit that his uncle gave him for Christmas encouraged him to tackle more complicated projects, which gave him a sense of purpose in a world he experienced as “largely chaotic, bewildering, [and] often indifferent.”

对某些孤独谱系群体而言，无线电的魅力更多源于技术层面。他们纯粹渴望亲手摆弄那些令其着迷的装置。一位名叫马克·古德曼的业余无线电爱好者，四岁时说出的第一个词就是"拉-迪-欧"（radio）。他发现客厅收音机里流淌出的柔和声调，比成年人的嗓音更令人安心。"那台覆着清漆的木质共鸣箱，"他回忆道，"成了我永恒的伙伴。"组装叔叔送的圣诞礼物——矿石收音机套件——激励他挑战更复杂的项目，在这个他眼中"混乱、令人困惑、时常冷漠"的世界里，这赋予了他存在的意义。



Goodman spent hours in the local library studying technical manuals and started making pilgrimages to radio supply stores in nearby San Francisco. Eventually he was able to restore a broken console to good working order. This boosted his confidence and had an unexpected side effect: he became emotionally invested in the stories he heard on the radio.

古德曼常在图书馆研读技术手册，并开始朝圣般前往旧金山附近的无线电器材店。最终他成功修复了一台故障收音机，使其恢复如初。这不仅增强了自信，还带来意想不到的收获：他开始对收音机里听到的故事产生情感共鸣。



Sometimes I tuned in episodes crafted for those my age from serials about Tom Mix, Jack Armstrong, and Superman, which until then I’d almost never listened to. I lay back, suffused with rare contentment, eyes closed, absorbed by the sounds emanating from the hunk of wood, iron, paper, wires, glass and whatever else it took to convert radio waves back into spoken words and music that played into my imagination. It was magic, those sounds originating hundreds or thousands of miles away, all delivered to my ears via a vibrating cone of stiff, black paper.

有时我会收听那些为我这个年龄段制作的系列节目，比如汤姆·米克斯、杰克·阿姆斯特朗和超人的故事，而在此之前我几乎从未听过。我躺下来，沉浸在难得的满足感中，闭着眼睛，全神贯注于从那块由木头、铁、纸、电线、玻璃以及其他材料构成的物件中传出的声音——正是这些材料将无线电波重新转化为话语和音乐，流入我的想象。这简直是魔法，那些来自数百或数千英里之外的声音，通过一个坚硬的黑色纸锥振动，全部传送到我的耳中。



As gratifying as these experiences were, they couldn’t save Goodman from having a rough time in school at a time when the signs of autism were not widely recognized. Picked on by a sadistic teacher, he took refuge in reading “gobs of science-fiction . . . finding myself more at home on impossibly remote, imaginary worlds than the alien world I was strapped to.” When he was twelve, his mother took him to Stanford for an evaluation, but the psychiatrist told her that he would eventually grow out of his problems relating to other people.

尽管这些体验令人愉悦，却无法拯救古德曼在学校里度过的艰难时光——那时孤独谱系的迹象尚未被广泛认知。他遭到一位虐待狂老师的刁难，于是躲进“大量科幻小说”中寻求庇护，“发现自己更能在那些遥不可及的虚构世界里找到归属感，而不是被束缚在这个陌生的现实世界。”十二岁时，母亲带他去斯坦福大学接受评估，但精神科医生告诉她，他最终会克服与人交往的问题。



Finding a copy of Calling CQ in the school library, Goodman was thrilled by the story of a young ham named Walter Stiles who became a hero after a tragic flood in Pennsylvania. One night during a heavy rainstorm, Stiles picked up a weak signal from an operator near Renovo, Pennsylvania, calling “QRR”—the equivalent of SOS. He transcribed the rest of the operator’s message describing a town already underwater, with more than two thousand people in immediate need of rescue and/or medical attention. “AIRPLANE LANDING IMPOSSIBLE COMMA DROP BY PARACHUTE,” the desperate transmission concluded, and then the signal failed. After alerting the Red Cross, Stiles and a group of friends set off for Renovo with a truck full of medical supplies, food, and a waterproof transmitter; finding the bridges washed out, they carted their equipment for miles to the site of the disaster. There, Stiles manned his telegraph key for forty-eight hours, relaying messages to the outside world through chains of other amateurs.

在学校图书馆找到一本《呼叫 CQ》时，古德曼被书中一位名叫沃尔特·斯泰尔斯的年轻业余无线电爱好者的故事深深吸引——他在宾夕法尼亚州一场惨烈洪灾中成为了英雄。某个暴雨倾盆的夜晚，斯泰尔斯捕捉到雷诺沃附近一位操作员发出的微弱信号，对方正呼叫着等同于 SOS 的"QRR"代码。他逐字记录下操作员后续的求救信息：一座城镇已遭洪水淹没，两千多人急需救援和医疗救助。"飞机无法降落，请空投物资，"这段绝望的通讯以这句话告终，随后信号中断。在通知红十字会之后，斯泰尔斯与一群朋友载着满车医疗物资、食物和防水发报机赶往雷诺沃。发现桥梁被冲毁后，他们扛着设备跋涉数英里抵达灾区。在那里，斯泰尔斯连续四十八小时坚守电报键，通过其他业余无线电爱好者组成的接力网络，向外界传递着信息。



Reading about the adventures of these young heroes, Goodman became determined to join their ranks. Soon he had built his own rig and earned his FCC license. But communicating by wireless could not provide all of the support and guidance he needed. He would spend the next several decades flunking out of school, losing jobs, going in and out of psychiatric institutions, and struggling to survive on disability.

阅读这些年轻英雄的冒险故事后，古德曼决心加入他们的行列。他很快便组装了自己的无线电设备，并获得了美国联邦通信委员会（FCC）的执照。然而，无线通信无法提供他所需要的全部支持与指导。在接下来的几十年里，他不断辍学、失业、进出精神病院，并依靠残障补助艰难求生。



He eventually sought help from more than twenty psychiatrists, psychologists, and therapists, but until the scope of the autism diagnosis was broadened to include adults, they couldn’t make sense of the challenges he was facing. Finally, at age seventy, Goodman was able to get the diagnosis and access to services he needed. Joining a support group for adults run by the Asperger’s Association of New England, he says, was “like coming ashore after a life of bobbing up and down in a sea that seemed to stretch to infinity in all directions.”

他最终向二十多位精神科医生、心理学家和治疗师寻求帮助，但直到孤独症诊断范围扩大到涵盖成年人，他们才理解他所面临的困境。最终，在七十岁时，古德曼获得了所需的诊断和相应的服务。加入新英格兰阿斯伯格综合征协会为成年人举办的互助小组后，他说这感觉就像“在看似无边无际的大海中沉浮一生后，终于登上了岸。”



IV



Gernsback died in 1967 as many of his predictions were coming true. TV—which made its public debut in 1928, in an experimental broadcast hosted by Gernsback’s radio station WRNY—was ubiquitous, and 172 spacecraft left the earth’s surface that year alone. By then, a new generation of visionaries raised on do-it-yourself electronics and pulp science fiction was laying the groundwork for a global network that would make the wireless revolution look quaint.

根斯巴克于 1967 年去世，彼时他的许多预言正逐渐成真。电视——1928 年通过根斯巴克的 WRNY 电台实验广播首次公开亮相——已无处不在，仅那一年就有 172 艘航天器离开地球表面。到那时，新一代受 DIY 电子学和低俗科幻小说熏陶的梦想家们，正为构建一个让无线革命显得过时的全球网络奠定基础。



The modern digital age began at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) in the late 1950s, where a mathematician and engineer named John McCarthy offered the first undergraduate course in computer programming. Instead of seeing the hulking mainframes of the day as glorified adding machines, he pondered ways of programming them so they could act in creative ways, learn to adapt to their environments, be linked in complex networks, and evolve to become smarter on their own. To describe this dynamic vision of computing, he coined the term artificial intelligence (AI).

现代数字时代始于 20 世纪 50 年代末的麻省理工学院（MIT），一位名叫约翰·麦卡锡的数学家兼工程师在那里开设了首个计算机编程本科课程。他没有将当时笨重的大型计算机视为被美化的加法机，而是思考如何对它们进行编程，使其能够以创造性的方式运作、学会适应环境、连接成复杂网络，并自主进化得更加智能。为了描述这种动态的计算愿景，他创造了“人工智能”（AI）这一术语。



A bearish man with thick black glasses, an unruly beard, and a crew cut that aspired to Mohawkhood, McCarthy was a legendary eccentric on a campus full of eccentrics. He had a habit of furiously pacing while thinking; if he was asked a question, he might just walk away without saying good-bye, only to reappear several days later with an answer as if the conversation had never been interrupted. If his colleagues wanted him to read a paper, instead of bringing it to his office where it would inevitably get lost, they would leave a copy on their own desk, and as McCarthy perambulated around the building, he would eventually stroll in, pick it up, and march off to read it, usually without uttering a word.

一位戴着厚黑框眼镜、留着桀骜不驯的胡须、发型是向往莫西干头的平头男子，麦卡锡是这所充满怪人的校园里一位传奇的怪人。他习惯在思考时狂躁地踱步；如果有人向他提问，他可能会不告而别，几天后才带着答案重新出现，仿佛对话从未中断。如果同事想让他阅读一篇论文，他们不会把论文送到他那注定会弄丢的办公室，而是把副本留在自己的桌上。当麦卡锡在楼里四处游荡时，他会溜达进来，拿起论文，然后大步走开去阅读，通常一言不发。



In Scientific Temperaments, writer Philip Hilts described his first encounter with McCarthy as unnerving:

在《科学气质》一书中，作家菲利普·希尔斯描述了他与麦卡锡的初次见面，称其令人不安：



His greeting consisted of an expectant stare. No words at all. Discourse by his visitor brought from McCarthy a set of mumbles, which slowly increased in volume and clarity, like the sound of a man emerging from a cave. Only when his mind reached the surface was something similar to normal conversation possible. His colleagues confirmed this: that John McCarthy’s mind is a vehicle streamlined for rapid passage through the fluid of thought, capable of maneuvering with little outside friction. But in the open social terrain, his streamlined concentration becomes awkward and unwieldy.

他的问候方式只是充满期待地凝视，一言不发。来访者的交谈让麦卡锡发出一连串含糊不清的咕哝声，这些声音逐渐变得响亮清晰，如同一个人从洞穴中走出时的声响。只有当他的思维浮出水面时，才可能进行近乎正常的对话。他的同事们证实了这一点：约翰·麦卡锡的思维如同一辆为快速穿越思想流体而设计的流线型车辆，能够以极小的外部摩擦灵活运转。但在开放的社会地形中，他那流线型的专注力却变得笨拙而难以驾驭。



McCarthy was equally ungainly in physical space, admitting to “an accumulated lack of success” in his PE classes at college. But he didn’t let that stop him from taking up mountain climbing, sailing, and piloting private planes. A fellow pilot recalled McCarthy coaching himself aloud through each step of a final approach—“prop feathered . . . mixture full rich . . . airspeed check . . . okay, now we’ll do this”—only to realize that he had already landed and the plane was racing along the airstrip.

麦卡锡在物理空间中也同样笨拙，他承认自己在大学的体育课上“屡屡受挫”。但这并未阻止他尝试登山、航海和驾驶私人飞机。一位飞行员同伴回忆道，麦卡锡在最后进近阶段会大声指导自己每一步操作——“螺旋桨顺桨……混合比调至全富油……空速检查……好了，现在我们要做这个”——结果却发现自己已经着陆，飞机正沿着跑道疾驰。



But his life’s work was never in doubt. When he was eight years old, McCarthy decided that he wanted to be a scientist, spurred on by Gernsbackian how-to guides like Electricity for Boys. His mother was a suffragette and his father was a union organizer and a member of the Communist party; their idealism would infuse his hope for computers as facilitators of democracy at a time when many left-wingers had a visceral distrust of technology. In high school, McCarthy taught himself calculus from college textbooks. At fifteen, he enrolled at the California Institute of Technology. There, he began thinking about designing machines that could simulate the human acquisition of knowledge, an interest he pursued further in his graduate work at Princeton.

但他一生的事业从未动摇。八岁时，麦卡锡受《男孩电学》这类根斯巴克式指南的启发，立志成为科学家。母亲是女权运动者，父亲是工会组织者兼共产党员——他们的理想主义精神，在他心中注入了对计算机作为民主助推器的期待，而当时许多左翼人士对技术怀有本能的不信任。高中时期，麦卡锡通过大学教材自学微积分。十五岁进入加州理工学院后，他开始构思能模拟人类知识获取过程的机器，这一兴趣在普林斯顿大学的研究生阶段得到深化。



In addition to his groundbreaking work on artificial intelligence, McCarthy was instrumental in developing the concept of time-sharing, which allowed multiple users to gain access to centralized computing resources through a distributed network of terminals. He advocated installing a terminal in every home, convinced that someday it would be commonplace for people to use them to read instantly updated news, order books by their favorite authors, buy plane tickets and reserve hotel rooms, edit documents remotely, and determine the efficacy of medical treatments by reading patient reviews. While this vision of information as a centralized utility, like water or power, was eventually overshadowed by the invention of personal computers and mobile devices, it survives in the vast networks of servers (“the cloud”) that make the Web possible.

除了在人工智能领域的开创性工作外，麦卡锡还推动了分时概念的发展，这一概念允许多个用户通过分布式终端网络访问集中式计算资源。他主张在每个家庭安装终端，坚信终有一天人们会普遍使用这些终端来阅读即时更新的新闻、订购喜爱作者的新书、购买机票和预订酒店、远程编辑文档，并通过阅读患者评价来判断医疗效果。尽管这种将信息视为像水或电力一样的集中化公共设施的愿景，最终因个人电脑和移动设备的发明而黯然失色，但它仍存在于支撑互联网的庞大服务器网络（即"云端"）之中。



One of the main hangouts for his students at MIT was the Tech Model Railroad Club in Building 20, a temporary plywood facility built to aid the war effort that had been taken over by geeks thrilled to discover a building on campus where they could saw holes in the floor without prompting concern. The ranks of TMRC were divided between the artsy club members who worked on the layout (a picturesque replica of small-town America) and the habitually unwashed, Coke-guzzling, Chinese-takeout-eating obsessives who ran the fantastically elaborate apparatus that made the whole thing go. The complex tangle of wires, switches, and relays under the layout—scavenged out of parts from a local electronic-surplus store—was known as “the System,” and the crew that managed it was called the Signals and Power Committee (SPC).

他在麻省理工学院的学生们常去的一个主要聚集地是 20 号楼的技术模型铁路俱乐部，这是一座为支援战争而建的临时胶合板建筑，后来被一群极客占据，他们兴奋地发现校园里有一栋楼，可以在地板上锯洞而不会引起担忧。TMRC 的成员分为两类：一类是负责布局设计的艺术型俱乐部成员（一个描绘美国小镇风景如画的复制品），另一类是习惯不洗澡、狂饮可乐、吃中式外卖的痴迷者，他们操控着让整个系统运转的极其精密的装置。布局下方错综复杂的电线、开关和继电器网络——这些零件是从当地一家电子剩余商店淘来的——被称为“系统”，而管理它的团队则被称为信号与电力委员会（SPC）。



Building 20 was nicknamed “the Magical Incubator,” and the particular brand of magic incubating there in the late 1950s was hacker culture. In the lexicon of TMRC, a “good hack” was some feat of technical virtuosity undertaken for pure pleasure rather than necessity, like programming a mainframe the size of a dozen refrigerators to play a song. As hard-core fans of science fiction, ham radio, and Japanese monster movies, MIT’s proto-hackers were addicted to obscure lingo for its own sake, and jargon coined by the SPC (such as mung, kluge, cruft, and foo) proliferated widely through computer culture for the next several decades. Between marathon hands-on sessions to improve the System, McCarthy’s students devised the first program that enabled a computer to play chess well—a good hack indeed.

20 号楼被称为“魔法孵化器”，而 20 世纪 50 年代末在那里孵化的独特魔法正是黑客文化。在 TMRC 的术语中，“好黑客”指的是纯粹出于乐趣而非必要而完成的技术壮举，比如让一台有十几台冰箱那么大的大型机播放一首歌。作为科幻小说、业余无线电和日本怪兽电影的狂热粉丝，麻省理工学院的早期黑客们沉迷于晦涩的行话本身，而 SPC 创造的术语（如 mung、kluge、cruft 和 foo）在接下来的几十年里在计算机文化中广泛传播。在马拉松式的动手改进系统过程中，麦卡锡的学生们设计了第一个能让计算机下好棋的程序——这确实是一个好黑客。



McCarthy’s most lasting contribution to his field was Lisp, a high-level programming language that enabled AI researchers to represent an unprecedented range of real-world events in their code. Unlike most programming languages of its vintage (with the sole exception of Fortran), it is still in wide use. But McCarthy was ready for a change in the early 1960s; when Stanford offered him a full professorship, he took it. He sold his house in Cambridge to two young Harvard professors promoting a tool for hacking the operating system of the human brain: LSD. Timothy Leary and Richard Alpert turned the alcove of McCarthy’s old library (which contained equal parts of “science, fiction, and science fiction”) into a rabbit hole that went down to a trip room lined with pillows, black lights, and psychedelic art.

麦卡锡对该领域最持久的贡献是 Lisp，这是一种高级编程语言，使人工智能研究者能够在代码中呈现前所未有的现实世界事件。与同时代的大多数编程语言（唯一的例外是 Fortran）不同，它至今仍被广泛使用。但麦卡锡在 20 世纪 60 年代初就准备做出改变；当斯坦福大学向他提供正教授职位时，他接受了。他将剑桥的房子卖给了两位年轻的哈佛教授，他们正在推广一种用于破解人脑操作系统的工具：LSD。蒂莫西·利里和理查德·阿尔珀特将麦卡锡旧图书馆的凹室（其中"科学、小说和科幻小说"各占三分之一）变成了一个通往迷幻室的兔子洞，室内铺满枕头、配有黑光灯和迷幻艺术。



McCarthy thrived in the hothouse of innovative ideas and technology that would soon be dubbed Silicon Valley, launching the famed Stanford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (SAIL). By the early 1980s, he was already living in the future he foresaw a decade earlier. By typing in a few commands on the terminal on his desk, he could fetch his e-mail, listen to the radio, revise and spell-check a paper on a remote server, play chess or Go, print out a document in Elvish (he wrote an unpublished sequel to The Lord of the Rings that was sympathetic to the orcs), run searches on stories moving over the Associated Press wire, or fetch an up-to-date list of restaurant recommendations (called “YUMYUM”) from programmers all over the world. His online .sig (signature) file and the license plate cover on his car featured the datacentric motto “Do the arithmetic or be doomed to talk nonsense.”

麦卡锡在不久后被称为硅谷的创新思想与技术温室中蓬勃发展，创立了著名的斯坦福人工智能实验室（SAIL）。到 20 世纪 80 年代初，他已经生活在十年前预见到的未来中。只需在桌上的终端输入几条命令，他就能收取电子邮件、收听广播、在远程服务器上修改并检查论文拼写、下国际象棋或围棋、用精灵语打印文档（他写了一部未出版的《魔戒》续集，对半兽人抱有同情）、搜索美联社新闻动态，或从全球程序员那里获取最新的餐厅推荐列表（名为“YUMYUM”）。他的在线签名文件和汽车牌照框上都印着以数据为中心的信条：“要么计算，要么注定胡说八道。”



Was McCarthy on the spectrum? He certainly displayed many of the classic features of Asperger’s syndrome: his brusqueness, his single-minded focus to the point of seeming rude, his physical clumsiness, and his habit of coaching himself aloud when under stress. He also had many clearly positive traits that Asperger associated with autism: a fascination with logic and complex machines, a gift for puns and aphorisms, an uncompromising personal ethic, and the ability to solve problems from angles that his more socially oriented colleagues missed. But McCarthy would have had no need to seek out a diagnosis, because he was able to carve out a niche in an emerging field that was perfectly suited to his strengths while being tolerant—indeed, appreciative—of his many eccentricities.

麦卡锡是否属于孤独谱系？他确实展现出许多阿斯伯格综合征的典型特征：言语生硬、专注到近乎失礼、动作笨拙，以及压力下会不自觉地自言自语。同时他也具备阿斯伯格所描述的诸多积极特质：对逻辑与复杂机械的痴迷、擅长双关语与格言、坚守个人道德准则，以及能从社交型同事忽略的角度解决问题。但麦卡锡无需寻求诊断，因为他已在一个新兴领域找到了完美契合自身优势的位置——这个领域不仅包容他的种种怪癖，甚至对此颇为欣赏。



His labs at MIT and Stanford were elaborate playgrounds for his extraordinary mind, as Cavendish’s estate on Clapham Common was for his own. They also became magnets for other scruffy geniuses who were equally committed to the vision of a world empowered by access to computing—including two young members of a group called the Homebrew Computer Club named Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak, who would go on to become the founders of Apple.

他在麻省理工学院和斯坦福大学的实验室，就像卡文迪什在克拉珀姆公地的庄园一样，成为他非凡思维的精心游乐场。这些实验室也吸引了其他同样致力于实现"人人皆可接触计算机"愿景的邋遢天才——其中包括"家酿计算机俱乐部"的两名年轻成员史蒂夫·乔布斯和史蒂夫·沃兹尼亚克，他们后来成为了苹果公司的创始人。



The culture of Silicon Valley began adapting to the presence of a high concentration of people with autistic traits even before the term Asperger’s syndrome was invented. In 1984, a therapist named Jean Hollands wrote a popular self-help book for women called The Silicon Syndrome about navigating what she called “high-tech relationships.” She described a distinctive breed of intensely driven “sci-tech” men who loved to tinker with machines, were slow to pick up on emotional cues, had few if any close friends outside their professional circles, approached life in rigorously logical and literal fashion like Mr. Spock, and tried to address problems in intimate relationships by “seeking data.” (Holland confessed that her husband—a proud sci-tech man himself—viewed her as a member of an “alien culture.”)

硅谷文化早在阿斯伯格综合征这一术语诞生之前，就已开始适应高密度孤独谱系特质人群的存在。1984 年，一位名叫琼·霍兰兹的治疗师为女性撰写了一本名为《硅谷综合征》的畅销自助手册，指导她们应对她所称的"高科技人际关系"。她描述了一种独特的、极度专注的"科技男"群体：他们痴迷于摆弄机器，对情感暗示反应迟钝，在专业圈子之外几乎没有亲密朋友，像斯波克先生一样以严谨的逻辑和字面意义对待生活，并试图通过"收集数据"来解决亲密关系中的问题。（霍兰兹坦言，她那以科技男自居的丈夫，始终将她视为"异星文化"的成员。）



When the book was published, Hollands received sympathetic letters from the wives of engineers, coders, and math and physics professors all over the world. François Mitterrand, the president of France, visited her office in Mountain View with his wife, Danielle, to express his urgent concern that French couples might face the same challenges if computers became popular in Europe. There was no mention of autism in the book, but ten years later Hollands could have swapped the term Asperger’s syndrome for silicon syndrome and barely changed another word in the text.

这本书出版后，霍兰兹收到了来自世界各地工程师、程序员、数学和物理学教授妻子的同情信。法国总统弗朗索瓦·密特朗携夫人达妮埃尔访问她在山景城的办公室，急切地表达了对欧洲普及计算机后，法国夫妇可能面临同样挑战的担忧。书中并未提及孤独症，但十年后，霍兰兹只需将书中"阿斯伯格综合征"一词替换为"硅谷综合征"，其他文字几乎无需改动。



Ultimately, the future of computing belonged not to the Big Iron mainframes and networks of “dumb terminals” that McCarthy loved but to the smart little machines that the members of the Homebrew Computer Club were soldering together in their garages. The task of claiming the power of the computing for the many remained to be done by Internet pioneers like Vint Cerf and Tim Berners-Lee—and an autistic engineer who launched the first social network for the people in a record store in Berkeley.

最终，计算技术的未来并不属于麦卡锡钟爱的大型主机和"哑终端"网络，而是属于家酿计算机俱乐部成员们在车库里焊接的智能小型机器。将计算能力普及大众的使命，最终由互联网先驱文特·瑟夫和蒂姆·伯纳斯-李——以及一位在伯克利唱片店为大众创建首个社交网络的孤独谱系工程师——共同完成。



V



Lee Felsenstein had engineering in his blood. His grandfather, William T. Price, made a fortune by shrinking the design of diesel engines so they could fit into trains and trucks. At Cornell, Price was known for giving lectures in short pants and was described by his classmates as a combination of Sherlock Holmes and A. J. Raffles, the gentleman thief created as the anti–Sherlock Holmes by Conan Doyle’s brother-in-law, E. W. Hornung. After graduation, he embarked on a bike tour of Europe, returning just a couple of days before his wedding. Price was confused that his fiancée was upset; hadn’t he come back in time as he said he would?

李·费尔森斯坦的血液里流淌着工程学的基因。他的祖父威廉·T·普莱斯通过缩小柴油发动机的设计，使其能够装入火车和卡车，从而发家致富。在康奈尔大学，普莱斯以穿着短裤授课而闻名，同学们形容他是夏洛克·福尔摩斯与 A·J·拉弗尔斯（柯南·道尔的妹夫 E·W·霍农创造的、作为反夏洛克·福尔摩斯形象的绅士盗贼）的结合体。毕业后，他踏上了欧洲自行车之旅，直到婚礼前两三天才返回。普莱斯对未婚妻的恼怒感到困惑：他不是如约及时回来了吗？



Like McCarthy, Felsenstein was also a Red Diaper Baby: his parents were members of the Communist party in the 1950s, and his father, Jacob, was a commercial artist who always made sure that there were plenty of art supplies around for his three children. In third grade, Lee would sketch exhaust pipes and compressors while coming up with schemes for redesigning automobiles to reduce air pollution. When a teacher accused him of daydreaming in class, he replied, “I’m not daydreaming, I’m inventing.”

和麦卡锡一样，费尔森斯坦也是“红色尿布婴儿”：他的父母在 20 世纪 50 年代是美国共产党成员，父亲雅各布是一名商业艺术家，总是确保三个孩子身边有充足的美术用品。三年级时，李一边构思重新设计汽车以减少空气污染的方案，一边画着排气管和压缩机的草图。当老师指责他上课走神时，他回答道：“我不是在走神，我是在发明创造。”



When he was eleven, Felsenstein inherited a half-assembled crystal radio kit from his older brother, strung up an antenna, and got it working. His first sight of a computer—a UNIVAC clacking away behind glass at the Franklin Institute Science Museum in Philadelphia—was so entrancing that he became a member of the museum so he could hang out near the machine all day.

十一岁那年，费尔森斯坦从哥哥那里继承了一套半成品矿石收音机套件，他架起天线，成功让它发出了声音。他第一次见到计算机——一台在费城富兰克林科学博物馆玻璃柜后咔嗒作响的 UNIVAC——便被深深吸引，为此他加入了博物馆会员，只为能整天待在那台机器旁边。



Then a friend of his father’s gave him a precious gift: a correspondence course in radio and TV repair that came complete with a voltmeter, an oscilloscope, and other apparatus that Felsenstein thought he’d never be able to afford, along with lessons on managing your own business. He started making house calls in the neighborhood to fix broken TVs as his basement filled up with glowing tubes and busted consoles, which he cannibalized for his experiments. He began to think of the basement as a holy sanctuary—his own personal monastery of technology. One night, he had a dream of being enmeshed in a luminous web of interconnected devices that were all working perfectly. He ended up running the UNIVAC exhibit at the institute in the summer between high school and college.

后来，父亲的一位朋友送给他一份珍贵的礼物：一套无线电与电视维修函授课程，附赠了万用表、示波器以及其他费尔森斯坦原以为永远买不起的仪器，还包含了经营小生意的课程。他开始在社区上门修理坏掉的电视机，地下室堆满了发光的电子管和破损的机壳，他从中拆取零件用于自己的实验。他渐渐将地下室视为神圣的庇护所——属于他个人的技术修道院。某夜，他梦见自己置身于一张由互联设备组成的发光网络中，所有设备都完美运转。最终，在高中毕业与大学入学之间的那个夏天，他负责运营了研究所里的 UNIVAC 展览。



Felsenstein was also inspired by his father’s work of organizing a neighborhood council to reform the zoning laws. When civil rights activists in the South began conducting sit-ins at lunch counters to protest segregation, he picketed a Woolworth’s to show his support. Enrolling at the University of California at Berkeley, he joined the anti–Vietnam War movement, which was just getting off the ground. The administration eventually clamped down on students staffing information tables at Bancroft and Telegraph Avenues, claiming that on-campus political activities were restricted to membership in the Democratic and Republican clubs. When campus police arrested a civil rights activist for refusing to show his ID, three thousand enraged students surrounded the car and prevented it from moving for thirty-six hours until the charges were dropped.

费尔森斯坦也受到父亲组织社区委员会改革分区法规工作的启发。当南方民权活动人士开始在午餐柜台静坐抗议种族隔离时，他通过纠察伍尔沃斯商店来表达支持。进入加州大学伯克利分校后，他加入了刚刚起步的反越战运动。校方最终镇压了在班克罗夫特大道和电报大道设置信息桌的学生，声称校园政治活动仅限于民主党和共和党俱乐部的成员。当校园警察因一名民权活动人士拒绝出示身份证而将其逮捕时，三千名愤怒的学生包围了警车，阻止其移动长达 36 小时，直到指控被撤销。



In December 1964, students demanded that the administration negotiate its regulation of on-campus political activities, conducting a sit-in at Sproul Hall. The leader of the emerging Free Speech Movement (FSM), Mario Savio, delivered a speech to the crowd that was so passionate it became a rallying cry for antiwar protesters worldwide: “There’s a time when the operation of the machine becomes so odious, makes you so sick at heart, that you can’t take part. You can’t even passively take part. And you’ve got to put your bodies upon the gears and upon the wheels . . . upon the levers, upon all the apparatus, and you’ve got to make it stop.” That night, nearly eight hundred students were carted off to jail. The resulting furor nearly shut down the university.

1964 年 12 月，学生们在斯普劳尔大厅举行静坐抗议，要求校方就校园政治活动管理规定进行谈判。新兴自由言论运动领袖马里奥·萨维奥向人群发表了激情澎湃的演讲，这场演说后来成为全球反战抗议者的战斗口号："当这台机器的运转变得如此可憎，让你痛彻心扉时，你不能再参与其中。你甚至不能被动参与。你必须用身体卡住齿轮和轮毂...卡住操纵杆，卡住所有装置，你必须让它停下来。"当晚，近八百名学生被押送监狱。由此引发的轩然大波几乎导致这所大学停摆。



The Free Speech Movement adopted Felsenstein, then nineteen, as its geek-in-residence. (Knowing how to run a mimeograph machine clinched the job.) One night, a group of students burst through the door shouting that police had surrounded the campus in preparation for another wave of mass arrests. One of the organizers turned to Felsenstein and said, “Quick, build us a police radio!” He knew that it wouldn’t be that simple, but the moment was a revelation for Felsenstein. “I realized that I had made a mistake about my position in society. Up to that point, I was waiting for orders from highly intelligent people who knew much more than I did about politics, sociology, and other subjects,” he says. “But then I realized that these people had no clue about what was actually possible with technology. That was my job: knowing what was possible and saying, ‘Well, you can’t have that, but you could have this instead.’ So instead of waiting for orders, I started defining what was technologically possible.”

自由言论运动接纳了当时 19 岁的费尔森斯坦作为驻场极客（会操作油印机让他得到了这份工作）。某天深夜，一群学生破门而入，高喊警察已包围校园准备新一轮大规模逮捕。一名组织者转向费尔森斯坦说："快，给我们造台警用无线电！"他明白这绝非易事，但那一刻让他恍然大悟。"我意识到自己搞错了社会定位。此前我一直等待那些在政治学、社会学等领域远比我博学的精英发号施令，"他说，"但此刻我明白，这些人根本不懂技术能实现什么。我的使命是：洞悉技术可能性，告诉他们'那个办不到，但这个可以替代'。于是我不再等待指令，开始定义技术能创造什么。"



The telephones at FSM headquarters became the nerve center for the emerging counterculture in Berkeley, but the organization’s filing system was very inefficient. If someone called up and offered to fix activists’ cars for free, a note would get tacked up on a wall that was already cluttered with similar notes. Felsenstein felt that there had to be a better way. He also observed that the role of leafleting on campus was changing. In 1964, when distributing leaflets was forbidden, a student passing them out would talk to each person who received one and inform them about the relevant issues. By 1967, however, they had become a crude broadcast medium. FSM activists would simply paste leaflets on walls in eye-catching patterns and hope that passersby would stop to read them.

FSM 总部的电话成为了伯克利新兴反文化运动的神经中枢，但该组织的文件归档系统效率极低。如果有人打电话来主动提出免费为活动人士修车，一张便条就会被钉在已经贴满类似便条的墙上。费尔森斯坦觉得一定有更好的办法。他还注意到校园里分发传单的方式正在发生变化。1964 年，当分发传单被禁止时，派发传单的学生会与每位接收者交谈，告知他们相关议题。然而到了 1967 年，传单已沦为粗放的广播媒介。FSM 的活动人士只是将传单以醒目的图案贴在墙上，希望路人驻足阅读。



It occurred to Felsenstein that if the counterculture was serious about building a new society that was not based on mass consumption and vacuous spectacle, it would have to design new forms of media that empowered individuals and local communities instead of relying on old broadcast models. The decentralized, user-driven future of computing was already taking shape in his mind.

费尔森斯坦意识到，如果反文化运动真要建立一个不依赖大众消费和空洞景观的新社会，就必须设计出赋能个人与本地社区的新型媒体形式，而非沿用旧有的广播模式。去中心化、用户驱动的计算未来已在他脑海中初具雏形。



—



FELSENSTEIN DIDN’T KNOW YET that he was autistic. As far as the psychiatric establishment was concerned, people like him didn’t exist. He just knew that his girlfriends often complained that he didn’t respond appropriately in social situations and that he never felt at home among people. By 1968, the stress of being an undiagnosed autistic in the middle of a cultural revolution had taken a heavy toll. After a crash into major depression, Felsenstein dropped out of Berkeley, commenced psychotherapy, and took a job at Ampex as a junior engineer.

费尔森斯坦当时还不知道自己属于孤独谱系。在精神病学界的认知中，像他这样的人根本不存在。他只知道自己总被女友抱怨在社交场合反应不当，也从未在人群中感到自在。到 1968 年，作为未被确诊的孤独谱系者身处文化革命浪潮中，这种压力已造成严重伤害。在陷入重度抑郁后，费尔森斯坦从伯克利辍学，开始接受心理治疗，并在安培公司担任初级工程师。



By reading manuals, he taught himself the state of the art of programming at the time: punching holes in paper tape that corresponded to individual bits and feeding the tape into a reader that sent commands to a computer. There was no operating system and no software—just spools of perforated tape. Felsenstein describes the first time he successfully programmed a computer to type the letter A as a “transcendent experience.”

通过阅读手册，他自学了当时最先进的编程技术：在纸带上对应每个比特的位置打孔，再将纸带送入读取器向计算机发送指令。没有操作系统，没有软件——只有一卷卷穿孔纸带。费尔森斯坦描述自己首次成功让计算机打出字母 A 的经历时，称之为"超验体验"。



While he was at Ampex, a researcher from Stanford named Doug Engelbart gave a presentation at a conference in San Francisco that would go down in history as “the Mother of All Demos.” Engelbart and McCarthy worked on opposite sides of campus and represented opposite sides of a philosophical divide. While McCarthy wanted to design machines that were powerful enough to replace human intelligence, Engelbart wanted to figure out ways of using computers to augment it. Over the course of ninety minutes, Engelbart set forth the fundamental elements of the modern digital age in a single seamless package: graphical user interfaces, multiple window displays, mouse-driven navigation, word processing, hypertext linking, videoconferencing, and real-time collaboration. The concepts in Engelbart’s presentation—refined by the work of Alan Kay and others at Xerox PARC—inspired Steve Jobs to build the Macintosh, the first personal computer (PC) designed for a mass market.

在安派克斯公司工作期间，斯坦福大学研究员道格·恩格尔巴特在旧金山的一场会议上进行了演示，这场演示后来被载入史册，被称为"所有演示之母"。恩格尔巴特和麦卡锡分属校园两端，也代表着哲学分歧的两端。麦卡锡希望设计出足以取代人类智能的强大机器，而恩格尔巴特则致力于探索利用计算机增强人类智能的方法。在九十分钟的演示中，恩格尔巴特将现代数字时代的基本要素完美融合呈现：图形用户界面、多窗口显示、鼠标导航、文字处理、超文本链接、视频会议和实时协作。恩格尔巴特演示中的这些概念——经艾伦·凯等人在施乐帕克研究中心完善后——启发了史蒂夫·乔布斯打造出麦金塔电脑，这是首款面向大众市场的个人计算机。



Meanwhile, the counterculture of the Bay Area was also evolving, though technologically it was still stuck in the precomputer era, depending on classified ads in underground newspapers, bulletin boards, telephone switchboards, and the post office for community organizing. It disturbed Felsenstein that valuable information was perpetually getting lost: if someone compiled a list of essential names or a box of helpful index cards and then went off to India to find a guru, the data he or she had accumulated tended to go astray. It occurred to him that computer networks could perform many of the functions of personal filing systems but much faster and better—and they didn’t forget anything.

与此同时，旧金山湾区的反主流文化也在演变，尽管在技术层面仍停留在前计算机时代，依赖地下报纸的分类广告、公告栏、电话交换台和邮局来组织社群。费尔森斯坦感到困扰的是，有价值的信息总是不断流失：如果有人整理了一份重要联系人名单或一盒有用的索引卡片，随后前往印度寻找导师，他或她积累的数据往往会不知所踪。他意识到，计算机网络可以承担个人档案系统的许多功能，而且速度更快、效果更好——并且它们不会遗忘任何信息。



Felsenstein was also fascinated by social critic Ivan Illich’s notion of promoting the use of tools that would facilitate “conviviality”—one of many aspects of social interaction that Felsenstein had always found difficult and confusing. With two fellow programmers named Efrem Lipkin and Mark Szpakowski, he began exploring ways of augmenting the community switchboards that had sprung up in subcultural hot spots like Berkeley and the Haight-Ashbury in San Francisco. The biggest practical obstacle to this noble undertaking was finding an affordable computer that was sufficiently powerful to do the job. That problem was solved when a programmer at a bustling commune in San Francisco called Project One wangled the long-term lease of an SDS 940 (retail cost: $300,000) from the Transamerica Corporation. This mighty machine—which was twenty-four feet long and required a fleet of air conditioners to stay cool—already had a storied history. It was the first computer designed to support McCarthy’s time-sharing scheme directly. It was also the computer Engelbart had used to power the Mother of All Demos. It was a chunk of hardware with unusually good karma.

费尔森斯坦同样着迷于社会批评家伊万·伊里奇提出的理念——推广能促进“共融性”的工具，而社交互动中的诸多层面正是费尔森斯坦始终觉得困难且困惑的。他与两位程序员同行埃弗雷姆·利普金和马克·斯帕科夫斯基开始探索如何增强那些在伯克利和旧金山海特-阿什伯里等亚文化热点地区涌现的社区交换台。这项崇高事业面临的最大现实障碍，是找到一台价格适中且性能足够强大的计算机。当旧金山一个名为“一号计划”的繁华公社的程序员从泛美公司成功争取到 SDS 940（零售价 30 万美元）的长期租赁权后，这个问题迎刃而解。这台长 24 英尺、需要配备多台空调才能保持冷却的庞然大物，本身已拥有传奇历史：它是首台直接支持麦卡锡分时共享方案的计算机，也是恩格尔巴特用来驱动“所有演示之母”的机器。这块硬件承载着非同寻常的机缘。



The hacker subculture incubated at MIT was thriving in places like SAIL, Xerox PARC, and the now legendary garages of Cupertino and San José. Soon Whole Earth Catalog impresario Stewart Brand would unleash this subculture on the unsuspecting inhabitants of Greater Mundania with the ultimate endorsement in Rolling Stone: “Computers are coming to the people. That’s good news, maybe the best since psychedelics.” The focus of the article was Spacewar, the seminal computer game developed in 1961 by four of McCarthy’s students high on the fumes of pulp science fiction. But one of the most compelling things about the game, Brand noticed, was the insidious way that it turned a glorified number cruncher into a “communication device between humans.”

在麻省理工学院孕育的黑客亚文化，在斯坦福人工智能实验室、施乐帕克研究中心，以及如今已成传奇的库比蒂诺和圣何塞车库中蓬勃发展。很快，《全球概览》杂志的掌门人斯图尔特·布兰德将这股亚文化释放到对"大曼达尼亚"世界毫无防备的居民面前，并在《滚石》杂志上给出了终极背书："计算机正在走向大众。这是好消息，或许是自迷幻剂以来最好的消息。"文章聚焦于《太空战争》——这款由麦卡锡的四名学生在科幻小说的熏陶下于 1961 年开发的早期电脑游戏。但布兰德注意到，这款游戏最引人入胜之处，在于它以一种潜移默化的方式，将一台被神化的数字计算器变成了"人与人之间的沟通设备"。



For people who struggled to express themselves in face-to-face situations like Felsenstein (and people who were incapable of speech altogether), computer networks held the potential for not just “augmenting” communication but making it possible, period—minus the stuff that normally made conversation so arduous, such as eye contact, body language, tone, and the necessity of making a good impression.

对于像费尔森斯坦这样在面对面交流中难以表达自己的人（以及完全无法说话的人），计算机网络不仅具有"增强"沟通的潜力，更让沟通本身成为可能——彻底摆脱了通常让对话变得艰难的那些要素，比如眼神接触、肢体语言、语调，以及必须留下好印象的压力。



The practical constraints of communicating online also required many aspects of social interaction that are normally implicit to be made explicit. Emoticons like :-)—originally proposed by Lisp hacker Scott Fahlman in 1982—were like social captioning for people who have trouble parsing sarcasm and innuendo.

在线交流的实际限制也要求许多通常隐含的社交互动方面变得明确。像:-)这样的表情符号——最初由 Lisp 黑客斯科特·法尔曼于 1982 年提出——就像是为那些难以理解讽刺和暗示的人提供的社交字幕。



—



WITH THE HELP OF Lipkin and Szpakowski, Felsenstein created the first electronic bulletin board in history, called Community Memory. On August 8, 1973, the first wide-open door to cyberspace was installed at the top of a staircase at Leopold’s Records on Telegraph Avenue in Berkeley.

在利普金和斯帕科夫斯基的帮助下，费尔森斯坦创建了历史上第一个电子公告板，名为“社区记忆”。1973 年 8 月 8 日，通往网络空间的第一扇敞开的大门被安装在伯克利电报大道利奥波德唱片店楼梯顶端的一个纸板箱里。



This portal to the digital future wasn’t much to look at: it was basically an overgrown typewriter (an ASR-33 teletype, designed for the Navy) in a cardboard box that Felsenstein lined with foam to muffle the clatter of the hammers, with a vinyl window on top and two holes in front with Velcro flaps (like cat doors) to enable access to the keys. As each person came up the stairs, someone from the commune whose job it was to keep the teletype from getting jammed (which happened constantly anyway) would invite them to sit down and use it.

这个通往数字未来的入口并不起眼：它基本上是一个过于庞大的打字机（ASR-33 电传打字机，为海军设计），放在一个纸板箱里，费尔森斯坦用泡沫衬里来掩盖锤子的咔嗒声，顶部有一个乙烯基窗户，前面有两个带魔术贴翻盖的洞（像猫门一样），以便接触按键。每当有人走上楼梯，公社里负责防止电传打字机卡纸（尽管这仍然频繁发生）的人就会邀请他们坐下使用。



The mission of Community Memory, as its founders explained in a flyer, was “a process whereby technological tools, like computers, are used by the people themselves to shape their own lives and communities in sane and liberating ways . . . We invite your participation and suggestions.” They dubbed the nascent network (which trickled across the Bay at a measly ten characters a second, via an Oakland telephone exchange that could make a free all-day call to San Francisco) an “information flea market.”

社区记忆的使命，正如其创始人在传单中所阐述的那样，是"让民众通过计算机等技术工具，以理性而解放的方式塑造自身生活与社区的过程……我们诚邀您的参与和建议。"他们将这个初生的网络（通过奥克兰电话交换局以每秒仅十个字符的缓慢速度跨越海湾，可免费全天候拨打旧金山）称为"信息跳蚤市场"。



The surprising answer to the question of who might be interested in such a resource was nearly everyone who ambled up the stairs. Because the terminal was located beneath a nonvirtual bulletin board (the kind with pushpins), many early postings to Community Memory were along the lines of “fusion-loving bass player seeks guitarist who digs ragas.” But soon all manner of users were logging on to exchange a myriad of items and services. A poet offered sample poems, while other users solicited lifts to Los Angeles; at one point, a Nubian goat was put up for sale. Some users posted ASCII art, and one posited a question that has vexed Bay Area residents for decades: “Where can I get a decent bagel?” (A baker replied by offering to provide free bagel-baking lessons.) Others held forth on Vietnam, gay liberation, and the energy crisis. Instead of merely being a computerized bulletin board, the network quickly became “a snapshot of the whole community,” Felsenstein says.

对于谁可能对这样一个资源感兴趣的问题，令人惊讶的答案是几乎所有走上楼梯的人。由于终端位于一个非虚拟公告板（那种用图钉的）下方，社区记忆的早期帖子大多是“热爱融合音乐的贝斯手寻找喜欢拉格曲风的吉他手”之类的内容。但很快，各种用户都登录上来交换各种物品和服务。一位诗人提供了诗歌样本，而其他用户则请求搭车去洛杉矶；有一次，甚至有人出售一只努比亚山羊。一些用户发布了 ASCII 艺术，还有人提出了一个困扰湾区居民几十年的问题：“我在哪里能买到像样的百吉饼？”（一位面包师回复说愿意免费提供百吉饼烘焙课程。）其他人则就越南问题、同性恋解放和能源危机发表了长篇大论。费尔森斯坦说，这个网络不仅仅是电脑化的公告板，它迅速成为了“整个社区的缩影”。



Inevitably, the first public social network also gave birth to the first online troll: a wag who called himself “Dr. Benway” (the name of a drug-addicted surgeon in the novels of William Burroughs) who peppered the ongoing dialogues with Grateful Dead references and droll non sequiturs like “sensuous keystrokes forbidden” and “personal attendance required: send no replica.” The identity of this mysterious pioneer of online snark was never uncovered.

第一个公共社交网络不可避免地也催生了第一个网络喷子：一个自称“本威博士”（威廉·巴勒斯小说中一位吸毒成瘾的外科医生的名字）的滑稽人物，在持续进行的对话中穿插着对“感恩而死”乐队的引用，以及诸如“禁止感性敲击键盘”和“需本人到场：勿寄替代品”之类滑稽的离题话。这位神秘的网络嘲讽先驱的身份从未被揭露。



Alas, without a sustainable economic model, the Project One commune was finally unable to support the considerable cost of maintaining the SDS 940. But as the prototype of a tool for promoting conviviality, Community Memory was a smashing success. Its popularity was particularly gratifying to Felsenstein, because a feeling of belonging to a community was precisely the thing that had always eluded him—even in the counterculture that was supposed to offer it to those who had never fit in anywhere else.

可惜的是，由于缺乏可持续的经济模式，“一号计划”公社最终无力承担维护 SDS 940 计算机的巨额费用。但作为促进友好交流的工具原型，“社区记忆”取得了巨大成功。它的受欢迎程度尤其让费尔森斯坦感到欣慰，因为归属感恰恰是他一直未能获得的东西——即使在那个本应接纳所有格格不入者的反主流文化中也是如此。



“As a kid, I had a feeling that I was ensconced in some sort of alcove, behind a wall, and that the street was out there,” Felsenstein recalls. “I could see everyone else walking around engaging in life, but I couldn’t go out there. So what I was doing with Community Memory was trying to expand the alcove.” He moved on to other projects, including designing the Osborne 1—the first truly portable personal computer, introduced three years before the Macintosh. But he continued to struggle with depression and an inability to read other people’s intentions despite years of psychotherapy.

“小时候，我总感觉自己被困在某个壁龛里，身后是墙，街道就在外面，”费尔森斯坦回忆道，“我能看见其他人在外面走动、生活，却无法走出去。所以我做社区记忆项目，就是想扩大这个壁龛。”后来他又参与了其他项目，包括设计奥斯本 1 号——第一款真正便携的个人电脑，比麦金塔电脑早问世三年。但尽管接受了多年心理治疗，他仍饱受抑郁困扰，始终无法读懂他人的意图。



Finally, in the 1990s, Felsenstein heard about Asperger’s syndrome and recognized not only himself in the description but other members of his family. There was his illustrious grandfather William Price, the gifted inventor who was a perpetual puzzlement to his wife. Price’s daughter, Caroline, never graduated from college but became one of the leading experts on bookbinding and restoration in New York City. In his interactions with her, Felsenstein found her opaque and emotionally distant. Her son Chris, who was Felsenstein’s age, always seemed odd, speaking in an overly emphatic manner and staring in an unnerving way. At fifty, Chris earned a PhD in physics, though he was still unable to hold a job for long because he had a hard time getting along with people. He was finally diagnosed with Asperger’s in the 1990s and suggested that Felsenstein also pursue an evaluation. Reading about autism online, Felsenstein came to think of his Asperger’s as more than just a set of deficits, but as his “edge”—the edge he inherited from grandfather, which he has put to work in his career in technology for forty years.

最终，在 1990 年代，费尔森斯坦听说了阿斯伯格综合征，不仅从描述中认出了自己，还认出了家族中的其他成员。他的祖父威廉·普莱斯是位天赋异禀的发明家，却始终让妻子困惑不解。普莱斯的女儿卡罗琳虽未从大学毕业，却成为纽约市顶尖的书籍装帧与修复专家之一。费尔森斯坦与她相处时，总觉得她难以捉摸且情感疏离。她的儿子克里斯与费尔森斯坦同龄，总是显得古怪——说话语气过分夸张，凝视他人时令人不安。五十岁时克里斯获得了物理学博士学位，却因难以与人相处而始终无法长期任职。他在 1990 年代被确诊为阿斯伯格综合征，并建议费尔森斯坦也接受评估。通过在线查阅孤独谱系相关资料，费尔森斯坦逐渐将阿斯伯格特质视为超越缺陷的存在——那是他从祖父那里继承的"优势"，四十年来一直助力他在科技领域的事业发展。



The text-based nature of online interaction eventually provided the foundation for something that Leo Kanner couldn’t have imagined: the birth of the autistic community. But two things had to happen first. Kanner’s notion that autism was a rare form of childhood psychosis would have to be permanently laid to rest. Then, as Asperger’s lost tribe finally emerged from the shadows, autistic people would have to overturn the notion that they were the victims of a global epidemic.

网络互动的文本特性最终为利奥·坎纳无法想象的事情奠定了基础：孤独谱系群体的诞生。但在此之前，必须完成两件事。首先，坎纳关于孤独症是罕见儿童精神病的观点必须被彻底摒弃。随后，当阿斯伯格笔下失落的部落终于从阴影中浮现时，孤独谱系群体必须推翻他们是一场全球性流行病受害者的观念。







---


NeuroTribes








Seven

七



FIGHTING THE MONSTER

与怪物搏斗



That my child, therefore, may have some small share in creating this new light, I tell her story.

为了让我的孩子也能为创造这束新光贡献一份微薄之力，我讲述她的故事。



—PEARL S. BUCK, THE CHILD WHO NEVER GREW

——赛珍珠，《永远长不大的孩子》



Fittingly, the man who consigned the theory of toxic parenting to the dustbin of history was the loving father of an autistic boy himself: a warm, garrulous, obsessively curious Navy psychologist named Bernard Rimland. By writing a book called Infantile Autism as a self-taught outsider in the field, he firmly established autism as an inborn condition based in genetics and neurology rather than the complexities of the developing psyche.

恰如其分地，将“有毒教养”理论送入历史垃圾堆的人，正是一位孤独症男孩的慈父：一位热情、健谈、充满好奇心的海军心理学家伯纳德·里姆兰。他以自学成才的局外人身份撰写《婴儿孤独症》一书，坚定地将孤独症确立为基于遗传学和神经学的先天状况，而非复杂心理发展过程的产物。



The book’s unexpected popularity inspired Rimland to launch the National Society for Autistic Children, which helped end decades of shame and isolation for families like his, and lobbied for legislation based on the principle that all children have the right to an education, including those with developmental disabilities. By crowdsourcing the search for effective autism treatments, he gave the parents in his network a sense of hope and progress at a time when research in the field was at a virtual standstill because the condition was still believed to be so rare. In many ways, his work set the stage for the rediscovery of Asperger’s lost tribe and the current surge of interest in autism research.

这本书出乎意料的受欢迎程度，促使里姆兰创立了全国孤独症儿童协会，帮助像他这样的家庭结束了数十年的羞耻与孤立，并游说立法机构通过基于所有儿童（包括发育障碍儿童）都有受教育权的法案。通过众包方式寻找有效的孤独症治疗方法，他在该领域研究几乎停滞（因为当时仍认为这种病症极为罕见）的时期，为网络中的父母带来了希望与进展。从许多方面来看，他的工作为重新发现阿斯伯格综合征的“失落部落”以及当前对孤独症研究的兴趣激增奠定了基础。



Ironically, Rimland bitterly opposed the notion of autism as a continuum at first, like his hero, Leo Kanner. Faced with the likelihood of children like his son, Mark, being doomed to spend their lives in institutions, he forged an alliance with a psychologist named Ole Ivar Lovaas to find ways of training them to become “indistinguishable from their peers,” as Lovaas put it. With the help of his parents’ network, he also pursued innovative methods of treating the most debilitating features of autism with special diets, megavitamin supplements, and alternative medicines.

讽刺的是，里姆兰起初像他的偶像利奥·坎纳一样，激烈反对将孤独症视为一个连续谱系的概念。面对像他儿子马克这样的孩子可能注定要在收容机构度过一生的现实，他与心理学家奥莱·伊瓦尔·洛瓦斯结盟，寻找训练方法，使这些孩子能够如洛瓦斯所言"与同龄人别无二致"。借助家长网络的支持，他还探索了创新疗法——通过特殊饮食、大剂量维生素补充剂和替代药物，来治疗孤独症最具破坏性的症状。



The controversial theories that Rimland developed—such as his notion that the allegedly unified condition called autism is composed of many distinct subtypes—anticipated major shifts in mainstream science by decades.
