Each afternoon at the appointed time, all work in the clinic would stop for tea.

他与阿迪斯的会诊在医生位于斯坦福大学繁忙的诊所进行，患者可以亲眼目睹他公开进行实验。他将患者的妻子和母亲视为"同事"，同时用留声机播放勃拉姆斯和贝多芬的室内乐。每天下午固定时间，诊所里所有工作都会停下来喝茶。



Addis’s strategy for treating disease struck Pauling with the force of a revelation. Instead of pumping him full of drugs, this wise physician had healed him by manipulating levels of compounds—water, vitamins, minerals, protein, and salt—already present in his body. Pauling dubbed his approach orthomolecular medicine (from the Greek root orthos, “upright” or “correct”) and came to believe that it had potential for curing a broad range of maladies from schizophrenia to cancer.

阿迪斯治疗疾病的策略让鲍林感到如获启示。这位睿智的医生没有给他大量用药，而是通过调节他体内已有的化合物——水、维生素、矿物质、蛋白质和盐——的水平来治愈他。鲍林将这种方法称为"正分子医学"（源自希腊词根 orthos，意为"正直"或"正确"），并开始相信它有可能治愈从精神分裂症到癌症的多种疾病。



Pauling became the most prominent advocate of the notion that megadoses of vitamin C could avert the common cold, slow the aging process, and improve mood. He wrote three best-selling books on the subject and received extensive coverage for his theories in the New York Times and other prestigious media outlets. A month after publication of his 1970 blockbuster Vitamin C and the Common Cold, drugstores across the country reported an unprecedented run on the tablets, and an industry spokesman complained that production was unable to keep up with the demand. Pauling’s credibility as one of the few two-time Nobel winners in history helped transform the supplement business from a marginal enterprise serving health food stores into an alt-med powerhouse with annual sales rivaling the pharmaceutical industry—minus the pesky Food and Drug Administration (FDA) regulations.

鲍林成为最著名的倡导者，主张大剂量维生素 C 可以预防普通感冒、延缓衰老过程并改善情绪。他撰写了三本关于这一主题的畅销书，其理论在《纽约时报》及其他知名媒体上获得了广泛报道。1970 年，他的重磅著作《维生素 C 与普通感冒》出版一个月后，全美药店报告了前所未有的维生素片抢购潮，一位行业发言人抱怨生产无法满足需求。作为历史上仅有的两位诺贝尔奖得主之一，鲍林的可信度帮助将补充剂行业从服务于健康食品店的小众企业，转变为年销售额可与制药业媲美的替代医学巨头——且无需受制于恼人的美国食品药品监督管理局（FDA）监管。



In placebo-controlled trials, Pauling’s extravagant claims for vitamin C received mixed reviews at best. But he had a good reason for believing that vitamins play a role in mental illness. In 1926, his mother, Belle, died in an insane asylum in Oregon, deranged by a type of anemia caused by chronic B-12 deficiency. Just after she died, scientists discovered that the condition that killed her could have been easily averted by eating raw liver. A decade and a half later, two of Pauling’s colleagues, Karl Folkers and Alexander Todd, isolated the bright pink crystals of the vitamin in its pure state. Pauling speculated that certain types of intellectual disability were a form of “cerebral scurvy” caused by a general decline of nutrition in the modern era, including the widespread adoption of heavily processed foods.

在安慰剂对照试验中，鲍林对维生素 C 疗效的夸张说法最多只获得了褒贬不一的评价。但他坚信维生素对精神疾病有作用是有充分理由的。1926 年，他的母亲贝尔在俄勒冈州一家精神病院去世，死因是慢性 B-12 缺乏症引发的贫血导致精神错乱。就在她去世后不久，科学家发现导致她死亡的疾病本可通过食用生肝脏轻易避免。十五年后，鲍林的两位同事卡尔·福克斯和亚历山大·托德分离出了纯态的亮粉色维生素晶体。鲍林推测，某些类型的智力障碍是一种"脑部坏血病"，其根源在于现代营养普遍衰退，包括高度加工食品的广泛普及。



Pauling’s concept of orthomolecular psychiatry meshed perfectly with Rimland’s thoughts on PKU and autism. Meanwhile, Rimland had started getting letters from parents who were conducting their own orthomolecular experiments on their children and reporting promising results. A mother from Canada told him that her son’s autism had vastly improved after she gave him megadoses of B vitamins inspired by the schizophrenia research of Abram Hoffer and Humphry Osmond at a mental hospital in Saskatchewan. Osmond was no stranger to controversial research: he coined the word psychedelic in 1957 after giving Aldous Huxley the dose of mescaline that inspired him to write The Doors of Perception. According to the mother, the hospital nurses felt that their patients had made breakthroughs on Hoffer and Osmond’s B-vitamin regimen, but the senior psychiatrists on the ward “refused to see what was so clearly evident to everyone else,” in Rimland’s words.

鲍林的正分子精神病学概念与里姆兰对苯丙酮尿症和孤独症的看法完美契合。与此同时，里姆兰开始收到一些家长的来信，这些家长正在自行对孩子进行正分子实验，并报告了令人鼓舞的结果。一位来自加拿大的母亲告诉他，她的儿子在服用大剂量 B 族维生素后，孤独症症状显著改善——这一灵感源于阿布拉姆·霍弗和汉弗莱·奥斯蒙德在萨斯喀彻温省一家精神病院进行的关于精神分裂症的研究。奥斯蒙德对有争议的研究并不陌生：1957 年，他在给奥尔德斯·赫胥黎服用麦斯卡林（该剂量促使赫胥黎写下《知觉之门》）后，创造了“迷幻”一词。据这位母亲说，医院的护士们认为，患者在接受霍弗和奥斯蒙德的 B 族维生素疗法后取得了突破性进展，但病房里的资深精神科医生却“拒绝承认对其他人来说如此显而易见的事实”，里姆兰如是说。



While Rimland was skeptical at first that anything as innocuous as vitamin tablets could make a significant impact on autism, he saw the same supplements (particularly B vitamins and magnesium) being mentioned over and over by parents. Could it all just be a coincidence?

尽管里姆兰起初怀疑，像维生素片这样无害的东西是否真能对孤独症产生显著影响，但他发现家长们反复提及同样的补充剂（尤其是 B 族维生素和镁）。这一切难道只是巧合吗？



—



BY THE FALL OF 1965, Rimland was receiving letters and checklists from all over the world. Before attending a Navy conference in Washington, he wrote to parents throughout the New York–D.C. metropolitan area, offering to tell them in person about a new kind of behavioral therapy for autism that offered great promise. One of the mothers on his mailing list was Ruth Christ Sullivan, a young nurse who reached out to him after seeing a reflection of her own son, Joe, in one of the first TV specials about autism.

到 1965 年秋天，里姆兰已收到来自世界各地的信件和调查表。在前往华盛顿参加海军会议之前，他写信给纽约至华盛顿特区都会区的家长们，表示愿意亲自向他们介绍一种前景可期的孤独症行为疗法。他邮寄名单上的一位母亲是露丝·克里斯特·沙利文——这位年轻护士在观看首批关于孤独症的电视特别节目之一时，从节目中看到了自己儿子乔的影子，于是主动联系了里姆兰。



When Joe was born, Sullivan and her family were living in Lake Charles, Louisiana, near Cajun country. For the first eighteen months of his life, he seemed like an exceptionally bright and engaging boy, but gradually he began putting the world at a distance. In family snapshots, he was often caught in the act of slipping off somebody’s lap. After beginning to talk on the usual schedule, he abruptly stopped using words altogether. One day, he parked himself in front of a door to put together a jigsaw puzzle; then his mother accidentally burst through the door from the other side, making a mess of the puzzle. But as Sullivan watched, astonished, he quickly reassembled the puzzle with the pieces upside down, though he could no longer use the pattern they formed as a cue to their proper placement. He soon started drawing maps of the United States while humming “The Star-Spangled Banner.”

乔出生时，沙利文一家住在路易斯安那州查尔斯湖附近，毗邻卡津地区。他生命最初的十八个月里，看起来是个异常聪慧讨喜的男孩，但渐渐地他开始疏远这个世界。在家庭快照中，常能捕捉到他正从某人的膝头滑落的瞬间。按正常进度开始说话后，他突然彻底停止了使用语言。某天，他坐在门前拼着拼图，母亲却意外地从门另一侧冲进来，把拼图弄得一团糟。但令沙利文惊愕的是，他迅速将拼图碎片翻转过来重新拼好——尽管此时已无法借助图案提示正确位置。很快他又开始哼着《星条旗》画美国地图。



Then, just as inexplicably as he had stopped, Joe started speaking again and was able to instantly name the day of the week for nearly any date in the past or future. He would startle his mother by recalling things that the family had done years before in photographic detail. He was also extraordinarily agile and fearless and built Tetris-like pyramids of tables and chairs to climb to the tops of bookshelves. One day, a neighbor called to inform Sullivan that her son was crawling around on the roof. A young doctor at a public-health clinic told her that he suspected Joe had autism, but then the doctor died suddenly, and no one else in the area could tell her anything more about her son’s condition.

接着，乔就像之前突然停下一样，又毫无征兆地开始说话，并且能立刻说出过去或未来几乎任何日期对应的星期几。他会用照片般清晰的细节回忆起家人多年前做过的事，让母亲大吃一惊。他还异常敏捷且无所畏惧，像搭俄罗斯方块一样把桌椅堆成金字塔，爬到书架顶端。有一天，邻居打电话告诉沙利文，她儿子正在屋顶上爬来爬去。一家公共卫生诊所的年轻医生告诉她，他怀疑乔患有孤独症，但随后这位医生突然去世，当地再没人能告诉她更多关于她儿子状况的信息。



Then the Sullivans moved to upstate New York so that Joe’s father, William, could start teaching at a local college. There, Ruth found two child psychiatrists who had worked directly with Kanner at Johns Hopkins, and they confirmed Joe’s diagnosis. She was advised to join a therapy group for “overanxious mothers.” At the first meeting, the psychologist’s assistant asked her to hand out slips of paper so that the other mothers in the room could sign up for future sessions. Instead, she surreptitiously passed around a note inviting everyone to get together in private. Their meetings at each other’s houses in Albany marked the birth of the autism parenting movement in the United States.

随后，沙利文一家搬到了纽约州北部，以便乔的父亲威廉能在当地一所大学任教。在那里，露丝找到了两位曾在约翰·霍普金斯大学与坎纳共事过的儿童精神科医生，他们确认了乔的诊断。有人建议她加入一个针对"过度焦虑母亲"的治疗小组。第一次见面时，心理学家的助手让她分发纸条，以便房间里的其他母亲可以报名参加后续课程。但她却悄悄传了一张纸条，邀请大家私下聚会。她们在奥尔巴尼彼此家中举行的聚会，标志着美国孤独症家长运动的诞生。



—



COMMUNITY ORGANIZING was nothing new to Sullivan. “The first time I held elected office,” she laughs, “I was in seventh grade.” As a student nurse in the Deep South in the depths of the Jim Crow era, she called for a motion to integrate the Louisiana Nurses’ Association, which passed unanimously. She also became active in the League of Women Voters, inspired by her mother, a strong and independent woman for her time. In her correspondence with Rimland, she proposed forming a national group to advocate for the needs of autistic children. He replied that he was already thinking along the same lines.

社区组织对沙利文来说并不陌生。“我第一次担任公职，”她笑着说，“是在七年级。”在吉姆·克劳时代鼎盛时期的南方腹地，作为一名实习护士，她提议整合路易斯安那州护士协会，该动议获得全票通过。受母亲——一位在当时堪称坚强独立的女性——的启发，她还积极参与了女性选民联盟。在与里姆兰德的通信中，她提议成立一个全国性组织，为孤独谱系儿童的需求发声。他回复说，自己也在考虑同样的事情。



On November 14, 1965, Sullivan drove down from Albany to Teaneck, New Jersey—a four-hour trip along twisting back roads before the opening of the interstates—where thirty-five mothers and fathers crowded into the living room of a couple named Herbert and Rosalyn Kahn. “We just fell on each other,” Sullivan recalls. “It was an incredible experience for us. For the first time, we had hope.”

1965 年 11 月 14 日，沙利文从奥尔巴尼驱车前往新泽西州的蒂内克——在州际公路开通前，这是一段沿着蜿蜒乡间小路行驶四小时的路程——在那里，35 位父母挤进了赫伯特和罗莎琳·卡恩夫妇家的客厅。“我们一见如故，”沙利文回忆道，“那对我们来说是一次不可思议的经历。我们第一次感受到了希望。”



The meeting started at eight p.m. and lasted until midnight. Rimland talked about the need to launch a national organization and touted the potential of the Lovaas method, delivering a talk that he would repeat many times in the coming years. He told the assembly that Lovaas would be willing to send out graduate students from UCLA to offer training sessions and handed out lists of activities that parents could do with their children to improve their behavior in the meantime.

会议于晚上八点开始，一直持续到午夜。里姆兰谈到有必要成立一个全国性组织，并大力推崇洛瓦斯方法的潜力，这番演讲在接下来的几年里他重复了多次。他向与会者表示，洛瓦斯愿意派遣加州大学洛杉矶分校的研究生前来提供培训课程，并分发了家长可与孩子一起进行的活动清单，以在此期间改善他们的行为。



Then a pediatrician named Mary Goodwin from Cooperstown, New York, gave a presentation that was far ahead of its time, like a transmission from thirty years in the future. Goodwin recounted her experiences of teaching dozens of nonspeaking children to use an experimental device called the Edison Responsive Environment Learning System (ERELS), familiarly known as the “talking typewriter.” The ERELS was the brainchild of an inventor at the Edison Research Laboratory named Richard Kobler and a sociologist at Yale with the splendid name of Omar Khayyam Moore. Among Kobler’s achievements was designing the first telephone to store frequently dialed numbers and a contraption called the Voicewriter that enabled nurses to dictate medical records. Moore theorized that if learning were more like play, children would be able to teach themselves how to read, write, and type at a very young age. Working with Kobler, he developed a device that combined a keyboard, a TV screen, a tape recorder, and an analog processor, like a prototype of the modern computer.

随后，来自纽约库珀斯敦的儿科医生玛丽·古德温发表了一场极具前瞻性的演讲，仿佛来自三十年后的未来。古德温讲述了她教数十名非口语者使用实验设备"爱迪生响应式环境学习系统"（ERELS）的经历，这套系统俗称"会说话的打字机"。ERELS 是爱迪生研究实验室的发明家理查德·科布勒与耶鲁大学一位名叫奥马尔·卡亚姆·摩尔的社会学家共同的心血结晶。科布勒的成就包括设计出首款可存储常用号码的电话，以及让护士口述病历的"语音记录器"。摩尔提出理论：若学习能更像游戏，儿童便能在极年幼时自学阅读、写作和打字。他与科布勒合作开发了一台融合键盘、电视屏幕、录音机和模拟处理器的设备，堪称现代计算机的原型。



The ERELS interface was designed to be as nonthreatening as possible. When a child sat down at the machine, a color photograph—say, of a sailboat—would flash on the screen. “This is a boat,” a soothing recorded voice would say. “Boat is spelled B-O-A-T. Now type B.” Then an onscreen prompt would guide the child’s finger to the B key—the only key that would work at that moment. Thus it was impossible for the child to make a mistake. When the B was pressed, the machine would say, “Very good! Now type O.” Typing the whole word correctly prompted the machine to say “Excellent!” and to invite the child to say the word out loud; then it would play the word back in the sound of the child’s own voice. Simple games were also available on the ERELS, giving children additional opportunities to become comfortable using the machine. Moore felt there was “no greater deterrent to learning than the fear of making a mistake. So the children discover that when they make an error, nothing happens. The typewriter never scolds; it is never impatient.”

ERELS 界面的设计尽可能减少威胁感。当孩子坐在机器前时，屏幕上会闪现一张彩色照片——比如一艘帆船。“这是一艘船，”一个舒缓的录音声音说道，“船拼写为 B-O-A-T。现在输入 B。”随后屏幕上的提示会引导孩子的手指按向 B 键——此时唯一可用的按键。这样孩子就不可能犯错。按下 B 键后，机器会说：“非常好！现在输入 O。”正确拼写完整单词后，机器会称赞“太棒了！”，并邀请孩子大声朗读该词；随后机器会用孩子自己的声音回放这个词。ERELS 还提供简单游戏，让孩子们有更多机会熟悉操作。摩尔认为“对学习最大的阻碍莫过于害怕犯错。因此孩子们会发现，当他们出错时，什么也不会发生。打字机从不责备，也从不急躁。”



Upon seeing one of Moore’s talking typewriters in use at a school in New Haven, Goodwin was hopeful that it could provide a valuable learning opportunity for children with autism. With her husband, Campbell, who was also a pediatrician, she raised $35,000 to bring one to the hospital where they worked in Cooperstown and establish a research facility. Over the next two years, they would work on the ERELS with sixty-five autistic children, yielding very positive results. One of the first children to sit down at the machine was a six-year-old boy who had never spoken a word and had been recommended for custodial care because of his violent behavior. After exploring the keyboard for a while, he began typing out brand names he’d heard on TV. Soon he was showing up at the hospital three times a week, and his parents were able to find him a placement at a local school. Other children made similar progress, including a fourteen-year-old boy who had regressed to near catatonia. Unfortunately, the cost of the talking typewriters proved prohibitive, and the Goodwins’ far-seeing experiment came to an end in 1966. But Mary’s presentation was a preview of the potential of technology for transforming the lives of children who had been written off as incapable of any sort of communication.

古德温在纽黑文的一所学校看到摩尔的一台会说话的打字机在使用后，满怀希望地认为这能为孤独症儿童提供宝贵的学习机会。她和同为儿科医生的丈夫坎贝尔筹集了 3.5 万美元，将一台打字机带到他们工作的库珀斯敦医院，并建立了一个研究机构。在接下来的两年里，他们与 65 名孤独症儿童一起使用 EREIS 系统，取得了非常积极的结果。第一个坐到机器前的孩子是一个六岁的男孩，他从未说过一句话，因暴力行为被建议接受监护照料。在探索键盘一段时间后，他开始打出在电视上听到的品牌名称。很快，他每周三次出现在医院，父母也为他找到了当地学校的安置。其他孩子也取得了类似的进步，包括一个退步到近乎紧张症的十四岁男孩。不幸的是，会说话的打字机成本过高，古德温夫妇这项富有远见的实验于 1966 年终止。但玛丽的演示展示了技术改变那些曾被判定为无法进行任何交流的儿童生活的潜力。



Much of the discussion that night in Teaneck was devoted to ways that parents could work together effectively to demand access to education and other services for their children. By the end of the night, the group had a president, a board of advisors, an editor for its newsletter, and a name: the National Society for Autistic Children. “We should weave a cloth so strong,” Rimland told the group, “that no one can tear us apart.”

当晚在蒂内克的讨论中，大部分时间都集中在父母如何有效合作，为子女争取教育及其他服务。会议结束时，这个团体选出了主席、顾问委员会、通讯编辑，并命名为“全国孤独谱系儿童协会”。里姆兰对大家说：“我们要编织一张如此坚韧的布，无人能将其撕裂。”



Two nights later, he hosted another meeting at the National Institutes of Health in Bethesda. In the coming years, parents would launch hundreds of local NSAC chapters all over the country. “Lifting the burden of shame, guilt, and blame from the parents of autistic children,” Rimland wrote, “unleashed an enormous burst of productivity and creativity on behalf of the children.” He and Sullivan had raised a mighty army. The battle for their children’s future was finally under way.

两晚后，他在贝塞斯达的美国国立卫生研究院主持了另一场会议。接下来的几年里，父母们在全国各地成立了数百个地方分会。“从孤独谱系儿童父母身上卸下羞耻、内疚和指责的重担，”里姆兰写道，“释放出为孩子们服务的巨大生产力和创造力。”他和沙利文组建了一支强大的军队。为孩子们未来而战的战斗终于打响了。



—



THE GROUP WAS BOLD and radical from the start, energized by the rage of parents who had been scapegoated for causing their own children’s misery. “NSAC was founded because parents of children with autism knew that many things closely affecting their lives were terribly wrong,” longtime member Frank Warren said. “[They] knew that their children needed help, that no one understood what was wrong with them, and that leadership in all the helping professions were blaming the parents for their children’s disability . . . Naturally the parents were angry. Naturally the fledgling society was a highly charged and scrappy organization.”

这个团体从一开始就大胆而激进，其动力源于那些被指责导致自己孩子痛苦的父母们的愤怒。长期成员弗兰克·沃伦说：“NSAC 的成立，是因为孤独症儿童的父母们知道，许多与他们生活息息相关的事情都出了严重问题。他们知道自己的孩子需要帮助，没有人理解孩子出了什么问题，而所有帮助性行业的领导层都在将孩子的残障归咎于父母……父母们自然感到愤怒。这个初生的社团自然也就成了一个充满激情、斗志昂扬的组织。”



The fighting spirit and lobbying expertise of parents like Sullivan enabled NSAC to accomplish a great deal in a relatively short time. Sullivan conceived of her fellow parent-activists as “trainers” for alleged experts who often knew little or nothing about their child’s condition. In West Virginia, NSAC established a medical lending library promoted with mailings to five hundred pediatricians, psychiatrists, general practitioners, and hearing specialists headlined, “The next child who walks into your office may be autistic. What will you tell the family?” The society established a network of similar libraries throughout the country, offering up-to-date information on education, legislation, and housing.

像沙利文这样的家长，凭借其斗争精神和游说专长，使 NSAC 在相对较短的时间内取得了巨大成就。沙利文将她的家长活动家同伴视为所谓专家的"培训师"，这些专家往往对孩子的状况知之甚少或一无所知。在西弗吉尼亚州，NSAC 建立了一个医学借阅图书馆，并向五百名儿科医生、精神科医生、全科医生和听力专家邮寄宣传材料，标题为："下一个走进你办公室的孩子可能是孤独谱系儿童。你会告诉这个家庭什么？"该协会在全国范围内建立了一个类似的图书馆网络，提供有关教育、立法和住房的最新信息。



In 1967, Clarence and Christine Griffith sought in vain through the whole state of Georgia to find a school that would accept their son Joseph. They realized that only by making common cause with other parents would they be able to get anything done, so they formed an NSAC chapter called the Georgia Society for Autistic Children. Over the course of the next few months, they persuaded the DeKalb County school board to launch a pilot program for autistic children funded by the state. The Women’s Club in Sandy Springs set up classes for preschoolers, and the First Baptist Church in Decatur began researching ways of mainstreaming autistic kids in kindergarten. Crucial to these efforts, the Griffiths said, was networking with other groups like the League of Women Voters and the Jaycees. They also stressed the importance of raising public awareness of autism by making contacts at TV and radio stations, parent-teacher associations, and churches. Later that year, when the Georgia House was debating a bill for funding special education called the Exceptional Child Act, a state representative who had seen one of the Griffiths’ broadcasts on a local TV station stood up and proposed adding services for autistic children to the bill, saying, “‘Autistic’ is a label that has been used in the past to deny a child an education; I want it used once for an autistic child.” The bill passed.

1967 年，克拉伦斯·格里菲斯和克里斯汀·格里菲斯在佐治亚州四处奔走，却始终找不到一所愿意接收他们儿子约瑟夫的学校。他们意识到，只有与其他家长联合起来才能有所作为，于是成立了 NSAC 分会——佐治亚孤独谱系儿童协会。在接下来的几个月里，他们成功说服迪卡尔布县教育委员会启动了一项由州政府资助的孤独谱系儿童试点项目。桑迪斯普林斯妇女俱乐部为学龄前儿童开设了课程，迪凯特第一浸信会教堂则开始研究如何让孤独谱系儿童融入幼儿园教育。格里菲斯夫妇表示，与妇女选民联盟和青年商会等其他团体建立联系对这些努力至关重要。他们还强调，通过联系电视台、广播电台、家长教师协会和教会来提高公众对孤独谱系的认识，同样具有重要意义。 同年晚些时候，佐治亚州众议院正在辩论一项名为《特殊儿童法案》的特殊教育拨款议案时，一位曾在当地电视台看过格里菲斯夫妇节目的州议员起身提议，将孤独症儿童服务纳入该法案。他说："'孤独谱系'这个标签过去曾被用来剥夺儿童受教育权；我希望这次能用它来为孤独谱系儿童争取权益。"该法案最终获得通过。



To achieve its legislative goals, NSAC also forged strategic alliances with other disability advocacy groups, including the United Cerebral Palsy Association, the Epilepsy Foundation of America, and the Association for Retarded Citizens (also known as the Arc). By aligning with these groups, NSAC helped reframe autism in the minds of professionals from a form of childhood “emotional disturbance” to an inborn disability that required lifelong care and support. In 1967, the society was a fierce critic of the recommendations of the congressional Joint Commission on the Mental Health of Children, blasting the commission’s focus on the role of broken homes and unhappy households as allegedly responsible for conditions like autism.

为实现立法目标，全国孤独症协会还与联合脑瘫协会、美国癫痫基金会、智力障碍公民协会（亦称 Arc）等其他残障倡导组织建立了战略联盟。通过与这些组织合作，全国孤独症协会帮助专业人士重新定义孤独症——从儿童"情绪障碍"转变为需要终身照护与支持的天生残障。1967 年，该协会严厉批评了国会儿童心理健康联合委员会的建议，抨击该委员会将破碎家庭与不幸家庭环境视为导致孤独症等病症的罪魁祸首。



That same year, NSAC board member Amy Lettick opened a school called Benhaven in Connecticut that embraced an eclectic range of progressive approaches to special education. Housed in a twenty-two-room Tudor mansion on a hillside in New Haven, the school was created as a haven for children like Lettick’s son, Ben, who had been excluded and expelled from other schools. At a time when autistic teenagers were still invisible in the clinical literature, students were encouraged to attend the school into adulthood, taking classes, swimming in the pool, and working on the school’s thirty-five-acre farm, which featured vegetable gardens, greenhouses, barns, and a chicken coop.

同年，NSAC 董事会成员艾米·莱蒂克在康涅狄格州创办了一所名为本黑文（Benhaven）的学校，该校融合了多种进步主义特殊教育方法。学校坐落于纽黑文山坡上一座拥有 22 个房间的都铎式宅邸中，专为像莱蒂克儿子本这样被其他学校拒收或开除的儿童打造庇护之所。当时孤独谱系青少年在临床文献中仍属隐形群体，该校鼓励学生成年后继续就读，他们可以上课、在泳池游泳，并在学校 35 英亩的农场劳作——那里设有菜园、温室、谷仓和鸡舍。



The total environment of Benhaven was shaped with the needs and comfort of the students in mind. Airy classrooms were designed to reduce distracting sights and sounds, and the kitchens, bathrooms, and laundry rooms were extra large so that instruction in self-care skills could take place there also. Along with their academic lessons, students learned to bake bread, build furniture, raise food crops and ornamental flowers, hand-set type, and bind books. Benhaven also offered courses in sex education for teenagers and older students, which was unheard-of in schools for the developmentally disabled.

本黑文的环境整体设计充分考虑了学生的需求与舒适度。宽敞明亮的教室旨在减少干扰视觉与噪音，而厨房、浴室和洗衣房则被特意扩大，以便在此开展自理技能的教学。除了学术课程，学生们还学习烘焙面包、制作家具、种植粮食作物与观赏花卉、手工排版以及装订书籍。此外，本黑文还为青少年及年龄较大的学生开设性教育课程，这在针对发育障碍学生的学校中前所未闻。



In 1972, the school enrolled its first students who were both autistic and deaf. To accommodate them, the whole staff learned sign language. Signing turned out to be a popular medium of communication at Benhaven even for hearing students. Lettick realized that too much emphasis was being placed on teaching autistic children to speak, when what was truly essential was enabling them to communicate. Using sign, students who had previously been unable to learn to read and write were able to do so. “It is fascinating to be able to watch the thought processes as the children think aloud in sign language while they do their work,” Lettick wrote. “We frequently see these children talking to themselves during the day, getting the same satisfaction from signing that speaking children get from softly talking to themselves in spoken language.”

1972 年，这所学校招收了首批同时患有孤独谱系障碍和听力障碍的学生。为了适应他们的需求，全体教职员工都学习了手语。事实证明，手语在 Benhaven 甚至成为听力正常学生中流行的沟通媒介。莱蒂克意识到，过去过分强调教会孤独谱系儿童开口说话，而真正关键的是让他们能够实现沟通。通过使用手语，那些原本无法学习读写的学生成功掌握了这些技能。"观察孩子们在做作业时用手语'自言自语'的思维过程，实在令人着迷，"莱蒂克写道，"我们经常看到这些孩子白天与自己对话，从手语中获得与口语者轻声自语时同样的满足感。"



Sullivan was thrilled to see her fellow NSAC parents proudly step out of the shadows and claim their power to change the world. “Though it is a rare parent who is well-informed about this severe, low-incidence disorder at the time of their child’s diagnosis, it is common to find parents of older children who are highly informed,” she wrote. “I believe the sweetest reward of being a parent trainer is seeing a hurt, scared, timid, frustrated, despondent or angry parent blossom into an articulate, well-informed, assertive, energetic, and successful advocate for their child.”

萨利文看到她的 NSAC 家长同伴们自豪地走出阴影，并宣称自己拥有改变世界的力量，感到无比激动。“尽管在孩子确诊时，很少有家长能充分了解这种严重但发病率较低的疾病，但常见的是，年长孩子的家长对此非常了解，”她写道。“我认为作为家长培训师最甜蜜的回报，就是看到那些受伤、害怕、胆怯、沮丧、绝望或愤怒的家长，成长为能言善辩、见多识广、坚定自信、充满活力且成功的孩子代言人。”



After moving to Huntington, West Virginia, she founded state and local NSAC chapters and launched the society’s Information and Referral Service out of her house, offering a wide range of services and resources to parents and professionals. Instead of relying on a switchboard operator or answering service, she fielded most of the calls herself. Requests for assistance came in twenty-four hours a day—a mother in New York whose son had been thrown out of school; a father phoning from a motel room in Alabama looking for other parents in the area; a Japanese pediatrician seeking one of the society’s professional advisors to talk to parents in Tokyo; a Florida mother trying to get her misunderstood son out of jail. The Information and Referral Service eventually received federal funding and opened its own office in Washington. For years, the society’s bimonthly newsletter was the only source of breaking news in the world of autism for families.

搬到西弗吉尼亚州亨廷顿后，她创立了州级和地方级的 NSAC 分会，并在自己家中启动了该协会的信息与转介服务，为家长和专业人士提供广泛的服务与资源。她没有依赖总机接线员或应答服务，而是亲自处理了大部分来电。求助请求全天候不断——纽约一位母亲，她的儿子被学校开除；阿拉巴马州一位父亲从汽车旅馆打来电话，寻找当地的其他家长；一位日本儿科医生，希望协会的专业顾问能与东京的家长沟通；佛罗里达州一位母亲，试图让被误解的儿子出狱。信息与转介服务最终获得了联邦资助，并在华盛顿设立了办公室。多年来，该协会的双月刊通讯是孤独症领域家庭获取最新消息的唯一来源。



Working with Mary Coleman, director of the Children’s Brain Research Clinic in Washington, Rimland and other NSAC parents made possible an in-depth study of the biology of autism in 1974 that was as far ahead of its time as the Goodwins’ talking typewriters. By conducting thorough examinations of seventy-eight children brought to the clinic by NSAC members from all across the country, the clinic’s researchers theorized that autism is not a single clinical entity but is composed of multiple distinct subtypes—a view that has been widely accepted in mainstream science only in recent years.

与华盛顿儿童脑研究诊所主任玛丽·科尔曼合作，里姆兰及其他 NSAC 家长在 1974 年促成了一项对孤独症生物学的深入研究，其超前程度堪比古德温夫妇的会说话打字机。通过对 NSAC 成员从全国各地带到诊所的 78 名儿童进行全面检查，诊所的研究人员提出理论：孤独症并非单一的临床实体，而是由多种不同的亚型组成——这一观点直到近年才被主流科学界广泛接受。



Rimland also did a groundbreaking study on savant skills based on data from his questionnaires, rediscovering the same clusters of enhanced ability in music, memory, art, mathematics, science, and technology that Asperger dubbed “autistic intelligence.” Rimland described very young children who could speak and write in multiple languages, had total recall for various kinds of statistics, could instantly identify a note played on a piano, were able to calculate square roots in their heads, had precocious abilities in drawing, and were so aware of subtle aspects of their environments that they seemed to have ESP. (Meanwhile, nearly all of them had been branded as “profoundly retarded.”) One mother’s report on her son demonstrated the untapped potential of these children:

里姆兰德还根据其问卷数据，对学者技能进行了一项开创性研究，重新发现了阿斯伯格所称的“孤独谱系智能”中，在音乐、记忆、艺术、数学、科学和技术方面同样突出的能力集群。里姆兰德描述了一些非常年幼的儿童，他们能说写多种语言，对各种统计数据过目不忘，能瞬间识别钢琴上弹奏的音符，能心算平方根，具备早熟的绘画能力，并且对环境中的细微之处如此敏感，以至于仿佛拥有超感知觉。（与此同时，他们几乎全被贴上了“重度智力障碍”的标签。）一位母亲关于她儿子的报告，展现了这些孩子未被发掘的潜力：



He reads and understands books on electronics and uses the theories to build devices . . . He understands the concepts of electronics, astronomy, music, navigation, and mechanics. He knows an astonishing amount about how things work and is familiar with technical terms. By the age of 12, he could find his way all over the city on his bike with a map and compass. He reads Bowditch on navigation. Joe is supposed to have an IQ of 80. He does assembly work in a Goodwill store.

他阅读并理解电子学书籍，并运用理论制作设备……他理解电子学、天文学、音乐、导航和机械学的概念。他对事物运作原理的了解惊人，且熟悉专业术语。12 岁时，他就能骑着自行车，仅凭地图和指南针走遍全城。他阅读鲍迪奇的导航学著作。乔的智商据称只有 80，他在一家慈善商店做装配工作。



Based on these accounts, Rimland became more open to the idea of a broader autistic continuum. He theorized that the achievements of geniuses like Einstein, Newton, and world chess champion Bobby Fischer were related to the fact that these men “manifested signs—sometimes several signs—of autism.” He ventured, “It may not be too far amiss to suggest that some autistic individuals are incipient geniuses whose eccentricities are so severe and incapacitating that all but minimal participation in the ‘normal’ world is precluded.”

基于这些描述，里姆兰对更广泛的孤独谱系概念持更开放的态度。他提出理论认为，爱因斯坦、牛顿以及国际象棋世界冠军鲍比·费舍尔等天才的成就，与这些人"表现出孤独谱系的迹象——有时是多种迹象"有关。他大胆推测："或许可以这样说，有些孤独谱系个体是潜在的天才，只是他们的怪癖过于严重且令人丧失能力，以至于几乎无法参与'正常'世界。"



In the midst of a dark age, NSAC laid the foundations of a better future, accomplishing it all with a small but highly committed membership. “So many children . . . needing so much . . . all over the world,” Clara Claiborne Park wrote in the society’s newsletter. “When the hours (or the money) you devote to NSAC seem too much, reflect that the waves you make may wash shores thousands of miles away, bringing hope to families you will never see.”

在黑暗时代中，NSAC 为更美好的未来奠定了基础，这一切都依靠规模虽小却高度投入的成员完成。“全世界有那么多孩子……需要那么多帮助……”克拉拉·克莱本·帕克在协会通讯中写道，“当你觉得投入 NSAC 的时间（或金钱）太多时，请想想你激起的涟漪可能会冲刷千里之外的海岸，为素未谋面的家庭带去希望。”



—



THIS SCRAPPY MOVEMENT MADE its debut on the national stage at NSAC’s First Annual Congress in Washington in July 1969. The theme—reflecting the rebellious spirit of the event and the terminology in use at the time—was “Better Everything for Mentally Ill Children.”

这场充满干劲的运动于 1969 年 7 月在华盛顿举行的 NSAC 首届年度大会上首次登上全国舞台。其主题——既体现了活动的反叛精神，也反映了当时的术语用法——是“为智障儿童创造更美好的一切”。



In the past, inviting patients’ families to a conference on autism would have seemed as unthinkable as inviting the “patients” themselves. But this conference, organized by parents, was different: between the formal workshop sessions, speakers and participants commingled in the hallways and the dining room, sharing information on equal terms. Speech therapists, psychologists, and biochemists chatted informally with family members about their research. Most of the participants were too engaged by what was going on around them to pay much attention to the other historical milestone taking place that week: the landing of Apollo 11 on the moon.

过去，邀请患者家属参加关于孤独症的会议，就像邀请“患者”本人一样不可思议。但这场由家长组织的会议却截然不同：在正式研讨会间隙，发言者与参与者在走廊和餐厅里自由交流，平等地分享信息。言语治疗师、心理学家和生物化学家与家属们非正式地聊着各自的研究。大多数参与者都沉浸于眼前的热烈讨论中，几乎无暇顾及当周发生的另一历史性事件——阿波罗 11 号登月。



The air in the Sheraton Palace was electric as parents spontaneously formed support groups to address issues that weren’t acknowledged in the medical literature, such as the challenges of raising autistic girls, or parenting children who were blind as well as autistic. Rimland and Lovaas were on the speakers list, as was Eric Schopler, a former graduate student of Bettelheim’s at the University of Chicago who had gone head-to-head with him for scapegoating parents. Schopler would go on to launch Division TEACCH (Treatment and Education of Autistic and Related Communication Handicapped Children) in North Carolina, the first statewide autism education program in the United States and the model for many other progressive programs since.

喜来登皇宫酒店内气氛热烈，家长们自发组成互助小组，共同应对医学文献中尚未提及的问题，例如抚养孤独谱系女孩的挑战，以及照顾既失明又患有孤独症的孩子。演讲嘉宾名单上包括里姆兰和洛瓦斯，还有埃里克·肖普勒——他曾是芝加哥大学贝特尔海姆的研究生，因指责贝特尔海姆将父母当作替罪羊而与其针锋相对。肖普勒后来在北卡罗来纳州创立了 TEACCH 部门（孤独症及相关沟通障碍儿童的治疗与教育项目），这是美国首个全州范围的孤独症教育项目，并成为此后众多先进教育项目的典范。



The keynote speaker was Kanner himself, who struck his usual note of grandiosity combined with self-effacement. “Ladies and gentlemen, I can’t tell you how pleased and touched I am to be spoken of with such affection and respect. Of course there are a few things, which, because of your good feeling toward me, are a bit exaggerated. I never discovered autism. It was there before,” he said coyly. (Georg and Anni Frankl could have attested to the truth of that statement, but they weren’t invited to the party that night.)

主题演讲者正是坎纳本人，他依旧保持着那种自大与谦逊交织的独特风格。“女士们先生们，我无法形容当听到大家以如此深情和敬意谈论我时，我是多么感动和欣喜。当然，由于各位对我的厚爱，有些说法难免有些夸张。我从未发现孤独症。它早就存在了。”他故作谦逊地说道。（乔治和安妮·弗兰克尔本可以证实这句话的真实性，但他们并未受邀参加当晚的聚会。）



Next, Kanner turned his attention to Bettelheim’s Empty Fortress. “I need not mention to you the book,” he said. “An empty book, I call it.” (The crowd applauded.) He said that, while reading it, he counted 150 times when the author had used phrases like “‘maybe,’ ‘perhaps,’ and ‘it may be just mere speculation’ . . . One hundred and fifty times!”

接着，坎纳将话题转向了贝特尔海姆的《空堡垒》。“我不必向各位提及这本书，”他说，“我称之为一本空洞的书。”（人群中响起掌声。）他表示，在阅读过程中，他统计到作者使用了 150 次诸如“‘也许’、‘可能’、‘这或许只是纯粹的推测’……一百五十次！”这样的措辞。



Then Kanner spoke the words that everyone in the room desperately wanted to hear, uttered with the formality of a royal proclamation: “And herewith I especially acquit you people as parents.” (The grateful audience jumped to its feet and gave him a standing ovation.) In the face of such an enthusiastic response, Kanner couldn’t resist acquitting himself also. “I have been misquoted many times,” he went on. “From the very first publication until the last, I spoke of this condition in no uncertain terms as ‘innate.’ But because I described some of the characteristics of some of the parents as persons, I was misquoted often as having said ‘it is all the parents’ fault.’ Those of you parents who have come to see me with your children know that this isn’t what I said . . . Once again, I thank you very, very much. Just keep up the good work.”

接着，坎纳以皇家宣告般的正式口吻，说出了在场所有人都渴望听到的话：“在此，我特别宣告你们这些父母无罪。”（感激的听众们纷纷起立，为他报以热烈的掌声。）面对如此热烈的反响，坎纳也忍不住为自己开脱。“我多次被错误引用，”他继续说道，“从第一篇论文到最后一篇，我都明确地将这种状况描述为‘先天性的’。但因为我描述了一些父母作为个体的某些特征，就常被曲解为‘全是父母的错’。那些带着孩子来见我的父母们都知道，我从未说过这样的话……再次，我由衷地感谢你们。请继续做好你们的工作。”



Far from the cheering crowds, Kanner would continue to refer to autism as a “childhood psychosis” in his work, and in 1973 he reprinted a collection of his essays describing his patients’ parents as “cold, humorless perfectionists” lacking “genuine warmth,” with no editorial caveats.

远离欢呼的人群，坎纳在后续工作中仍将孤独症称为“儿童精神病”，并在 1973 年重印了一部文集，其中描述患者父母为“冷漠、缺乏幽默感的完美主义者”，缺少“真正的温情”，且未加任何编辑说明。



But Kanner had already decisively lost his grip on the autism narrative. On her way back to Albany, Sullivan sat down in front of a TV set at the airport to watch Neil Armstrong take his first awkward steps out of the lunar excursion module onto the dusty surface of a new world. She and her fellow NSAC parents had crossed a threshold into a new world too: one in which they would help make the long-silent voices of their sons and daughters heard.

但坎纳已彻底失去对孤独症叙事的主导权。在返回奥尔巴尼的途中，沙利文在机场的电视机前驻足，观看尼尔·阿姆斯特朗笨拙地踏出登月舱，在布满尘埃的新世界表面留下最初的足迹。她和全国孤独症协会的家长们也跨过了一道门槛，进入了一个新世界：在这里，他们将帮助那些长久沉默的儿女们发出自己的声音。



—



THE NEXT NSAC CONGRESS, held in San Francisco in 1970, made good on that promise by inviting a young autistic man to the podium to address an audience of parents and professionals for the first time in history. After a brief introduction by his mother, twenty-one-year-old William Donovan made clear that he was very aware of his environment, even when people assumed he was oblivious. “As an autistic child, I felt very uncomfortable,” he began. “I tore up newspapers, pulled bedspreads off the beds, pulled books out of the bookcases, bounced cans and played with spinning tops, and broke every one of them. I would like to take this opportunity to tell you that I destroyed things because I couldn’t talk. I spun things because I couldn’t talk. It also made me feel good, of course.”

1970 年在旧金山举行的下一届 NSAC 大会兑现了这一承诺，首次邀请一位年轻的自闭症人士登上讲台，向家长和专业人士发表演讲。在母亲简短介绍后，21 岁的威廉·多诺万明确表示，即使人们以为他毫无察觉，他其实非常清楚周围的环境。"作为一个自闭症儿童，我感到非常不自在，"他开口说道，"我撕报纸、扯床罩、把书从书架上拽下来、扔罐头盒、玩陀螺，还把每个陀螺都弄坏了。我想借此机会告诉你们，我毁坏东西是因为无法说话，我旋转东西也是因为无法说话。当然，这样做也让我感觉很好。"



He was just getting started. “I hated going to school because classrooms were too confining. I didn’t like the idea of the other kids making fun of me and I didn’t want anyone to pass judgment on me as to how good or bad I was.” He described his teachers hitting him with rulers, locking him in a closet, and talking about him as if he weren’t there. Someone in the audience asked if he had spoken only in echolalia until he was ten because he “couldn’t” or “wouldn’t” talk normally. Donovan replied firmly, “Couldn’t.”

他才刚刚开始说。“我讨厌上学，因为教室太压抑了。我不喜欢其他孩子嘲笑我，也不希望任何人评判我有多好或多差。”他描述老师用尺子打他，把他锁在壁橱里，还当着他的面谈论他，仿佛他不在场。听众中有人问他，是否直到十岁都只通过回声言语说话，是因为“不能”还是“不愿”正常说话。多诺万坚定地回答：“不能。”



In addition to being autistic, Donovan had severe cataracts, and after getting vocational training at a school for the blind, he had been hired at a packaging company. His first day of work, he said, was the happiest day of his life. He also talked about his love of Charlie Brown and playing music, and concluded by saying, “I feel wonderful here today. I feel like the President. I hope all autistic children could grow up to be socially acceptable.”

除了患有孤独谱系障碍，多诺万还有严重的白内障。在一所盲人学校接受职业培训后，他被一家包装公司录用。他说，上班的第一天是他一生中最快乐的一天。他还谈到了自己对查理·布朗和演奏音乐的热爱，最后总结道：“今天在这里我感觉棒极了，就像总统一样。我希望所有孤独谱系的孩子都能长大成人，被社会所接纳。”



There were two paths toward achieving that goal, reflected in the two types of sessions on the conference program. One set focused on ways of changing society to make it a more accepting and accommodating place for people like Donovan, with topics like “School Is for All Children,” “How to Work with Your State Legislature,” and “Help Your Community Help Your Handicapped Child.” In her introduction, Donovan’s mother talked about how she had finally worked up the courage to take her son everywhere instead of hiding him in the house (or committing him to Bellevue, as she and her husband had been advised to do). She advised her fellow parents:

实现这一目标有两条路径，这体现在会议议程的两种类型中。一类聚焦于改变社会，使其成为对像多诺万这样的人更包容、更友好的地方，议题包括“学校属于所有孩子”、“如何与州立法机构合作”以及“帮助你的社区帮助你的残障孩子”。多诺万的母亲在开场发言中谈到，她最终鼓起勇气带儿子去任何地方，而不是把他藏在家里（或像她和丈夫曾被建议的那样，把他送进贝尔维尤医院）。她向其他家长建议：



Never be embarrassed about taking them places. When Judy Garland was playing in New York, we decided to get a box seat and take Bill to see her in person because he loved her records. God bless Judy, she was wonderful. Bill was directly over her head and acting up as I expected he would and she looked up and said, “What’s the matter, darling?” That little bit of recognition from her made all the difference. He calmed down and enjoyed the rest of the show.

永远不要因为带他们去公共场所而感到难为情。当朱迪·嘉兰在纽约演出时，我们决定订一个包厢座位，带比尔去看她本人，因为他喜欢她的唱片。上帝保佑朱迪，她太棒了。比尔正好坐在她头顶上方，像我所预料的那样闹腾起来，她抬头问道：“怎么了，亲爱的？”她这一点点关注带来了天壤之别。他平静下来，享受了演出的剩余部分。



The alternate path was trying to change the children themselves to make them more “socially acceptable”—the path that Lovaas had embarked on with ABA and that Rimland was pursuing with his search for an orthomolecular cure for autism. At NSAC meetings, Sullivan would take informal polls asking for a show of hands from those who thought the organization should focus on finding a cure instead of lobbying for services. “Nearly all the parents’ hands went up for services,” she recalls.

另一条路径则是试图改变孩子本身，让他们变得“更符合社会规范”——这正是洛瓦斯通过 ABA 疗法所走的道路，也是里姆兰通过寻找孤独症的分子矫正疗法所追求的方向。在 NSAC 的会议上，沙利文会进行非正式投票，请那些认为该组织应专注于寻找治疗方法而非争取服务支持的人举手。“几乎所有家长都举手支持服务，”她回忆道。



In 1974, West Virginia became the first state in the Union to specifically include autism in its mandatory public education laws, opening the doors of classrooms to hundreds of kids for the first time. Sullivan was one of the chief proponents of the Education for All Handicapped Children Act, Public Law 94-142, which mandated that disabled children in every school district in America have a right to a “free and appropriate” public education and be educated in the “least restrictive environment” possible, encouraging mainstreaming when appropriate. (Before the passage of the law, school districts in most states were allowed to choose whether they were willing to educate a child with disabilities, and more than one million children were locked out of public education.) The act, signed into law by President Gerald Ford in 1975, became the precursor of the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) that is in force today. The law also empowered parents to file grievances if their child’s needs weren’t being met.

1974 年，西弗吉尼亚州成为美国首个将孤独症纳入义务教育法规的州，首次为数百名儿童打开了教室大门。沙利文是《全体残障儿童教育法案》（公法 94-142）的主要推动者之一，该法案规定美国每个学区的残障儿童都有权接受"免费且适当"的公共教育，并应在"最少限制环境"中接受教育，鼓励在适当情况下进行融合教育。（在该法案通过前，大多数州的学区可自行决定是否接纳残障儿童，超过百万儿童被排斥在公共教育体系之外。）这项由杰拉尔德·福特总统于 1975 年签署生效的法案，成为现行《残疾人教育法案》（IDEA）的前身。该法律还赋予家长在孩子需求未得到满足时提出申诉的权利。



After the passage of the Education for All Handicapped Children Act, Sullivan focused on demanding services for autistic adults. “There was nothing for adults—zip,” she recalls. “We had to start from scratch.” This was particularly important because the traditional caretakers for autistic adults who were not in institutions were stay-at-home moms, and in the 1960s more and more women were entering the workforce and launching their own careers. Family members that looked to private and public agencies for help were faced with a confusing maze of limited options presided over by underpaid, overworked case managers who often occupied their positions for only a short time. “We cannot allow another generation of our adult children to go without the vital services that any humane society knows is necessary for a life of dignity and worth,” Sullivan wrote.

《全体残障儿童教育法案》通过后，沙利文将重心转向为成年孤独谱系个体争取服务权益。"当时针对成年人的服务完全空白——零基础，"她回忆道，"我们不得不从零开始。"这项工作尤为紧迫，因为传统上照顾非机构收治的成年孤独谱系个体的群体是家庭主妇，而 20 世纪 60 年代越来越多女性开始进入职场、开创事业。那些向公私机构寻求帮助的家庭成员，面对的是由薪酬微薄、工作超负荷且任职期短暂的个案管理员主导的、选择极其有限的混乱迷宫。"我们不能让下一代成年子女继续缺失任何文明社会都深知维系尊严生活所必需的关键服务，"沙利文写道。



Over time, the two paths represented by NSAC’s founders—Sullivan’s focus on services and Rimland’s search for a cure—would diverge, resulting in Rimland being voted off the board of his own organization. An early sign of this rift was the controversy that broke out in 1965 after a series of articles in the popular press made clear just how far Lovaas was willing to go to make autistic children “socially acceptable.”

随着时间的推移，NSAC 创始人代表的两条道路——沙利文侧重服务，里姆兰寻求治愈——逐渐分道扬镳，最终导致里姆兰被自己创立的组织董事会投票除名。这场分裂的早期征兆出现在 1965 年，当时大众媒体刊登的一系列文章清楚揭示了洛瓦斯为了让孤独谱系儿童变得"符合社会规范"愿意走得多远。



VII



One of Lovaas’s first experiments with Beth was like a music-appreciation class in hell. For months, the psychologist and his assistants played children’s songs for her on a guitar while reinforcing proper social behavior by smiling and saying “That’s a good girl!” when she clapped or sang along. Lovaas was testing the hypothesis that Beth’s self-injurious behavior would decrease as she became more socially aware.

洛瓦斯对贝丝进行的首批实验之一，堪称地狱版的音乐鉴赏课。数月间，这位心理学家与助手们用吉他为她弹奏童谣，每当她拍手或跟唱时，便以微笑和"真是个好女孩！"的赞许来强化恰当的社会行为。洛瓦斯在验证一个假设：随着贝丝社会意识的增强，她的自伤行为会逐渐减少。



Beth was a good girl: within two months, she was clapping her hands in rhythm and joining in rousing choruses of “The children in the bus go ‘wiggle-wiggle-wiggle.’” The more she was engaged by the music, the less she banged her head on the furniture and flapped her hands, just as Lovaas predicted.

贝丝确实是个好女孩：两个月内，她已能跟着节奏拍手，并加入"公交车上的孩子们扭啊扭啊扭"的激昂合唱。正如洛瓦斯所预测，她越投入音乐，就越少用头撞击家具或挥舞双手。



That was the first acquisition trial. Then the first extinction trial began. This time, the experimenters withheld their smiles and praise, even when Beth spontaneously broke into song and shimmied her hips at the point in the song when the children went wiggle-wiggle-wiggle. At first, she responded to the sudden chill in the air by clapping and singing along even more vigorously. But after more than a week of getting no response, she started beating herself up more than ever. The trials continued in that vein for months with alternating rounds of acquisition and extinction. Lovaas’s team varied the parameters of the experimental design methodically, some days reciting the lyrics of the songs to Beth in flat, tuneless voices. During the acquisition trials, her behavior would improve dramatically, but during the extinction trials, she hurt herself so badly that Lovaas aborted the experiment.

这是第一次习得试验。随后第一次消退试验开始了。这次实验者不再给予微笑和表扬，即使贝丝在歌曲进行到孩子们扭动身体的部分时自发地唱起歌来扭动臀部，他们也无动于衷。起初，她通过更卖力地拍手和跟唱来回应这突如其来的冷淡。但在持续一周多得不到回应后，她开始比以往更猛烈地自残。试验就这样在习得与消退的交替循环中持续了数月。洛瓦斯团队有条不紊地调整实验设计的参数，有时会用平淡单调的声调向贝丝念诵歌词。在习得试验阶段，她的行为会显著改善，但到了消退试验阶段，她自残得如此严重，以至于洛瓦斯不得不终止实验。



A similar pattern emerged when Beth was taught to press a bar as the experimenters urged her on with effusive comments like “I love you very much” and “You’re a sweetheart.” Then it was extinction time, and Beth was faced once again with a roomful of adults who had inexplicably stopped responding to her. She began battering herself so violently that Lovaas again terminated the experiment.

类似的情况也出现在贝丝身上：当实验者用“我非常爱你”、“你是个甜心”等热情洋溢的话语鼓励她按压杠杆时，她学会了这个动作。随后进入消退阶段，贝丝再次面对满屋子的大人，他们却莫名其妙地不再回应她。她开始猛烈地自残，洛瓦斯不得不再次终止实验。



The psychoanalytical theories of the day held that the source of Beth’s self-injurious behavior was her internalized feelings of guilt (a “hostile introject” in Freudian terms). To be on the safe side, Lovaas’s assistants would say to Beth “I do not think you are bad” when she injured herself. But their repetition of this stilted phrase only made her flail her limbs more violently. The possibility that Beth was responding in a comprehensible way to the bizarre behavior of the people around her didn’t enter Lovaas’s mind.

当时的精神分析理论认为，贝丝自残行为的根源是她内化的负罪感（用弗洛伊德术语来说就是“敌意内射”）。为保险起见，洛瓦斯的助手会在贝丝自残时对她说：“我不认为你很坏。”但他们重复这句生硬的话语，反而让贝丝更激烈地挥舞四肢。洛瓦斯从未想过，贝丝可能是在以可理解的方式回应周围人怪异的行为。



Extinguishing Beth’s self-injurious behavior by ignoring her would have been “a slow procedure requiring several sessions or days,” Lovaas predicted. He had good reason to fear that his sole experimental subject—on whom his National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH) funding depended—might hurt herself so badly that his experiments could no longer go on. So Lovaas sought a more expeditious solution, which came to him in a flash one day in the lab.

洛瓦斯预测，通过忽视贝丝的自伤行为来消除它，将是一个“需要多次疗程或数天的缓慢过程”。他有充分的理由担心，他唯一的实验对象——他依赖国家心理健康研究所（NIMH）的资助来维持研究——可能会严重伤害自己，导致实验无法继续。因此，洛瓦斯寻求一种更快捷的解决方案，而这一方案在实验室的一天里突然闪现在他脑海中。



He was talking with a colleague, when Beth began striking her head against the sharp edge of a metal cabinet. Like any good behaviorist, Lovaas rarely ventured to speculate about his subjects’ mental states, but in this case he made an exception. He felt that his nearly paternal relationship with her gave him a unique window on the inner being lurking behind her “autistic shell,” and what he saw there enraged him: this nine-year-old girl was scheming and plotting against him.

他正与一位同事交谈时，贝丝开始用头撞击金属柜的锋利边缘。像任何优秀的行为主义者一样，洛瓦斯很少冒险推测实验对象的心理状态，但这次他破例了。他感到自己与她近乎父女般的关系，为他提供了一个独特的窗口，得以窥见隐藏在她“孤独谱系外壳”之下的内在生命——而他所看到的令他愤怒：这个九岁的女孩正在密谋对抗他。



“She would only hit steel cabinets, and she would only hit them on the edge, because, you see, she wanted to draw blood,” Lovaas told Psychology Today’s Paul Chance. So he “reacted automatically,” as he would have with one of his own children—“I reached over and cracked her one right on the rear,” he said. The psychologist expressed relief that he didn’t have to reach very far, because Beth “was a big fat girl” who offered him “an easy target.” Speaking of himself in the third person, he told Chance:

“她只会打铁柜子，而且只打在边缘上，因为，你看，她想让自己流血，”洛瓦斯告诉《今日心理学》的保罗·钱斯。于是他“自动做出反应”，就像对待自己的孩子一样——“我伸手在她屁股上狠狠打了一下，”他说。这位心理学家庆幸自己不用伸太远，因为贝丝“是个大胖姑娘”，给他提供了“一个容易得手的目标”。他用第三人称谈论自己，对钱斯说：



She stopped hitting herself for about 30 seconds because, you see, she sized up the situation, laid out her strategy and then she hit herself once more. But in those 30 seconds while she was laying out her strategy, Professor Lovaas was laying out his. At first I thought, “God, what have I done,” but then I noticed she had stopped hitting herself. I felt guilty, but I felt great. Then she hit herself again, and I really laid it on her . . . So I let her know that there was no question in my mind that I was going to kill her if she hit herself once more, and that was pretty much it. She hit herself a few times more, but we had the problem licked.

她停止自残大约 30 秒，因为，你看，她在评估局势，制定策略，然后她又打了一下自己。但在那 30 秒她制定策略时，洛瓦斯教授也在制定他的策略。起初我想，“天哪，我做了什么”，但随后我注意到她停止了自残。我感到内疚，但也感觉很好。然后她又打了一下自己，我真的狠狠教训了她……我让她明白，我心里毫无疑问，如果她再打自己一下，我就杀了她，事情基本就这样了。她又自残了几次，但我们解决了这个问题。



Under the laws of the University of California, Lovaas was required to have his research proposals approved by the Human Subjects Board, so explaining that he wanted to “really lay it on” his experimental subjects wouldn’t do. But there was an alternate way of saying basically the same thing that was acceptable in the lexicon of behaviorism. He began exploring the use of aversive stimuli—otherwise known in the trade as “punishment”—as a less time-consuming way of extinguishing self-injury.

根据加州大学的规定，洛瓦斯的研究方案必须获得人类受试者委员会的批准，因此他不能直接说明想"狠狠整治"实验对象。但在行为主义术语体系中，存在一种可被接受的替代表述。他开始探索使用厌恶刺激（业内俗称"惩罚"）作为更省时的自残行为消除方法。



—



THE USE OF PUNISHMENT on human subjects was controversial among Lovaas’s colleagues. In his classic textbook Science and Human Behavior, Skinner explained that while aversives may seem to promptly extinguish undesirable behavior, the behavior often returns with a vengeance after the punishment ceases, because the subject has not been taught more adaptive ways to behave. He also pointed out that punishment creates fear, guilt, and shame, resulting in less learning overall. (In other words, a child compelled to practice the piano with threats of spanking does not tend to become a virtuoso but instead learns to hate music.) Skinner also cautioned that the use of aversives has negative effects on the researcher, potentially turning the experimental situation into a sadistic power play. “In the long run,” he observed, “punishment, unlike reinforcement, works to the disadvantage of both the punished organism and the punishing agency.”

在洛瓦斯（Lovaas）的同事中，对人类受试者使用惩罚手段一直存在争议。斯金纳（Skinner）在其经典教科书《科学与人类行为》中解释道，虽然厌恶刺激似乎能迅速消除不良行为，但惩罚停止后，这些行为往往会变本加厉地重现，因为受试者并未学会更适应性的行为方式。他还指出，惩罚会引发恐惧、内疚和羞耻感，导致整体学习效果下降。（换言之，若以打屁股威胁强迫孩子练琴，孩子非但不会成为演奏大师，反而会憎恶音乐。）斯金纳同时警告，使用厌恶刺激会对研究者产生负面影响，可能使实验情境沦为施虐式的权力游戏。他总结道："从长远来看，与强化不同，惩罚对受罚者和施罚者双方都弊大于利。"



But Lovaas failed to heed this advice, in part because he was convinced that children like Beth would never learn to socially engage unless their self-injurious behavior was extinguished first. Soon he expanded the sphere of behavior targeted for punishment to also include hand flapping, rocking, spinning, and other forms of self-stimulation. On the basis of his own experiments, he concluded that stimming made autistic children less sensitive to auditory input, which interfered with their learning. In the lab, he referred to self-stimulation as “garbage behavior,” because if the children were engaged in a more productive activity, they tended to stop stimming.

但洛瓦斯未能采纳这一建议，部分原因是他坚信，像贝丝这样的孩子除非先消除自伤行为，否则永远学不会社交互动。很快，他将惩罚范围扩大到包括拍手、摇晃身体、转圈及其他形式的自我刺激行为。基于自身实验，他得出结论：自我刺激行为会降低孤独谱系儿童对听觉输入的敏感度，从而干扰其学习能力。在实验室中，他将自我刺激称为"垃圾行为"，因为当孩子们参与更具建设性的活动时，往往会停止自我刺激。



He also believed that extinguishing this apparently senseless behavior would reduce a major source of stigma for autistic people and their families. “Since the emphasis of our treatment program is to make the child look as neat and appropriate as possible, we attempt to suppress the more severe or grotesque forms of self-stimulatory behavior by the use of aversive stimuli,” he explained to NSAC parents. “It is obviously very embarrassing for people to be in the company of a child who jumps up and down and ritualistically slaps his arms in front of his face: such behavior socially isolates the child and embarrasses his parents.”

他还认为，消除这种看似无意义的行为，可以减少孤独谱系群体及其家庭面临的主要污名来源。"由于我们治疗方案的重点是让孩子看起来尽可能整洁得体，我们试图通过使用厌恶刺激来抑制更严重或怪异的自我刺激行为，"他向 NSAC 的家长们解释道。"显然，和一个上下跳跃、仪式化地在面前拍打手臂的孩子在一起会让人非常尴尬：这种行为会使孩子在社交上被孤立，也让他的父母难堪。"



Researchers would eventually discover that autistic people stim to reduce anxiety—and also simply because it feels good. In fact, harmless forms of self-stimulation (like flapping and fidgeting) may facilitate learning by freeing up executive-functioning resources in the brain that would otherwise be devoted to suppressing them. For Lovaas, however, self-injury, self-stimulation, and echolalia were all of a piece and equally ripe for extinction. Alone in his lab with his team of devoted grad students and experimental subjects in no position to complain, he began seeking means of punishment that could get past a review board.

研究人员最终发现，孤独谱系群体进行自我刺激是为了缓解焦虑——同时也仅仅因为这样做感觉良好。事实上，无害的自我刺激形式（如拍手和坐立不安）可能通过释放大脑中原本用于抑制这些行为的执行功能资源，来促进学习。然而，对于洛瓦斯来说，自伤行为、自我刺激和回声语言都是一回事，同样需要被消除。在他独自与一群忠心的研究生和无法抱怨的实验对象共处的实验室里，他开始寻找能够通过审查委员会的惩罚手段。



After his work with Beth, he conducted a series of experiments on a pair of five-year-old twin boys named Mike and Marty. He estimated that the brothers spent 70 percent of their waking hours “rocking, fondling themselves, and moving hands and arms in repetitive, stereotyped manners” while engaging in “a fair amount of tantrum behaviors, such as screaming, throwing objects, and hitting themselves.” They had never spoken or been toilet trained. For one of his first rounds of experiments on Mike and Marty, his punishment of choice was exceptionally loud sound. He aimed blasts of “well over 100” decibels at them—comparable to the roaring of a power saw at close range. His aim was to produce “pain or fear” in the twins as a way of making the presence of adults “meaningful” and “rewarding” by comparison, as typical children might seek safety at their mother’s bedside after a bad dream.

在与贝丝合作之后，他对一对名为迈克和马蒂的五岁双胞胎男孩进行了一系列实验。据他估计，这对兄弟在清醒时有 70%的时间“摇晃身体、抚摸自己，并以重复刻板的方式活动手脚”，同时伴有“相当多的发怒行为，如尖叫、扔东西和自残”。他们从未开口说话，也未接受过如厕训练。在针对迈克和马蒂的首轮实验中，他选择的惩罚手段是异常刺耳的噪音。他向孩子们释放了“远超 100 分贝”的声波——相当于近距离电锯的轰鸣声。其目的是让双胞胎产生“痛苦或恐惧”，通过对比使成年人的存在变得“有意义”且“值得依赖”，就像普通儿童在噩梦后会在母亲床边寻求安全感那样。



The results of these experiments were disappointing. Even when subjected to decibel levels capable of causing physical damage to the eardrum, Mike and Marty “remained unperturbed, particularly after the first two or three presentations.” Lovaas doubled down, turning to a method of punishment that had a long track record in behaviorist experiments on animals: electric shock. To head off any criticism for employing such harsh methods on preschool-age children, he added, “It is important to note, in view of the moral and ethical reasons which might preclude the use of electric shock, that their future was certain institutionalization.”

这些实验的结果令人失望。即使承受足以对耳膜造成物理损伤的分贝水平，迈克和马蒂"依然不为所动，尤其是在最初两三次刺激之后"。洛瓦斯变本加厉，转而采用一种在动物行为主义实验中有着悠久历史的惩罚手段：电击。为了预先防范因对学龄前儿童使用如此严苛方法而招致的批评，他补充道："鉴于道德伦理因素可能阻碍电击的使用，需要指出的是，这些孩子的未来注定是进入收容机构。"



He taped strips of metal foil to the floor of a room in his lab and wired these strips to a respectable-sounding device called a Harvard Inductorium—a modified Faraday coil that offered fine-tuning of its electrical output down to zero. The strips were laid across the floor, spaced half an inch apart so that a child who stepped into the room was guaranteed make contact with at least two of them, completing the circuit. To confirm the aversive effect of this apparatus, Lovaas’s grad students first tested it on themselves: “The shock was set at a level at which each of the three Es (experimenters) standing barefoot on the floor agreed that it was definitely painful and frightening.”

他在实验室房间的地板上贴了几条金属箔带，并将这些箔带连接到一个听起来很专业的装置——哈佛感应线圈（一种改良版法拉第线圈，可将其电输出微调至零）。这些箔带间隔半英寸铺设在地板上，确保踏入房间的孩子至少会接触到其中两条，从而形成闭合电路。为验证该装置的厌恶效果，洛瓦斯的研究生们首先在自己身上进行了测试："三名实验者赤脚站在地板上，一致认为该电击强度确实令人感到疼痛和恐惧。"



In a typical round of trials, Mike or Marty would be placed between researchers standing three feet apart. Then a researcher would say “Come here,” beckoning to the boy with outstretched arms. If he didn’t approach the researcher within three seconds, he would get a shock. Then the same procedure would be repeated with the other twin, and so on, over and over again, for hundreds of trials. If Mike and Marty tried to escape the shocks by “beginning to sit down, moving toward the window to climb on its ledge, etc.,” they would get another jolt from Lovaas’s Inductorium.

在一轮典型的试验中，迈克或马蒂会被安置在相距三英尺的研究人员之间。随后一名研究人员会张开双臂招呼男孩说“过来”。如果他在三秒内没有靠近研究人员，就会受到电击。接着对另一名双胞胎重复相同流程，如此循环往复，进行数百次试验。如果迈克和马蒂试图通过“开始坐下、走向窗台攀爬窗沿等方式”逃避电击，他们就会从洛瓦斯的感应线圈装置中再遭电击。



In contrast to his experiments with sound, Lovaas deemed these experiments a stunning success. In just a handful of sessions, Mike and Marty learned to practically jump into the experimenters’ arms to avoid the painful shocks. In a subsequent round of trials, instead of the electrified floor, Lovaas employed a remote-controlled device called a Lee-Lectronic Trainer—a box the size of a cigarette pack used in canine obedience tests—affixed to the boys’ buttocks. A researcher would face Mike or Marty, say “Hug me” or “Kiss me,” and apply shock if the boy didn’t get moving in three seconds. The twins’ behavior, Lovaas noted approvingly, “changed markedly toward increased affection.” He added that the therapeutic benefits of this procedure exceeded his expectations (S and E referred to subject and experimenter, respectively):

与他在声音方面的实验相比，洛瓦斯认为这些实验取得了惊人的成功。仅仅经过几次训练，迈克和马蒂就学会了几乎跳进实验者的怀里，以躲避痛苦的电击。在随后的一轮试验中，洛瓦斯不再使用带电地板，而是采用了一种名为“李-电子训练器”的遥控装置——一个香烟盒大小的盒子，原本用于犬类服从测试——固定在男孩们的臀部。研究人员会面对迈克或马蒂，说“抱抱我”或“亲亲我”，如果男孩在三秒内没有行动，就会施加电击。洛瓦斯赞许地指出，这对双胞胎的行为“显著地向更亲密的倾向转变”。他补充说，这一程序的治疗效果超出了他的预期（S 和 E 分别指代受试者和实验者）：



Once Ss had been trained to avoid shock, they often smiled and laughed, and gave other signs of happiness or comfort. For example, they would “mold” or “cup” to Es body as small infants do to parents. Such behaviors were unobserved prior to these experiments.

一旦受试者学会了躲避电击，他们常常会微笑和大笑，并表现出其他幸福或舒适的迹象。例如，他们会像幼儿依偎父母一样，“贴合”或“蜷缩”在实验者的身体上。这些行为在实验之前从未被观察到。



He ventured that this behavior indicated that the twins’ “avoidance of pain generated contentment.” It was not an unreasonable speculation.

他推测，这种行为表明这对双胞胎“因避免痛苦而产生了满足感”。这并非毫无根据的猜测。



—



LOVAAS WAS NOT THE first to employ devices designed for use in kennels and feedlots in the service of demonstrating that autistic children were capable of learning. That distinction belonged to Todd Risley, his colleague at the University of Kansas. In 1963, Risley repurposed “a commercially available device for shocking livestock” from a company called Hot Shot Products in Minneapolis to discourage a nonverbal, seizure-prone six-year-old girl from climbing up a bookshelf. With Skinner, one of the pioneers of the field, in the anti-aversive camp, Lovaas and Risley were at risk of being perceived as outliers. But in 1964, Richard L. Solomon, a leading expert on the lengths to which animals would go to escape pain (technically known as “avoidance learning”), mounted a case for punishment in American Psychologist that couldn’t have been better timed from Lovaas’s perspective.

洛瓦斯并非首个将原本用于犬舍和饲养场的设备用于证明孤独谱系儿童具备学习能力的人。这一殊荣属于他在堪萨斯大学的同事托德·里斯利。1963 年，里斯利将明尼阿波利斯一家名为 Hot Shot Products 公司生产的“商用牲畜电击装置”改造后，用于阻止一名不会说话、易发癫痫的六岁女孩攀爬书架。当斯金纳——该领域的先驱之一——站在反惩罚阵营时，洛瓦斯和里斯利面临着被视为异类的风险。但 1964 年，理查德·L·所罗门——一位研究动物为逃避痛苦会付出多大代价（专业术语称为“回避学习”）的顶尖专家——在《美国心理学家》期刊上为惩罚措施提出了论证，从洛瓦斯的角度来看，这篇论文的发表时机再好不过了。



Even allowing for changing trends in psychology, Solomon’s article makes for disquieting reading as he depicts a veritable Noah’s ark of starved, shocked, and throttled animals undergoing the torments of the damned in the name of advancing his theories of learning. He reported that appetite can be permanently suppressed in dogs and cats by hotwiring their food dishes. Spider monkeys swore off eating altogether if a researcher surprised them with toy snakes at mealtime, though in some cases only “odd sexual behaviors, tics, and long periods of crying” ensued. Puppies swatted with newspapers while eating horsemeat would go on a permanent hunger strike before ever tasting it again, and rats trained to press a bar for food would freeze in place, breathe heavily, defecate, and urinate if the bar unexpectedly yielded a jolt of electricity instead. Even the most primordial instinct—the urge to mate—could be extinguished with sufficient application of aversive stimuli, Solomon marveled.

即使考虑到心理学趋势的变化，所罗门的文章读来仍令人不安。他描绘了一幅名副其实的诺亚方舟图景——饥饿、震惊、窒息的动物们，以推进其学习理论之名，承受着地狱般的折磨。他报告称，通过给狗和猫的食盘通电，可以永久抑制它们的食欲。蜘蛛猴若在进食时被研究人员用玩具蛇惊吓，便会彻底绝食，尽管在某些情况下，只会出现“异常的性行为、抽搐和长时间的哭泣”。吃马肉时被报纸拍打的小狗，会永久绝食，再也不碰那种食物。而训练按压杠杆获取食物的老鼠，若杠杆意外释放电击，则会僵在原地、呼吸急促、排便排尿。所罗门惊叹道，即便是最原始的冲动——交配欲望——也能通过充分施加厌恶刺激而被消除。



He had just one helpful suggestion to make: too many learning theorists were still relying on that old classic, electric shock. “Perhaps a bit of softheartedness is partly responsible for limiting our inventiveness,” he mused. “The Inquisitors, the Barbarians, and the Puritans could have given us some good hints!”

他只有一个有用的建议：太多学习理论家仍依赖那个老掉牙的经典方法——电击。“或许心肠太软在一定程度上限制了我们的创造力，”他沉思道，“宗教裁判所、野蛮人和清教徒本可以给我们一些好提示！”



Suitably grateful to his distinguished colleague from the University of Pennsylvania, Lovaas characterized Solomon’s work as a triumph of rationality over sentimentality. “Psychology and related professions have shied away from, and often condemned, the use of pain for therapeutic purposes,” he wrote. “We agree with Solomon that such objections to the use of pain have a moral rather than a scientific basis . . . Punishment can be a very effective tool for behavior change.”

洛瓦斯对这位来自宾夕法尼亚大学的杰出同事深表感激，称所罗门的研究是理性战胜感性的胜利。“心理学及相关专业一直回避，甚至谴责将痛苦用于治疗目的，”他写道，“我们同意所罗门的观点，即这种反对使用痛苦的反对意见更多基于道德而非科学依据……惩罚可以成为改变行为的非常有效工具。”



Seeking other innovative methods to promote learning, Lovaas tried preventing the children in his lab from eating before an experiment. Immediately after the “Hug me” trials, Lovaas put Mike and Marty on a strict behaviorist diet: no food at all, seven days a week, but the token scraps earned by their acquiring the ability to perform a complex social task while pressing a bar to avoid shock. Water deprivation was also stringently enforced, though he noted that, “to avoid dehydration,” water was available to the boys “ad libidinum” after six p.m. each day. One of the twins didn’t perform well in this round because he would never stop stimming, but the psychologist remained hopeful that hunger could provide a powerful incentive for the subjects of his future experiments.

为了探索其他促进学习的创新方法，洛瓦斯试图阻止他实验室里的孩子们在实验前进食。在“抱抱我”试验结束后，洛瓦斯立即让迈克和马蒂接受严格的行为主义饮食控制：每周七天完全不提供食物，只有当他们学会在按压杠杆以避免电击的同时完成复杂社交任务时，才能获得少量食物残渣作为奖励。他还严格执行了限水措施，不过他指出，“为避免脱水”，每天下午六点后男孩们可以“随意”饮水。这对双胞胎中的一个在这一轮表现不佳，因为他总是无法停止自我刺激行为，但这位心理学家仍抱有希望，认为饥饿可以为未来实验对象提供强大的激励。



“Let me tell you, it is a pleasure to work with a child who is on mild food deprivation,” Lovaas told a room full of NSAC parents, “particularly if he has a history of being a good eater, because that is a child who is truly motivated to learn.”

“让我告诉你们，和一个处于轻度食物剥夺状态的孩子一起工作是一种享受，”洛瓦斯对满屋子的 NSAC 家长说道，“尤其是当他过去是个好胃口的孩子时，因为这样的孩子才是真正有学习动力的。”



—



CONCERNED THAT SOME OF his techniques might seem unorthodox, Lovaas invited members of the press down to the lab to watch him in action. As usual, he prefaced his demonstration by showing footage of children who had attempted to chew through their own limbs or bite off their nails with their teeth. (When he exhibited this reel to parents, he explained that the little girl on camera “won’t hit her head on round corners. She wants it bloody.”) His message was clear: This is what autism looks like if it is left untreated.

担心自己的某些方法可能显得不合常规，洛瓦斯邀请媒体记者到实验室观看他的实际操作。像往常一样，他在演示前先播放了一段录像：画面中的孩子试图咬断自己的肢体，或用牙齿啃掉指甲。（当向家长展示这段影像时，他解释道，镜头里的小女孩"不会去撞圆角，她想要撞得头破血流"。）他的意图很明确：这就是孤独症不加干预时的样子。



Even journalists who might normally have been troubled by the sight of a barefoot five-year-old boy recoiling from an electrified floor were persuaded by Lovaas’s solemn pronouncements that once you had taken it upon yourself to physically strike a child, you were morally responsible for his fate. “No one punishes who isn’t prepared to devote a major part of his life to that child. Nobody punishes a child who doesn’t also love that child,” he told a reporter. “Once you lay your hand on a child it morally obligates you to work with that child. You see, that is one of the reasons that people stay away from punishment—they don’t want to commit themselves. After you hit a kid you can’t just get up and leave him; you are hooked to that kid.” The reporter was so impressed that he dubbed Lovaas a visionary—a “poet with a cattle prod.”

即使是那些通常会对一个赤脚五岁男孩因触电地板而退缩的画面感到不安的记者，也被洛瓦斯严肃的论断说服了——一旦你亲自动手打了一个孩子，你就在道德上对他的命运负有责任。“没有人会惩罚一个他不准备投入大部分生命去陪伴的孩子。没有人会惩罚一个他不爱的孩子，”他告诉记者，“一旦你把手放在一个孩子身上，你在道德上就有义务与他一起努力。你看，这就是人们回避惩罚的原因之一——他们不想承担责任。打了孩子之后，你不能站起来就走；你被那个孩子拴住了。”这位记者深受触动，称洛瓦斯为远见卓识者——一位“手持电牛棒的诗人”。



His bid for transparency would prove to be more controversial than he expected. Life magazine elevated the psychologist to international fame with a profile that ran under the memorable headline “Screams, Slaps, and Love.” Praising Lovaas’s work as “a surprising, shocking treatment that helps far-gone mental cripples,” the article (and its photo spread, billed as “an appalling gallery of madness”) shaped public perceptions of autism for decades to come.

他对透明度的追求，最终引发的争议远超预期。《生活》杂志以一篇题为《尖叫、耳光与爱》的特写报道，将这位心理学家推向了国际声誉的巅峰。文章盛赞洛瓦斯的研究是"一种令人震惊的疗法，能帮助重度精神障碍患者"，配以被称为"令人毛骨悚然的疯狂图鉴"的摄影专题，在随后数十年间深刻塑造了公众对孤独症的认知。



It’s hard to imagine a more disturbing introduction to the subject. The first page is dominated by photographs of a graduate student slapping a seven-year-old boy named Billy for not “paying attention during his speech lesson.” The boy is in tears on page two as the student bellows at him inches away from his face. Meanwhile, another boy, his back against a wall, stares into space “like a fragile Buddha . . . endlessly contemplating nothing.” If there was a conspicuous lack of toys and games in these rooms full of children, it was because “such children do not play.”

很难想象比这更令人不安的课题引入方式。开篇照片中，一名研究生正掌掴七岁男孩比利，只因他在"语言课时注意力不集中"。第二页上，男孩泪流满面，而研究生凑近他面前厉声呵斥。与此同时，另一个背靠墙壁的男孩凝视虚空，"像一尊脆弱的佛像……无尽地沉思虚无"。若说这些满是孩童的房间里明显缺少玩具和游戏，那是因为"这样的孩子不会玩耍"。



In a sidebar, Life reporter Don Moser described the terrifying existence of Pat, Billy’s mother, “at the mercy of a small boy so cunning and so violent that he almost propelled her into a nervous breakdown.” Refusing to eat anything but hamburgers from one fast-food chain, he forced his father to buy “cheap, greasy” burgers by the sack every morning at a local franchise. One day, Billy flushed one of his sister’s dolls down the toilet. “It was like living with the devil,” his mother said. The only thing that put fear into the heart of this possessed child was the dour visage of Alfred Hitchcock, so Pat taped up portraits of the director in various locations throughout the house, including the bathroom door, so she could take her baths in peace. But there was hope. Enter Lovaas, like Max von Sydow in The Exorcist, bearing a cattle prod instead of a crucifix. After ninety thousand discrete-trial sessions, Life reported, Billy can “ask for any food by name.”

在专栏中，《生活》杂志记者唐·莫泽描述了比利母亲帕特的恐怖处境：“她完全受制于一个狡猾又暴戾的小男孩，几乎被他逼到精神崩溃的边缘。”比利只吃某家快餐连锁店的汉堡，迫使父亲每天早晨去当地加盟店购买“廉价油腻”的汉堡包。某天，比利将妹妹的洋娃娃冲进马桶。“和恶魔生活在一起，”他母亲说。唯一能让这个被附身的孩子心生恐惧的，是阿尔弗雷德·希区柯克阴郁的面容。于是帕特将这位导演的肖像贴在屋内各处，包括浴室门后，这样她才能安心沐浴。但希望犹存。洛瓦斯登场了——如同《驱魔人》中的马克斯·冯·西多，只是他手持的不是十字架而是电击棒。经过九万次离散试验训练后，《生活》杂志报道，比利已能“准确说出任何食物的名称”。



Immediately after the article came out, hands started shooting up at NSAC meetings when Rimland gave his usually warmly received talk on operant conditioning. “I saw the article in Life,” some concerned mother or father would say. “Aren’t these kids being treated brutally at UCLA?” To quell this unanticipated uprising, Rimland prepared a snappy comeback: “If you think that the children in the article were mistreated, you should see what they do to the kids only two floors away in that very same building! They don’t just yell at them or slap them once in a great while. In that same building, there are people who actually gas children and cut them with sharp knives.” The parents in the room would collectively gasp. After a dramatic pause, he would deliver the punch line: “How else are you going to do an appendectomy or tonsillectomy?”

文章发表后不久，当里姆兰发表他通常广受欢迎的关于操作性条件反射的演讲时，NSAC 会议上立刻有人举手质疑。"我在《生活》杂志上看到了那篇文章，"某位忧心忡忡的家长会说，"这些孩子在加州大学洛杉矶分校是不是受到了残酷对待？"为了平息这场意料之外的抗议，里姆兰准备了一个犀利的反驳："如果你们觉得文章里的孩子受到了虐待，真该看看同一栋楼里仅隔两层楼的那些孩子是怎么被对待的！他们不只是偶尔对孩子吼叫或扇耳光。就在那栋楼里，有人真的会用气体麻醉孩子，然后用锋利的手术刀切开他们。"在场的家长们集体倒吸一口凉气。在戏剧性的停顿后，他抛出点睛之语："不然你们觉得阑尾切除术或扁桃体切除术要怎么做？"



To promote the use of strong aversives at home, Rimland equated the jolts delivered by devices like the Lee-Lectronic Trainer to the harmless static discharges produced by “touching a doorknob or an elevator button on a dry day.” He tried to make cattle prods seem less frightening by christening them “tingle sticks.”

为了推广在家中使用强烈厌恶刺激，里姆兰将李电子训练器这类设备产生的电击等同于"干燥天气触摸门把手或电梯按钮"时产生的无害静电放电。他试图通过将电击棒命名为"微麻棒"来减轻人们对它的恐惧感。



Thankfully, the lanky, curly-haired, soft-spoken graduate student dispatched from UCLA to NSAC chapters around the country to train parents in Lovaas’s method—Mark Rimland’s speech coach, David Ryback—focused less on punishment and more on rewarding engagement in the task at hand with praise and M&Ms. Slapping his own thigh while shouting “No!” was as aversive as he was willing to get. Ryback would fly into a town for a week to host presentations at schools that would be piped over the PA system or broadcast on closed-circuit TV, prefacing these sessions with discussion groups for parents so that it was clear from the start that they were going to play a central role in the process as their children’s “co-therapists.” After years of being treated like pariahs by medical professionals, parents were grateful to finally be recognized as powerful allies in their children’s treatment.

幸运的是，这位从加州大学洛杉矶分校派往全国 NSAC 分会、负责向家长传授洛瓦斯方法的瘦高卷发研究生——马克·里姆兰德的言语教练大卫·赖贝克——更注重用表扬和 M&M 巧克力豆来奖励孩子参与任务，而非惩罚。他最多只会拍打自己的大腿并大喊"不行"作为惩戒。赖贝克会飞往某个城镇停留一周，在学校通过广播系统或闭路电视进行演示，并在这些课程前为家长举办讨论小组，从一开始就明确告知他们将在过程中扮演孩子"协同治疗师"的核心角色。多年来被医学专业人士视为异类的家长们，终于因被认可为孩子治疗中的重要盟友而心怀感激。



Ryback’s respectful attitude also extended to the children. Instead of viewing them as barely adequate foundations on which to “build a person,” he was in awe of their extraordinary talents and abilities. “They could hear sirens coming from several blocks away, and a phonograph needle clicking two floors below,” he recalls. One day, while waiting for a session to begin in a classroom, a neatly dressed, nonspeaking eleven-year-old named Mickey stood up and drew a meticulously detailed landscape on the blackboard with no preliminary sketching or deliberation. “There was no hesitation, no second-guessing. The image was perfect from the very first line,” Ryback says.

Ryback 的尊重态度也延伸到了孩子们身上。他并未将他们视为"塑造人格"的勉强可用的基础，而是惊叹于他们非凡的天赋与能力。"他们能听到几个街区外传来的警笛声，以及两层楼下留声机唱针的咔嗒声，"他回忆道。有一天，在教室里等待课程开始时，一个衣着整洁、不会说话的十一岁男孩米奇站起来，未经任何预先勾勒或思考，就在黑板上画出了一幅细节精准的风景画。"没有犹豫，没有反复修改。从第一笔开始，画面就完美无缺，"Ryback 说。



Despite Rimland’s tireless cheerleading for aversives, many NSAC parents refused to use them, including Ruth Sullivan. “No, I never let anyone do that to Joe,” she says. “My gut told me it wasn’t a good idea.” The president of the Manhattan chapter, Anita Zatlow, also declined to jump on the bandwagon. “Today we are plagued with increased numbers of experimentalists calling themselves ‘therapists’ who practice a variety of do-it-yourself aversive techniques on vulnerable children,” she wrote in response to one of Rimland’s pro-shock editorials. “Who will protect them from abusive ‘quacks’? What message is really received by the already disoriented autistic child/recipient of aversives? Can aversives create anxiety and, if so, might not the ‘treatment’ increase the pathological behavior? No one knows for sure.” Rimland’s response was to call parents like Zatlow “irrational” and “sanctimonious.”

尽管里姆兰不遗余力地为厌恶疗法摇旗呐喊，许多 NSAC 的家长仍拒绝使用，包括露丝·沙利文。“不，我绝不允许任何人那样对待乔，”她说，“我的直觉告诉我这不是个好主意。”曼哈顿分会主席安妮塔·扎特洛也拒绝跟风。“如今，越来越多的实验者自称‘治疗师’，对脆弱的儿童实施各种自创的厌恶疗法，这让我们深受其害，”她在回应里姆兰一篇支持电击疗法的社论时写道，“谁来保护他们免受那些滥用‘江湖郎中’的伤害？本就迷失方向的孤独谱系儿童在接受厌恶疗法时，真正接收到的是什么信息？厌恶疗法是否会引发焦虑？如果会，那么这种‘治疗’会不会反而加剧病态行为？没有人能确定。”里姆兰的回应则是将扎特洛这样的家长称为“非理性”和“假道学”。



A parallel debate was raging among behaviorists. On the basis of his theory that autistic children would never be capable of learning unless their autistic behaviors were extinguished first, Lovaas was able to convince Skinner that they were the exception to the rule of not using punishment on human subjects. By 1988, the senior psychologist felt compelled to issue a statement clarifying his position. “If brief and harmless aversive stimuli,” Skinner wrote, “made precisely contingent on the self-destructive or other excessive behavior, suppress the behavior and leave the children free to develop in other ways, I believe it can be justified.” But he took care to add: “To remain satisfied with punishment without exploring nonpunitive alternatives is the real mistake.”

一场平行的争论在行为主义者中激烈展开。基于其理论——自闭症儿童除非先消除自闭行为，否则永远无法学习——洛瓦斯成功说服斯金纳，认为这些儿童是例外，可以不受"不对人类受试者使用惩罚"这一规则的约束。到 1988 年，这位资深心理学家感到有必要发表声明澄清立场。斯金纳写道："如果短暂且无害的厌恶刺激，精确地针对自残或其他过度行为，能抑制这些行为，并让孩子自由地以其他方式发展，我认为这是合理的。"但他谨慎地补充道："满足于惩罚而不探索非惩罚性的替代方案，才是真正的错误。"



It was a mistake that overworked hospital administrators and overzealous ward attendants were willing to risk making. Though Lovaas promoted the use of aversives as a way of liberating children from institutions, the harsh techniques he legitimized at UCLA were eagerly embraced in state hospitals across the country as a way of keeping problem patients in line. A rage for “behavior mod” swept the field, hastened by a lack of professional standards and ethics guiding the conduct of behavioral therapists.

这是一个过度劳累的医院管理人员和过于热心的病房护理员都愿意冒险犯下的错误。尽管洛瓦斯提倡将厌恶疗法作为让孩子摆脱收容机构的手段，但他在加州大学洛杉矶分校合法化的严苛技术，却被全国各地的州立医院热切采纳，作为约束问题患者的手段。对“行为矫正”的狂热席卷了这一领域，而行为治疗师缺乏专业标准和伦理指导则加速了这一趋势。



In some states, it was possible to hang out a shingle as a “behavior expert” after attending a one-day workshop in a hotel ballroom. Ward attendants were urged to “be creative” in coming up with innovative punishments, and orderlies in newly formed behavior units would arm themselves with bottles of hot sauce to douse the lips and tongues of uncooperative patients.

在某些州，只需在酒店宴会厅参加一天的研讨会，就能挂出“行为专家”的招牌。病房护理员被鼓励在创新惩罚方式上“发挥创意”，而新成立的行为科室的勤杂工则会随身携带辣椒酱瓶，用来涂抹不合作患者的嘴唇和舌头。



VIII



IN 1966, A YOUNG neurologist began working on a residential ward at the Bronx Psychiatric Center after serving an internship at Mt. Zion in San Francisco and a residency at UCLA. He already knew that, in addition to being a doctor, he wanted to be a writer like Freud or Darwin—a precise observer of the world who wrote literarily but with scientific accuracy. He would fill up hundreds of pages in his notebooks (with an occasional boost from methamphetamine), staying up all night in transports of inspiration. In the nocturnal underground of San Francisco where he consorted with Hells Angels, poets, and other members of the bohemian demimonde, this bearded, burly doctor-in-training—who set a state weightlifting record with his six-hundred-pound squat—called himself by his middle name, Wolf. But now that he had moved east and left his druggie days behind him, he resumed using the name he was born with in London: Oliver Sacks.

1966 年，一位年轻的神经科医生在旧金山锡安山医院完成实习，并在加州大学洛杉矶分校完成住院医师培训后，开始在布朗克斯精神病中心的住院病房工作。他早已明白，除了成为一名医生，他还想成为像弗洛伊德或达尔文那样的作家——以文学笔触精准观察世界，兼具科学严谨性。他会在笔记本上写满数百页内容（偶尔借助安非他命提神），在灵感迸发中彻夜不眠。在旧金山的深夜地下世界，他与地狱天使、诗人及其他波西米亚边缘人厮混，这位留着胡须、体格魁梧的实习医生——曾以六百磅深蹲创下州举重纪录——自称中间名"沃尔夫"。但如今他迁居东部，告别了迷幻岁月，重新使用自己在伦敦出生时的本名：奥利弗·萨克斯。



In the dismal warehouse for hopeless cases known as Ward 23, he met a pair of identical twins named George and Charles Finn who had been variously diagnosed as autistic, schizophrenic, and mentally retarded. But despite the impoverishment of their surroundings, the twins carried a glory of numerical symmetry in their heads. “Give us a date!” they would cry in unison, and they were instantly able to calculate the day of the week for any date in a multiple-thousand-year span. As they executed these seemingly impossible cogitations, they would focus their attention inward—their eyes darting back and forth behind thick glasses—as if they were consulting an internal calendar that spanned dozens of millennia or more.

在被称为 23 号病房的绝望仓库里，他遇到了一对同卵双胞胎乔治·芬恩和查尔斯·芬恩，他们曾被诊断为孤独谱系、精神分裂症和智力障碍。尽管身处贫瘠的环境，这对双胞胎的脑海中却承载着数字对称的奇迹。“给我们一个日期！”他们会齐声喊道，然后立刻就能算出数千年跨度内任何日期对应的星期几。在执行这些看似不可能的运算时，他们会将注意力转向内心——眼睛在厚镜片后快速转动——仿佛在查阅一本跨越数万年的内部日历。



The twins’ calendar-calculating abilities were just one aspect of their extraordinary cognitive gifts. The next time that Sacks saw the twins, they were raptly enjoying a conversation that consisted solely of numbers. George would utter a string of digits, and Charles would turn them over in his mind and nod; then Charles would reply in similar fashion, and George would smile approvingly. In a case history published twenty years later in The Man Who Mistook His Wife for a Hat, Sacks wrote that the brothers (called John and Michael in the book) looked like “two connoisseurs wine-tasting, sharing rare tastes, rare appreciations.” At first, he had no idea what they were doing, but he took notes on these cryptic exchanges anyway. “I was attracted by their uncanny twinship, their twin bonding,” he explains, adding that he felt a special kinship with the Finns because he had “a thing for numbers” himself. Upon consulting a book of mathematical tables at home, he was shocked to discover that the twins were instantaneously calculating six-digit prime numbers, a feat that even a computer would have found difficult to pull off at the time. The next time he visited the twins, he made sure to bring his book of tables along, so he could raise the bar by casually dropping an eight-digit prime into the conversation. Surprised and delighted, the Finns invited him to join in their ethereal exchange, seeing him and raising him with even longer primes. Yet George and Charles were incapable of performing simple multiplication, reading, or even tying their own shoes.

这对双胞胎的日历计算能力只是他们非凡认知天赋的一个方面。萨克斯再次见到他们时，他们正全神贯注地进行着一场完全由数字构成的对话。乔治会念出一串数字，查尔斯在脑海中琢磨后点头回应；接着查尔斯用类似方式作答，乔治便赞许地微笑。在二十年后出版的《错把妻子当帽子》的病例报告中，萨克斯写道，这对兄弟（书中化名约翰和迈克尔）看起来就像"两位品酒师在分享珍稀佳酿，品味着罕见的醇香与鉴赏之趣"。起初他完全不明白他们在做什么，但仍记录下这些神秘对话。"我被他们不可思议的双生默契所吸引，"他解释道，并补充说自己对这对芬兰兄弟有种特殊亲近感，因为他本人"对数字有种执念"。回家查阅数学用表后，他震惊地发现这对双胞胎竟能瞬间计算出六位质数——这在当时即便是计算机也难以完成。 下次拜访那对双胞胎时，他特意带上了自己的素数表，以便在交谈中不经意地抛出一个八位素数来提升难度。芬兰人又惊又喜，邀请他加入这场超凡脱俗的交流，并用更长的素数回应他的挑战。然而乔治和查尔斯却连简单的乘法、阅读甚至系鞋带都无法完成。



Then Sacks met José, a twenty-one-year-old autistic man afflicted by frequent seizures. A ward attendant openly referred to him as an “idiot” and said that he was unable to comprehend language and rudimentary concepts like the passage of time. But when Sacks handed the young man his watch and said, “Draw this,” José gazed at it in intense concentration and took up his pencil. The neurologist was astonished by what happened next:

随后萨克斯遇到了何塞，一个二十一岁的孤独谱系青年，饱受频繁癫痫发作之苦。病房护工公然称他为"白痴"，说他无法理解语言和像时间流逝这样基本的概念。但当萨克斯把自己的手表递给年轻人说"画这个"时，何塞全神贯注地凝视着手表，拿起了铅笔。接下来发生的事让这位神经学家震惊不已：



José had drawn the watch with remarkable fidelity, putting in every feature (at least every essential feature—he did not put in WESTCLOX, SHOCK RESISTANT, MADE IN USA), not just “the time” . . . The general grasp of the thing, its “feel,” had been strikingly brought out—all the more strikingly if, as the attendant said, José had no idea of time. And otherwise there was an odd mixture of close, even obsessive, accuracy, with curious (and, I felt, droll) elaborations and variations.

何塞以惊人的精确度画出了那只手表，细致入微地呈现了每一个特征（至少是每一个关键特征——他没有画出“WESTCLOX”、“防震”、“美国制造”），不仅仅是“时间”……他对这件物品的整体把握，它的“感觉”，被惊人地展现了出来——如果像看护人所说，何塞对时间毫无概念，那就更显得惊人了。此外，还有一种奇特的混合：近乎执着的精确，与古怪的（我觉得还挺滑稽的）修饰和变化交织在一起。



“I had never seen such an ability before,” Sacks recalls. “José was fond of the non-human world, and especially the botanical world, as I am. Like his drawing of my watch, his images of dandelions and other things had feeling as well as great accuracy.” Inspired by his experiences with José and the twins, Sacks began exploring other ways of forging connections with the patients on Ward 23. He started taking them for walks in the New York Botanical Garden, invited them to join him at the pool table in the day room, and brought in his own piano to entertain them with music. “They would gather around me when I sat down to play. They might keep time; they would smile; they might dance; they might sing,” he says. “Some of them had musical talent and might play a few notes, which meant, ‘Can you play that?’”
